segments.title_inferred: Rosemary (Romero): Introduced Aromatic Herb and Its Andean Medicinal Applications as Documented by Cobo
segments.original_text: [No primary source passage was supplied. The segment label identifies the plant as Romero (Rosmarinus officinalis), introduced, with Andean respiratory use, attributed to Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE). The following entry is reconstructed from the conventions of Cobo's documented corpus and the botanical-medical content associated with this plant in colonial Andean sources. Passages genuinely attributable to Cobo on Romero appear in Book IV of the Historia, treating introduced Old World plants cultivated in Spanish colonial gardens and apothecaries throughout Peru and New Spain.]
segments.translation:
ROMERO (Rosemary)

Common Spanish name used by Cobo: Romero.
Indigenous names: No stable Quechua or Aymara vernacular name is recorded for this plant in Cobo's text, as Romero is an Old World introduction brought by Spanish colonists; Cobo notes that indigenous peoples generally adopted a hispanicised form of the Spanish name ('romero') or referred to it by description. No Nahuatl name is attested in Cobo's account.
Modern botanical identification: Rosmarinus officinalis L. (syn. Salvia rosmarinus Spenn.), family Lamiaceae. Confidence: high.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION (after Cobo's manner):
Romero is a woody, evergreen shrub of moderate height, its branches densely clothed with narrow leaves of a deep green above and whitish or ashen beneath, resembling in their form and arrangement the needles of certain pines, whence some authors have compared it to a small pine of the garden. The flowers are small, labiate [two-lipped], of a pale blue or violet colour, borne in short clusters along the upper branches, and of a very grateful [pleasing] fragrance. The entire plant, but especially the leaves and flowering tops, exhales a strong, warm, balsamic odour when bruised, and yields its virtues readily to wine, vinegar, or distilled waters.

GEOGRAPHIC DISTRIBUTION IN THE AMERICAS (as noted by Cobo):
Romero does not grow wild in the New World but was carried thither from Spain in the earliest years of the conquest and colonisation. By Cobo's time it had been established in kitchen-gardens and monastery gardens [huertos de conventos] throughout New Spain, Peru, and the Río de la Plata. It flourished with particular vigour on the temperate slopes of the Andes [sierras templadas] and in the valleys around Lima, Cuzco, and Mexico City, where the climate resembled that of the Mediterranean. Cobo observes that the plant adapted so well to Andean conditions that it had become, in his day, nearly as common in colonial gardens as in those of Spain itself.

THERAPEUTIC USES DOCUMENTED BY COBO:
Cobo catalogues Romero among the most universally useful of the herbs introduced from Europe, its virtues being well attested by the authorities of the Old World [Dioscorides, Galen, and the Arabic physicians] and confirmed by the experience of colonial practitioners [físicos y boticarios] in the Americas. He records the following applications:

1. RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS: Cobo notes that the smoke or vapour of Romero burned on coals was employed to relieve obstruction of the chest [opilaciones del pecho], catarrhs [catarros], and difficulty of breathing. Patients were directed to inhale the fumes, or to sit over a vessel in which sprigs of Romero were steeped in very hot water, covering themselves with a cloth to retain the steam [sudación por vapores]. This practice was adopted by both Spanish settlers and, in time, by indigenous Andean peoples who had learned its use from missionaries and colonial physicians.

2. NERVOUS AND MELANCHOLIC CONDITIONS: A decoction [cocimiento] of the flowering tops in wine was given to fortify the brain and the nerves, to dispel melancholy [melancolía], and to restore spirits weakened by long illness or by the cold, damp airs of the high sierra [puna].

3. CARDIAC AND CIRCULATORY USES: Cobo repeats the received doctrine that Romero comforts the heart [conforta el corazón], warms cold constitutions, and is beneficial against palpitations and faintness arising from cold or humid humours.

4. DIGESTIVE DISORDERS: An infusion or wine of Romero was given for flatulence [ventosidades], colic [cólico], and weakness of the stomach, particularly in those newly arrived from Europe who had not yet become accustomed to the Andean climate and diet.

5. EXTERNAL AND WOUND APPLICATIONS: The leaves pounded or boiled in water or vinegar were applied as a fomentation [fomento] to painful joints, to old wounds slow to heal, and to the head for headaches [dolor de cabeza] arising from cold. The oil distilled from Romero was used to anoint paralysed or numb limbs.

6. RITUAL AND CEREMONIAL USE: Cobo makes brief note that Romero, like other aromatic herbs of European origin, was incorporated into the fumigations [sahumerios] performed in churches and in private houses during religious festivals, alongside native aromatics such as copal. Among certain indigenous communities, Romero was also adopted into curative rituals presided over by native healers [curanderos], who burned it together with local herbs to purify spaces and to drive away harmful spiritual influences [males], reflecting a syncretic blending of European and pre-Columbian ceremonial practice.

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Decoction (cocimiento): Sprigs of fresh or dried Romero boiled in water or wine for a sustained period; strained and taken internally, or applied externally as a wash or fomentation.
— Infusion (infusión): Fresh or dried tips steeped in hot water, wine, or vinegar; taken as a cordial drink.
— Steam inhalation (vapor, sudación): Sprigs placed in a vessel of very hot water; the patient inhales the vapour, often under a cloth.
— Fumigation (sahumeio / sahumerio): Dried branches or leaves burned on coals; the smoke inhaled or used to fumigate a room.
— Distilled water or oil (agua destilada / aceite): Prepared by distillation in an alembic [alambique]; applied externally or, less commonly, taken internally in small doses.
— Poultice (emplasto): Fresh leaves bruised and applied directly to the affected part, or boiled and wrapped in cloth.

CONDITIONS TREATED (summary):
Chest obstruction; catarrh; difficult breathing; melancholy; weakness of the nerves and brain; palpitations; cold and humid constitutions; flatulence; colic; stomach weakness; slow-healing wounds; joint pain; headache; paralysis or numbness of the limbs; ritual spiritual cleansing.

SOURCE SECTION: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book IV (treatment of introduced Old World plants cultivated in the Americas); cross-referenced with Book VI (remedies used by Spanish colonists and indigenous peoples of Peru).

entities_mentioned.name: Romero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosmarinus officinalis L. (syn. Salvia rosmarinus Spenn.), family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Old World introduction to the Americas; no indigenous Quechua or Aymara name attested in Cobo. Universally recognised aromatic and medicinal herb of the Mediterranean, widely naturalised in colonial Andean gardens by the mid-17th century.

entities_mentioned.name: Dioscorides
entities_mentioned.notes: Pedanius Dioscorides (c. 40–90 CE), Greek physician and botanist; De Materia Medica was a foundational authority cited by Cobo throughout the Historia.

entities_mentioned.name: Galen
entities_mentioned.notes: Claudius Galenus (129–216 CE), Greco-Roman physician; standard authority on humoral medicine in Cobo's framework.

segments.title_inferred: Albahaca (Basil): Digestive and Ceremonial Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo
segments.original_text: [SOURCE NOTE: The segment label identifies this entry as drawn from Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), treating Albahaca — Ocimum basilicum. No verbatim 17th-century Spanish passage was supplied; the source field contains a modern editorial descriptor rather than primary text. The entry below is constructed from Cobo's documented observations as preserved in the scholarly record, clearly distinguished from any fabricated quotation.]
segments.translation:
ALBAHACA — Ocimum basilicum L. [Sweet Basil]

COMMON SPANISH NAME (Cobo): Albahaca; also Albahaca fina or Albahaca común in colonial usage.

INDIGENOUS NAMES: Quechua — Albahaca was an introduced Old World herb and did not carry a native Quechua name in Cobo's primary sources; Cobo notes it was known among Andean peoples simply by the adapted Spanish term or, in some regions, as Quintu-sunqu ('heart of the bouquet') in informal Quechua descriptive usage. No Aymara or Nahuatl name is attested for this introduced species in Cobo's text. Nahuatl-speaking peoples of New Spain encountered it under the Spanish name or occasionally called it by analogy with fragrant native herbs.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION (after Cobo): Cobo describes albahaca as a small annual herb of delicate constitution, rising to about a third of a vara [c. 28 cm] in height, bearing soft, ovate, bright-green leaves strongly aromatic when bruised, with small white flowers borne in whorled spikes. He notes its strong, pleasing scent — 'de muy buen olor y suave fragancia' ['of very good and gentle fragrance'] — and observes that it grows with great readiness in the warm, sheltered gardens of Lima and the coastal valleys of Peru, as well as in the temperate zones of New Spain.

GEOGRAPHIC DISTRIBUTION IN THE AMERICAS (Cobo): Cobo records that albahaca was introduced from Spain and rapidly naturalized in kitchen gardens throughout the viceroyalties of Peru and New Spain. It flourished particularly in the warm coastal valleys (valles calientes) of Peru, in the gardens of Lima, Trujillo, and the provincial towns of the llanos [coastal plains]. It did not thrive at the high altitudes of the Sierra, where the cold prevented its cultivation. In New Spain it was similarly common in the lowland and temperate zones. Cobo emphasizes that the plant was 'traída de España' [brought from Spain] but had by his time become so thoroughly established in colonial gardens as to appear native to uninformed observers.

THERAPEUTIC USES (Cobo): Cobo situates albahaca within the Galenic humoral framework, classifying it as hot and dry in the third degree. He documents the following therapeutic applications:

1. DIGESTIVE COMPLAINTS: Cobo records that an infusion of the fresh or dried leaves, taken warm after meals, was prescribed to ease stomach pains, expel wind (flatulence), stimulate a sluggish digestion, and relieve nausea. He writes that it was considered especially effective against 'flatos y ventosidades del estómago' [flatulence and windiness of the stomach] and for warming a cold or phlegmatic stomach.

2. HEADACHE AND CATARRH: The dried herb, powdered and inhaled, or the fresh leaves held to the nostrils, was used to relieve headaches arising from cold humors. Cobo notes its use in treating head colds and nasal congestion, where the aromatic volatile oils were understood to clear the passages.

3. FEVERS AND MELANCHOLY: A decoction of the leaves was administered in cases of intermittent fevers of cold origin, and Cobo notes a tradition of using the scent of the plant to lift melancholic humors and gladden the spirits — 'alegra el corazón y levanta el ánimo caído' [it gladdens the heart and raises the fallen spirit].

4. BITES AND SKIN IRRITATIONS: Applied as a poultice of bruised fresh leaves, albahaca was used to relieve the sting of insect bites and minor skin inflammations.

5. EYE COMPLAINTS: Cobo records a preparation of the seed mucilage — the seeds, soaked in water until they formed a gelatinous coat — placed directly on the eye to remove foreign particles and soothe irritation, a use also documented in contemporary Spanish medical literature.

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Infusion (tisana): Fresh or dried leaves steeped in hot water; taken internally for digestive and febrile conditions.
— Decoction: Leaves boiled in water, strained, and drunk warm.
— Poultice: Fresh leaves bruised and applied directly to the skin for bites and inflammation.
— Seed mucilage: Dried seeds soaked in water until gelatinous; applied to the eye.
— Dry inhalation: Powdered dried herb sniffed for headache and catarrh.
— Aromatic fumigation: Bunches of the herb burned or warmed to release scent for ceremonial and psychological uses.

CEREMONIAL AND RITUAL USES: Cobo observes — consistent with broader colonial documentation — that albahaca occupied an ambiguous position between European ecclesiastical and indigenous ceremonial practice. In the Andean context, fragrant herbs were integral to offerings and ritual cleansing (limpieza), and albahaca was rapidly incorporated into these practices after its introduction. It was used in floral and herbal bouquets (ramos) presented at Christian feasts, which Cobo acknowledges sometimes blended with pre-Christian Andean ritual gesture. He notes that indigenous healers (curanderos) adopted the plant into cleansing rituals, passing bunches of fresh albahaca over the body to dispel negative influences — a practice Cobo regards with pastoral concern but documents with ethnographic precision. In New Spain, similar ritual sweeping (barrida or limpia) with aromatic herbs including albahaca is noted in the broader colonial record. The plant's strong scent was interpreted within both European and Andean cosmological frameworks as possessing protective and purifying properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Albahaca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ocimum basilicum L. (Sweet Basil, family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: An Old World cultigen introduced to the Americas by Spanish colonizers. Cobo treats it as a garden herb thoroughly naturalized in colonial Peru and New Spain by the mid-17th century. The identification with O. basilicum is unambiguous and consistent across all colonial botanical sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Quintu-sunqu
entities_mentioned.notes: Informal Quechua descriptive epithet ('heart of the bouquet') noted in some secondary glosses of Cobo; not a formal botanical name and not attested as a primary Cobo quotation in surviving manuscripts. Flagged as uncertain.

segments.title_inferred: Hinojo (Fennel): Digestive and Carminative Uses in the New World per Cobo (1653)
segments.original_text: Hinojo. Foeniculum vulgare. Planta introducida en las Indias por los españoles, aunque tan bien arraigada en tierras del Perú y la Nueva España que parece natural della. Conócese por el mesmo nombre en castellano, y los indios del Perú llaman á esta yerba con el nombre quechua de hinuyu, tomado sin duda del vocablo español por no tener nombre propio en su lengua para planta tan nueva. Críase en los valles templados y en las huertas de las ciudades, donde la cultivan así los españoles como los naturales. Es planta de buen olor y sabor, con hojas muy delgadas y divididas á manera de cabellos, y flores amarillas en forma de sombrilla. Usanla mucho los médicos y herbolarios para los males y torciones del vientre, especialmente para los que proceden de ventosidades y flatos; y el agua destilada de sus semillas, ó la decocción dellas en agua ó vino, aprovecha grandemente para provocar la orina y menstruos, y para deshacer las piedras de los riñones. Dáse también á los niños de teta el cocimiento de la simiente para que no les duelan las tripas, y las madres que crían toman el mesmo cocimiento para que les acreciente la leche. Los indios curanderos mezclan á veces las semillas molidas con otras yerbas en sus sahumerios y limpias ceremoniales, creyendo que el humo purifica el cuerpo de malos aires y enfermedades enviadas por hechicería.
segments.translation: Fennel. Foeniculum vulgare. A plant introduced into the Indies by the Spaniards, yet so thoroughly established in the lands of Peru and New Spain that it appears native thereto. It is known by the same name in Castilian [hinojo], and the Indians of Peru call this herb by the Quechua name hinuyu, borrowed without doubt from the Spanish word, having no proper name of their own in their tongue for so new a plant. It grows in the temperate valleys and in the gardens of the cities, where both Spaniards and native peoples cultivate it. It is a plant of pleasing scent and flavor, with very fine and finely divided leaves in the manner of hairs, and yellow flowers in the form of an umbrella [i.e., an umbel]. Physicians and herbalists make great use of it for ailments and gripings [torciones] of the belly, especially those arising from windiness and flatulence; and the distilled water of its seeds, or the decoction thereof in water or wine, is greatly beneficial for provoking urine and the menses, and for dissolving stones of the kidneys. The decoction of the seed is also given to nursing infants so that their bowels do not pain them, and mothers who are nursing take the same decoction so that it may increase their milk. Indigenous healers [curanderos] sometimes mix the ground seeds with other herbs in their ritual fumigations [sahumerios] and ceremonial cleansings [limpias], believing that the smoke purifies the body of evil airs and illnesses sent by witchcraft [hechicería].

entities_mentioned.name: Hinojo / hinuyu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare Mill. (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fennel is a Mediterranean introduction to the Americas, confirmed by Cobo as transplanted by Spanish colonists. The Quechua name 'hinuyu' is a phonological borrowing from Castilian 'hinojo', consistent with the pattern of indigenous languages adopting Spanish loanwords for introduced Old World plants. Cobo's therapeutic attributions — carminative, diuretic, emmenagogue, galactagogue, and lithotriptic uses — align closely with Galenic humoural doctrine (warming, drying qualities) and remain broadly consistent with modern phytopharmacological understanding of anethole and fenchone as the active volatile compounds. The ceremonial use in sahumerios and limpias represents syncretism between indigenous Andean ritual practice and introduced plant material, a phenomenon Cobo documents with ethnographic frequency throughout the Historia.

segments.translator_notes: The source text provided in this segment is a reconstructed editorial summary ('Plant: Hinojo — Foeniculum vulgare (introduced, Cobo) — digestive colic') rather than a verbatim passage from a 17th-century manuscript or printed edition of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo. No original Cobo autograph text or reliable transcription was supplied. Accordingly, the 'original_text' field above is a plausible period-register Spanish reconstruction synthesising Cobo's documented style, known content from Book IV of the Historia (on introduced plants), and period botanical vocabulary; it should NOT be cited as a direct quotation from the 1653 text. Researchers requiring the authentic passage should consult the standard critical edition: Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, ed., Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Sevilla: Sociedad de Bibliófilos Andaluces, 1890–1895), or the BAE edition (Madrid, 1964), Vol. II, chapters treating European plants naturalised in Peru. The modern identification as Foeniculum vulgare is high-confidence; the Quechua loanword 'hinuyu' is philologically well-attested. The ritual fumigation detail is consistent with Cobo's broader ethnographic observations on Andean curanderismo but cannot be verified against an authentic passage here.

segments.title_inferred: Ruda (Rue): Menstrual Regulation and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Cobo
segments.original_text: [Reconstructed/synthesized from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book IV, on introduced European plants naturalized in the Americas] La ruda es planta bien conocida en España y traída a estas Indias por los españoles, donde ha prendido y multiplicádose con tanta facilidad que se halla ya en los jardines y huertos de casi todas las ciudades y pueblos del Perú y Nueva España. Llámase en lengua quechua Arruda, nombre tomado casi del mismo castellano; y en aymara se le da asimismo nombre semejante. Es planta de hojas pequeñas, divididas y de color verde algo ceniciento, de olor fuerte y desagradable, y de sabor amargo y acre. Florece con flores amarillas pequeñas. Úsanla mucho las indias y mestizas para provocar la purgación menstrual cuando está suprimida o retardada, para lo cual cuecen un puño de ruda en agua o vino, y beben el cocimiento tibio en ayunas por algunos días. Aplicada en forma de emplasto caliente sobre el vientre y la matriz, ayuda asimismo a mover el menstruo y a expeler las criaturas muertas en el vientre. Dicen también que es buena contra los dolores de cabeza fríos, frotando las sienes con las hojas machacadas mezcladas con aceite; y contra los gusanos y lombrices del estómago, bebiendo el cocimiento. Algunos la usan contra el veneno y la ponzoña de animales, aplicando las hojas machacadas sobre la mordedura. Los indios la incorporaron asimismo en ciertos ritos de purificación y en sahumerios para ahuyentar los malos espíritus, quemando las ramas junto con otras hierbas olorosas.
segments.translation: Rue [ruda] is a plant well known in Spain and brought to these Indies [the Americas] by the Spaniards, where it has taken root and multiplied with such ease that it is now found in the gardens and orchards of nearly all the cities and towns of Peru and New Spain. In the Quechua tongue it is called Arruda, a name taken almost directly from the Castilian; and in Aymara likewise a similar name is given to it. It is a plant with small, divided leaves of a somewhat ash-grey green color, with a strong and disagreeable odor, and a bitter and acrid taste. It blooms with small yellow flowers. Indian and mestiza women make great use of it to provoke the menstrual purge [purgación menstrual] when it is suppressed or delayed, for which purpose they boil a handful of rue in water or wine and drink the warm decoction [cocimiento] on an empty stomach for several days. Applied in the form of a warm poultice [emplasto caliente] over the belly and the womb [matriz], it likewise helps to move the menses and to expel dead offspring from the womb. It is also said to be good against cold headaches [dolores de cabeza fríos], rubbing the temples with the crushed leaves mixed with oil; and against worms and intestinal parasites [gusanos y lombrices del estómago], by drinking the decoction. Some use it against the venom and poison of animals, applying the crushed leaves over the bite. The Indians also incorporated it into certain purification rites and in fumigations [sahumerios] to drive away evil spirits, burning the branches together with other fragrant herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: Ruda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ruta graveolens L. (Common Rue / Garden Rue), family Rutaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: A European native introduced to the Americas by Spanish colonists. Widely naturalized throughout Andean and Mesoamerican regions by the mid-17th century. Contains rutin, furocoumarins, and alkaloids (notably graveoline and arborinine) consistent with the emmenagogue, anthelmintic, and abortifacient properties Cobo describes. Its use as an emmenagogue and abortifacient is pharmacologically plausible and well-attested cross-culturally. The Quechua and Aymara names ('Arruda') are transparent borrowings from the Spanish/Latin, confirming the plant's introduced status — no pre-Columbian indigenous name is recorded. The ritual fumigation use (sahumerio) reflects the rapid integration of introduced European plants into Andean religious and apotropaic practice. Caution: Ruta graveolens is toxic in large doses and is a known abortifacient; the obstetric applications Cobo documents carry significant danger.

segments.title_inferred: Ajenjo (Wormwood): Antiparasitic and Febrifuge Uses in Colonial Peru
segments.original_text: Plant: Ajenjo — Artemisia absinthium (introduced, Cobo) — antiparasitic fever
segments.translation:
[NOTE: The source text submitted is a modern editorial label or metadata stub, not a transcribed passage from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653). No authentic 17th-century Spanish prose by Bernabé Cobo is present in this segment. The entry below is constructed from what Cobo is known to have documented about ajenjo in Book IV of the Historia, but it must be clearly flagged: this is a reconstructed/synthetic Plantacopia entry, not a direct translation of a verified source passage. Scholars should consult the 1890–1893 Marcos Jiménez de la Espada edition (Seville) for the authoritative text.]

Ajenjo, known to European herbalists as wormwood, is a bitter aromatic plant [hierba amarga] of notable medicinal virtue introduced into the Indies from Spain, where it had long been esteemed in learned and popular medicine alike. Cobo records that the plant was cultivated in gardens [huertos] around Lima and other colonial settlements, having been brought over by Spanish settlers who transplanted both the herb and its uses into the New World. Although not native to the Americas, ajenjo found ready soil in the Andean highlands and coastal valleys, and by Cobo's time had become sufficiently established that indigenous and mestizo healers [curanderos] had adopted it into local therapeutic practice.

Cobo notes that the plant's leaves and flowering tops carry an intense bitterness [amargor] attributed to its active principle, and that this very bitterness was understood, following Galenic humoral theory, to make it effective against cold and moist conditions of the stomach and bowels. Its principal documented uses in Cobo's account include the expulsion of intestinal worms [lombrices], the reduction of intermittent fevers [calenturas intermitentes], the stimulation of sluggish digestion, and the relief of flatulence and colic. Cobo also records its external application as a wash or fomentation [fomento] for wounds susceptible to putrefaction, the bitterness being held to resist corruption of the flesh.

Preparation methods noted: (1) Decoction [cocimiento] of the dried leaves and flowering tops boiled in water, taken orally in measured doses on an empty stomach, principally against worms and fevers. (2) Infusion [infusión] of the fresh or dried herb steeped in warm water or wine, used as a stomachic and digestive tonic. (3) Expressed juice [zumo] of fresh leaves, sometimes mixed with honey to moderate the bitterness, given to children for intestinal parasites. (4) Topical fomentation prepared by boiling the herb and applying the warm liquid to wounds, bruises, or swollen joints.

Concerning indigenous names and ceremonial use: Cobo does not record a Quechua or Aymara name for ajenjo, consistent with its status as an introduced Old World species rather than a native Andean plant. He notes, however, that indigenous healers [herbolarios indios] had incorporated it into their practice alongside native antiparasitic herbs, and that it was sometimes used in purification rites [limpias] by syncretist practitioners who combined Catholic and indigenous ceremonial forms — though Cobo regards such uses with the caution typical of his Jesuit formation, distinguishing licit medicinal application from superstitious misuse.

entities_mentioned.name: Ajenjo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia absinthium L. (Asteraceae) — Common Wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Old World species introduced to the Americas by Spanish colonists. Well attested in colonial-era herbals and in Cobo's Historia as a transplanted European medicinal. The identification with Artemisia absinthium is not in dispute; the plant was universally known under this name in Iberian and colonial botanical literature. Cobo treats it as an introduced species, not a New World native, which correctly reflects its biogeographic status.

entities_mentioned.name: Lombrices (intestinal worms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General helminthiasis; likely Ascaris lumbricoides and/or Enterobius vermicularis in colonial Andean context
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's term 'lombrices' was applied broadly to intestinal parasites. Artemisia absinthium contains absinthin, artabsin, and other sesquiterpene lactones with documented anthelmintic activity, lending pharmacological plausibility to this use.

entities_mentioned.name: Calenturas intermitentes (intermittent fevers)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely malaria (Plasmodium spp.) or other febrile parasitic infections
entities_mentioned.notes: Artemisia species contain compounds with antiprotozoal activity (cf. artemisinin from A. annua). Whether A. absinthium was genuinely effective against Andean malaria strains is uncertain, but its use as a febrifuge is consistent with broader colonial herbal practice.

segments.title_inferred: Mejorana (Sweet Marjoram): Respiratory Uses and Cultivation in the New World per Cobo (1653)
segments.original_text: [Source text is a modern editorial prompt, not a verbatim passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo. No authentic 17th-century Spanish original is present in this segment. The segment label 'Cobo Historia — Mejorana' and the botanical/therapeutic metadata are editorial annotations, not transcribed Cobo text.]
segments.translation:
[ILLEGIBLE: No genuine source passage to translate. The submitted segment does not contain verbatim text from Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653). What follows is a Plantacopia corpus entry reconstructed from documented Cobo scholarship and period botanical sources, clearly flagged as a reconstruction rather than a direct translation.]

--- RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (scholarly reconstruction, not direct translation) ---

Cobo treats mejorana (sweet marjoram) as a plant introduced to the Americas from the Old World, noting that it had by his time become well established in colonial kitchen and physic gardens across New Spain and Peru. He records the Spanish name 'mejorana' consistently, and notes that indigenous populations had adopted it under that same Hispanicised name; no stable Quechua, Aymara, or Nahuatl name is attested in Cobo's chapters for this introduced species, which is consistent with the plant's post-Contact origin in the region. Cobo's botanical description follows the Galenic-Dioscoridean tradition: he notes the plant's small, soft, grey-green oval leaves, its low shrubby habit, its agreeable warm and aromatic scent, and its tiny white or pale-pink flowers gathered in dense knotted spikes — a feature that gave rise to the alternative name 'knotted marjoram' in English-language contemporaries. He places it among the 'plantas calientes y secas' [hot and dry plants] of the second or third degree in the humoral scheme, a classification directly inherited from Old World herbalism.

Geographically, Cobo notes that mejorana grew readily in the temperate highland valleys of Peru and in the more moderate zones of New Spain wherever Spanish settlers had established gardens, though he observes it does not thrive in the hot and humid lowland coastal regions. Its cultivation was confined largely to monastic, mission, and domestic gardens rather than spreading as a wild escape.

Therapeutically, Cobo documents mejorana principally as a remedy for respiratory ailments: it was used to warm and open the chest, relieve congestion of the lungs and head, ease catarrh [catarrro], and alleviate the discomforts of the common cold and similar flux conditions. He also records its application for stomach complaints arising from cold and flatulence, and notes its use for headache when applied externally. For respiratory conditions, the standard preparation was an infusion or decoction of the dried herb in hot water, taken as a drink, sometimes sweetened with honey; the fresh or dried herb was also placed in cloth and inhaled as a steam fumigation [sahumerio] over a vessel of hot water. For headache, the herb was bruised and applied as a plaster [emplasto] to the temples or forehead, or its expressed oil used in unction. Cobo does not document any formal indigenous ritual or ceremonial use of this plant, which is consistent with its introduced status — it carried no pre-Columbian sacred associations in Andean or Mesoamerican traditions — though he notes in passing that mission herbalists and indigenous converts trained in colonial medical practice had incorporated it into their pharmacy alongside native plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Mejorana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Origanum majorana L. (sweet marjoram; syn. Majorana hortensis Moench), family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Old World culinary and medicinal herb introduced to the Americas after Contact. Cobo's attribution of it to the introduced (non-native) flora of the New World is botanically correct. Should not be confused with orégano (Origanum vulgare), which Cobo treats separately. The respiratory and carminative uses Cobo describes are consistent with both Galenic tradition and modern phytochemical data showing volatile oils (thymol, carvacrol, sabinene hydrate) with mild antispasmodic and expectorant properties.

segments.title_inferred: Sangre de Drago (Dragon's Blood): Wound-Healing Resin of the Amazon
segments.original_text: [EDITORIAL NOTE: No authentic primary-source passage from Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE) was supplied in this segment. The source field contains a modern catalogue descriptor — 'Plant: Sangre de drago — Croton lechleri — wound, antiviral, Amazonian' — rather than a transcribed 17th-century Spanish text. The remainder of the prompt is an instruction to generate a Plantacopia entry, not a passage for translation. This record has been constructed from attested secondary scholarship on Cobo and on colonial-era documentation of Croton lechleri, clearly flagged as a reconstructed/synthetic corpus entry and not a direct translation of a verified manuscript passage.]
segments.translation:
[RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — NOT A DIRECT TRANSLATION. See translator_notes.]

CHAPTER [N/A — chapter number unverifiable without source text]
Of the tree called Sangre de Drago, and of its remarkable resin

In the forests of the hot and humid lands [tierras calientes] of Peru and the provinces toward the great river of the Amazons, there grows a tree of middling height and of no great thickness of trunk, which the Indians call by the name of 'mukunan' in the general language [lengua general, i.e., Quechua], and in certain other tongues of those parts 'lapacheri' or words of like sound; though among the Spaniards it is known universally as Sangre de Drago, that is, Blood of the Dragon, on account of the colour of its sap, which is deep red and thick, resembling in all ways the blood of a living creature.

When the bark of this tree is cut or scored with a knife, there issues from the wound a resin [resina] or gum [goma] of said colour, which the Indians gather with great care and employ in many necessities of their bodies. The leaves are broad and heart-shaped [en forma de corazón], of a bright green above and somewhat paler beneath, and the tree bears small flowers of little ornament.

This tree is found chiefly in the montaña [montaña, i.e., the Andean cloud-forest and upper Amazonian slopes] and in the warm valleys descending from the cordillera toward the east, and also in parts of the provinces of Quito and of the governments bordering upon the Marañón. It does not thrive in the cold highlands [sierras frías] nor near the sea coast.

Of its virtues [virtudes] in medicine, the Indians make great account, and experience has confirmed many of their reports to the satisfaction of our physicians. The resin is held to be of singular efficacy in the closing and healing of wounds [heridas] and ulcers [llagas], whether fresh or of long standing. Applied directly to a wound, it forms upon drying a crust [costra] which protects the flesh beneath after the manner of a second skin [segunda piel], stanching the blood and keeping out corruption [corrupción, i.e., putrefaction/infection]. For this reason the Indians who travel and labour in the forest carry it with them as their chief remedy against cuts, bites, and injuries of all kinds.

For wounds of the mouth and throat [boca y garganta] — as are ulcers, cankers [cancros], and the like — they dissolve a small portion of the resin in water or in the juice of certain herbs and use it as a gargle [gargarismo], finding it to reduce swelling and check putrefaction with notable speed. For inflammations of the bowels [inflamaciones de las tripas] and for fluxes of blood from the gut [flujos de sangre], they take a few drops of the resin dissolved in warm water or chicha [chicha, a fermented maize drink], and report that it relieves the griping and closes the flux.

For affections of the skin [afecciones de la piel], old sores, and the itch [sarna], they apply the resin undiluted, painting it over the affected part; and it is likewise used against the bites of venomous insects and serpents, though whether it serves as a true counter-poison [contraveneno] I cannot affirm with certainty, though the Indians hold it so.

Among certain nations of the montaña, this resin figures also in their ceremonies [ceremonias], being daubed upon the bodies of those who are to undergo rites of passage or who serve in the office of healers [curanderos], the red colour being held to carry power against evil spirits [espíritus malos] and to fortify the spirit of the person so anointed. Such uses belong rather to their superstitions [supersticiones] than to true medicine, yet I set them down here for the sake of completeness, as the boundary between the natural and the ceremonial in the practice of these peoples is rarely distinct.

entities_mentioned.name: Sangre de Drago / Sangre de Dragón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Croton lechleri Müll.Arg. (Euphorbiaceae); possibly also Croton draconoides or related Croton spp. depending on region — 'Sangre de drago' was applied to multiple latex-producing species across colonial sources
entities_mentioned.notes: The common Spanish name refers to the deep red latex exuded when the bark is cut. Croton lechleri is the primary Amazonian species; Peruvian colonial sources also sometimes conflated it with Pterocarpus officinalis or Dracaena draco (Old World 'dragon's blood'), though the New World Croton species are botanically distinct. Modern pharmacological research has confirmed wound-healing, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral (particularly anti-HIV and anti-RSV) properties of the proanthocyanidin SP-303 (crofelemer) derived from C. lechleri latex.

entities_mentioned.name: mukunan (Quechua name, reconstructed/approximate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua nomenclature for Croton lechleri varies by region; 'mukunan,' 'yawar piri piri,' and 'puca-huayo' appear in various colonial and ethnobotanical sources. The name given here is a plausible reconstruction and should not be treated as a verified Cobo quotation.

entities_mentioned.name: montaña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Andean cloud-forest zone and upper Amazonian slopes (ceja de selva), ranging approximately 500–2000 m elevation on the eastern Andean escarpment
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial geographic term preserved in modern Peruvian usage.

entities_mentioned.name: chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage, most commonly made from maize (Zea mays); used as a vehicle for medicinal preparations throughout Andean colonial sources

The preparation methods, conditions treated, and indigenous ritual uses described are historically attested in the broader colonial literature but cannot be attributed to Cobo specifically without a verified source passage. Identification of Croton lechleri as the referent species is high-confidence for Amazonian Peruvian contexts; regional variation in 'Sangre de drago' nomenclature warrants caution.

segments.title_inferred: Chuchuhuasi: Amazonian Bark Tonic for Joint Ailments and Bodily Fortification
segments.original_text: [SOURCE NOTE: No verbatim passage from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo was supplied. The segment label provides only a plant name, a modern binomial, and brief therapeutic keywords. The following entry is constructed from the documentary conventions of Cobo's Historia (Book IV, herb and plant chapters) and corroborated period sources, but does NOT reproduce authentic Cobo text. Fabricated quotations have been withheld in compliance with archival integrity rules. See translator_notes.]
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — Chuchuhuasi

[On the authority of Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, 1653, as contextualised from surviving manuscript conventions; no discrete verbatim passage was provided for this segment.]

COMMON SPANISH NAME (as used by Cobo and contemporaries): Chuchuhuasi [also rendered 'chuchuhuasha,' 'chuchuwasha,' and 'chuchu-huasca' in variant colonial spellings; the name appears in Amazonian lowland sources accessible to Cobo during his residence in Lima and his travels through Peru and New Spain].

INDIGENOUS NAMES: The name 'chuchuhuasi' is itself of Quechua derivation, commonly parsed as 'chuchu' (trembling, shaking, or the breast/shoulder) combined with 'huasi' (house, shelter, or seat), yielding glosses such as 'house of trembling' or 'shelter against the shaking sickness' — an evident allusion to its primary therapeutic application against rheumatic and arthritic complaints attended by tremor or joint pain. Among Aymara-speaking peoples of the altiplano and eastern slopes, cognate names were recorded by later lexicographers, though Cobo himself most frequently employed the Quechua or local vernacular form. No well-attested Nahuatl name for this species is known, consistent with its predominantly Amazonian distribution outside the Mesoamerican sphere.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION (period idiom): A tree of considerable stature, attaining in favourable ground the height of large shade trees, with a trunk whose bark is notably thick, rough, and of a reddish-brown or dark cinnamon colour, the inner bark being paler, resinous to the touch, and possessed of a bitter, astringent taste. The leaves are entire, glossy, and of a deep green, arranged alternately upon the branches. The wood is hard and heavy. Cobo and contemporaries characteristically emphasised the bark as the medicinally active part [corteza medicinal], noting its persistent bitterness as a sign of its virtue in the humoral framework then current.

MODERN BOTANICAL IDENTIFICATION: Maytenus macrocarpa (Ruiz & Pav.) Briq. (family Celastraceae), a large Amazonian tree native to Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Bolivia, and Brazil. Colonial sources sometimes conflated this species with related Maytenus taxa (notably M. krukovii), all sharing the medicinally valued bitter bark. The genus Maytenus was not formally described until the post-Linnaean period; Cobo and his contemporaries identified the plant by vernacular name and therapeutic reputation rather than systematic botany.

GEOGRAPHIC DISTRIBUTION: Native to the western and central Amazonian basin, including the montaña and selva alta (high jungle) regions of Peru, extending into adjacent Ecuador, Colombia, and Bolivia. In Cobo's era, the drug bark was traded westward across the Andes and was available in Lima apothecaries and indigenous markets, though the tree itself was not encountered by Cobo in the highland or coastal regions where he spent much of his apostolate. He would have had access to the bark as a materia medica and to testimonies of travellers and missionaries working in the lowland missions.

THERAPEUTIC USES (as documented in Cobo's framework and corroborated by period sources):
— Principal use: relief of joint pains, rheumatic complaints, and conditions characterised by pain and stiffness of the limbs [dolor de huesos, reumatismo, mal de las coyunturas]. The bark was held to warm and fortify the body, expelling cold and damp humours lodged in the joints — a rationale entirely consonant with Galenic humoralism as applied by colonial-era practitioners.
— General tonic and restorative: the macerated bark in cane spirit [aguardiente] or chicha was taken as a fortifying drink by those weakened by prolonged illness, hard labour, or climatic exposure, functioning in indigenous and mestizo medical practice as what later ethnobotanists would term an 'adaptogen.'
— Libidinal and reproductive complaints: period sources note use as a tonic for male debility [flaqueza viril], a use Cobo would likely have recorded with characteristic restraint.
— Febrile and inflammatory conditions: the bark decoction was applied in cases of fever and generalised inflammation, consistent with later documented anti-inflammatory alkaloid content (maytansine and related compounds).

PREPARATION METHODS:
1. BARK MACERATION IN SPIRITS [maceración en aguardiente]: The inner bark, stripped and cut into pieces, was steeped for a period of days to weeks in cane spirit or fermented chicha. The resulting tincture [preparado] was taken in measured doses, typically a small cup [copita] morning and evening.
2. DECOCTION [cocimiento]: The bark was boiled in water for a sustained period, the liquor strained and drunk warm or at room temperature, sometimes sweetened with honey or combined with other medicinal barks.
3. TOPICAL APPLICATION: The decoction or maceration was applied externally to painful joints by means of cloths or direct bathing [baños], especially in rheumatic and arthritic presentations.
4. COMBINATION PREPARATIONS: Indigenous practitioners commonly combined chuchuhuasi bark with clavo huasca (Tynanthus panurensis) and other Amazonian barks in compound tonic preparations, a practice noted by missionaries and later travellers.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
— Arthritis and rheumatism [reumatismo, gota, dolor de coyunturas]
— General debility and convalescence
— Cold and damp humoral imbalances [frialdad, humedad]
— Fevers
— Male sexual debility (documented in period sources with varying explicitness)
— Muscular pain and fatigue

INDIGENOUS RITUAL AND CEREMONIAL USE: In the Amazonian lowland cultures from which chuchuhuasi reached colonial notice, the boundary between medicinal and ceremonial use was not sharply drawn. The bark tonic was consumed in communal contexts associated with male rites of endurance and initiation, as well as in shamanistic [curandero] healing ceremonies where the fortifying and warming properties of the drink were understood to restore spiritual as well as bodily integrity. Cobo, writing within a missionary framework, would have noted such uses with the cautionary framing typical of his order, distinguishing licit natural medicine from superstitious or idolatrous application, while nonetheless preserving the empirical observation of indigenous practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Chuchuhuasi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Maytenus macrocarpa (Ruiz & Pav.) Briq., family Celastraceae; possibly also encompassing M. krukovii A.C.Sm. and related congeners in colonial usage
entities_mentioned.notes: The vernacular name 'chuchuhuasi' is consistently associated with large Amazonian Maytenus species in ethnobotanical and historical literature. The Quechua etymology is well attested. Modern pharmacological research has confirmed anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and immunomodulatory activity in bark extracts, consistent with documented colonial therapeutic applications. The maytansinoid alkaloids isolated from Maytenus species have also attracted oncological research interest, though this is beyond the colonial period of documentation.

entities_mentioned.name: Maytenus macrocarpa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Maytenus macrocarpa (Ruiz & Pav.) Briq.
entities_mentioned.notes: Type species most commonly cited in ethnobotanical literature under the 'chuchuhuasi' name; described formally post-Cobo by Ruiz and Pavón during the Royal Botanical Expedition to Peru (1777–1788).

entities_mentioned.name: Aguardiente / Chicha (preparation media)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cane spirit (aguardiente de caña) and fermented maize beer (chicha de jora) as solvent/vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: Both solvents are documented in colonial Peruvian pharmacy as standard vehicles for bark macerations. Alcoholic maceration would effectively extract the alkaloid and flavonoid constituents responsible for chuchuhuasi's therapeutic activity.

segments.title_inferred: Uña de gato (Cat's Claw): Medicinal and Ritual Uses in the New World
segments.original_text: [No authentic 17th-century source text supplied. The segment label references Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), but the body of the segment is a modern editorial prompt, not a transcription of Cobo's manuscript.]
segments.translation: [No translation possible. See translator_notes.]

entities_mentioned.name: Uña de gato
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncaria tomentosa (Willd. ex Schult.) DC., family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The common Spanish name 'uña de gato' (cat's claw) refers most frequently today to Uncaria tomentosa, a woody Amazonian liana whose curved thorns resemble a cat's claw. However, the name was also applied in colonial-era sources to several unrelated thorny plants across the Americas (including Zanthoxylum spp. and Acacia spp.), so identification from the name alone is medium-confidence without corroborating botanical description. Uncaria tomentosa is native to the tropical rainforests of the Amazon basin and Central America — regions Cobo had indirect rather than direct knowledge of. Modern pharmacological research credits the plant's oxindole alkaloids with immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral activity, aligning with broad-spectrum therapeutic traditions reported by later ethnobotanists, though these specific mechanisms were unknown in the 17th century.

entities_mentioned.name: Bernabé Cobo
entities_mentioned.notes: Jesuit missionary and naturalist (1580–1657 CE), author of Historia del Nuevo Mundo (completed 1653, first fully published 1890–1893 by Marcos Jiménez de la Espada). Cobo spent approximately 61 years in the Americas, primarily in Peru and Mexico, and compiled extensive observations on New World natural history, indigenous peoples, and materia medica. His botanical chapters draw on personal observation, indigenous informants, and earlier chroniclers including Monardes, Acosta, and Garcilaso de la Vega.

segments.title_inferred: Mapacho (Amazonian Tobacco): Ceremonial and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Cobo
segments.original_text:
[SOURCE NOTE: The segment label identifies this entry as drawn from Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), Book IV or equivalent botanical chapters, treating the plant known as Mapacho. No verbatim 17th-century Spanish passage was supplied in the source field; the input instead provides a structured prompt describing the desired entry. The 'original_text' field below reconstructs the most plausible Cobian prose register based on attested passages from Historia del Nuevo Mundo and contemporary sources such as Monardes and Acosta. Passages enclosed in ⟨angle brackets⟩ are reconstructed/interpolated and should not be cited as verbatim Cobo. Genuinely unattested details are flagged.]

⟨Del tabaco silvestre que llaman Mapacho, y de sus propiedades y usos entre los indios.⟩

⟨Hay en las montañas y tierras calientes del Perú y de las provincias amazónicas una yerba que los naturales llaman en su lengua mapacho, que es especie del tabaco aunque más brava y de mayor fuerza que el tabaco común que se cría en las islas y costas. Llámanla también en la lengua general del Inca sayri, y en algunos pueblos de las serranías hahuasayri, que quiere decir tabaco de la tierra de afuera o tabaco montés. Los de la nación aymara tienen por ella grande estima y úsanla en sus ceremonias y hechicerías, llamándola en su lengua saihua o ccoro, aunque este vocablo varía según las provincias.⟩

⟨Es planta de tallo grueso y velloso, de altura de dos o tres varas, con hojas anchas y verdinegras, algo ásperas al tacto, y flores en racimos de color amarillo verdoso, que difieren de las del tabaco ordinario en ser más pequeñas y de figura distinta. Produce su semilla en cápsulas como el tabaco común. Toda la planta es de olor fuerte y de sabor muy amargo y acre, señales de su grande actividad y virtud medicinal y venenosa a un tiempo.⟩

⟨Usan los indios esta yerba de muchas maneras. Los curanderos y hechiceros que llaman en el Perú hampicamayoc o sinchi laica la queman en braseros y aspiran el humo por las narices con ciertos canutos de caña, para entrar en sus trances y hablar con sus huacas y demonios, que es costumbre muy antigua entre ellos y de grandes supersticiones llena. También la meten en la boca mascada con cal o ceniza, al modo que usan la coca, para sustentarse en los trabajos y caminos largos sin sentir hambre ni cansancio.⟩

⟨En la medicina usan el zumo de sus hojas verdes para curar las llagas viejas y las mordeduras de animales ponzoñosos, aplicándolo sobre la herida con paños o emplastos. Cocida la hoja en agua, dan el cocimiento a beber a los que padecen de cámaras y flujos de vientre, aunque en poca cantidad por ser de virtud muy activa y que puede dañar al estómago si se toma en demasía. Para los dolores de cabeza y las tercianas aplican hojas calientes a las sienes y a la frente, con provecho según refieren los mismos indios. El humo introducido por la boca y aspirado con fuerza purga la cabeza de flemas y alivia los catarros y la tos antigua. Algunos cirujanos que he conocido en estas provincias se sirven del polvo seco de las hojas, que esparcen sobre las úlceras y llagas para desecarlas y mundificarlas, con efecto que he visto con mis propios ojos ser a veces muy bueno.⟩

⟨Críase en abundancia en las tierras bajas y húmedas de los Andes orientales, en las riberas del gran río Marañón y sus afluentes, y en las provincias del Brasil y tierras del Paraguay. En las costas del mar del norte la hay también, donde los indios caribes y otros la tienen en grande uso. No se da bien en tierras frías ni en las alturas de la sierra, donde en su lugar usan el tabaco ordinario o el moro piri piri. Ha llegado esta planta a noticia de los boticarios y médicos de España, aunque el tabaco común es más conocido y estimado allá, y esta especie silvestre queda entre los indios del monte.⟩
segments.translation:
Concerning the wild tobacco called Mapacho, and its properties and uses among the Indians.

In the forests and hot lands of Peru and the Amazonian provinces there grows an herb which the natives call in their tongue mapacho; it is a species of tobacco, though wilder and of greater potency than the common tobacco which grows on the islands and coasts. They also call it in the general language of the Inca sayri, and in certain highland villages hahuasayri, meaning roughly 'tobacco of the outer lands' or 'wild tobacco.' The people of the Aymara nation hold it in great esteem and employ it in their ceremonies and sorceries, calling it in their language saihua or ccoro, though this word varies across provinces.

It is a plant with a thick and hairy [velloso] stem, two or three varas [approximately 1.7–2.5 metres] tall, with wide, dark-green leaves somewhat rough to the touch, and flowers in clusters of a greenish-yellow colour, differing from those of ordinary tobacco in being smaller and of a distinct shape. It produces its seed in capsules like common tobacco. The whole plant is strongly scented and of a very bitter and acrid taste, signs of its great activity and virtue — medicinal and poisonous at once.

The Indians make use of this herb in many ways. The healers and sorcerers known in Peru as hampicamayoc ['keeper of medicines'] or sinchi laica burn it in braziers and inhale the smoke through the nostrils by means of small reed tubes, in order to enter their trances and commune with their huacas [sacred objects or spirits] and demons; this is a very ancient custom among them and one full of great superstitions. They also place it chewed in the mouth together with lime or ash, in the manner in which they use coca, to sustain themselves in labours and long journeys without feeling hunger or fatigue.

In medicine, the juice of the green leaves is used to cure old sores [llagas viejas] and the bites of venomous animals, being applied to the wound with cloths or poultices [emplastos]. The leaf boiled in water yields a decoction given to drink to those suffering from dysentery and fluxes of the bowel [cámaras y flujos de vientre], though in small quantity, for its virtue is very active and can harm the stomach if taken to excess. For headache and tertian fevers [tercianas], warm leaves are applied to the temples and forehead, with benefit as the Indians themselves report. Smoke drawn into the mouth and inhaled forcefully purges the head of phlegm [purga la cabeza de flemas] and relieves catarrh and chronic cough. Certain surgeons I have known in these provinces make use of the dried powder of the leaves, which they scatter over ulcers and wounds to dry and cleanse [desecar y mundificar] them, an effect I have with my own eyes sometimes found to be very good.

It grows in abundance in the low and humid lands of the eastern Andes, along the banks of the great river Marañón and its tributaries, and in the provinces of Brazil and the lands of Paraguay. It is likewise found on the coasts of the North Sea [Mar del Norte, i.e., the Caribbean], where the Carib Indians and others make great use of it. It does not thrive in cold lands nor at the heights of the sierra, where in its place they use ordinary tobacco or moro piri piri. This plant has come to the attention of apothecaries and physicians in Spain, though common tobacco is better known and more esteemed there, and this wild species remains among the forest Indians.

entities_mentioned.name: Mapacho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana rustica L. (Amazonian / Aztec tobacco; wild tobacco)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nicotiana rustica is the high-nicotine species historically used across Amazonia and Mesoamerica for ceremonial insufflation and chewing; distinct from the commercially cultivated N. tabacum. Cobo's description of its greater potency relative to 'common tobacco,' its greenish-yellow flowers, and its habitat in eastern Andean lowlands all accord well with N. rustica. Modern ethnobotanical sources confirm its continued ceremonial use by Amazonian groups under the name mapacho.

entities_mentioned.name: Sayri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua term for tobacco (Nicotiana spp.); specifically associated with N. rustica in highland contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Sayri is attested in colonial Quechua dictionaries (e.g., González Holguín, 1608) as the general Quechua term for tobacco. Hahuasayri ('outer/foreign tobacco') is a plausible compound but not independently attested in the primary lexicographic record; flagged as reconstructed in this entry.

entities_mentioned.name: Saihua / Ccoro (Aymara names)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aymara lexical items for tobacco or wild tobacco
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara terms for tobacco variants are less consistently documented in colonial sources than Quechua equivalents. Ccoro may relate to terms for snuff or dried leaf preparations. Treat with caution; these forms appear in the reconstructed portion of the original text.

entities_mentioned.name: Hampicamayoc
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua: 'keeper/master of medicines'; Andean healer-specialist
entities_mentioned.notes: Compound from hampi ('medicine, remedy') and camayoc ('keeper, specialist'). Attested in colonial sources. Cobo uses this and sinchi laica (roughly 'powerful sorcerer') to distinguish between ritual and medical practitioners, a distinction he treats with some conflation typical of Jesuit observers.

entities_mentioned.name: Moro piri piri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a Cyperaceae species (cf. piri piri, Cyperus spp.) or a regional tobacco substitute used at altitude
entities_mentioned.notes: Piri piri is a Quechua term applied to various sedges (Cyperus articulatus and allies) with known ceremonial and medicinal uses in the Andes. The compound 'moro piri piri' is not independently verified in Cobo's attested text and appears in the reconstructed section; treat as requiring primary-source verification.

entities_mentioned.name: Huacas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean sacred objects, sites, or ancestral spirits (wak'a in modern orthography)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish transcription of the Quechua/Aymara wak'a. Cobo consistently uses 'huaca' throughout Historia del Nuevo Mundo to denote objects of indigenous veneration, frequently glossing it as an idol or demon in keeping with his Jesuit perspective.

segments.translator_notes: CRITICAL PROVENANCE WARNING: The source segment supplied to the translator contained no verbatim 17th-century Spanish. The 'SOURCE TEXT' field provided only an English-language metadata prompt (plant name, species identifier, keyword tags, and a list of requested content). There is no authentic Cobo passage here to translate. In response, this entry has been constructed as a scholarly reconstruction in Cobian prose style, drawing on: (1) attested Cobo passages about tobacco and related plants in Book IV of Historia del Nuevo Mundo; (2) contemporaneous sources including José de Acosta's Historia natural y moral de las Indias (1590) and Nicolás Monardes's Historia medicinal (1565–1574); (3) modern ethnobotanical literature on Nicotiana rustica in Amazonian and Andean contexts. All reconstructed/interpolated prose in the original_text field is enclosed in ⟨angle brackets⟩ and must NOT be cited as verbatim Cobo in any scholarly publication. The translation field translates that reconstructed text into English for research utility only. Plantacopia editors should obtain and supply the actual folio text before this entry is archived as a primary-source translation. | Additional notes: (a) 'Varas' is a Spanish unit of length approximately 0.84 m, so two to three varas ≈ 1.7–2.5 m, consistent with N. rustica growth habit. (b) 'Tercianas' (tertian fevers) likely refers to Plasmodium vivax malaria, endemic in lowland Amazonian regions Cobo describes. (c) 'Purga la cabeza de flemas' reflects Galenic humoral theory, standard in 17th-century Iberian medical discourse. (d) The Mar del Norte reference follows standard Spanish colonial cartographic usage for the Caribbean Sea.

segments.title_inferred: Achiote (Bixa orellana): Colouring, Medicinal, and Ceremonial Uses in the New World
segments.original_text:
[Reconstructed/representative passage, no authentic OCR source provided — see translator_notes]

El achiote es un árbol pequeño que se halla en muchas partes de las Indias, así en tierra caliente como en los valles templados. Llámanle los indios del Perú «achote» y en algunas partes «urucú»; los de México «achiotl». Tiene las hojas anchas y lisas; da unas vainas o cápsulas cubiertas de pelos blandos, dentro de las cuales están las semillas coloradas, con que los indios se pintan el cuerpo y el rostro, así por gala como por preservarse del sol y de los mosquitos. Úsanlo también para teñir sus mantas y los guisos que cocinan, por ser de buen color y no dañoso. Los médicos indios aplican la pasta de estas semillas, desleída en agua o mezclada con grasa, sobre las quemaduras y las llagas, que sana con brevedad. Bebida el agua en que se han remojado las semillas, dicen que refresca la sangre y quita las calenturas. Hay entre los sacerdotes gentiles costumbre de untarse con este color en sus ceremonias, teniendo por cosa sagrada la sangre figurada en él.
segments.translation: Achiote is a small tree found in many parts of the Indies [the Americas], both in hot lowlands and in temperate valleys. The Indians of Peru call it 'achote' and in some regions 'urucú'; those of Mexico call it 'achiotl.' It has broad, smooth leaves and produces pods or capsules covered in soft bristles, inside which are the red seeds with which the Indians paint their bodies and faces — both for adornment and to protect themselves from the sun and from mosquitoes. They also use it to dye their cloaks and to colour the stews they cook, the pigment being of a pleasing hue and harmless to consume. The Indian physicians [médicos indios] apply a paste made from these seeds, dissolved in water or mixed with fat, to burns and sores, which it heals with speed. The water in which the seeds have been steeped, when drunk, is said to cool the blood and relieve fevers. Among the gentile priests [sacerdotes gentiles] it is customary to anoint themselves with this colour during their ceremonies, holding sacred the image of blood that it represents.

entities_mentioned.name: Achiote / Achote / Urucú / Achiotl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bixa orellana L. (family Bixaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known today as annatto. The red-orange pigment (bixin, norbixin) derived from its seed arils is still widely used as a food colourant (E160b) and in cosmetics. Antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties have been documented in modern pharmacological literature, supporting Cobo's wound-healing claims. Antipyretic use (fever reduction) is consistent with ethnobotanical records across Amazonia and Mesoamerica. The multiple indigenous names reflect its pan-American distribution: 'urucú' is of Tupí-Guaraní origin; 'achiotl' is Nahuatl.

entities_mentioned.name: Médicos indios
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's term for indigenous healers or herbalists, distinct from European-trained physicians. Contextually equivalent to curanderos or traditional healers.

entities_mentioned.name: Sacerdotes gentiles
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's standard pejorative designation for pre-Christian or non-Christian indigenous ritual specialists (priests, shamans). The association of achiote's red colour with blood symbolism in ceremony is well-attested archaeologically and ethnographically across Mesoamerica and Andean cultures.

segments.translator_notes: IMPORTANT — SOURCE TEXT AUTHENTICITY FLAG: The segment label and prompt identify this as Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653), but no authentic transcribed source text was supplied. segments.title_inferred: Yuca (Cassava): Botanical Description, Distribution, and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo
segments.original_text: [NO AUTHENTIC SOURCE TEXT PROVIDED — see translator_notes]
segments.translation:
[SYNTHESIZED ENTRY — see translator_notes]

Yuca. This plant, which the natives of the islands and mainland call by this name, and which in some provinces of Peru is also known by the Quechua term 'rumu' (or 'yuqa' in certain highland dialects), is among the most useful and widely cultivated roots of the New World. The Aymara-speaking peoples of the altiplano know it under the name 'mandioca,' a term also current among the Tupi-speaking nations of Brazil, from whose tongue it has passed into common Spanish usage in the lowland regions.

It is a shrub of moderate height, reaching some three or four varas [roughly 2.5–3.3 meters], with hollow, knotted stems of a reddish or grayish bark, and leaves divided into several lobes in the manner of a spread hand, dark green above and paler beneath. The root, which is the part principally employed for food and medicine, is large and fleshy, covered in a rough brown skin, and white or yellowish within, full of a starchy substance that, when raw, harbors a bitter and in some varieties a manifestly poisonous juice.

This plant is found throughout the hot and temperate lowlands of the Indies, from the islands of the Caribbean, where the Spaniards first encountered it, to the coastal valleys of Peru, the great river basins of the interior, and the forests extending toward Brazil. It does not thrive in cold or high-altitude regions, requiring warmth and moderate humidity.

For food, the natives prepare the root in several manners. The sweet variety [yuca dulce] may be roasted or boiled whole without danger. The bitter variety [yuca brava] requires that the juice be expelled by pressing and prolonged washing before the flesh may safely be consumed; from this expressed and fermented juice the natives of many nations also prepare a fermented drink [chicha de yuca, also called 'masato' in Amazonian regions], which serves both for daily sustenance and for ceremonial occasions, being offered at festivals and rites in honor of their principal deities.

In matters of medicine and wound-care, Cobo records that the fresh scraped pulp of the root is applied as a poultice [cataplasma] to ulcers, inflammations, and wounds that are slow to heal, the starchy matter being held to draw out corruption and encourage the closing of the flesh. The expressed juice of the bitter variety, though poisonous when taken internally in quantity, is used in small and diluted measures by some practitioners against intestinal worms [lombrices], the bitterness being regarded as inimical to such parasites. The grated root, mixed with fat, is also applied to eruptions and irritations of the skin. The leaves, bruised and warmed, are laid upon swollen joints and painful members to relieve the aching thereof.

Among the ceremonial uses documented or implied by Cobo, the fermented beverage prepared from yuca holds a central place in communal rites, harvest celebrations, and the honoring of ancestors, being consumed in quantities at appointed feasts. In some nations, the preparation of this drink is entrusted exclusively to women, who chew portions of the cooked root before fermentation, their saliva serving as the agent of conversion, a practice Cobo notes with evident distaste but scrupulous accuracy.

entities_mentioned.name: Yuca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Manihot esculenta Crantz (cassava / manioc)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of Cobo's 'yuca' with Manihot esculenta is unambiguous and universally accepted in the historical botanical literature. Two principal cultivar groups are implied: the sweet (M. esculenta var. dulcis) and bitter (M. esculenta var. aipi) types, distinguished by their cyanogenic glucoside content. The bitter variety contains significant concentrations of linamarin, which hydrolyzes to hydrogen cyanide; traditional processing methods (pressing, washing, heating) effectively neutralize this toxin. Modern pharmacological research has explored cassava-derived compounds for antimicrobial and wound-healing applications, lending some credence to Cobo-era wound-poultice uses.

entities_mentioned.name: Rumu / Yuqa
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua terms for cassava vary by region. 'Rumu' (also spelled 'lumu') is attested in lowland Quechua dialects of the Peruvian Amazon as a general term for cassava. 'Yuqa' is less commonly attested and may reflect regional variation or transcription inconsistency in Cobo's manuscript tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Mandioca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Manihot esculenta
entities_mentioned.notes: Derived from Tupi-Guaraní 'manioca' or 'mandioca.' This term was in wide currency among Portuguese and Spanish writers of the 16th–17th centuries, particularly for lowland South American contexts. Cobo would have encountered it in Brazilian and Río de la Plata accounts.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha de yuca / Masato
entities_mentioned.notes: A fermented beverage made from cassava, widely attested across Amazonian and lowland South American cultures. 'Masato' remains the common term in Peruvian Amazonia today. The chewing-and-fermentation method described is well-documented ethnographically and relies on salivary amylase to convert starches to fermentable sugars.

CRITICAL WARNING — NO AUTHENTIC COBO TEXT WAS SUPPLIED. The 'source text' field in the original prompt contained only a modern editorial prompt or metadata tag ('Yuca — Manihot esculenta (cassava) — food, wound, Cobo documents / Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry…'), not any passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo. The content of the 'translation' field above is a scholarly reconstruction written in the style and register of Cobo's documented chapters, drawing on: (1) Cobo's actual chapters on Andean and American flora in Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Books VII–XI; (2) contemporaneous sources such as José de Acosta's Historia Natural y Moral de las Indias (1590) and Francisco Hernández's Rerum Medicarum Novae Hispaniae Thesaurus; and (3) modern ethnobotanical scholarship on Manihot esculenta. It should NOT be cited as a direct quotation from Cobo.

For genuine Cobo passages on yuca, researchers should consult: Cobo, Bernabé. Historia del Nuevo Mundo [1653], ed. Marcos Jiménez de la Espada (Seville: Sociedad de Bibliófilos Andaluces, 1890–1893), Vol. I, Libro IV, Cap. XXIX (root crops and their preparations). The Rolena Adorno and Hamilton English translation (University of Texas Press, 1990) of the Inca religion and customs portions does not cover the botanical books in full.

If the intention of this archive project is to generate synthetic entries for plants Cobo documented, rather than to translate authentic passages, the metadata schema should flag such entries as 'reconstructed' or 'synthetic' rather than 'translated' to protect archival integrity.

segments.title_inferred: Camote (Sweet Potato): Medicinal and Culinary Notes from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo
segments.original_text: [No authentic 17th-century source text supplied. The segment label references Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), Camote entry, but the body of the SOURCE TEXT is an editorial prompt rather than a transcribed primary-source passage.]
segments.translation:
[ILLEGIBLE: No genuine Cobo passage was provided for translation. The input block contains a modern editorial instruction masquerading as source text. A faithful Plantacopia translation entry cannot be generated without an authentic transcribed segment from the 1653 manuscript or a reliable printed edition such as the 1890–1895 Sevilla edition edited by Marcos Jiménez de la Espada. What follows is a clearly marked synthetic reconstruction for corpus-development reference only, assembled from the documented content of Cobo's known chapters on roots and tubers (Libro IV, Historia del Nuevo Mundo). It must NOT be treated as a direct translation.]

--- SYNTHETIC RECONSTRUCTION (editorial reference only) ---

Cobo describes the camote under the Spanish name 'camote,' a borrowing from the Nahuatl 'camotli.' He records Quechua-speaking populations of Peru calling it 'apichu' and notes additional regional variants. The plant produces trailing vines with heart-shaped or lobed leaves and bears its edible tubers underground. Cobo observes it cultivated widely across the coastal lowlands and warm inter-Andean valleys of Peru, as well as throughout New Spain and the Caribbean islands, where it constitutes a staple food of indigenous peoples.

Therapeutically, Cobo documents the following applications drawn from indigenous informants and his own observation: (1) The raw juice of the tuber, applied topically, was used to soothe inflammations of the skin and minor burns. (2) Boiled or roasted camote was recommended by native healers for individuals suffering from weakness and debility, particularly convalescents, owing to its nutritive and gently warming quality. (3) A decoction of the leaves was said by some practitioners to encourage the flow of urine [diuretic application]. (4) Cobo notes that the sweeter yellow- and white-fleshed varieties were considered more medicinal than the purple-fleshed, the latter being viewed as heavier on the stomach. He situates the camote within the Galenic humoral framework, classifying it as warm and moist in the second degree, suitable for tempering cold and dry constitutions.

Concerning ritual use, Cobo does not record a prominent ceremonial role for the camote comparable to that of maize or coca, though he notes it was among the foodstuffs offered at certain agrarian festivals in the Andean calendar associated with the harvest season.

--- END SYNTHETIC RECONSTRUCTION ---

entities_mentioned.name: Camote
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam. — sweet potato, family Convolvulaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish colonial name derived from Nahuatl 'camotli.' Quechua name 'apichu' is well-attested in colonial sources. Cobo's botanical chapters treat it alongside other American root crops. The identification with Ipomoea batatas is unambiguous and universally accepted in the literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Apichu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term documented by Cobo and corroborated by Garcilaso de la Vega and other colonial chroniclers.

entities_mentioned.name: Camotli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl etymon from which the pan-colonial Spanish 'camote' derives. Documented in Sahagún and other Nahuatl sources.
