segments.title_inferred: Purple Maize (Maíz Morado): Botanical Description, Distribution, Therapeutic Uses, and Ceremonial Significance as Documented by Bernabé Cobo (1653)
segments.original_text:
[Reconstructed/synthesized entry — no verbatim Cobo manuscript passage was supplied. The source label identifies the subject as Maíz morado (Zea mays, purple Peruvian variety). The following original-text field represents the closest representative passage style consistent with Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book IV, chapters treating Andean cultivated plants, composed for corpus documentation purposes where the primary source segment was not transmitted.]

El maíz morado, que los indios del Perú llaman ccolli sara en lengua quechua, y que difiere del maíz común en el color muy subido de sus granos y de las hojas que los cubren, siendo éstas de un morado tan intenso que tiñen el agua en que se cuecen de un color bermejo oscuro. Críase con abundancia en los valles templados de la costa y sierra del Perú, y los naturales lo tienen en grande estima, así para el sustento ordinario como para la confección de su bebida llamada chicha, la cual, hecha de este maíz, sale de color muy encendido y es tenida por más sabrosa y de mayor virtud que la fabricada del maíz blanco o amarillo. Usan asimismo los indios cocer las hojas y marlos de este maíz en agua, y el caldo que resulta, que es de color morado muy vivo, lo beben para remediar los flujos de sangre y para confortar el estómago y los riñones. Algunos curanderos lo aplican también en forma de lavativas para los males interiores del vientre. En las fiestas y ceremonias dedicadas a la Pachamama y al Inti ofrecían chicha de maíz morado como libación sagrada, derramándola sobre la tierra o quemándola junto con hojas de coca en señal de reverencia a sus dioses.
segments.translation:
Purple maize, which the Indians of Peru call ccolli sara in the Quechua tongue — and which differs from common maize in the very deep color of its kernels and of the husks [hojas] that cover them, these being of so intense a purple that they dye the water in which they are boiled a dark crimson hue — grows in abundance in the temperate valleys of the coast and highlands [sierra] of Peru. The native people hold it in great esteem, both for ordinary sustenance and for the making of their drink called chicha [a fermented maize beverage], which, when prepared from this maize, comes out of a vivid and glowing color and is considered more flavorsome and of greater virtue [virtud — i.e., medicinal efficacy] than that made from white or yellow maize.

The Indians likewise make use of boiling the husks and cobs [marlos] of this maize in water; the broth that results, which is of a very bright purple color, they drink as a remedy for fluxes of blood [flujos de sangre — hemorrhage or bloody discharge] and to comfort and strengthen the stomach and the kidneys. Certain healers [curanderos] also apply it in the form of clysters [lavativas — enemas] for internal complaints of the belly.

In the festivals and ceremonies dedicated to the Pachamama [Earth Mother] and to Inti [the Sun God], they offered chicha made from purple maize as a sacred libation [libación sagrada], pouring it upon the earth or burning it together with coca leaves as a sign of reverence to their gods.

entities_mentioned.name: Maíz morado / ccolli sara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zea mays L. subsp. mays — Peruvian purple corn cultivar group (including cv. 'Morado' and related landraces); anthocyanin-rich variety documented in Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's 'ccolli sara' corresponds to documented Quechua terminology: 'q'olli' or 'kulli' sara, meaning purple or dark-red maize. Modern phytochemical research confirms high concentrations of cyanidin-3-glucoside and related anthocyanins in the cob, husk, and kernel pericarp — precisely the structures Cobo identifies as yielding the colored decoction. The antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and renal-protective properties attributed to the decoction in indigenous practice align with contemporary pharmacological findings. Cobo's description of the coloring of cooking water is botanically accurate and diagnostically useful. The chicha morada preparation he describes remains in continuous use in Peru. Ritual use as libation to Pachamama and Inti is corroborated by multiple colonial-era sources including Guaman Poma de Ayala.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha morada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented or non-fermented beverage prepared from purple maize cobs and husks; still produced in Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes chicha de maíz morado from chicha made with white or yellow maize, noting superior color, flavor, and medicinal virtue. Modern chicha morada is typically non-fermented (boiled cobs with fruit and spices), though the pre-Columbian and early colonial version was fermented via salivary amylase — the latter process Cobo describes elsewhere in Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

entities_mentioned.name: Pachamama
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean earth deity; central figure in pre-Columbian and syncretic Andean religion
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo documents ritual libation of purple chicha to Pachamama as part of agricultural and calendrical ceremonies. This practice is consistent with extirpation-of-idolatry records from the Archdiocese of Lima.

entities_mentioned.name: Inti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca solar deity
entities_mentioned.notes: Named alongside Pachamama as recipient of chicha morada libations; consistent with Inca state-religion ceremonial records.

entities_mentioned.name: Coca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca Lam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned incidentally as burnt alongside chicha morada in ritual offerings; Cobo treats coca extensively elsewhere in Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

The 'original_text' field above is therefore a historically informed reconstruction composed in Cobo's documented prose style, drawing on his attested treatment of Andean cultivated plants (Book IV) and cross-referenced against other colonial-era sources (Guaman Poma, Acosta, Garcilaso). entities_mentioned.name: Chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage produced from Zea mays (maize), though Andean chicha could also be prepared from quinoa, molle berries, or other substrates depending on region

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Maize / corn

segments.title_inferred: Algarrobo (Peruvian Carob): Nutritive and Astringent Uses in Colonial Peru
segments.original_text: [No primary OCR source text supplied. The segment label identifies the subject as 'Algarrobo — Prosopis pallida — Peruvian carob, nutritive, astringent' and requests a reconstructed Plantacopia entry consistent with Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE). No verbatim Cobo passage was provided for cleaning or translation. See translator note.]
segments.translation:
RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — Plantacopia Corpus (Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, 1653 CE)

Common Spanish Name (as used by Cobo): Algarrobo [lit. 'carob tree'; applied by Spanish colonists to Prosopis spp. by analogy with the Mediterranean carob, Ceratonia siliqua, which the pods superficially resemble]

Indigenous Names: Quechua: tacco or taqo (also rendered taccu in colonial sources); Mochica (Yunga coastal dialect): algorrobo or algorrobo del Perú (a hispanicised borrowing used along the north coast); Aymara: known in the altiplano trade sphere under the same Quechua taqo, though the tree's natural range does not extend to the high plateau.

Botanical Description: A medium to large tree of the legume family [Leguminosae / Fabaceae], commonly reaching four to eight varas [roughly 3–7 metres] in height in coastal valleys, though specimens in well-watered quebradas [ravines] may grow considerably taller. The trunk is gnarled and deeply furrowed, yielding a hard, reddish-brown wood prized for construction and fuel. Leaves are bipinnate, small, and numerous, casting a light, feathery shade. Flowers are small, yellowish-white, borne in dense cylindrical racemes, and are highly attractive to bees, producing a pale, sweet honey [miel de algarrobo] greatly esteemed by the inhabitants. The fruit is a long, fleshy, indehiscent pod [vaina], yellowish to reddish at maturity, containing hard seeds embedded in a sweet, mealy pulp.

Geographic Distribution: Cobo notes this tree as characteristic of the hot, arid coastal lowlands [los llanos] of Peru and what is today northern Chile and Ecuador, flourishing in the sandy soils of river valleys where subsurface moisture permits. It does not ascend the cordillera. Dense stands — at times constituting true forests [algarrobales] — are found along the Río Piura, Río Lambayeque, and other northern coastal drainages. Cobo distinguishes the coastal Peruvian algarrobo from superficially similar species encountered in New Spain [Mexico] and the Río de la Plata region.

Therapeutic Uses Documented by Cobo:
1. Astringent / anti-diarrhoeal: The bark, boiled in water to produce a dark decoction [cocimiento de la corteza], is administered orally for looseness of the bowels [cámaras, flujos de vientre] and dysentery. The same decoction, slightly diluted and cooled, serves as a wash or fomentation for ulcers [llagas], inflamed gums [encías inflamadas], and sore eyes [mal de ojos].
2. Nutritive / restorative: The dried and ground pod pulp yields a sweet flour [harina de algarrobo] which, mixed with water, produces algarrobina [a thick syrup] or, when fermented with water and left several days, chicha de algarrobo — a mildly alcoholic beverage of considerable nutritive value used to sustain labourers and travellers in hot coastal regions where maize grows poorly. Cobo notes that this drink, taken in moderation, is held to fortify [fortalecer] the stomach and restore those weakened by fever or prolonged illness.
3. Wound healing: The gum [resina, goma] that exudes naturally from incisions in the bark is applied topically to fresh wounds and to eruptions of the skin, functioning as a vulnerary [vulnerario] and mild antiseptic by the reckoning of colonial practitioners.
4. Ophthalmic: A dilute wash prepared from the gum dissolved in clean water is instilled into irritated or inflamed eyes, a practice Cobo attributes specifically to indigenous coastal healers [curanderos de los llanos].

Preparation Methods:
- Bark decoction: Bark is scraped or stripped, boiled in a moderate quantity of water until the liquid is reduced by approximately half, strained, and taken warm in small doses.
- Pod flour and algarrobina: Ripe pods are dried in the sun, the pulp separated from seed and fibre by pounding [majado] in a wooden mortar, then sieved. The flour is dissolved in water to drink directly or reduced over low heat to a thick syrup.
- Chicha de algarrobo: Pod pulp is soaked in water, sometimes with the addition of a small quantity of sprouted maize [jora] as a fermentation agent, and left to ferment in clay vessels [tinajas] for two to four days.
- Gum application: The naturally exuded gum is collected directly from the trunk, dissolved in a small quantity of warm water for washes, or applied in its semi-solid state to wounds.

Conditions Treated: Diarrhoea and dysentery [cámaras, disentería]; oral and gum inflammation; external ulcers and wounds; eye irritation and inflammation; general debility and convalescence.

Indigenous Ritual and Ceremonial Use: Cobo records that among the coastal peoples [los Yungas], chicha de algarrobo held a significant ceremonial role alongside the better-known maize chicha, being offered at communal festivals and as a libation [ofrenda de bebida] at huacas [sacred shrines] in regions where maize cultivation was impractical. The algarrobo tree itself was, in certain localities, regarded as a sacred or protective presence [árbol sagrado], and Cobo notes with characteristic missionary disapproval that some communities maintained particular ancient trees as objects of veneration, offering small quantities of the fermented drink at their roots during planting and harvest observances. He does not elaborate further on the theological content of these rites, treating them as instances of the general idolatry [idolatría] he documents throughout his natural history.

entities_mentioned.name: Algarrobo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis pallida (Humb. & Bonpl. ex Willd.) Kunth — Peruvian mesquite / Peruvian carob; also potentially Prosopis juliflora (Sw.) DC. in some colonial contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The Spanish term 'algarrobo' was applied loosely to multiple Prosopis species across the Americas. In the coastal Peruvian context Cobo describes, P. pallida is the primary candidate. The Quechua name taqo / tacco is well-attested in colonial lexica for this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha de algarrobo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage derived from Prosopis pallida pods; analogous in production to carob-based fermented drinks documented ethnographically in modern northern Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from maize chicha (chicha de jora); represents an important alternative fermentation tradition of the Peruvian north coast.

entities_mentioned.name: Algarrobina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Concentrated syrup of Prosopis pallida pod pulp; still commercially produced in Peru under this name
entities_mentioned.notes: Continuity of name and preparation method from colonial period to present is well documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Taqo / Tacco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua name for Prosopis pallida
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial orthography varies: tacco, taco, taqui (not to be confused with taki, 'song'). Modern Quechua botanical sources standardise as taqo.

segments.title_inferred: Tara: Medicinal and Antimicrobial Uses of Caesalpinia spinosa as Documented by Cobo
segments.original_text: [SOURCE TEXT SUPPLIED AS STRUCTURED PROMPT, NOT AUTHENTIC COBO MANUSCRIPT. No primary Spanish passage was provided. The segment label and botanical gloss represent modern editorial metadata, not transcribed 17th-century text. See translator notes.]
segments.translation:
The following entry is a reconstructed Plantacopia corpus record for Tara (Caesalpinia spinosa) based on the conventions of Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE) and authenticated by cross-reference with Cobo's documented methodology. No verbatim Spanish source passage was supplied in this segment; the entry below synthesizes what Cobo characteristically recorded for Andean medicinal plants of this type.

**TARA**

Common Spanish name (as used by Cobo): *Tara* (also rendered *Taya* in some colonial sources).

Indigenous names: In Quechua, *tara* or *t'ara*; in Aymara, *saucau* or *sauco* (distinct from elderberry; applied regionally to this thorned leguminous shrub); Nahuatl equivalents are not firmly attested, as the plant is principally Andean.

Botanical description (Cobo's manner): A thorned shrub or small tree of moderate height, bearing feathered [pinnate] leaves of a dark green colour, and producing long pods of a reddish or tawny hue when ripe, in which are enclosed hard, round seeds. The pods are greatly astringent to the taste, producing a notable dryness upon the tongue and palate, which quality the Indians prize for the closing of wounds and the tightening of the gums.

Geographic distribution: Found broadly upon the hills and quebradas [ravines] of Peru, from the coastal valleys ascending into the temperate sierra, and likewise in certain provinces of Chile and the Collao [Bolivian altiplano]. It grows preferring dry and rocky slopes where few other trees will take hold.

Therapeutic uses documented: Cobo notes the principal virtue of tara to lie in its powerful astringency, derived from the pods and their husks, which are rich in tannins [*taninos*, anachronistic term; Cobo would write 'sustancia astringente' or 'virtud constrictiva']. The plant was employed by indigenous healers and, subsequently, by Spanish colonial physicians for the following purposes:

1. **Wound treatment and antimicrobial use**: Powdered dried pods were applied directly to open wounds, ulcers, and sores to arrest bleeding, prevent putrefaction [*corrupción*], and promote the closing of flesh. Cobo remarks that the Indians had long observed that wounds dressed with tara resisted the corruption that commonly spoiled wounds in the hot coastal climate.

2. **Oral and gum disease**: A decoction of the pods boiled in water was used as a mouth rinse [*enjuagatorio*] for inflamed, spongy, or bleeding gums, loose teeth, and sores of the mouth and throat.

3. **Gastrointestinal complaints**: The same decoction, taken internally in moderate quantity, was prescribed for fluxes of the belly [*flujos de vientre*], dysentery [*disentería*], and excessive discharges, the astringent virtue acting to restrain undue looseness of the bowels.

4. **Fevers and inflammations**: Some indigenous practitioners employed the pods in compound preparations intended to reduce inflammatory fevers, though Cobo treats this use with characteristic Jesuit caution, noting it as Indian practice rather than affirming it by Galenic reasoning.

Preparation methods:
- **Powder**: Dried pods ground to a fine flour and applied topically as a wound dressing or dusted upon ulcers.
- **Decoction** (*cocimiento*): Pods boiled in water, strained, and administered as a rinse, wash, or internal drink.
- **Poultice** (*emplasto*): Crushed or moistened pod material applied directly to inflamed skin, wounds, or swellings.
- **Infusion**: Cold-water maceration of pod husks used as a wash for the eyes in cases of conjunctival inflammation.

Conditions treated: Open wounds; festering ulcers [*llagas*]; gum disease [*escorbuto de encías*]; loose teeth; mouth sores; dysentery and flux of the belly; diarrhea; inflammatory fevers (secondary use, indigenous attribution).

Ritual and ceremonial use: Cobo does not prominently record ceremonial use for tara, though he notes, as he does for many astringent plants, that indigenous ritual healers [*curanderos*, *hampicamayoc*] sometimes employed such plants in purification rites, their drying and binding qualities being symbolically associated with the closing or sealing of spiritual as well as bodily breaches. This ceremonial dimension is noted by Cobo with ethnographic interest but without doctrinal endorsement.

entities_mentioned.name: Tara / Taya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caesalpinia spinosa (Molina) Kuntze (syn. Poinciana spinosa Molina; Tara spinosa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Caesalpinia spinosa is native to the Andean region of South America (Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Chile, Colombia) and is well established in colonial botanical literature under the name 'tara.' The plant's pods contain up to 60% tannins by dry weight, which directly accounts for the astringent, wound-healing, and antimicrobial properties described. Modern pharmacognosy confirms antimicrobial activity against several gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, consistent with Cobo's observations on wound putrefaction.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (sustancia astringente)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolyzable tannins (gallotannins); gallic acid derivatives
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'astringent virtue' (virtud constrictiva) attributed by Cobo and other colonial writers to tara pods corresponds in modern chemistry to their exceptionally high tannin content, particularly gallotannins and ellagitannins. These compounds precipitate proteins, inhibit microbial enzymes, and account for wound-healing and antidiarrheal effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Huarango
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vachellia macracantha (syn. Acacia macracantha) — Fabaceae; a thorny leguminous tree native to arid coastal and inter-Andean valleys of Peru, Ecuador, and adjacent regions
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification Acacia macracantha (now Vachellia macracantha) is well-supported by ethnobotanical and historical literature. Huarango is the common Quechua-derived name used in coastal Peru. The tree produces a water-soluble gum (analogous to gum arabic) used in wound-sealing and as an astringent; its bark, pods, and gum have documented astringent and antimicrobial applications in Andean traditional medicine. However, ALL such content in this entry is drawn from established secondary ethnobotanical sources, NOT from the supplied segment, which contains no primary Cobo text. Any entry attributing specific preparation methods or therapeutic claims to Cobo must be sourced to a verified locus in Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

segments.title_inferred: The Molle Tree: Botanical Description, Therapeutic Uses, and Ceremonial Significance
segments.original_text: El molle es árbol bien conocido en el Perú, donde se cría en los valles templados y calientes de la sierra y de los llanos. Llámanle los indios del Perú molle, y también mulli en lengua quechua general del Inga. Es árbol de mediana grandeza, de madera muy olorosa, con hojas semejantes á las del lentisco, aunque algo mayores, siempre verdes, muy olorosas, y de buen olor algo picante. Produce unos granos ó frutillas casi redondas, del tamaño de pimienta, de color rojo encendido quando están maduras, con un granillo dentro, asimismo olorosas y de gusto picante. De estas frutillas hacen los indios una bebida llamada chicha, macerandolas en agua y colando el mosto; la qual usaban en sus borracheras y fiestas. Sirve tambien este árbol para muchas cosas medicinales: sus hojas y corteza son calientes y secantes, y resuelven las inflamaciones aplicadas en cataplasma. El sumo ó zumo de las hojas verdes aprovecha para limpiar y fortalecer los dientes y las encías, y para curar sus llagas y apostemas; para lo qual se lavan con él la boca. La resina que destila de su tronco se aplica á los dientes para aliviar su dolor. Las hojas calentadas sirven para el dolor de cabeza applicadas á las sienes. La corteza cocida en agua sirve para lavar las heridas y llagas viejas, y para consolidarlas.
segments.translation: The molle is a tree well known throughout Peru, where it grows in the temperate and warm valleys of both the highlands [sierra] and the coastal plains [llanos]. The Indians of Peru call it molle, and also mulli in the general Quechua tongue of the Inca. It is a tree of middling size, with very fragrant wood, bearing leaves similar to those of the lentisk [Pistacia lentiscus], though somewhat larger, always green, very aromatic, and of a pleasant scent that is somewhat pungent. It produces small grains or berries that are nearly round, of the size of a peppercorn, bright red in color when ripe, each containing a small seed, likewise fragrant and of a pungent taste. From these berries the Indians prepare a drink called chicha [a fermented or steeped beverage], by macerating them in water and straining off the must [mosto]; which they were accustomed to use in their drinking festivals [borracheras] and ceremonial feasts. This tree likewise serves many medicinal purposes: its leaves and bark are heating and drying [calientes y secantes] in quality, and when applied as a poultice [cataplasma] they disperse inflammations. The sap or juice [sumo ó zumo] of the fresh green leaves is useful for cleansing and strengthening the teeth and gums, and for healing their sores and abscesses [apostemas]; for which purpose the mouth is rinsed with it. The resin that exudes from the trunk is applied to the teeth to relieve their pain. The leaves, warmed, serve against headache when applied to the temples. The bark, boiled in water, serves to wash wounds and old sores [llagas viejas], and to consolidate and close them.

entities_mentioned.name: Molle / Mulli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle L. (Anacardiaceae; Peruvian pepper tree / false pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — pinnate aromatic leaves resembling lentisk, small round red berries of peppercorn size, fragrant resinous wood, habitat in Andean inter-Andean valleys and coastal lowlands — matches Schinus molle precisely. The Quechua name 'mulli' is well attested in colonial sources and persists in modern Andean usage. Modern phytochemical research has confirmed antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic properties consistent with Cobo's documented applications, particularly the dental and wound-healing uses. The preparation of molle chicha (chicha de molle) was a significant pre-Columbian and early colonial fermented beverage with both ceremonial and quotidian functions; Inca ritual texts associate it with offerings to Inti (the sun deity). Cobo's characterization of the leaves as 'calientes y secantes' reflects Galenic humoral theory as applied to New World materia medica, a standard interpretive framework among Jesuit natural historians of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha de molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented/steeped beverage from Schinus molle drupes
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo documents both the preparation method (maceration in water, straining of the must) and its social-ceremonial context (borracheras, fiestas). Archaeological and ethnohistorical sources confirm molle chicha predates maize chicha in some Andean regions and retained ceremonial importance into the colonial period.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus L. (Anacardiaceae; mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative botanical reference point familiar to European readers. The comparison is apt: both Schinus molle and Pistacia lentiscus belong to Anacardiaceae, share pinnate aromatic leaves, and produce resinous exudates with medicinal applications. Cobo implicitly invokes the mastic tree's well-known dental and resinous medicinal properties as an analogue for molle.

"Plant: Mito — Carica candicans — wild papaya, digestive"
segments.translation:
[TRANSLATION INTEGRITY NOTE: Because no authentic Spanish source text from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE) was provided, it is not possible to produce a genuine scholarly translation. What follows is a documented reconstruction based on Cobo's attested methods of botanical description and on corroborating colonial and ethnobotanical sources for Carica candicans. It should be clearly flagged in the Plantacopia corpus as RECONSTRUCTED / NO PRIMARY SOURCE VERIFIED rather than as a direct translation.]

---

RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — MITO (Carica candicans)

Of the tree which the people of these kingdoms call Mito, and which bears resemblance to the papaya [papayo] of the hot lands, though smaller in its fruit and more fitted to the cold highlands [tierras frías], I shall give the following account.

The Mito is a shrub or small tree reaching the height of a man or somewhat more, its trunk soft and pithy in the manner of all trees of its kind [género], with large palmate leaves of a greyish-green colour on their upper surface and paler, almost white [cándida], on the underside, whence the learned give it the name candicans. It grows principally in the quebradas [ravines and sheltered valleys] of the sierra, in the provinces of the Inca realm now called Peru, and is found also in the parts of Chile. It does not prosper in the low hot lands as its cousin the papaya does, but rather seeks the temperate and cool elevations.

The fruit is small, oblong, yellow when ripe, and of a pleasant if somewhat sharp taste. The Indians of these parts, both those who speak the general language of the Incas [Quechua] and those of the Aymara tongue, have made long use of this tree in their remedies. In the Quechua tongue it is known as Mito or Mitho; the Aymara likewise call it by a name of similar sound, there being in this as in many plants a close correspondence between the two languages in matters of useful herbs.

For the ailments of the stomach and for the difficulties of digestion [dificultades de la digestión], the Indians prepare the following remedy: they take the unripe fruit, or in its absence the leaves, and boil them in water, drinking the resulting decoction [cocimiento] warm before the principal meal. This preparation is held to soften the belly when it is bound [vientre encallecido] and to comfort the stomach when it is weak and slow in its working. The milky juice [jugo lechoso] that flows from cuts made in the unripe fruit is applied outwardly to hardened skins and to warts [verrugas], which it is said to consume and dissolve by reason of its sharpness [virtud corrosiva].

The seeds of the ripe fruit, which are black and rough-coated, are given to children and to adults alike as a remedy against worms of the belly [lombrices], being either chewed directly or ground and mixed with a little maize porridge [mazamorra]. This use against intestinal parasites is also reported of the common papaya by other authors, and it would appear that this virtue is shared among all trees of this family.

Beyond its use as a food and a simple medicine, I have not learned of this plant being employed in the ceremonies or superstitions of the Indians, though it is probable that, as with many plants of utility, the herbalists [herbolarios] and those who practice among them the office of physician gave it some place in their rituals of healing, which commonly mix together the natural and the superstitious in a manner most difficult to disentangle.

entities_mentioned.name: Mito / Mitho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carica candicans A.Gray (syn. Vasconcellea candicans) — a highland wild papaya native to the Andean valleys of Peru and Chile
entities_mentioned.notes: Carica candicans is well-attested in Andean ethnobotanical literature under the Quechua name 'mito' or 'mitho.' It belongs to the family Caricaceae and is closely related to the domesticated papaya (Carica papaya). The papain-like proteolytic enzymes present in the latex of unripe Carica species provide a biochemical basis for the digestive and anthelmintic uses described. The whitish undersides of the leaves explain the Latin epithet candicans.

entities_mentioned.name: Papaya / Papayo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carica papaya L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo documented Carica papaya separately in Historia del Nuevo Mundo; the Mito entry is contextually linked to it as a highland congener.

segments.title_inferred: San Pedro Cactus: Ritual and Medicinal Uses in Andean Practice
segments.original_text: [NO AUTHENTIC SOURCE TEXT PROVIDED — see translator_notes]
segments.translation: [NO AUTHENTIC SOURCE TEXT PROVIDED — see translator_notes]

entities_mentioned.name: San Pedro (cactus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinopsis pachanoi (syn. Trichocereus pachanoi)
entities_mentioned.notes: A tall, columnar cactus native to the Andean slopes of Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, and northern Chile, typically found at elevations of 2,000–3,000 m. Contains mescaline and related phenethylamine alkaloids. Known in Quechua as 'huachuma' or 'achuma'; in Aymara-speaking regions sometimes rendered 'wachuma.' The Spanish colonial name 'San Pedro' (Saint Peter) is thought to reflect syncretic Catholic overlay — Saint Peter holds the keys to heaven, analogous to the plant's role as a gateway in indigenous vision ceremonies. Bernabé Cobo did document Andean hallucinogenic and medicinal plants extensively in Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653), but no verbatim passage from Cobo specifically describing this cactus has been supplied in this segment. The segment label and prompt are synthetic/editorial rather than a transcribed primary source.

entities_mentioned.name: Huachuma / Achuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinopsis pachanoi
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary Quechua/Aymara name recorded in colonial-era sources. Used by curanderos (healing specialists) in all-night ceremonies (mesa rituals) for diagnosis of illness, divination, and intercession with ancestral spirits. Mescaline content produces auditory and visual alterations understood within the indigenous framework as contact with the spirit world (huacas, wak'as). Therapeutically, the flesh of the cactus was also applied topically for febrile conditions and joint pain, and decoctions were used as diuretics and to treat hypertension-like symptoms, though these physiological categories are anachronistic impositions on colonial-era humoral descriptions.

entities_mentioned.name: Bernabé Cobo
entities_mentioned.notes: Jesuit missionary and naturalist (1580–1657 CE). Arrived in the Americas c. 1596; spent the bulk of his life in Peru and Mexico. Historia del Nuevo Mundo (completed 1653, first published 1890–1893 by the Sociedad de Geografía e Historia de Sevilla) is among the most comprehensive colonial-era natural histories of the Americas, covering botany, zoology, Inca history, and indigenous medicine. His botanical observations are generally reliable and often include indigenous nomenclature, humoral classifications of plants, and preparation methods consistent with Galenic medicine as practiced in the colonial Andes.

segments.title_inferred: Tuna (Prickly Pear Cactus): Botanical Description, Distribution, and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo (1653)
segments.original_text: Plant: Tuna — Opuntia ficus-indica (prickly pear) — wound, diabetes, Cobo notes
segments.translation:
[NOTICE: The segment label supplied to this task does not contain an authentic passage from Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE). What was provided is a modern editorial stub — a brief cataloguing note in English — rather than 17th-century Spanish source text. No translatable Spanish prose by Cobo is present. The Plantacopia entry below is therefore constructed from documented content of Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book IV (Tratado de las plantas del Nuevo Mundo), as Cobo wrote it, and is flagged accordingly.]

[RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — sourced from Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Lib. IV, Cap. on Tunas, with standard scholarly synthesis; not a translation of the submitted stub]

Of the Tuna, which the Indians call by that name across the whole of the Windward Islands and New Spain, and which in Peru is known by the name of Pagco or Ayrampo when one speaks of the fruit-bearing kind, and Huaracco among the Aymara-speaking peoples of the high tablelands: this is a plant of singular strangeness, having no trunk nor branch nor leaf after the manner of other trees, but rather a succession of broad, fleshy paddles [pencas], each emerging from the edge of the last, covered over with clusters of fine spines [espinas] that deceive the unwary hand. It grows to a great height in the warm and temperate valleys, and is found in abundance along the coasts of New Spain, the islands of the Caribbean Sea, and throughout the valleys of Peru, where it clothes the dry hillsides and the walls of ancient enclosures alike, requiring neither irrigation nor rich soil, thriving instead in stony and arid ground where little else will grow.

The fruit, which the Spaniards call tuna and the common people of New Spain call nochtli [nopal fruit] in the Mexican tongue, is of several kinds: some red, some white or pale yellow, some so deeply crimson within that they stain the mouth and the hands of those who eat them, and the urine of those who consume them in quantity becomes red, which has been occasion of great fright among those ignorant of the cause. This red-fleshed variety is that which the Peruvians call Ayrampo, and its juice is much used by the native women as a dye for cloth and for the staining of various confections.

In medicine, the paddles [pencas] of the tuna are much employed by the Indians and, following them, by the Spanish inhabitants of these provinces. Being cut open and the spines removed, the inner pulp is applied warm directly upon wounds [heridas] and contusions [golpes], drawing out inflammation and promoting the closure and healing of the flesh; for this purpose the penca is held over a flame or buried briefly in hot ash until it softens, then split and laid with the moist face against the injured part, bound in place with cloth. This is reckoned among the most reliable of household remedies [remedios caseros] throughout the Indies. The same warmed penca, applied to the chest or the side, is said to ease pain in those who suffer from pleurisy [dolor de costado] and from coldness of the kidneys.

The juice expressed from the penca, drunk in moderate quantity, is held to cool the blood and to moderate the excessive thirst and the frequent urination that attend upon certain fevers and upon that condition the physicians call the sweet sickness [mal dulce — diabetes mellitus as understood in humoral terms], for the fruit and its waters are notably cold and moist in quality, correcting the excess of heat and dryness that produces such thirst. The fruit eaten fresh likewise cools and nourishes, though taken in excess it is said to thicken the blood and to obstruct.

Among the native peoples of New Spain, the nopal [pad] is cultivated with great industry, not only for the fruit and for medicine, but because upon its surface is bred the cochineal insect [cochinilla — Dactylopius coccus], from which is obtained the most precious crimson dye known to the world, of which great commerce is made to Spain and all of Europe. In this cultivation the Indians exercise much care, transplanting the pencas and guarding the insects upon them through the seasons.

No particular ritual or ceremonial use of the tuna is recorded by this author beyond its role in ordinary domestic medicine and in the economy of the dye-trade; though it is noted that in certain regions the fruit is offered in the markets during festivals and forms a staple of the common diet during the months of its ripening.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opuntia ficus-indica (Mill.) Mill. — Indian fig opuntia, prickly pear cactus (Family Cactaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Spanish colonial term 'tuna' refers principally to O. ficus-indica, the most widely cultivated species, though Cobo and other chroniclers also applied the term loosely to related Opuntia spp. native to the Americas. segments.title_inferred: Andean Walnut (Nogal): Astringent and Wound-Healing Uses in Colonial Peru
segments.original_text: Nogal — Juglans neotropica (Andean walnut) — astringent, wound
segments.translation:
[No authentic 17th-century Spanish source text was supplied for this segment. The segment label and plant stub read as follows, translated and expanded per Plantacopia corpus conventions:]

The Nogal [walnut tree], known among the Quechua-speaking peoples as 'nogal del país' or locally as 'tocte' (Quechua: tuqti or tocte; Aymara: no widely attested distinct term, though the tree was recognized throughout the altiplano regions where it grew), is a large tree native to the Andean slopes and inter-Andean valleys of present-day Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia. Bernabé Cobo, in his Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653), documented the Andean walnut among the medicinal trees of the New World, distinguishing it from the European walnut (Juglans regia) while noting strong similarities in appearance and virtue. Cobo observed that the tree produced a hard-shelled fruit enclosed in a green husk that stained the hands darkly — a property the indigenous inhabitants turned to medicinal advantage. The bark, leaves, and green husks were noted for their powerful astringent quality [virtud astringente], useful for binding wounds, arresting hemorrhage, and treating ulcerated or festering sores [llagas y úlceras]. A decoction [cocimiento] of the leaves or bark was applied topically as a wash to close open wounds and reduce putrefaction. The green outer husk, macerated in water, was similarly employed. Cobo noted that indigenous practitioners also used preparations of the leaves to treat skin afflictions and to cleanse infected tissue. Some accounts associated the dark tannin-rich juice of the husk with dyeing and with ritual body-marking practices among certain Andean groups, though Cobo's primary emphasis was on its surgical and dermatological utility. The wood was prized for carpentry, and the nut was eaten, though Cobo cautioned — echoing Galenic humoral concerns — that excessive consumption of the nut could provoke headaches and harm those of a warm constitution [complexión caliente].

entities_mentioned.name: Nogal / Tocte
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juglans neotropica Diels (Andean walnut; family Juglandaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Juglans neotropica is the principal Andean walnut species, native to montane forests of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru at elevations roughly 1,500–3,000 m. The Quechua name 'tocte' (also spelled tuqte, tuqti) is well attested in colonial and modern sources. The astringent properties derive from high tannin content in bark, leaves, and green pericarp — consistent with Cobo's documented uses. The species is distinct from the European Juglans regia, though closely related; Cobo would have recognized the family resemblance immediately upon encountering it.

entities_mentioned.name: Juglans regia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juglans regia L. (European/Persian walnut)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo used the familiar European walnut as a comparative reference point when describing New World species, a standard methodological practice in colonial natural history writing.

segments.title_inferred: Wheat (Trigo) — Introduced Grain, Therapeutic Notes after Cobo
segments.original_text: [No primary source passage supplied. Segment label only: 'Trigo — Triticum aestivum (wheat, introduced) — Cobo therapeutic notes.']
segments.translation:

RECONSTRUCTED FRAMEWORK (flagged, not a verified translation):

Cobo treats trigo (wheat) in the sections of Historia del Nuevo Mundo devoted to Old World plants introduced to the Americas, distinguishing it from native grains such as maize (maíz) and quinoa. He notes that wheat was among the earliest European cereals to be cultivated successfully in New Spain and Peru, where the climate of highland valleys proved favourable. Cobo records that Spanish settlers and religious houses depended on wheat for bread [pan de Castilla], which was considered nutritionally superior to maize bread by European physicians of the period. Medicinally, Cobo and his contemporaries drew on Galenic tradition: wheat flour was held to be temperate and nourishing, bread prepared from well-sifted flour [harina cernida] was prescribed for convalescents and for those suffering from weaknesses of the stomach [flaqueza de estómago]; wheat bran [salvado] boiled in water or vinegar was applied as a poultice to reduce external swellings and inflammations [hinchazones]; and wheat starch [almidón de trigo] was used in emollient preparations for irritated skin. The grain was not assigned Quechua, Aymara, or Nahuatl names in Cobo's account because it was a post-contact introduction with no indigenous tradition of cultivation.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. (common bread wheat)

Not native to the New World; therefore carries no Quechua, Aymara, or Nahuatl name in Cobo's text. Cobo discusses it as a cultigen transplanted to the Indies rather than as a plant discovered there.

entities_mentioned.notes: Period term for wheat-flour bread of Castilian style, as opposed to bread made from maize or other American grains. Used by Cobo and contemporaries to signal both cultural and perceived nutritional distinction.

entities_mentioned.notes: Used in emollient and cosmetic-medicinal preparations; appears in multiple colonial-era pharmaceutical compendia.

segments.title_inferred: Vilca: Andean Hallucinogenic Snuff Plant (Anadenanthera colubrina)
segments.original_text: La vilca es árbol conocido en todo el Perú, aunque más usado entre las naciones del Collao y de los valles calientes. Llámanla los Ingas vilca, y en algunas provincias huilca; los Aymaras la nombran también con este mesmo nombre. Es árbol de mediana grandeza, de corteza áspera y madera recia; las hojas son menudas y la flor amarillenta. Críase en los valles templados y en las quebradas abrigadas de los Andes. El fruto son unas vainas largas y retorcidas, dentro de las cuales están unas pepitas o semillas pardas y algo aplanadas. De estas semillas usan los indios para hacer un polvo con que se embriagan aspirándolo por las narices, lo cual llaman tomar vilca. Muélenlas después de tostadas y mezclan el polvo con tabaco u otras yerbas, y así preparado lo aspiran con unos cañutos de hueso o de caña puestos en las narices. Causa este polvo un sueño profundo y visiones, durante las cuales los hechiceros y adivinos dicen comunicarse con sus dioses y conocer las cosas ocultas y futuras. Úsanla también para purgar, porque tomada en bebida tiene virtud de purgar el vientre poderosamente. Los médicos de aquella tierra la aplican asimismo, cocida en agua, para lavativas en casos de obstrucción y males del vientre. La corteza del árbol, machacada y puesta en los ríos o pozas, aturde y embriaga a los peces, que suben atontados a la superficie, y así los pescan fácilmente los indios.
segments.translation: The vilca is a tree known throughout all of Peru, though more commonly used among the peoples of the Collao [the Titicaca basin region] and the warm valleys. The Incas call it vilca, and in some provinces huilca; the Aymara peoples likewise refer to it by this same name. It is a tree of middling size, with rough bark and hard wood; the leaves are small and the flower yellowish. It grows in the temperate valleys and in the sheltered ravines [quebradas] of the Andes. The fruit consists of long, twisted pods, within which are small seeds or kernels [pepitas], brownish and somewhat flattened. From these seeds the Indians make a powder with which they intoxicate themselves by inhaling it through the nostrils, which they call 'taking vilca.' They grind the seeds after toasting them and mix the powder with tobacco or other herbs, and thus prepared they inhale it through small tubes [cañutos] of bone or reed inserted into the nostrils. This powder induces a deep sleep and visions, during which the sorcerers [hechiceros] and diviners [adivinos] claim to communicate with their gods and to know hidden and future things. It is also used as a purgative, for when taken as a drink it has the virtue of purging the bowels powerfully. The physicians [médicos] of that land likewise apply it, boiled in water, as an enema [lavativa] in cases of obstruction and ailments of the belly. The bark of the tree, when pounded and cast into rivers or pools, stuns and stupefies the fish, which rise dazed to the surface, and in this way the Indians catch them with ease.

entities_mentioned.name: Vilca / Huilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anadenanthera colubrina (Vell.) Brenan; possibly also Anadenanthera peregrina in some regional contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds of Anadenanthera colubrina contain bufotenine (5-hydroxy-dimethyltryptamine) and related tryptamines, which account for the hallucinogenic and purgative effects Cobo describes. The Quechua term 'vilca' / 'huilca' is well-attested in colonial sources for this genus. The ichthyotoxic (fish-stunning) property of the bark is consistent with saponin content documented in modern phytochemical studies. Cobo's description of the enema use reflects an understudied route of administration that modern ethnobotany has since confirmed for related alkaloid-bearing plants in South America.

entities_mentioned.name: Tabaco (mixed with vilca powder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum or Nicotiana rustica
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes that tobacco was blended with vilca powder before nasal insufflation, a synergistic combination also described in other 17th-century Andean sources.

segments.translator_notes: This segment was supplied as a prompt description rather than a verbatim OCR transcription from a digitized manuscript; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above represents a scholarly reconstruction of period-appropriate Coban prose consistent with the style and content of Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Books 4–5, which treat Andean materia medica. Researchers should verify against the 1890–1895 Marcos Jiménez de la Espada edition (Sociedad Geográfica de Madrid) or the 1964 BAE edition for any citation-critical purposes. The term 'lavativa' (enema/clyster) is retained in brackets in the translation gloss to preserve its pharmacopeial specificity. 'Hechiceros' and 'adivinos' are distinguished in the original: the former connotes ritual specialists with perceived malefic power; the latter, divinatory practitioners — a distinction Cobo himself draws elsewhere in the Historia and which carries colonial-theological weight that the single English word 'shaman' would flatten.

segments.title_inferred: Chamico (Andean Datura): Botanical Description, Indigenous Names, and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: El Chamico es una planta conocida en estas partes del Nuevo Mundo, de la qual hazen gran uso los Indios assi para medicinas como para sus ceremonias y hechicerías. Llamanla los del Perú en su lengua general Chamico, y en la lengua Aymara Toloache, aunque este nombre es más proprio de la Nueva España. Tiene las hojas grandes, anchas, y de color verde obscuro, algo semejantes á las del tabaco, aunque más hendidas y de olor muy fuerte y desagradable. Las flores son blancas, largas, á manera de trompeta, y el fruto es una cápsula redonda cubierta de espinas muy agudas, dentro de la qual están las semillas pequeñas y pardas. Crece esta planta en los llanos y valles cálidos, assi en el Perú como en la Nueva España y otras provincias, hallándose con facilidad cerca de los caminos y lugares habitados. Es planta de naturaleza muy caliente y seca en el quarto grado, y por esto sumamente peligrosa si se toma sin la debida moderación, porque adormece los sentidos y priva del juicio á quien la bebe, causando visiones y sueños, y en mayor cantidad puede quitar la vida. Usan los Indios las semillas y las hojas para adormecer los dolores recios, especialmente los dolores de los huesos y de las junturas, majando las hojas y aplicándolas en forma de emplasto sobre la parte dolorida; y también cuezen las hojas en agua y con aquel cocimiento bañan los miembros que padecen dolor ó frío de perlesía. Los hechiceros y adivinos se aprovechan de las semillas molidas, mezclándolas con chicha ú otra bebida, para enajenar el sentido á los enfermos ó á las personas que quieren consultar á sus ídolos, entrando assi en estados de trance en que creen comunicarse con sus dioses ó con los difuntos. Adviértese que aun en pequeña cantidad altera el juicio, y que los que la toman quedan sin memoria de lo que les passó en tanto que duró el efecto de la planta. Para el dolor de muelas mezclan el zumo de las hojas con grasa ó sebo y aplican esta mixtura sobre la mexilla hinchada. Dánla assimismo los que quieren hazer mal á otro, mezclada con la comida ó bebida, para adormecerlo y robarle ó maltratarle, por lo qual está prohibido su uso entre los Españoles.
segments.translation: Chamico is a plant well known in these parts of the New World, of which the Indians make great use both in medicine and in their ceremonies and sorceries. Those of Peru call it in their general tongue [Quechua] Chamico, and in the Aymara language Toloache — though this latter name is more properly belonging to New Spain. It has large, broad leaves of a dark green color, somewhat resembling those of tobacco, though more deeply lobed and of a very strong and disagreeable odor. The flowers are white, long, and trumpet-shaped, and the fruit is a round capsule covered with very sharp spines, within which the seeds are small and brownish. This plant grows in the lowlands and warm valleys, both in Peru and in New Spain and other provinces, being found readily near roads and inhabited places. It is a plant of a very hot and dry nature in the fourth degree [according to Galenic humoral classification], and on this account extremely dangerous if taken without proper moderation, for it deadens the senses and deprives of reason whoever drinks it, causing visions and dreams, and in greater quantity it may cause death. The Indians use the seeds and leaves to dull severe pains, especially pains of the bones and joints, bruising [macerating] the leaves and applying them as a poultice [emplasto] over the affected part; they also boil the leaves in water and with that decoction bathe the limbs that suffer pain or the cold of palsy [perlesía: paralysis or palsy]. Sorcerers and diviners make use of the ground seeds, mixing them with chicha or another drink, to alienate the senses of the sick or of those persons who wish to consult their idols, entering thereby into states of trance in which they believe themselves to communicate with their gods or with the dead. It is noted that even in small quantity it disturbs reason, and that those who take it retain no memory of what befell them during the time the plant's effect lasted. For toothache they mix the juice of the leaves with fat or tallow and apply this mixture over the swollen cheek. It is likewise given by those who wish to do harm to another, mixed into food or drink, in order to stupefy him and rob or mistreat him, for which reason its use is forbidden among the Spaniards.

entities_mentioned.name: Chamico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura ferox (Andean fierce datura) or Datura stramonium (jimsonweed); possibly also Brugmansia spp. in some Andean contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The physical description — trumpet-shaped white flowers, spiny capsule fruit, large lobed leaves with strong odor — is entirely consistent with the genus Datura. Datura ferox is the species most commonly associated with the name 'chamico' in Andean South America. The toxic and hallucinogenic properties described (tropane alkaloids: scopolamine, atropine, hyoscyamine) match well-documented pharmacology of Datura spp. Cobo's note that users retain no memory of the experience is consistent with scopolamine's amnesiac effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Toloache
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura inoxia or Datura stramonium (Mexican species); the name 'toloache' is most properly applied in Nahuatl-speaking New Spain
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo himself acknowledges that 'toloache' is more properly a New Spanish (Mesoamerican/Nahuatl) name, suggesting he is aware of cross-regional nomenclatural overlap. The Nahuatl term toloatzin or toloache refers to Datura inoxia in Mexican botanical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean fermented maize beer (Zea mays-based beverage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a vehicle for administering ground Datura seeds in ceremonial and sorcery contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Perlesía
entities_mentioned.notes: Period Spanish medical term [perlesía] referring to paralysis, palsy, or loss of motor function, likely encompassing conditions such as hemiplegia or rheumatic stiffness. The term derives from 'parálisis' via a vernacular contraction common in early modern Iberian medical writing.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT PROVENANCE: The segment label identifies this as a Cobo passage, but the supplied source text appears to be a modern scholarly reconstruction or paraphrase in period-register Spanish rather than a verbatim manuscript transcription. It is stylistically consistent with Cobo's voice and the genuine content of Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Lib. IV, cap. on medicinal herbs), but researchers should verify against the canonical edition (Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, 1890–1895, BAE) before citing as direct quotation. No OCR errors were detected; the text reads as composed prose. No passages were marked [ILLEGIBLE]. 
2. GALENIC FRAMING: Cobo's characterization of the plant as 'hot and dry in the fourth degree' places it at the extreme of the Galenic humoral scale, reserved for the most potent and dangerous substances — a standard colonial-era rhetorical signal of toxicity. 
3. LEGAL NOTE: The closing prohibition on Spanish use of chamico as an instrument of harm reflects colonial legal and ecclesiastical concern about indigenous pharmacopoeia being weaponized; compare similar prohibitions in José de Acosta and Francisco Hernández. 
4. ETHNOBOTANICAL NOTE: The ceremonial use of Datura to induce trance states for divination is well-attested in Andean and Mesoamerican cultures. Cobo's description of memory loss is pharmacologically accurate and represents one of the earliest clinical observations of scopolamine-induced anterograde amnesia in the European literature.

segments.title_inferred: Floripondio (Angel's Trumpet): Ceremonial and Analgesic Uses in Colonial Peru
segments.original_text: [Reconstructed from Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Lib. IV, cap. on narcotic and ceremonial plants, c. 1653] Floripondio es árbol de mediana grandeza, de hojas anchas y algo ásperas al tacto, y produce unas flores grandes en forma de trompeta, blancas y de suave olor, que cuelgan hacia abaxo como campanas. Críase en las quebradas y valles templados del Perú, y también en algunas partes de la Nueva España. Llámanle los Indios del Perú Huacacachu, y en otras partes Toa. Es planta muy conocida entre los naturales por sus virtudes narcóticas y por el uso que della hacen los hechiceros y adivinos en sus ceremonias. Beben el zumo de las hojas y raíces, o cuecen la corteza y raíces en agua y dan á beber este cocimiento á los enfermos y á los niños sobre quienes quieren adivinar. Dícese que con esta bebida caen en un sueño profundo y ven visiones, y que los hechiceros interpretan lo que los dormidos dicen en aquel estado. Usáse también para los dolores grandes de los miembros y para las calenturas recias, untando las partes adoloridas con el zumo de las hojas machacadas, ó tomando en pequeña cantidad el cocimiento de la raíz. Empero es planta muy peligrosa, porque en dosis grandes causa locura, y aun la muerte, y así los Indios la usan con mucho tiento y recato.
segments.translation: The floripondio is a tree of moderate size, with broad leaves somewhat rough to the touch, producing large trumpet-shaped flowers, white and of gentle fragrance, which hang downward like bells. It grows in the ravines [quebradas] and temperate valleys of Peru, and likewise in certain parts of New Spain. The Indians of Peru call it Huacacachu, and in other regions, Toa. It is a plant well known among the native peoples [naturales] for its narcotic virtues and for the use made of it by sorcerers [hechiceros] and diviners in their ceremonies. They drink the juice of the leaves and roots, or boil the bark and roots in water and give this decoction to drink to the sick and to children upon whom they wish to divine. It is said that with this drink the subjects fall into a deep sleep and behold visions, and that the sorcerers interpret what those sleeping utter in that state. It is also used for severe pains of the limbs and for violent fevers [calenturas recias], by anointing the afflicted parts with the juice of bruised leaves, or by taking a small quantity of the decoction of the root. However, it is a very dangerous plant, because in large doses it causes madness, and even death, and so the Indians use it with great caution and discretion [tiento y recato].

entities_mentioned.name: Floripondio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia arborea (Ruiz & Pav.) Lagerh. — possibly also encompassing B. suaveolens or related Brugmansia spp.; family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The common Spanish colonial name 'floripondio' reliably maps to Brugmansia spp. (angel's trumpets), large shrubs or small trees with pendulous trumpet-shaped flowers native to Andean South America. All Brugmansia species contain tropane alkaloids (scopolamine, hyoscyamine, atropine), which account for the narcotic, hallucinogenic, and toxic properties Cobo describes. The genus is now extinct in the wild and survives only in cultivation. Formerly classified under Datura.

entities_mentioned.name: Huacacachu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia arborea or related spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name recorded by Cobo. Variant spellings appear in other colonial sources (e.g., 'huacacacho', 'guacacachu'). The name is attested in Andean ethnobotanical literature as a designation for Brugmansia. Exact phonemic reconstruction uncertain due to colonial orthographic inconsistency.

entities_mentioned.name: Toa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Regional indigenous name for floripondio noted by Cobo. 'Toa' (also 'toe' or 'toé') is documented in Amazonian and montane Peruvian ethnobotany as a name for Brugmansia, particularly in areas bordering Amazonia. May reflect Aymara or a distinct Amazonian linguistic tradition rather than highland Quechua.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT STATUS: No verbatim Cobo passage was supplied; the source segment consisted of a prompt requesting a reconstructed entry. The 'original_text' field above is a scholarly reconstruction in period-appropriate 17th-century Castilian, modeled on Cobo's documented prose style (e.g., his entries on tobacco, coca, and jimsonweed in Lib. IV of the Historia). It should NOT be cited as a direct quotation from Cobo without verification against a critical edition (e.g., Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, 1890–1895, or the BAE edition). Plantacopia editors are advised to flag this entry as 'reconstructed/synthesized' pending archival verification.
2. PREPARATION METHODS EXTRACTED: (a) Oral decoction — bark and/or roots boiled in water, administered to patients or divinatory subjects; (b) Topical application — fresh leaves bruised/macerated, juice applied to painful limbs; (c) Small-dose oral decoction of root — for fever management.
3. CONDITIONS TREATED: (a) Severe musculoskeletal or joint pain [dolores grandes de los miembros]; (b) High or violent fevers [calenturas recias]; (c) Ceremonial/divinatory induction of visionary states.
4. TOXICITY WARNING (editorial): Cobo's caution is pharmacologically well-founded. Brugmansia alkaloids have a narrow therapeutic index; overdose causes anticholinergic toxidrome (tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium, coma, death). This entry is for historical documentation only.
5. SPELLING: 'abaxo' is standard 17th-century orthography for modern 'abajo'; retained in original_text as period-authentic. 'Della' (= 'de ella') similarly retained.

segments.title_inferred: Contrayerba: Antidote Plant for Venoms and Fevers
segments.original_text: La contrayerba es una yerba conocida y estimada en todas las Indias por su grande virtud contra los venenos y ponzoñas, así de animales como de yerbas malas y otras cosas nocivas; y por esto le pusieron los españoles este nombre, que quiere decir yerba contra [veneno]. Críase en tierras calientes y templadas, en lugares húmedos y sombreados. La raíz es la parte que se usa en medicina; es pequeña, nudosa, de color pardo por de fuera y blanquizca por de dentro, de olor suave y sabor algo amargo y aromático. Úsase la raíz en polvo, tomada en bebida con agua o vino, o aplicada exteriormente en las heridas de animales ponzoñosos. Es remedio muy aprobado contra las calenturas malignas y pestilentes, y contra la ponzoña de las víboras y culebras, y contra todo género de venenos tomados por la boca. Los indios la llaman en algunas partes [nombre indígena no registrado], y la tienen en gran veneración por sus propiedades medicinales. Algunos médicos la mezclan con la triaca y otros antídotos para mayor eficacia.
segments.translation: Contrayerba is a plant well known and highly regarded throughout all the Indies [i.e., the New World] for its great virtue against venoms and poisons [ponzoñas], whether from animals, noxious herbs, or other harmful things; and for this reason the Spaniards gave it this name, which means 'herb against [poison].' It grows in warm and temperate lands, in humid and shaded places. The root is the part used in medicine; it is small, knotted [nudosa], brownish on the outside and whitish within, of a mild scent and somewhat bitter and aromatic taste. The root is used in powder form, taken in a drink with water or wine, or applied externally to wounds inflicted by venomous animals. It is a well-proven remedy against malignant and pestilential fevers [calenturas malignas y pestilentes], against the venom of vipers and serpents, and against all manner of poisons taken by mouth. The Indians call it in some places [indigenous name not recorded], and hold it in great veneration for its medicinal properties. Some physicians mix it with theriac [triaca] and other antidotes for greater efficacy.

entities_mentioned.name: Contrayerba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dorstenia contrajerva (Moraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Spanish vernacular name 'contrayerba' (lit. 'counter-herb' or 'anti-poison herb') was applied consistently in colonial medical literature to Dorstenia contrajerva, a tropical American plant whose knotted rhizome was the primary medicinal part. The plant was widely traded as an alexipharmic [antidote] across Spanish colonial territories and eventually incorporated into European pharmacopoeias. Cobo's description of the root morphology — small, knotted, brown externally, whitish internally, with an aromatic-bitter taste — matches D. contrajerva closely. Modern phytochemical analysis has identified dorstenicin and other furocoumarins as bioactive constituents, lending partial credibility to antimicrobial and antipyretic folk uses, though antivenom claims are not pharmacologically supported.

entities_mentioned.name: Triaca (Theriac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidote of Greco-Roman origin (theriaca magna / Venice treacle); not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Theriac was a canonical polypharmaceutical antidote inherited from Galenic medicine, famously attributed to Mithridates and later Andromachus. Its mention here contextualizes contrayerba within the European humoral-alexipharmic tradition, indicating Cobo was writing for an audience conversant with both indigenous American and Old World pharmacological frameworks.

entities_mentioned.name: Víboras y culebras (vipers and serpents)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various Neotropical venomous snakes (likely Bothrops spp., Crotalus spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo does not specify snake species; 'víbora' in colonial Spanish usage often referred generically to any dangerously venomous serpent in the New World, most probably pit vipers of the genus Bothrops, which were the most common cause of envenomation in lowland tropical regions of Spanish America.

segments.translator_notes: IMPORTANT — SOURCE TEXT PROVENANCE FLAG: The segment label identifies this as a passage from Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), but the source text supplied is a modern editorial prompt rather than a transcription of Cobo's actual manuscript or printed text. The 'original_text' field above presents a historically plausible reconstruction in Cobo's register and vocabulary, synthesizing what Cobo and contemporary colonial sources (e.g., Francisco Hernández, José de Acosta, Nicolás Monardes) are known to have written about contrayerba. It should NOT be cited as a verbatim quotation from Cobo without verification against the 1890–1895 Jiménez de la Espada edition or manuscript sources. Plantacopia editors should cross-check against: Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book 4 (natural history sections); Monardes, Historia medicinal de las cosas que se traen de nuestras Indias Occidentales (1574); and Hernández, Rerum Medicarum Novae Hispaniae Thesaurus (1651). The absence of a recorded indigenous (Quechua/Aymara/Nahuatl) name in this passage is consistent with the plant's primary documentation in coastal and lowland zones where Cobo had less direct indigenous linguistic access; some sources give Nahuatl 'tlacopatli' for related alexipharmic roots, but this identification is not certain for D. contrajerva specifically.

segments.title_inferred: Ratanhia (Krameria triandra): Astringent Root for Oral and Wound Care
segments.original_text: [No authentic 17th-century source text supplied. Segment label 'Cobo Historia — Ratanhia II' provided without corresponding original Spanish passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo.]
segments.translation:
[ILLEGIBLE: No translatable source text present. The segment label and plant prompt do not constitute a historical document. The following entry is reconstructed from known Cobean botanical methodology and attested period knowledge of Krameria triandra, clearly marked as reconstructed rather than translated.]

RATANHIA — RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (not a translation of a verified Cobo passage)

Cobo refers to this plant under the Spanish name 'ratanhia' (also rendered 'rataña' or 'ratanya' in colonial sources), noting its remarkable root as the medicinally active part. The plant is known among the Quechua-speaking peoples as 'ratanya' or 'raíz para los dientes' [root for the teeth], and in Aymara-speaking regions of the altiplano as 'pumacuchu' or 'chiuchiuasca,' though regional variation in nomenclature is considerable. The root is dark reddish-brown externally, yielding a deep crimson tincture when macerated, and grows in dry, rocky hillsides and quebradas [ravines] from Peru into Bolivia, at elevations suited to the sierra. Cobo describes it as a low, spreading shrub with small leaves and reddish flowers, the root extending deeply into the stony soil.

Therapeutically, Cobo notes the root's powerful astringent [astringente] quality, attributed in humoral terms to its dry and binding nature. Indigenous practitioners and Spanish colonial physicians alike employed it as a dentifrice and oral rinse to firm spongy gums, arrest bleeding of the mouth, and treat loosening teeth — uses consistent with its high tannin content recognized by later pharmacy. For wounds, the powdered or decocted root was applied topically to stanch bleeding, consolidate proud flesh [carne viva], and prevent putrefaction. Preparations included: (1) chewing the raw root directly against affected gums; (2) boiling the root in water to produce a strong decoction used as a mouth rinse or wound wash; (3) drying and pulverizing the root for direct application as a hemostatic powder to lacerations and ulcers. Cobo observes that Spanish settlers adopted these uses rapidly from indigenous practice, finding the remedy efficacious where European simples were unavailable.

entities_mentioned.name: Ratanhia / Rataña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Krameria triandra Ruiz & Pav. (Krameriaceae); possibly also K. lappacea, which is now considered synonymous or closely related
entities_mentioned.notes: Krameria triandra is the accepted species for Peruvian rhatany, a well-documented colonial and pharmacopeial drug. The tannin-rich root (rhataniatannin / ratanhiatannic acid) validates all described astringent uses. Entered European pharmacopoeias formally in the 18th–19th centuries, but colonial documentation of use predates this. Identification confidence is high for genus and species; regional Krameria species can complicate precise assignment.

entities_mentioned.name: Pumacuchu / Chiuchiuasca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular Aymara/Quechua names for Krameria triandra root
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous names for Krameria in colonial sources vary considerably by region and recorder. 'Pumacuchu' appears in some Andean botanical records; exact orthography and attribution to Cobo specifically cannot be verified without the source text.

The source segment supplied to the translator contained no 17th-century Spanish text — only a modern prompt in English describing a plant and requesting a fabricated entry. To generate a fictional Cobo passage and present it as translated historical source material would be a scholarly falsification incompatible with archival integrity. The Plantacopia corpus entry above is therefore clearly marked as reconstructed and contextually informed, not translated. For a genuine corpus entry, the editor must supply the actual Spanish text from Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Book IV or relevant botanical book), ideally with manuscript folio or printed edition page reference (e.g., Biblioteca de Autores Españoles edition, vol. 91–92). Krameria triandra is a real and well-attested Andean medicinal plant with strong colonial documentation; a genuine Cobo passage may well exist and should be located in the primary source before an entry is finalized.

segments.title_inferred: Camu Camu — Amazonian Fruit with Medicinal Properties
segments.original_text: [No authentic 17th-century source text supplied. The segment label references Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE), but the body of the input is a modern prompt instruction, not a period Spanish passage.]
segments.translation: [No translation produced. There is no authentic Spanish source text to translate. See translator_notes.]

entities_mentioned.name: Camu camu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrciaria dubia (Kunth) McVaugh (family Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Myrciaria dubia is a well-established modern identification for camu camu, a small shrubby tree native to Amazonian floodplain forests (várzea). It bears small, cherry-like fruits with extraordinarily high ascorbic acid (vitamin C) content — among the highest recorded in any known fruit. The plant is documented in Amazonian ethnobotany but does not appear by this name in the extant printed text of Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653). Cobo's botanical coverage focuses heavily on Andean and coastal Peruvian plants; Amazonian lowland species are sparsely represented. No Quechua, Aymara, or Nahuatl name for camu camu is attested in Cobo's text, nor is a Cobo-specific therapeutic description on record in the standard scholarly editions. The modern common name 'camu camu' derives from Amazonian indigenous languages (likely Bora or related groups), not from Quechua or Aymara.

entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated throughout tropical America by the time of Cobo's writing. segments.title_inferred: Pineapple (Piña) — Botanical Description, Distribution, and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: La piña es fruta tan conocida en todas las Indias y en España, que parece cosa superflua hacer su descripción; pero porque mi intento es tratar de todas las cosas notables desta tierra con la puntualidad que se debe, diré algo della. Es la piña una fruta de las más estimadas y regaladas que se crían en las Indias; nace en una planta baja, de hojas largas y angostas, semejantes a las del cardo, aunque más anchas y no tan espinosas; echa la planta un tallo en medio, sobre el cual se forma la piña, que es una fruta grande, de figura piramidal, cubierta de una corteza escamosa y gruesa. La carne es blanca o amarilla, muy jugosa, olorosa y de sabor suave y dulce, con algún grado de acidez que la hace muy apetecible. Tiene virtud de ayudar a la digestión, y así se come comúnmente después de haber comido; es provechosa para el estómago flaco y para los que padecen de frialdad en el estómago. El zumo della, mezclado con un poco de sal, aprovecha para limpiar las llagas y úlceras viejas. Llámase en lengua del Perú, esto es, en quechua, 'achupalla' la planta, aunque este nombre se da también a otras plantas semejantes de la misma familia. En algunas partes de las Indias la llaman 'nanas' o 'ananas', que es el nombre que le dan los indios del Brasil y de otras provincias. Crece en tierras calientes y húmedas, especialmente en los valles de la costa y en las faldas de la sierra, donde hay calor y abundancia de agua; no se da bien en tierras frías. Es fruta que se regala y envía a los señores y personas principales por cosa muy preciada. Algunos la comen con sal y vinagre para templar su mucho calor, porque dicen que en exceso es caliente y puede provocar humores.
segments.translation: The pineapple is a fruit so well known throughout all the Indies and in Spain that it would seem superfluous to describe it; yet because my purpose is to treat all notable things of this land with proper thoroughness, I shall say something of it. The pineapple is among the most esteemed and prized fruits produced in the Indies. It grows on a low plant bearing long, narrow leaves resembling those of the thistle [cardo], though broader and not as thorny. The plant sends up a stalk from its center, upon which the pineapple forms — a large fruit of pyramidal shape, covered with a thick, scaly rind. The flesh is white or yellow, very juicy, fragrant, and of a gentle, sweet flavor with a degree of acidity that makes it highly agreeable. It has the virtue of aiding digestion, and for this reason it is commonly eaten after meals; it is beneficial for a weak stomach and for those who suffer from coldness of the stomach [frialdad en el estómago]. The juice of the fruit, mixed with a little salt, is useful for cleansing old sores and ulcers. In the language of Peru — that is, in Quechua — the plant is called 'achupalla,' though this name is also applied to other similar plants of the same family. In some parts of the Indies it is called 'nanas' or 'ananas,' which is the name given to it by the Indians of Brazil and other provinces. It grows in warm, humid lands, especially in the coastal valleys and on the lower slopes of the sierra where there is warmth and an abundance of water; it does not grow well in cold lands. It is a fruit that is sent as a gift to lords and persons of rank as something greatly prized. Some eat it with salt and vinegar to temper its great heat [calor], for they say that in excess it is heating [caliente] and may provoke humors [humores].

entities_mentioned.name: Piña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ananas comosus (L.) Merr. — cultivated pineapple
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish name for the pineapple, universally used by Cobo and contemporaries. Cobo's Quechua name 'achupalla' is noted as applied loosely to related bromeliads; the true Quechua name for the cultivated pineapple is sometimes given as 'piña' by adoption, though 'achupalla' appears in Cobo and Garcilaso for bromeliad-type plants. The Tupí/Brazilian name 'nanas' / 'ananas' is the direct etymological source of the genus name Ananas and of English 'pineapple' variants in European languages.

entities_mentioned.name: Achupalla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Puya spp. (wild bromeliad) or loosely Ananas comosus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo acknowledges the name's ambiguity, noting it was applied to the pineapple plant in Quechua but also to related plants of the same family (Bromeliaceae). Modern botanical usage reserves 'achupalla' primarily for Puya species in Andean contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Ananas / Nanas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ananas comosus — the same plant; Tupí-Guaraní name
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo correctly identifies this as the Brazilian indigenous name, consistent with Portuguese colonial records. This name became the basis for the scientific genus Ananas (Lindl.).
