(7) Crocodile fat [from Crocodylus niloticus] — a prestige and magico-medical ingredient; applied topically, particularly to the scalp in hair-growth and hair-darkening preparations, and used in ointments for specific ailments; its use may reflect both empirical and ritual/symbolic dimensions of Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Castor oil (kiki)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ricinus communis (castor bean)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the Ebers Papyrus. The Egyptian term 'kiki' is well-attested and consistently identified with castor. Modern pharmacology confirms ricinoleic acid as the active laxative component. Also validated as an antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory agent topically.

entities_mentioned.name: Olive oil (merhet / nehet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea (olive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used in the ancient Mediterranean world. Egyptian olive cultivation is attested archaeobotanically. The Ebers Papyrus references olive oil predominantly as a base/vehicle and emollient. Rich in oleic acid and polyphenols; modern pharmacology validates anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Moringa oil / Desert date oil (bak)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moringa peregrina or Balanites aegyptiaca
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term 'bak' is debated: some scholars assign it to Moringa peregrina (ben oil), others to Balanites aegyptiaca (desert date). Both were native to Egypt and used for oil extraction. Ben oil (Moringa) is particularly valued for its stability and odour-absorption properties, making it ideal as a perfume/ointment base. Modern analysis of Moringa oil confirms high oleic acid content and antioxidant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Linseed oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum (flax)
entities_mentioned.notes: Flax was extensively cultivated in ancient Egypt primarily for fibre (linen), but its seeds and oil also had documented medicinal uses. Linseed oil is rich in alpha-linolenic acid (omega-3); modern pharmacology confirms emollient, laxative, and anti-inflammatory properties consistent with ancient usage.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose fat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anser anser or related Anatidae (domestic/wild goose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal fats were widely differentiated in Egyptian pharmacy, with specific therapeutic properties attributed to each species. Goose fat appears frequently in hair and scalp preparations. Modern lipid analysis suggests its high unsaturated fat content may have made it a genuinely effective penetrating emollient.

entities_mentioned.name: Beef fat (tallow)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos taurus (domestic cattle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Rendered cattle tallow used as an ointment base and suppository vehicle. High saturated fat content; modern pharmacology does not strongly validate specific therapeutic action, but its role as a stable, occlusive emollient base is chemically consistent.

entities_mentioned.name: Crocodile fat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crocodylus niloticus (Nile crocodile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in hair preparations and specialized topical ointments. Crocodile fat may carry significant magico-ritual significance in addition to empirical application. Modern pharmacological validation is absent; its use likely reflects the symbolic prestige of crocodile-derived materials in Egyptian culture as much as observed therapeutic effect.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a scholarly descriptor rather than a direct transcription of a specific column passage; accordingly, the translation synthesizes documented uses across multiple Ebers Papyrus columns (notably Ebers 63–85 and related sections on ointments and compound remedies) rather than representing a single discrete textual unit. The identification of 'bak' as either Moringa peregrina or Balanites aegyptiaca remains contested in Egyptological and archaeobotanical literature; both identifications have strong proponents. Egyptian pharmacy did not draw a sharp boundary between 'active ingredient' and 'vehicle': oils and fats functioned simultaneously as therapeutically active agents and as delivery media, a dual role now recognized in modern pharmaceutical sciences under the concept of 'excipient bioactivity.' The differentiation of animal fats by species source (goose, crocodile, beef, etc.) suggests a sophisticated empirical taxonomy of lipid materials in ancient Egyptian medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Honey and Bee Products as Medicinal Agents in Ancient Egyptian Practice
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Honey and bee products: honey as the primary wound dressing in ancient Egyptian medicine. Also beeswax (for ointments), bee larvae (protein), propolis (antibacterial).
segments.translation: Honey and its associated bee products occupy a central role in ancient Egyptian medical practice as documented in the Ebers Papyrus. Honey served as the primary topical wound dressing, applied directly to lacerations, burns, and infected tissue to promote healing and prevent putrefaction. Beeswax was employed as a binding and emollient agent in the formulation of ointments and salves. Bee larvae, rich in protein, appear as a nutritive or therapeutic ingredient in certain preparations. Propolis, the resinous substance collected by bees from plant sources, functioned as an antibacterial and sealing agent in medical compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey [bit / bjt in ancient Egyptian]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey — raw, unprocessed honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Honey is among the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in hundreds of prescriptions. Its antimicrobial properties are now well-understood: low water activity, hydrogen peroxide production, and low pH collectively inhibit bacterial growth. Modern clinical research (Molan, 2006; Cooper et al.) validates its use in wound care, particularly Manuka honey in contemporary medicine. Egyptian honey was applied topically and also taken internally in compound remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Beeswax [mnj or related term]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cera alba — beeswax from Apis mellifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Beeswax functioned as an excipient and base in Egyptian pharmaceutical preparations, analogous to the role of petrolatum in modern ointments. It provided consistency, skin-barrier protection, and slow-release of active ingredients. Its chemical stability and hypoallergenic nature make it suitable for wound and skin applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Bee larvae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Larvae of Apis mellifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Bee larvae appear in some ancient Egyptian preparations likely as a protein-rich or bioactive ingredient. Apitherapy traditions across cultures have attributed healing properties to larval secretions. Modern science identifies royal jelly and brood secretions as containing proteins, fatty acids (notably 10-hydroxy-2-decenoic acid), and antimicrobial peptides, though direct clinical validation of larval use as documented in the Ebers Papyrus context is limited.

entities_mentioned.name: Propolis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Propolis — resinous mixture collected by Apis mellifera from plant exudates
entities_mentioned.notes: Propolis is a complex resinous substance used by bees to seal hive structures. Its antibacterial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory properties are extensively validated in modern pharmacology; constituent flavonoids (pinocembrin, galangin) and phenolic acids are primary bioactive compounds. Reference to propolis in the Ebers Papyrus context is inferred from the broader category of bee products; explicit ancient Egyptian terminology for propolis as distinct from honey or wax is debated in Egyptological literature.

segments.translator_notes: This segment is a modern scholarly description of bee-product usage in the Ebers Papyrus rather than a direct transcription of a specific papyrus column or prescription. As such, there is no corresponding ancient Egyptian source text to transliterate. The Ebers Papyrus dates to approximately 1550 BCE but draws on traditions potentially as old as 3000 BCE. Honey prescriptions appear throughout the text in numerous columns (e.g., Eb. 2, Eb. 105, Eb. 456, among many others); specific column references should be verified against the Wreszinski or von Deines/Grapow editions for citation accuracy. The pharmacological annotations (antibacterial, protein source) reflect modern retrospective analysis and were not concepts articulated in the original Egyptian text.

segments.title_inferred: Mineral Drugs in the Ebers Papyrus: Natron, Malachite, Ochre, Galena, Alum, Calcite, and Salt
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Mineral drugs: natron (sodium carbonate), malachite (copper carbonate), red ochre (iron oxide), lead sulfide (galena/kohl), alum, calcite, salt. All with specific therapeutic applications.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of several mineral substances as therapeutic agents, each assigned to specific medical conditions and preparation methods: (1) Natron [Egyptian: nṯry / netjery] — hydrated sodium carbonate/bicarbonate, sourced from desert lake deposits (notably Wadi Natrun); used as a cleansing, preservative, and antiseptic agent, applied topically and internally. (2) Malachite [Egyptian: wꜣḏ / wadj, 'the green one'] — copper carbonate hydroxide, ground and applied as an ophthalmic remedy for eye diseases including conjunctivitis and trachoma; its copper ions confer genuine antimicrobial efficacy. (3) Red Ochre [Egyptian: mnšt or similar] — iron oxide (hematite), used in wound dressings, skin treatments, and as a styptic/haemostatic agent; also carried ritual-protective valence. (4) Lead Sulfide / Galena [Egyptian: msdmt / mesdemet] — the primary component of kohl eyeliner; applied periocularly to treat and prevent eye infections and reduce glare; modern research suggests lead(II) ions may stimulate nitric oxide production, conferring a degree of antimicrobial protection, though chronic toxicity is a significant concern. (5) Alum [Egyptian: approximately ḥmt or referenced by function] — potassium aluminium sulfate; used as an astringent, styptic, and antiseptic in wound care and gynecological preparations. (6) Calcite [Egyptian: šsp or inr HD, 'white stone'] — calcium carbonate; ground and incorporated into medicinal pastes and suppositories; used as a base/filler and possibly as an antacid. (7) Salt [Egyptian: ḥmꜣt / hemat] — sodium chloride; widely employed as a preservative, antiseptic wound wash, and component of compound remedies; used both internally and externally.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate / sodium bicarbonate (Na₂CO₃ / NaHCO₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian name nṯry (netjery). Harvested from evaporite deposits, especially Wadi Natrun. Used in mummification, ritual purification, and medicine. Antiseptic and alkalizing properties are pharmacologically valid. Referenced throughout the Ebers Papyrus in wound care, oral hygiene, and compound remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Malachite
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper(II) carbonate hydroxide [Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂]
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian name wꜣḏ (wadj), meaning 'green.' Ground into fine powder and mixed with fat or water for ophthalmic application. Copper ions possess documented bacteriostatic and fungistatic activity. Particularly prescribed for eye conditions (e.g., Ebers columns 56–62). One of the most frequently cited minerals in the papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Red Ochre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iron(III) oxide / Hematite (Fe₂O₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a styptic and in dermatological preparations. Ritual and symbolic associations with blood and regeneration. May appear under several Egyptian designations. Limited modern pharmacological validation beyond mechanical wound-packing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Galena / Kohl (Lead Sulfide)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lead(II) sulfide (PbS)
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian name msdmt (mesdemet). Applied around the eyes as kohl. Ebers Papyrus prescribes it for eye diseases. Modern studies (e.g., Tapsoba et al., 2010, Analytical Chemistry) suggest PbS nanoparticles may stimulate NO production and offer localized antimicrobial benefit, but chronic lead exposure poses significant neurotoxic risk. Confidence in identification is high; therapeutic validation is nuanced.

entities_mentioned.name: Alum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium aluminium sulfate [KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O]
entities_mentioned.notes: The precise Egyptian term is debated; alum is identified by context and chemical analysis of residues. Used as an astringent in wound care, gynecological treatments, and mouth preparations. Astringency and mild antiseptic action are pharmacologically well-established.

entities_mentioned.name: Calcite
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian 'white stone' (inr HD or šsp). Used as an excipient/base in medicinal pastes and suppositories. Possible antacid use. Identification is based primarily on mineralogical analysis of archaeological pharmaceutical residues rather than unambiguous textual terminology.

entities_mentioned.name: Salt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian ḥmꜣt (hemat). Ubiquitous in Ebers prescriptions as antiseptic wash, preservative, and compound ingredient. Used topically (wound irrigation) and internally. Osmotic antimicrobial mechanism is well-established. Appears in scores of Ebers formulae.

segments.translator_notes: This segment functions as a categorical summary of mineral materia medica rather than a transcription of a specific Ebers column. Egyptian mineral terminology is partially reconstructed from Egyptological scholarship (Nunn 1996, 'Ancient Egyptian Medicine'; Manniche 1989, 'An Ancient Egyptian Herbal') and archaeochemical analyses. Egyptian phonemic renderings are given in simplified transliteration. Where column-specific citations are absent from the source segment, pharmacological notes draw on cross-referenced Ebers columns (e.g., cols. 56–93 for ophthalmic treatments) and modern analytical studies. Translator recommends cross-referencing with the Hearst Papyrus and Edwin Smith Papyrus for corroborating mineral drug usage. The galena/kohl entry warrants particular care in any modern-facing summary due to lead toxicity implications.

segments.title_inferred: Garlic (Allium sativum) in the Ebers Papyrus: Therapeutic Applications and Cultural Context
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Garlic (Allium sativum) in Egyptian medicine: extensive use across the papyrus — 22 separate prescriptions. For heart disease, tumours, infestations, infections. Workers at Giza paid in garlic.
segments.translation: Garlic [Ancient Egyptian: likely 'ḥdw' or 'ipt', though orthography varies by column] appears in twenty-two distinct prescriptions throughout the Ebers Papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the entire text. Its therapeutic applications span a broad range of conditions, including: diseases of the heart [Egyptian conceptualisation of cardiac and circulatory disorders], tumorous or abnormal growths [swellings, cysts, or neoplastic lesions as understood in Egyptian medical theory], parasitic and microbial infestations [including intestinal worms and putrefactive infections], and generalised infections [both internal and topical]. Garlic was administered via multiple preparation methods including oral decoction, direct topical application, and incorporation into compound remedies. Of additional historical and economic significance, documentary evidence records that labourers engaged in construction at the Giza plateau were remunerated in part with garlic rations, attesting to its perceived value as both a nutritive and prophylactic substance in ancient Egyptian society.

entities_mentioned.name: Garlic
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium sativum
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most extensively documented medicinal plants in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in 22 separate prescriptions. Modern pharmacology validates many of its traditional uses: allicin and related organosulfur compounds demonstrate antimicrobial, antifungal, antiparasitic, antithrombotic, and hypotensive properties, lending credence to its use for infections, infestations, and heart-related conditions. The economic role of garlic as worker compensation at Giza is corroborated by ancient administrative records and has been widely cited in Egyptological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Heart disease [Egyptian conceptualisation]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cardiovascular disease / circulatory disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient Egyptian medicine attributed a wide range of systemic ailments to the heart and its associated vessels ('metu'). 'Heart disease' in this context may encompass hypertension, palpitations, oedema, and other circulatory conditions rather than a precise modern cardiac diagnosis.

entities_mentioned.name: Tumours [Egyptian swellings/growths]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Neoplastic lesions, cysts, abscesses, or inflammatory swellings
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term used for such growths is not specified in this segment. Egyptian medical texts categorise various swellings under terms that may refer to benign cysts, abscesses, or tumour-like masses. Modern identification as true neoplastic tumours should be treated cautiously.

entities_mentioned.name: Infestations
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Intestinal parasites (e.g., helminths, Ascaris spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Parasitic infection, particularly intestinal worms, was a major medical concern in ancient Egypt. Allium sativum has documented anthelmintic properties that partially validate this traditional application.

entities_mentioned.name: Giza plateau construction workers
entities_mentioned.notes: Historical reference: administrative papyri and archaeological evidence from the Giza workers' settlement indicate garlic was distributed as part of worker rations, reflecting its dual status as food and medicine.

segments.translator_notes: This segment functions as a scholarly summary annotation rather than a direct transcription of an Egyptian-language passage, and therefore no hieratic or hieroglyphic source text is present to translate directly. The entry has been rendered as a full scholarly corpus description consistent with Plantacopia archive standards. The ancient Egyptian name for garlic is not provided in this segment; researchers should cross-reference individual column citations within the Ebers Papyrus (e.g., Eb. 163, Eb. 247, and others) for precise terminology and column-level philological detail. The figure of 22 separate prescriptions is widely cited in secondary literature and is treated here as reliable, though independent verification against Joachim (1890) and Grundriss der Medizin (von Deines, Grapow, Westendorf) is recommended.

segments.title_inferred: Onion (Allium cepa) in Egyptian Medical Practice: Prescriptions, Therapeutic Uses, and Archaeological Context
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Onion (Allium cepa) in Egyptian medicine: 14 prescriptions. For coughs, fevers, skin conditions. Found in the tombs of pharaohs.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus records fourteen distinct prescriptions involving the onion (Allium cepa) within the corpus of Egyptian medical practice. These prescriptions address a range of conditions including coughs, febrile states [fever], and disorders of the skin. Onions have additionally been attested archaeologically through their recovery from the funerary contexts of pharaonic tombs, indicating both their medicinal and symbolic or ritual significance in ancient Egyptian culture.

entities_mentioned.name: Onion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium cepa (common onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The onion is one of the most frequently cited plant remedies in the Ebers Papyrus. Its ancient Egyptian name is attested as 'hdj' or variants thereof in hieratic script. Modern pharmacological research supports antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant properties consistent with its documented uses for coughs, fevers, and skin conditions. Archaeological specimens have been recovered from multiple royal and non-royal tomb contexts, including those associated with Ramesses IV and Tutankhamun, suggesting the plant held both practical medicinal and possible apotropaic or ritual roles. The 14 prescriptions referenced span topical applications, oral preparations, and likely fumigation methods, though specific column references are not provided in this source segment.

segments.translator_notes: This source segment is a modern scholarly summary/description of Ebers Papyrus content rather than a direct transcription or transliteration of the original hieratic Egyptian text. As such, no ancient Egyptian column references, hieratic plant names, or original-language preparation instructions are recoverable from this segment alone. The figure of '14 prescriptions' appears to derive from compiled scholarly analyses (e.g., Manniche, Ghaliounghui) and should be cross-referenced against specific Ebers column numbers (e.g., Eb. 261–283 range) for full archival accuracy. No genuinely illegible passages are present. Modern pharmacological validation of Allium cepa's antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties is well-documented in peer-reviewed literature and is consistent with the therapeutic applications described.

segments.title_inferred: Sycamore Fig (Ficus sycomorus) as Medicinal Vehicle and Therapeutic Agent in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Fig (Ficus sycomorus, sycamore fig) in Egyptian medicine: the primary sweet vehicle for medicines. Used in 59 prescriptions. Also specific therapeutic actions.
segments.translation: The sycamore fig (Ficus sycomorus), known in ancient Egyptian as 'nehet' (nḥt) for the tree and 'dab' (dꜣb) for the fruit, functions in the Ebers Papyrus chiefly as the primary sweet vehicle [pharmaceutical excipient] for compounding medicines. It appears in approximately 59 distinct prescriptions throughout the papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited botanical ingredients in the entire corpus. Beyond its role as a carrier or masking agent for bitter or unpalatable drugs, the sycamore fig is also attributed with specific independent therapeutic actions, including laxative properties, wound-healing applications, and use in treating gastrointestinal complaints. The fruit's natural sweetness and soft, pulpy texture made it ideal for combining with powdered minerals, dried herbs, and other medicinal substances to produce palatable oral preparations. It was employed in decoctions, pastes, and direct topical applications. The fig tree itself — its fruit, sap, and bark — held both practical and ritual significance in Egyptian culture, further reinforcing its prominent role in medical texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Sycamore fig / nehet (nḥt) / dab (dꜣb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ficus sycomorus (sycamore fig)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently attested plants in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in roughly 59 prescriptions. The Egyptian term 'nehet' (nḥt) typically refers to the sycamore fig tree, while 'dab' (dꜣb) refers to the fruit. Modern pharmacological investigation supports mild laxative and anti-inflammatory properties consistent with ancient therapeutic claims. The fruit contains sugars, mucilage, and proteolytic enzymes (ficin) that may contribute to wound-cleansing and digestive actions documented in the papyrus.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct transcription of a specific papyrus column; accordingly, no column reference (e.g., Eb. column number) is provided in the source. Where column-level data becomes available, entries should be cross-referenced to specific prescription lines. The figure of '59 prescriptions' is drawn from standard Egyptological literature (cf. Manniche, Germer) and should be treated as an approximate consensus figure subject to variation across scholarly editions. The dual Egyptian naming convention (tree vs. fruit) is worth preserving in full corpus entries for indexing accuracy.

segments.title_inferred: Acacia (Acacia nilotica, shont) in Egyptian Medicine: Uses, Preparations, and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Acacia (Acacia nilotica, shont) in Egyptian medicine: pods, bark, gum, seeds used across 47 prescriptions. Antibacterial, astringent. Key ingredient in contraceptive pessaries.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of Acacia (known in ancient Egyptian as 'shont'), identified as Acacia nilotica, across 47 distinct medical prescriptions. Multiple parts of the plant were employed therapeutically, including the pods, bark, gum, and seeds. The plant was recognised for its antibacterial and astringent properties. Notably, it served as a principal active ingredient in contraceptive pessaries [vaginal suppositories used to prevent conception].

entities_mentioned.name: shont (šnt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia nilotica (also known as Egyptian thorn, gum arabic tree, or sant tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient Egyptian name 'shont' (variously transliterated as šnt or sont) is consistently identified by Egyptologists and botanists with Acacia nilotica, a species native to the Nile Valley. All major plant parts — pods, bark, gum, and seeds — were utilised. Modern pharmacological research confirms significant antibacterial and astringent activity attributable to high tannin content (particularly in pods and bark) and to compounds such as gallic acid. Its use in contraceptive pessaries is one of the most discussed passages in the Ebers Papyrus; the pessary recipe (e.g., Eb. 783) typically combines acacia tips/gum with honey and other substances, formed into a vaginal suppository. Modern analysis suggests acacia gum ferments to produce lactic acid, a recognised spermicidal agent, lending partial pharmacological plausibility to this ancient contraceptive use. Acacia gum was also widely employed as an excipient (binder/carrier) in other pharmaceutical preparations throughout the papyrus.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a modern scholarly summary description rather than a direct transcription of hieratic Egyptian text; accordingly, the 'translation' rendered here is an expansion and clarification of that summary into formal archival English suitable for the Plantacopia corpus. The figure of 47 prescriptions is a widely cited scholarly count across the full Ebers Papyrus corpus and should be verified against the most current critical edition (Westendorf 1999; Bardinet 1995) as numbering conventions vary. The term 'pessary' is preserved as period/technical vocabulary consistent with the medical history literature. No original Egyptian column references were included in the source segment; where specific recipes are referenced in future entries, column (Eb.) numbers should be cited directly.

segments.title_inferred: Castor Oil Plant (Ricinus communis / degem) in Egyptian Medicine: 30 Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Castor oil plant (Ricinus communis, degem) in Egyptian medicine: 30 prescriptions. Laxative, skin conditions, head complaints, hair growth.
segments.translation: The castor oil plant, known in ancient Egyptian as 'degem' (Ricinus communis), is documented across 30 separate prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus. Its recorded therapeutic applications include use as a laxative [purgative agent], treatment of various skin conditions, remedies for complaints of the head, and promotion of hair growth.

entities_mentioned.name: degem (ḏgm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ricinus communis (castor oil plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian name 'degem' (variously transliterated as ḏgm or dgm) is widely accepted by Egyptologists and medical historians as referring to Ricinus communis. The plant's seeds yield castor oil, a well-documented stimulant laxative containing ricinoleic acid. Its use in 30 prescriptions makes it one of the most frequently cited botanicals in the Ebers Papyrus. Modern pharmacology validates its laxative properties; topical applications for skin and scalp are consistent with castor oil's known emollient and antimicrobial properties. Hair growth promotion is a traditional use still encountered in ethnobotanical contexts, though clinical evidence remains mixed. Caution: raw castor seeds contain ricin, a potent toxin; ancient preparations likely used expressed oil rather than raw seed material.

segments.translator_notes: This segment functions as a summary or header entry rather than a verbatim prescription passage, collating the aggregate presence of the castor oil plant across the papyrus. The figure of 30 prescriptions is consistent with scholarly analyses (e.g., Manniche 1989; Nunn 1996) that count degem among the most prevalent plant entries. No specific column references are provided in this source segment; individual prescriptions would carry distinct column (Eb.) numbers. The therapeutic categories listed — laxative, skin conditions, head complaints, hair growth — align with known uses documented in scholarly translations by Ebbel (1937) and Westendorf (1999). 'Head complaints' likely encompasses headache, scalp conditions, and possibly conditions of the eyes or ears as contextually adjacent in the original papyrus structure.

segments.title_inferred: Pomegranate (Punica granatum) as Anthelmintic and Digestive Remedy
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Pomegranate (Punica granatum, inhemen) in Egyptian medicine: bark used as anthelmintic (tapeworm). Fruit for digestive issues. One of the most scientifically validated Egyptian remedies.
segments.translation: The pomegranate, known in ancient Egyptian as 'inhemen' (inḥmn), is documented in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal plant of considerable importance. The bark of the pomegranate was employed as an anthelmintic agent, specifically prescribed for the expulsion of tapeworms [internal parasites]. The fruit was additionally administered for the treatment of digestive complaints. This remedy is regarded as one of the most robustly scientifically validated treatments recorded in the Egyptian medical corpus.

entities_mentioned.name: inhemen (inḥmn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum (Pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian name 'inhemen' is well-attested in Egyptological literature as corresponding to the pomegranate. The plant was widely cultivated in ancient Egypt and appears repeatedly across the Ebers Papyrus. Modern pharmacology confirms the anthelmintic properties of pomegranate bark rind, attributed to punicalagins, ellagic acid, and notably pelletierine — an alkaloid with demonstrated efficacy against tapeworms (cestodes). The use of pomegranate fruit for digestive disorders is also corroborated by its tannin content, which has astringent and antimicrobial properties relevant to gastrointestinal complaints.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct transcription of hieratic or hieroglyphic source material. No specific column reference (e.g., Eb. column number) is provided in this segment, which limits precise cross-referencing within the papyrus. Pomegranate bark as a tapeworm remedy is one of the most frequently cited examples of ancient Egyptian pharmacological accuracy; the active compound pelletierine remained in use in Western medicine into the early 20th century. The Egyptian term 'inhemen' is sometimes transliterated as 'inḥmn' in scholarly literature and should be cross-referenced with column citations in the full Ebers corpus for completeness.

segments.title_inferred: Frankincense (Boswellia sacra) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Applications Across 43 Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Frankincense (Boswellia sacra, sonter) in Egyptian medicine: 43 prescriptions. For respiratory disease, skin problems, wounds, and ritual purification. Anti-inflammatory (modern validation).
segments.translation: Frankincense, known in ancient Egyptian as 'sonter' (sntṛ), appears across 43 discrete prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), making it one of the most frequently cited therapeutic and ritual substances in the text. Its applications span four primary domains: (1) Respiratory disease — employed in fumigation preparations and inhalation therapies to treat conditions of the chest, throat, and airways; (2) Skin problems — applied topically, often in combination with fats or oils, to address dermatological complaints including lesions, pustules, and inflammatory skin conditions; (3) Wound care — used as a wound dressing agent, likely exploiting its known antimicrobial and resinous sealing properties; (4) Ritual purification — burned as incense in contexts where the boundary between medicinal and sacred practice was deliberately maintained, consistent with Egyptian therapeutic ideology. Modern pharmacological investigation has validated the anti-inflammatory activity of frankincense resin, attributing this action primarily to boswellic acids (notably acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid, AKBA), which inhibit 5-lipoxygenase, a key enzyme in the leukotriene inflammatory pathway. This modern validation lends pharmacological credibility to its pervasive use in ancient Egyptian wound and respiratory therapies.

entities_mentioned.name: sonter (sntṛ) — Frankincense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra (also Boswellia carterii; resin sourced from the Boswellia genus, likely B. sacra or B. papyrifera in an Egyptian context)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term 'sonter' (variously transliterated as sntṛ, snṯr, or sntr) is broadly accepted by Egyptologists and medical historians as referring to frankincense resin. The source region for ancient Egyptian frankincense was the Land of Punt (likely modern Somalia, Eritrea, or southern Arabia), with which Egypt maintained active trade. Boswellic acids — the primary bioactive constituents — have been confirmed in multiple clinical and laboratory studies to possess anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antimicrobial properties, directly corroborating its wound-care and respiratory applications in the papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Boswellic acids (AKBA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid and related pentacyclic triterpene acids
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern pharmacological marker compounds responsible for the anti-inflammatory activity attributed to Boswellia resin. Not named in the ancient text, but cited here as the mechanism underlying modern validation of ancient therapeutic claims.

segments.translator_notes: This segment functions as a scholarly summary entry rather than a direct transcription of a specific papyrus column or prescription, aggregating data across 43 attested prescriptions. Individual column references (e.g., Eb. 1–877 numbering system per Joachim/Westendorf editions) are not specified in the source text and should be cross-referenced with the Grundriss der Medizin der alten Ägypter (Westendorf, 1999) or the Wreszinski facsimile edition for precise column attribution. The dual framing of frankincense as both medicinal and ritual ('purification') is consistent with Egyptian medical epistemology, wherein therapeutic and sacral functions were not categorically distinguished. The 43-prescription figure is consistent with counts reported in secondary scholarship (e.g., John Nunn, 'Ancient Egyptian Medicine', 1996). No OCR errors detected; segment is scholarly descriptive prose rather than primary hieratic/demotic source.

segments.title_inferred: Myrrh (antyw) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Applications and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Myrrh (Commiphora myrrha, antyw) in Egyptian medicine: 31 prescriptions. Antiseptic, analgesic, for wounds and infections. One of the most exported Egyptian drugs.
segments.translation: Myrrh — known in ancient Egyptian as 'antyw' — appears in 31 distinct prescriptions throughout the Ebers Papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the text. It was employed primarily for its antiseptic and analgesic properties, applied to wounds, abscesses, and various infectious conditions of the skin and body. Myrrh was among the most widely traded and exported pharmaceutical commodities of ancient Egypt, prized across the ancient Near East and Mediterranean world for its therapeutic and ritual value.

entities_mentioned.name: antyw (𓌀𓈖𓏏𓇌𓅱)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha (Myrrh resin, Burseraceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term 'antyw' is consistently identified by Egyptologists and historians of medicine with myrrh, the aromatic resin derived from Commiphora myrrha and related species native to the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Modern pharmacology confirms antimicrobial (antiseptic) and analgesic properties in myrrh resin, validating its ancient use for wound treatment and infection management. Active compounds include terpenoids, sesquiterpenes, and furanoeudesma-1,3-diene. Myrrh was obtained by Egypt primarily through trade with Punt and Arabia, and its 31 appearances in the Ebers Papyrus make it one of the single most prescribed substances in the entire corpus.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a modern scholarly summary rather than a direct transcription of a specific column or prescription from the Ebers Papyrus itself. No single hieratic column reference is given; the figure of 31 prescriptions represents a compiled count across the full papyrus. The Egyptian name 'antyw' is well-attested and broadly accepted; however, some scholars note that 'antyw' may occasionally refer to other aromatic resins in different contexts, so 'high' confidence reflects the dominant scholarly consensus rather than absolute certainty. Pharmacological validation of antiseptic and analgesic properties is well-documented in modern literature (e.g., Al-Mobeeriek & Andijani, 2011; Dolara et al., 2000).

segments.title_inferred: Blue Lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) in Egyptian Medicine: Psychoactive and Medicinal Properties
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Blue lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) in Egyptian medicine: psychoactive and medicinal. Contains apomorphine and nuciferine. Used for relaxation, cardiac conditions, reproductive issues.
segments.translation: The blue lotus (Nymphaea caerulea), known in ancient Egyptian medical tradition, is documented in the Ebers Papyrus for its psychoactive and medicinal properties. The plant contains the alkaloids apomorphine and nuciferine. It was employed therapeutically for the induction of relaxation and sedation, the treatment of cardiac [heart] conditions, and the management of reproductive ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: Blue lotus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphaea caerulea (Blue Egyptian Water Lily)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in ancient Egyptian as 'seshen' (sšn). A sacred and widely used plant in Egyptian ritual and medicine. Modern pharmacological analysis confirms the presence of apomorphine, a dopamine agonist with potential cardiac and psychoactive effects, and nuciferine, an antispasmodic and sedative alkaloid. Its use for relaxation aligns with documented sedative-anxiolytic properties. Cardiac and reproductive applications remain subjects of ongoing pharmacological investigation. Frequently prepared as a decoction or steeped in wine for ingestion; also used in fumigation rites.

entities_mentioned.name: Apomorphine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apomorphine hydrochloride (synthetic derivative also used clinically for Parkinson's disease and erectile dysfunction)
entities_mentioned.notes: A naturally occurring alkaloid in Nymphaea caerulea. Acts as a dopamine agonist. Modern clinical use supports historical associations with both cardiac regulation and reproductive function.

entities_mentioned.name: Nuciferine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuciferine (aporphine alkaloid found in Nymphaea and Nelumbo species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrates antispasmodic, sedative, and antipsychotic properties in modern pharmacological studies. Supports the ancient Egyptian use of blue lotus for relaxation and calming therapeutic purposes.

segments.translator_notes: This segment is a modern scholarly description of the blue lotus as documented in the Ebers Papyrus rather than a direct transcription or translation of a specific papyrus column. No original hieratic or column reference is provided in the source text, which limits precise cross-referencing. The Ebers Papyrus contains multiple references to aquatic plants including the water lily family; the identification of 'seshen' with Nymphaea caerulea is well-supported in Egyptological literature (e.g., Manniche, 'An Ancient Egyptian Herbal'). The pharmacological claims (apomorphine, nuciferine content) are validated by modern phytochemical studies. Archivists should note this entry synthesizes ancient usage with modern identification rather than providing a verbatim translation of a discrete papyrus passage.

segments.title_inferred: Moringa Oil in Ancient Egyptian Medicine: Anti-Inflammatory, Wound Healing, and Cosmetic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Moringa (Moringa peregrina, balanites) in Egyptian medicine: oil used extensively. Anti-inflammatory, wound healing, cosmetic. Found in ancient Egyptian tombs.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the extensive use of Moringa (identified with Moringa peregrina and/or Balanites aegyptiaca) in ancient Egyptian medical practice. The oil extracted from this plant was employed broadly for its anti-inflammatory properties, its capacity to promote wound healing, and for cosmetic applications. Physical specimens and residues associated with this plant have been recovered from ancient Egyptian tomb contexts, corroborating its documented importance.

entities_mentioned.name: Moringa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moringa peregrina (desert moringa / Ben tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Moringa peregrina is native to the Red Sea hills and Sinai region and was well known in ancient Egypt. Its seed oil ('ben oil') was highly prized for cosmetic and medicinal use. Ancient Egyptian name tentatively identified as 'bak' (bȝk) in some Egyptological literature, though attribution is debated. Oil was cold-pressed from seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: Balanites
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Balanites aegyptiaca (desert date / heglig tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Balanites aegyptiaca is sometimes conflated with or listed alongside Moringa peregrina in scholarly discussions of Egyptian medicinal plants, as both yielded oils used medicinally and cosmetically. Its ancient Egyptian name is sometimes given as 'iSd' or 'ished.' The two plants are botanically distinct but were used in overlapping therapeutic contexts. Modern pharmacology has validated anti-inflammatory and wound-healing bioactivity in both species.

entities_mentioned.name: Moringa oil / Ben oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fixed oil expressed from seeds of Moringa peregrina
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern pharmacological studies confirm antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties of Moringa seed oil, consistent with ancient therapeutic claims. The oil's long shelf life and resistance to rancidity made it particularly valuable as a cosmetic base and wound dressing medium.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment appears to be a modern scholarly description or catalogue note summarising Papyrus Ebers references to Moringa/Balanites, rather than a direct transcription or transliteration of the Egyptian hieratic source text. No specific column (Eb.) reference numbers are provided in the source; these would typically be cited (e.g., Eb. 69, Eb. 337) for precision. The parenthetical 'Moringa peregrina, balanites' suggests the cataloguer has noted that Egyptological scholarship sometimes conflates or associates these two distinct taxa. Researchers should consult the primary hieratic columns for exact recipe and dosage details. The claim that moringa has been 'found in ancient Egyptian tombs' is well substantiated archaeobotanically.

segments.title_inferred: Cumin (Cuminum cyminum / tepnen) in the Ebers Papyrus: Medicinal Uses and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Cumin (Cuminum cyminum, tepnen) in Egyptian medicine: 19 prescriptions. Digestive, expectorant, externally for pain. Combined with goose fat as ointment base.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus records cumin — known in ancient Egyptian as 'tepnen' — across 19 distinct prescriptions. It was employed internally as a digestive remedy and expectorant, and applied externally for the relief of pain. A common preparation involved combining cumin with goose fat to form an ointment base [for topical application].

entities_mentioned.name: tepnen (ancient Egyptian) / Cumin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuminum cyminum (Apiaceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently attested spice-drugs in the Ebers Papyrus. The ancient Egyptian name 'tepnen' is well established in Egyptological scholarship. Cumin seeds contain cuminaldehyde and other volatile oils with documented carminative, antispasmodic, and mild analgesic properties, supporting its recorded use as a digestive aid and pain-relief agent. Modern pharmacological studies confirm carminative and anti-inflammatory activity consistent with its ancient therapeutic applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose fat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered fat from Anser anser domesticus or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Goose fat was a standard excipient and ointment base in ancient Egyptian pharmacy, valued for its emollient properties and ability to carry active botanical ingredients transdermally. It appears as a vehicle in numerous Ebers Papyrus formulations.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a modern scholarly description of the Ebers Papyrus content rather than a direct transcription of the hieratic original; it summarises aggregated data across 19 prescription entries. The Egyptian name 'tepnen' is retained from the source as a known and accepted transliteration. The count of 19 prescriptions reflects the tally compiled by Egyptological and pharmacobotanical analysts (e.g., Manniche, Nunn). No column-specific references are provided in this segment; if individual prescription columns are available in the broader corpus, cross-referencing is recommended for granular entry creation.

segments.title_inferred: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum) in Egyptian Medical Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Coriander (Coriandrum sativum, shaw) in Egyptian medicine: seeds in prescriptions for digestive disorders, infections, pain relief.
segments.translation: Coriander, known in ancient Egyptian as 'shaw' (šꜣw), appears in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal seed ingredient incorporated into compound prescriptions addressing digestive complaints, infectious conditions, and the alleviation of pain. The seeds were employed in formulations that likely included decoctions and topical preparations consistent with broader Egyptian pharmaceutical practice.

entities_mentioned.name: shaw (šꜣw) — Coriander
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coriandrum sativum L. (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian term 'shaw' (variously transliterated šꜣw or shaw) is widely accepted among Egyptologists and medical historians as referring to coriander (Coriandrum sativum). Coriander seeds have been recovered archaeologically from Egyptian tomb sites, including Tutankhamun's tomb, confirming its presence and use in ancient Egypt. The seeds contain linalool, flavonoids, and volatile oils with documented carminative, antimicrobial, and mild analgesic properties, lending modern pharmacological support to its recorded uses. In the Ebers Papyrus, coriander features in prescriptions targeting gastrointestinal disturbances (e.g., bloating, digestive cramps), infection-related complaints, and pain management. Preparation methods documented across the papyrus for similar ingredients include decoction in water or beer, incorporation into compound pastes, and topical application. Coriander's carminative action is well-validated pharmacologically, supporting its use for digestive disorders; its antimicrobial constituents (particularly linalool) offer a plausible basis for infection-related applications.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a scholarly descriptive gloss rather than a direct transliteration or translation of a specific hieratic passage; it summarizes the plant's role across multiple Ebers Papyrus prescriptions rather than citing a single column reference. No specific column (Eb.) number is provided in this segment. Future corpus entries should cross-reference specific Ebers column citations (e.g., Eb. 251, Eb. 261, Eb. 875) where coriander is individually attested. The Egyptian name 'shaw' is the conventionally accepted identification but some scholarly variation in transliteration exists. Modern pharmacological validation is noted as applicable for carminative and antimicrobial properties.

segments.title_inferred: Fenugreek (Hemayt) in the Ebers Papyrus: Therapeutic Applications and Modern Validation
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum, hemayt) in Egyptian medicine: 18 prescriptions. Digestive, anti-inflammatory, wound healing. Validated by modern research.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents fenugreek — known in ancient Egyptian as 'hemayt' (ḥmꜣyt) — across 18 distinct prescriptions, making it one of the more frequently cited botanicals in the corpus. Its recorded therapeutic applications encompass digestive complaints, inflammatory conditions, and wound healing. These uses have received corroboration from modern pharmacological research.

entities_mentioned.name: hemayt (ḥmꜣyt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trigonella foenum-graecum (Fenugreek)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently attested medicinal plants in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in at least 18 prescriptions. The ancient Egyptian name 'hemayt' is well-established in Egyptological and archaeobotanical literature. Fenugreek seeds and leaves contain steroidal saponins, flavonoids, and mucilaginous fibers, which modern pharmacology associates with anti-inflammatory, hypoglycemic, and gastroprotective properties — broadly consistent with the digestive and wound-healing applications documented in the papyrus. Fenugreek seeds have been recovered archaeologically from ancient Egyptian sites, confirming its cultivation and use in the period.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a modern scholarly summary rather than a direct transcription of hieratic or hieroglyphic source material. It condenses multiple Ebers Papyrus columns into a single descriptive entry. Individual prescription details (specific column references, exact preparation methods such as decoction, topical application, or fumigation, and specific conditions treated per prescription) would require enumeration from the primary columns (e.g., Ebers 251–283 and related sections). The figure of 18 prescriptions is consistent with standard Egyptological counts but may vary slightly depending on editorial methodology. Modern validation references are implied but not cited; key studies include work by Bahmani et al. (2016) and Nagulapalli Venkata et al. (2017) on fenugreek pharmacology.

segments.title_inferred: Thyme (Thymus species) as Antimicrobial, Respiratory, and Wound-Care Agent in Egyptian Medicine
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Thyme (Thymus species) in Egyptian medicine: as antimicrobial agent, for chest complaints, wound dressing. Used in mummification process.
segments.translation: Thyme (Thymus species) is documented in Egyptian medical practice as an antimicrobial agent, employed in the treatment of chest complaints [respiratory ailments], used as a wound dressing [topical application to injuries or lesions], and incorporated into the mummification process [funerary/preservative rites].

entities_mentioned.name: Thyme
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus species (most likely Thymus vulgaris or Thymus capitatus, the latter native to the Mediterranean and Near East)
entities_mentioned.notes: Thyme contains thymol and carvacrol, volatile phenolic compounds with well-documented modern antimicrobial and antifungal properties, lending pharmacological plausibility to its use as an antimicrobial agent and wound dressing. Its inclusion in mummification is consistent with archaeological evidence of aromatic plant resins and herbs found in Egyptian burial contexts. The ancient Egyptian name for this specific plant is not preserved in the source segment; some scholars associate it with the Egyptian term 'tham' or related aromatics, but identification remains debated. Its use for chest complaints (respiratory conditions) is consistent with the known bronchodilatory and expectorant properties of thymol. Modern pharmacology strongly validates antimicrobial and wound-healing applications. The mummification use likely exploited both its preservative (antimicrobial) and aromatic properties.

segments.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct transcription or translation of a hieratic or hieroglyphic passage from the Papyrus Ebers; no specific column reference (e.g., Eb. column number) or original Egyptian-language term is provided in the source. The ancient Egyptian name for thyme is not definitively established in the Egyptological literature — some candidates have been proposed but consensus identification remains uncertain. Pharmacological validation of thymol's antimicrobial activity is robust and high-confidence. The mummification reference is consistent with archaeobotanical findings but is not always directly tied to a specific Ebers prescription. Researchers should cross-reference with specific column citations (e.g., Eb. 94–95 for chest remedies) when available.

segments.title_inferred: Mandrake (rerery) in Egyptian Medicine: Soporific, Analgesic, and Anesthetic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Mandrake (Mandragora officinarum, rerery) in Egyptian medicine: soporific, pain relief, anesthesia during procedures. Associated with fertility.
segments.translation: The plant known in ancient Egyptian as 'rerery' (mandrake; Mandragora officinarum) was employed in Egyptian medical practice for several therapeutic purposes: as a soporific agent to induce sleep, as an analgesic for the relief of pain, and as an anesthetic to render patients insensible during medical or surgical procedures. The plant was further associated with fertility and reproductive concerns in the Egyptian medical and ritual tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: rerery (rrry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mandragora officinarum (mandrake)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian name 'rerery' is the most widely accepted scholarly identification for mandrake in the Ebers Papyrus. Mandragora officinarum contains tropane alkaloids — principally scopolamine, hyoscyamine, and atropine — which pharmacologically validate its ancient uses as a soporific, analgesic, and anesthetic agent. The plant's association with fertility is attested both in Egyptian medical texts and in broader ancient Near Eastern and Mediterranean traditions (cf. Genesis 30:14–16). Its berries were sometimes called 'love apples.' Modern pharmacology confirms robust CNS-depressant, anticholinergic, and analgesic activity consistent with the recorded uses.

segments.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct transcription of a specific Ebers Papyrus column or prescription passage; no column (Eb.) reference number is provided in the source text. Translators and archivists should note that direct papyrus column citations (e.g., Eb. 207, Eb. 708) would strengthen corpus traceability. The identification of 'rerery' with Mandragora officinarum, while widely accepted, is based on philological and archaeobotanical inference; a small minority of scholars have proposed alternative identifications. The fertility association may reflect both pharmacological properties (mild aphrodisiac effects at low doses) and symbolic/mythological layering in Egyptian culture.

segments.title_inferred: Aloe vera in Egyptian Medicine: Wound Healing, Burns, and Skin Conditions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Aloe vera (Aloe succotrina) in Egyptian medicine: wound healing, burns, skin conditions. Cleopatra reportedly used aloe for skin care.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of aloe [ancient Egyptian name uncertain; possibly referenced as a bitter, gel-bearing plant] in the treatment of wounds, burns, and various skin conditions. The plant identified with modern Aloe vera or the closely related Aloe succotrina was applied topically, its gel or expressed juice used as a soothing and wound-closing agent [Egyptian: likely a direct topical application or poultice]. Classical and later tradition holds that Cleopatra VII incorporated aloe preparations into her personal skin care regimen, suggesting its cosmetic as well as medicinal esteem in the ancient Egyptian world.

entities_mentioned.name: Aloe vera / Aloe succotrina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera (L.) Burm.f. or Aloe succotrina Lam. (Cape aloe / Socotrine aloe)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Ebers Papyrus references a bitter, gel-bearing plant consistent with aloe species. Modern scholarship generally identifies this with Aloe vera or Aloe succotrina, the latter historically traded from the island of Socotra. The ancient Egyptian term for this plant has not been definitively established. Modern pharmacological research confirms anti-inflammatory, wound-healing (acemannan, glycoproteins), and antimicrobial properties of Aloe vera gel, lending strong validation to its documented therapeutic uses. The Cleopatra tradition is classical/anecdotal and not directly attested in the papyrus text itself.

segments.translator_notes: This segment as supplied appears to be a modern scholarly summary or catalogue description rather than a direct transcription or hieratic transliteration of a specific Ebers Papyrus column/recipe passage. No specific column number (e.g., Eb. col. 69 etc.), Egyptian-language plant name, or formulation instructions are given in the source text. The Cleopatra reference is a widely repeated tradition but is not sourced from the Ebers Papyrus itself (Cleopatra VII lived c. 69–30 BCE; the papyrus dates to c. 1550 BCE). A direct papyrus column reference and the original hieratic or transliterated Egyptian plant name should be supplied if available for full Plantacopia corpus compliance. Pharmacological validation of wound-healing and anti-inflammatory properties of Aloe vera is well-supported in modern literature.

segments.title_inferred: Carob (djaret) in Egyptian Medicine: Digestive and Anti-Diarrheal Applications
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Carob (Ceratonia siliqua, djaret) in Egyptian medicine: pods in digestive remedies, as sweetener base, anti-diarrheal. 14 prescriptions.
segments.translation: The carob — known in ancient Egyptian as 'djaret' (Ceratonia siliqua) — appears across 14 distinct prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus. The pods of this plant were employed in digestive remedies, serving both as a functional therapeutic agent and as a sweetening base to render medicinal preparations more palatable. Its primary documented therapeutic application is as an anti-diarrheal agent [Egyptian medical text employs carob as a binding, astringent agent in gastrointestinal complaints]. The pods were likely prepared as decoctions or incorporated as a paste or powder into compound remedies, consistent with Egyptian pharmaceutical practice of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: djaret
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ceratonia siliqua (Carob tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian term 'djaret' is broadly accepted in Egyptological scholarship as corresponding to the carob tree (Ceratonia siliqua). The carob pod's high tannin and pectin content provides a pharmacological basis for its anti-diarrheal use, and its natural sugars explain its role as a sweetener base in compound prescriptions. Modern medicine validates carob pod powder as an effective anti-diarrheal agent, particularly in pediatric contexts, lending strong pharmacological plausibility to the ancient applications described. Fourteen prescriptions featuring this ingredient indicates it was among the more commonly employed botanical agents in the Ebers Papyrus corpus.

segments.translator_notes: The source text is a modern scholarly description of the ancient Egyptian material rather than a direct transcription of the hieratic papyrus itself. No column reference (e.g., Eb. column number) is provided in the source segment; future corpus entries should cross-reference specific Ebers column citations where available to allow direct verification against the primary hieratic source. The Egyptian name 'djaret' is here treated as the standard scholarly transliteration; variant transliterations (e.g., 'd3rt') may appear in other Egyptological literature. The figure of 14 prescriptions appears to reflect a compiled scholarly count and should be validated against a full concordance of the Ebers Papyrus.

segments.title_inferred: Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Uses and Preparations
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) in Egyptian medicine: cedar oil imported from Lebanon. Used in 14 prescriptions for skin diseases, wounds, ear complaints, as insect repellent.
segments.translation: Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) as documented in the Ebers Papyrus: Cedar oil, imported from Lebanon, appears in at least 14 distinct prescriptions within the papyrus. Its recorded therapeutic applications include treatment of skin diseases, wound care, remedies for ear complaints, and use as an insect repellent.

entities_mentioned.name: Cedar / Cedar Oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cedrus libani (Cedar of Lebanon); cedar oil derived from wood, bark, or resin of Cedrus libani
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient Egyptian name likely 'ash' (Egyptian: 'ꜥš'), referring to cedar wood and its derived oil. Cedar of Lebanon was a prized luxury import in ancient Egypt, traded via Phoenician and Levantine trade routes. Modern pharmacological research has validated cedar oil's antibacterial, antifungal, and insecticidal properties (cedrol and cedrene as active sesquiterpene compounds), lending support to its recorded uses for skin diseases, wound treatment, and insect repellency. Its use for ear complaints may relate to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The 14 prescriptions reflect the high value placed on this imported commodity in Egyptian materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Lebanon (provenance)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Modern Lebanon / Levantine coast (ancient Phoenicia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Lebanon is explicitly identified as the source of cedar oil imports, consistent with extensive archaeological and textual evidence of cedar trade between Egypt and the Levant throughout the Bronze Age.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment appears to be a modern scholarly summary/description of the Ebers Papyrus's cedar entries rather than a direct transcription of the original Egyptian text or a column-specific citation. No specific column (Eb.) reference numbers are provided in the source; a complete Plantacopia entry would ideally cross-reference the individual column citations (e.g., Eb. 63, Eb. 66, etc.) where cedar prescriptions appear. The figure of 14 prescriptions is drawn from secondary Egyptological literature (e.g., Manniche, Nunn). The Egyptian term 'ꜥš' is the standard scholarly reconstruction for cedar in medical papyri contexts. Modern pharmacological validation of cedar oil's antibacterial and insecticidal properties is well-established in peer-reviewed literature.

segments.title_inferred: Calamus (Sweet Flag) as Aromatic Stimulant and Carminative in Egyptian Medicine
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Calamus/sweet flag (Acorus calamus) in Egyptian medicine: aromatic stimulant in compound medicines, especially kyphi. Carminative, anti-spasmodic.
segments.translation: Calamus (sweet flag; Acorus calamus) appears in Egyptian medicine as an aromatic stimulant incorporated into compound medicinal preparations, most notably kyphi — the sacred incense-medicine used in temple ritual and therapeutic contexts. It was employed for its carminative properties (relieving gas and digestive discomfort) and its anti-spasmodic action (reducing involuntary muscular contractions and cramps).

entities_mentioned.name: Calamus / Sweet Flag
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus (family Acoraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in ancient Egyptian medical texts and referenced in the context of kyphi formulations. Acorus calamus contains active compounds including beta-asarone and acorone, which have demonstrated carminative, antispasmodic, and sedative properties in modern pharmacological studies. Historically traded from South and Southeast Asia or the Levant into Egypt. The ancient Egyptian name is tentatively associated with 'sw' or similar terms in some scholarly reconstructions, though direct hieratic identification remains debated. Kyphi (ancient Egyptian: kp.t or qp.t) was a multi-ingredient compound used as incense and as an oral or fumigation medicine, with recipes preserved in the Ebers Papyrus and in temple inscriptions at Edfu and Philae.

entities_mentioned.name: Kyphi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound Egyptian incense-medicine (multi-ingredient formulation)
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested Egyptian compound preparation combining numerous plant ingredients including calamus, wine, honey, raisins, resin, and other aromatics. Used as fumigation, ingested medicine, and ritual incense. Its recipe variants are recorded in the Ebers Papyrus and in Ptolemaic temple inscriptions. Modern analyses of surviving residues have confirmed the presence of several identified botanicals.

segments.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly descriptive gloss rather than a direct transcription of a hieratic passage; it summarises the role of Acorus calamus across relevant Ebers Papyrus sections rather than citing a single column reference. No specific column (Eb.) number is provided in the source. For full corpus integration, cross-referencing with Eb. 1–3 (kyphi recipes) and related compound medicine columns is recommended. The carminative and antispasmodic actions noted align with modern pharmacological validation of Acorus calamus essential oil constituents.

segments.title_inferred: Poppy (Papaver species) in Egyptian Medicine: Pain Relief and Children's Colic Remedies
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Poppy (Papaver species, sepen) in Egyptian medicine: opium-containing prescriptions for pain relief — especially notable in children's colic remedies (poppy seeds + fly excrement).
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of the poppy plant, known in ancient Egyptian as 'sepen,' in a range of medicinal preparations containing opium-active constituents, prescribed principally for the relief of pain. Of particular note is a remedy formulated for infant colic, in which poppy seeds are combined with fly excrement [likely dried insect frass used as a binding or activating agent in compound prescriptions] and administered to young children to quiet excessive crying and alleviate abdominal discomfort. This represents one of the earliest recorded uses of an opiate-containing substance in pediatric medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: sepen (poppy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum (opium poppy) or Papaver rhoeas (field poppy)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian term 'sepen' is widely accepted by Egyptologists and pharmacobotanists as corresponding to the poppy genus. Papaver somniferum is the most pharmacologically significant species, containing morphine, codeine, and related alkaloids. Its use in a children's colic remedy is attested in Ebers column 782 (Eb. 782), where a preparation of poppy seeds mixed with fly excrement is prescribed to prevent excessive crying in infants — a strikingly early instance of sedative use in pediatric care. Modern pharmacology fully validates the analgesic and antispasmodic properties of opium alkaloids, though the dosing and safety profile in the ancient context remains uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: fly excrement
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried frass or excreta of Musca domestica or related dipteran species
entities_mentioned.notes: The precise identification and intended pharmacological role of fly excrement in this compound is unclear. It may have served as a vehicle, binder, or been included on the basis of sympathetic or magical principles. No modern pharmacological validation for therapeutic efficacy of this ingredient exists.

segments.translator_notes: The children's colic remedy combining poppy seeds and fly excrement is one of the most frequently cited prescriptions from the Ebers Papyrus in discussions of ancient pharmacology and pediatric medicine. It underscores that opiate use in medicine predates Greek and Roman texts by centuries. The segment as supplied is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct hieratic transcription; accordingly, the 'original_text' field preserves the scholarly source description verbatim, and the 'translation' field contextualizes and expands it as a corpus entry. Column-level citation (e.g., Eb. 782) should be cross-referenced against Wreszinski's 1913 facsimile and Grundriss der Medizin der alten Ägypter (Deines, Grapow, Westendorf) for full textual authority.

segments.title_inferred: Dill (Anethum graveolens) in Egyptian Medicine: Digestive, Carminative, and Antiparasitic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Dill (Anethum graveolens) in Egyptian medicine: digestive, carminative, for stomach complaints and intestinal worms.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the medicinal use of dill (Anethum graveolens) in ancient Egyptian medical practice. Dill was employed as a digestive aid and carminative agent, prescribed for stomach complaints and to expel intestinal worms [parasitic infestation]. The plant appears in multiple formularies within the papyrus as a component of compound remedies addressing gastrointestinal disturbances, including bloating, flatulence, and helminthic [worm] infestations. Preparations likely included decoctions of the seeds or aerial parts, administered orally. Dill's aromatic volatile oils — principally carvone and limonene — are recognized in modern pharmacology as having antispasmodic, carminative, and mild antimicrobial properties, lending scientific credibility to its ancient therapeutic applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Dill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anethum graveolens (Apiaceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in ancient Egyptian texts; the Egyptian name 'imset' or variants have been proposed by scholars. The plant's seeds and aerial parts were used. Modern pharmacological studies confirm carminative and antispasmodic activity via volatile oils (carvone, limonene, anethole). Anthelmintic [worm-expelling] properties have some experimental support but are considered moderate. Widely attested across ancient Near Eastern and Mediterranean medical traditions contemporaneous with the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE).

entities_mentioned.name: Intestinal worms
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Helminthic infestation (general); possibly Ascaris lumbricoides or tapeworm species prevalent in ancient Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: Intestinal parasites were a documented health burden in ancient Egypt, evidenced by paleopathological findings in mummies. The Ebers Papyrus contains numerous anthelmintic prescriptions, reflecting the prevalence of this condition.

entities_mentioned.name: Stomach complaints
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrointestinal disturbances including dyspepsia, flatulence, and colic
entities_mentioned.notes: Broad category of digestive ailments addressed throughout the Ebers Papyrus. Dill's carminative action is pharmacologically validated as relevant to these conditions.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a scholarly descriptive summary rather than a direct transliteration or translation of the Egyptian hieratic source text. No specific column reference (Eb. column number) is provided in the segment; researchers consulting the original papyrus should cross-reference Ebers columns relating to gastrointestinal remedies (notably columns 38–60 and worm treatments around columns 6–7 and 63–65) to locate dill-specific prescriptions. The ancient Egyptian name for dill remains debated among Egyptologists; proposed identifications include 'imset' and 'iaret,' but certainty is limited. Modern pharmacological validation noted in translation is consistent with peer-reviewed ethnobotanical literature.

segments.title_inferred: Lettuce (abu) in Egyptian Medicine: Aphrodisiac, Fertility, and Cooling Applications
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Lettuce (Lactuca sativa, abu) in Egyptian medicine: sacred to Min (fertility god). Used in aphrodisiac and fertility preparations. Also cooling properties.
segments.translation: Lettuce, known in ancient Egyptian as 'abu' (𓇑 / transliterated: ꜣbw), was a plant of significant ritual and medicinal importance in Egyptian medicine. It was sacred to Min, the god of fertility and virility, and featured prominently in aphrodisiac and fertility-enhancing preparations. The plant was also recognised for its cooling properties, likely employed in treatments for febrile conditions, inflammation, or heat-related ailments. As documented in the Ebers Papyrus, lettuce was administered in preparations intended to stimulate sexual potency and promote reproductive capacity, consistent with its ritual association with the cult of Min. Its cooling humoral character further positioned it as a therapeutic agent in contexts requiring reduction of bodily heat.

entities_mentioned.name: abu (ꜣbw) — Lettuce
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lactuca sativa (cultivated lettuce) or possibly Lactuca serriola (wild/prickly lettuce); ancient Egyptian representations most closely resemble a tall, cos-type variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Lettuce was among the most culturally loaded plants in ancient Egypt. Its milky white latex, when the stalk is cut, was likely interpreted as symbolic of semen, reinforcing its association with Min and fertility. Modern pharmacological analysis of Lactuca species confirms the presence of lactucarium (lactucopicrin, lactucin), compounds with mild sedative, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory properties — consistent with the attested cooling and possibly anaphrodisiac-to-aphrodisiac dual uses noted in scholarly literature. Some scholars (e.g., Manniche, 1989) note that lettuce's ancient reputation as an aphrodisiac may be counterintuitive given its modern classification as mildly sedative, but the symbolic and ritual dimensions likely dominated medicinal prescription in this context.

entities_mentioned.name: Min
entities_mentioned.notes: Min was the ancient Egyptian god of fertility, sexual potency, rain, and harvest. His cult centre was at Koptos (Qift) and Akhmim. Lettuce was the primary sacred plant of Min's cult, frequently depicted growing in his garden shrines. The association is well-attested in iconographic and textual sources across the pharaonic period.

segments.translator_notes: This segment appears to be a scholarly descriptive gloss rather than a direct verbatim transcription of a specific Ebers Papyrus column or prescription. No specific column reference (e.g., Eb. 1–110) or preparation formula is provided in the source text, which limits precise cross-referencing. Lettuce does appear across multiple Ebers prescriptions (e.g., columns relating to digestive, dermatological, and gynaecological treatments), as well as in the Kahun Gynaecological Papyrus. Researchers should consult Manniche (1989) 'An Ancient Egyptian Herbal' and Nunn (1996) 'Ancient Egyptian Medicine' for full prescription contexts. The dual characterisation as both aphrodisiac and cooling agent is not contradictory in Egyptian humoral logic, as fertility enhancement and heat reduction could be co-therapeutic goals in reproductive medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Willow (Salix spp.) in Egyptian Medicine: Bark Decoctions for Pain and Fever
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Willow (Salix species, tjereret) in Egyptian medicine: bark decoctions for pain and fever — the ancient salicylic acid. Anti-inflammatory validated by modern pharmacology.
segments.translation: The willow tree, known in ancient Egyptian as 'tjereret' (Salix species), is documented in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal plant of significant therapeutic value. Decoctions prepared from its bark were prescribed for the relief of pain and the reduction of fever. This application represents one of the earliest recorded uses of what modern pharmacology identifies as salicylic acid — the active principle from which aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is derived. The anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties of willow bark have been extensively validated by contemporary pharmacological research, confirming the empirical efficacy observed and recorded by ancient Egyptian physicians.

entities_mentioned.name: tjereret (tjereret / ṯrrt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salix species (Willow); most likely Salix safsaf (Nile Willow) or related riparian Salix spp. native to the Nile Valley
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient Egyptian name 'tjereret' is consistently associated with willow in Egyptological and archaeobotanical scholarship. Willow bark contains salicin, which the human body metabolises into salicylic acid, producing analgesic, antipyretic, and anti-inflammatory effects. Modern clinical studies corroborate these traditional therapeutic claims. The tree was abundant along the Nile and thus readily accessible to Egyptian physicians. Bark decoctions — prepared by boiling the bark in water — represent the primary documented preparation method.

entities_mentioned.name: Salicylic acid (active compound, not named as such in source)
