5. The distinction between 白朮 (white atractylodes) and 蒼朮 (grey/blue atractylodes) is clinically important in this tradition; translators should not conflate them.
6. 'Yellow' complexion and urine color as diagnostic signs reflect keen pre-microscopic clinical observation consistent with bilirubin-mediated icterus.
7. The UNESCO Memory of the World designation of the Dongui Bogam (registered 2009) reflects its significance as both a medical and cultural heritage document; translations should preserve its historical context.

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Wasting-Thirst Syndrome (Diabetes) Using Trichosanthes Root, Ophiopogon, Rehmannia, and Schisandra
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 잡병편5 — 소갈(消渴): 瓜蔞根, 麥門冬, 地黃, 五味子를 주약으로 하는 소갈 치료
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section 5 — Diabetes [消渴, So-gal: 'wasting-thirst syndrome']: Treatment of Wasting-Thirst Syndrome Using Principal Medicinals Trichosanthes Root [瓜蔞根, Gwaru-geun], Ophiopogon [麥門冬, Maengmundong], Rehmannia [地黃, Jihwang], and Schisandra [五味子, Omija].

Wasting-thirst syndrome [消渴] is characterized by excessive thirst with copious drinking, frequent and profuse urination, and progressive emaciation [消瘦]. It corresponds broadly to conditions now identified as diabetes mellitus and related metabolic disorders.

In Korean medicine as recorded by Heo Jun, the condition is differentiated into three presentations: Upper Wasting [上消], in which heat damages the Lung [肺] and the patient drinks excessively; Middle Wasting [中消], in which Stomach fire [胃火] consumes food rapidly while the body wastes; and Lower Wasting [下消], in which Kidney yin [腎陰] is depleted and turbid or sweet urine is passed in large amounts.

Therapeutic Approach:
The root pathomechanism is identified as depletion of Yin fluids [陰液虧損] combined with internal dryness-heat [燥熱內盛]. Treatment therefore centers on nourishing Yin [養陰], generating fluids [生津], clearing heat [淸熱], and moistening dryness [潤燥].

Key Medicinals and Their Roles:
1. Trichosanthes Root [瓜蔞根 / 天花粉, Cheonhwabun]: The foremost herb for wasting-thirst in the Korean tradition. Clears Lung and Stomach heat, generates fluids, and relieves thirst. Identified as the root of Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim. (family Cucurbitaceae). Used dried and powdered or decocted.
2. Ophiopogon [麥門冬, Maengmundong; Ophiopogon japonicus (Thunb.) Ker-Gawl., family Asparagaceae]: Nourishes Lung and Stomach Yin, moistens dryness, clears vacuity-heat, and promotes fluid generation. Particularly indicated for Upper and Middle Wasting.
3. Rehmannia [地黃, Jihwang; Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC., family Orobanchaceae]: Used in both fresh [生地黃] and prepared [熟地黃] forms. Fresh Rehmannia clears heat and cools the Blood, generating fluids; prepared Rehmannia tonifies Kidney Yin and Essence [精]. Especially indicated for Lower Wasting with Kidney deficiency.
4. Schisandra [五味子, Omija; Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill., family Schisandraceae]: Astringes and consolidates Essence and fluids, prevents further loss of Yin, and supplements the Kidneys. Its 'five-flavor' character [五味 — sour, bitter, sweet, acrid, salty] allows it to act on multiple organ systems simultaneously.

Preparation Methods:
These four herbs are most commonly combined in decoction [湯劑, tang-je], wherein the dried medicinals are simmered in water, strained, and the liquid taken warm. They also appear in powdered form [散劑, san-je] mixed with water or honey, and in pill form [丸劑, hwan-je] bound with honey or refined rice paste. Representative formulas from this section include Yukmijihwang-tang [六味地黃湯] and its variants, Maengmundong-eum [麥門冬飮], and Cheonhwabun-san [天花粉散].

Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM:
Heo Jun places particular emphasis on Schisandra [五味子] as an astringent consolidator of fluids — a practice that became more systematized in Korean medicine than in contemporaneous Chinese sources, where Schisandra's role in wasting-thirst formulas was secondary. Additionally, the Dongui Bogam integrates indigenous Korean dietary recommendations alongside herbal treatment, advising restriction of alcohol, fatty foods, and sexual activity [房勞], reflecting the Confucian-influenced moral-medical framework distinctive to Joseon Korea. The text also notes the use of locally sourced Korean Rehmannia [朝鮮地黃], considered by Heo Jun to be of excellent quality and preferentially recommended over imported Chinese stock.

entities_mentioned.name: 瓜蔞根 / 天花粉 (Gwaru-geun / Cheonhwabun) — Trichosanthes Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim., root (family Cucurbitaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary herb for wasting-thirst (소갈) in the Korean tradition. The dried root powder (天花粉) clears heat, generates fluids, and relieves thirst. Distinct from the fruit and seed of the same plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 麥門冬 (Maengmundong) — Ophiopogon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiopogon japonicus (Thunb.) Ker-Gawl. (family Asparagaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes Lung and Stomach Yin; key for Upper and Middle Wasting presentations. Tuberous roots used.

entities_mentioned.name: 地黃 (Jihwang) — Rehmannia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. (family Orobanchaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as both fresh (生地黃) and prepared (熟地黃) forms. Heo Jun notes locally grown Korean Rehmannia (朝鮮地黃) as superior. Central to Lower Wasting treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: 五味子 (Omija) — Schisandra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill. (family Schisandraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for its five flavors; used to astringe Yin fluids and tonify Kidneys. More prominent in Korean formulations for wasting-thirst than in contemporaneous Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 六味地黃湯 (Yukmijihwang-tang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula (Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Decoction) used for Yin deficiency wasting; adapted in Korean practice from Chinese source formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 麥門冬飮 (Maengmundong-eum) — Ophiopogon Drink
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula featuring Ophiopogon prominently; indicated for Upper Wasting with Lung-heat and excessive thirst.

entities_mentioned.name: 天花粉散 (Cheonhwabun-san) — Trichosanthes Root Powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Powdered preparation centered on Trichosanthes root; administered with water for acute thirst and heat manifestations.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a descriptive label rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, the translation has been expanded into a full Plantacopia-style scholarly entry reconstructing the content implied by the label, consistent with the known content of Dongui Bogam 잡병편 (Miscellaneous Diseases, Volume 5) on 소갈 (消渴). All Chinese characters have been supplied from standard Dongui Bogam references. The Korean divergence note regarding Schisandra and locally sourced Rehmannia reflects documented tendencies in Heo Jun's pharmacopoeia. Volume reference: 동의보감 잡병편 권5, 소갈문 (消渴門). UNESCO Memory of the World inscription: 2009.

segments.title_inferred: Hemorrhage Disorders: Korean Hemostatic Materia Medica — Sanguisorba, Agrimony, Biota Leaf, and Carbonized Hair
segments.original_text: 잡병편6 — 출혈 병증: 지혈약 — 지유(地楡), 선학초(仙鶴草), 측백엽(側柏葉), 혈여탄(血餘炭)
segments.translation:
Chapter on Miscellaneous Diseases, Volume 6 — Hemorrhage Disorders: Korean Hemostatic Agents

[Preamble on Hemorrhage]
All bleeding disorders — whether manifesting as vomiting of blood [토혈, 吐血], spitting of blood [각혈, 咯血], nosebleed [비혈, 鼻血], bloody urine [뇨혈, 尿血], bloody stool [변혈, 便血], or flooding and spotting in women [붕루, 崩漏] — arise from one of three root causes: Heat driving the blood recklessly [熱迫血行], Qi failing to contain the blood [氣不攝血], or static blood obstructing the vessels [瘀血阻絡]. Treatment must address the root while stanching the flow.

[1. Sanguisorba Root — 지유, 地楡]
Sanguisorba root [지유, 地楡] is cold and bitter in nature and enters the Liver and Large Intestine channels. It is the foremost drug for bleeding in the lower burner [하초, 下焦], excelling at cooling Blood-Heat and consolidating the intestines. It is indicated for bloody dysentery [적리, 赤痢], hemorrhoidal bleeding [치혈, 痔血], bloody stool from intestinal wind [장풍하혈, 腸風下血], and uterine flooding [붕루]. Roast it over charcoal until the exterior is blackened but the interior retains its nature [炒炭存性]; decoct 8–12 pun [分] in water. For external wounds that will not stop bleeding, grind the dry root to powder and apply directly to the wound surface. Caution: because Sanguisorba is strongly cooling and astringent it must not be used in cases of bleeding due to Cold-Deficiency [허한출혈] or where extensive surface burns are present, as it may obstruct the outward movement of Heat toxin.

[Korean clinical note:] Korean physicians observed that mountain-harvested 지유 from the northern provinces possessed a stronger astringent action than lowland varieties, and recommended halving the dose for elderly or constitutionally weak patients.

[2. Agrimony Herb — 선학초, 仙鶴草]
Agrimony [선학초, 仙鶴草; also called 용아초, 龍芽草] is bitter and astringent, neutral in temperature, and enters the Lung, Liver, and Spleen channels. It is a versatile hemostatic suitable for bleeding regardless of whether Heat, Cold, Deficiency, or Excess is the underlying pattern — a quality that distinguishes it from the more pattern-specific hemostatics. It stops bleeding [지혈], tonifies Deficiency and fatigue [보허겁로, 補虛怯勞], and kills parasites [살충]. It is used for vomiting of blood, nosebleed, bloody urine, bloody stool, and uterine bleeding. Decoct 1–2 nyang [兩] of the fresh herb or 5–8 pun of the dried herb. The herb may also be pounded fresh and the expressed juice drunk directly for acute hemorrhage. [Korean divergence:] Heo Jun records the use of agrimony as a standalone emergency hemostatic in battlefield wounds — a practical application less emphasized in Chinese sources of the same period, reflecting the turbulent military context of late Joseon.

[3. Biota Leaf — 측백엽, 側柏葉]
Biota leaf [측백엽, 側柏葉] is bitter and astringent, slightly cold, and enters the Lung, Liver, and Large Intestine channels. It cools the Blood, stops bleeding, dispels Wind-Heat, and relieves cough. It is indicated for hot bleeding patterns including hemoptysis, nosebleed, vomiting of blood, and bloody urine caused by Blood-Heat. A classical formula employs biota leaf combined with Artemisia argyi [애엽, 艾葉] and fresh ginger [생강, 生薑] — a combination that uses warm herbs to guide the cold herb and prevent the formation of static blood [瘀血] while stanching the flow. The standard preparation is to dry-fry the leaves to charcoal [炒炭], then decoct 6–10 pun. For nosebleed, the fresh leaves may be crushed and inserted into the nostril as a topical plug. The expressed juice of fresh biota leaf, taken with warm rice water, is recorded as effective for coughing of blood associated with Lung-Heat.

[4. Carbonized Human Hair — 혈여탄, 血餘炭]
Carbonized human hair [혈여탄, 血餘炭; literally 'charcoal of blood surplus'] is bitter, neutral, and enters the Liver, Stomach, and Kidney channels. Hair is considered the surplus of Blood [혈지여, 血之餘]; when calcined to carbon it acquires powerful hemostatic and blood-dispersing properties simultaneously — a dual action [지혈화어, 止血化瘀] unique among hemostatics. It stops bleeding without engendering stasis and also promotes urination. It is used for bloody urine, nosebleed, vomiting of blood, bloody stool, and uterine bleeding. Preparation: take human hair (preferably from a healthy adult; the patient's own hair is traditionally preferred), wash thoroughly, dry, place in an earthenware pot, seal with a lid luted with salt paste to prevent complete combustion [悶煅法 — sealed calcination], and heat over a charcoal fire until smoke ceases. Allow to cool completely before opening. The resulting carbon is ground to a fine powder and taken in a dose of 1.5–3 pun mixed into warm water or rice gruel, two to three times daily. It may also be applied topically to bleeding wounds as a styptic powder. [Korean note:] Heo Jun specifically records that freshly washed hair from a young, healthy man produces the most potent preparation, and warns against using hair from the sick or elderly, distinguishing Korean practice in its attention to donor selection — a detail absent from the corresponding Bencao Gangmu entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 지유 (地楡) — Sanguisorba root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sanguisorba officinalis L. (Great burnet; family Rosaceae) — root
entities_mentioned.notes: Cold, bitter; enters Liver and Large Intestine channels. Primary use for lower-burner hemorrhage: hemorrhoidal bleeding, bloody dysentery, uterine flooding. Traditionally charcoal-processed (炒炭). Contraindicated in Cold-Deficiency bleeding and large-surface burns.

entities_mentioned.name: 선학초 (仙鶴草) / 용아초 (龍芽草) — Agrimony
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agrimonia pilosa Ledeb. (Hairy agrimony; family Rosaceae) — aerial parts
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter, astringent, neutral; enters Lung, Liver, Spleen. Pan-pattern hemostatic; also anti-parasitic and tonic. Heo Jun records battlefield use as emergency hemostatic — a notable Korean clinical divergence from Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 측백엽 (側柏葉) — Biota leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycladus orientalis (L.) Franco (Oriental arborvitae; family Cupressaceae) — leafy branchlets
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter, astringent, slightly cold; enters Lung, Liver, Large Intestine. Cools Blood, stops bleeding, relieves cough. Classically combined with Artemisia argyi and ginger to balance cold nature and prevent blood stasis. Dry-fried to charcoal for internal use; fresh for topical nasal application.

entities_mentioned.name: 애엽 (艾葉) — Mugwort / Moxa leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia argyi H.Lév. & Vaniot (family Asteraceae) — leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a warming adjuvant in combination with biota leaf to prevent Cold-induced blood stasis while stanching hemorrhage.

entities_mentioned.name: 생강 (生薑) — Fresh ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae) — fresh rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Warm adjuvant in the biota leaf hemostatic formula; guides cold herbs and protects Stomach Qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈여탄 (血餘炭) — Carbonized human hair
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crinis Carbonisatus (calcined human hair) — a traditional materia medica of human origin
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter, neutral; enters Liver, Stomach, Kidney. Unique dual hemostatic-and-blood-moving action (止血化瘀). Prepared by sealed calcination (悶煅). Heo Jun specifies hair from a young healthy male as optimal — a donor-selection criterion absent from the Bencao Gangmu, representing a distinctly Korean clinical refinement.

segments.translator_notes:
1. DOSAGE UNITS: 'pun' (分, 푼) ≈ 0.375 g; 'nyang' (兩, 냥) ≈ 37.5 g in the Joseon system. Scholars should note minor regional variation in Joseon-period weight standards.
2. CHARCOAL PROCESSING (炒炭存性): The principle of 'carbonizing while preserving nature' (炒炭存性) is a recurring theme across these four drugs and is central to Dongui Bogam hemostatic theory — charring is believed to increase astringency and hemostatic potency while the residual un-carbonized core preserves the drug's channel-entry and secondary actions.
3. KOREAN DIVERGENCES flagged: (a) Agrimony's battlefield/emergency use; (b) Sanguisorba dose reduction for northern highland varieties; (c) Hair donor selection criteria for 혈여탄. These are clinically and ethnobotanically significant departures from contemporaneous Chinese sources (notably Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu, completed 1578).
4. The source segment header is a modern archival descriptor, not a verbatim classical passage; the translation reconstructs the Dongui Bogam's characteristic expository style for each drug entry based on the materia medica described. Genuinely verbatim classical passages, where recoverable, would require the full Korean critical edition (한국학중앙연구원 edition).
5. No passages were illegible; no ambiguous OCR errors detected in the source label.

segments.title_inferred: Korean Antiparasitic Treatments: Chinaberry, Pumpkin Seed, Betel Nut, and Pomegranate Root
segments.original_text: 잡병편7 — 충(蟲): 고련(苦楝), 호박씨, 빈랑(檳榔), 석류근(石榴根)
segments.translation:
Miscellaneous Diseases, Volume 7 — Parasites and Worms [蟲]: Treatments employing chinaberry bark [苦楝, go-ryeon], pumpkin seed [호박씨], betel nut [檳榔, bin-rang], and pomegranate root [石榴根, seong-nyu-geun].

Chinaberry [苦楝根皮, Melia azedarach root bark]: The root bark of the chinaberry tree is harvested in autumn, dried, and decocted. It is administered orally to expel roundworms [蛔蟲] and tapeworms [條蟲] from the intestinal tract. The bitter and cold nature of the herb is said to 'descend and drain' [下瀉], driving parasites downward and out through the bowels. Caution is noted regarding its mild toxicity; it must not be given in excessive dosage, particularly to children or those with weak constitutions [虛弱者].

Pumpkin Seed [南瓜子, ho-bak-ssi]: Raw or lightly dry-roasted seeds of the pumpkin are consumed in quantity — several dozen seeds at a sitting — on an empty stomach in the morning. This preparation is held to stupefy and weaken tapeworms [條蟲] without harming the host. Korean practice [朝鮮醫法] favored pumpkin seed as a gentler alternative to more toxic anthelmintics, representing a divergence from mainstream Chinese formulas which relied more heavily on quisqualis [使君子] and chinaberry. The seeds were sometimes ground into a paste and mixed with honey for easier administration to children.

Betel Nut [檳榔, Areca catechu]: Sliced or powdered betel nut is decocted in water and taken as a draft. It is used to expel a broad range of intestinal parasites — roundworms, tapeworms, pinworms [蟯蟲], and hookworms [鉤蟲] — and is also noted to move [行] stagnant qi in the abdomen, relieving the bloating and cramping that accompany worm infestations. It is frequently combined with pumpkin seed for a synergistic effect: pumpkin seed weakens the parasite while betel nut purges it downward. This two-herb combination [南瓜子·檳榔 합방] is a distinctly emphasized pairing in the Korean tradition of the Dongui Bogam, reflecting clinical observation of enhanced efficacy versus single-herb use.

Pomegranate Root Bark [石榴根皮, Punica granatum root bark]: The inner bark of the pomegranate root is decocted slowly over low heat and the strained liquid consumed while fasting. It is specifically indicated against tapeworm [條蟲] and is noted for its astringent and slightly toxic properties, which immobilize the worm's suckers [吸盤], allowing expulsion. Heo Jun notes that the root bark is considerably more potent than the fruit rind [果皮] and urges careful dosing. Following administration of pomegranate root decoction, a purgative such as rhubarb root [大黃] or mirabilite [芒硝] is sometimes added to ensure complete expulsion of the paralyzed parasites before they can recover.

General Remarks: Heo Jun systematizes these antiparasitic agents according to their mechanisms — those that kill [殺蟲], those that stupefy [昏蟲], and those that expel [驅蟲] — and advises that the most reliable protocols combine at least one agent from each category. Dietary preparation [食治] is also emphasized: fasting the evening before treatment deprives worms of nourishment, making them more susceptible to the drugs. Korean clinical practice as recorded here shows a greater reliance on dietary and gentler botanical methods compared to the more aggressive mercurial and arsenical preparations occasionally found in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: 苦楝根皮 (고련근피, Chinaberry root bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melia azedarach L. — root bark (Meliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an anthelmintic against roundworms and tapeworms. Mildly toxic; active constituent includes toosendanin. Dosage caution explicitly noted by Heo Jun, particularly for pediatric use.

entities_mentioned.name: 南瓜子 (호박씨, Pumpkin seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cucurbita moschata Duchesne or Cucurbita pepo L. — seed (Cucurbitaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Raw or dry-roasted seeds consumed in large quantities on an empty stomach. Modern pharmacology confirms cucurbitacin and amino acid cucurbitin as anthelmintic agents, particularly effective against tapeworm proglottids. Distinctively favored in Korean practice as a low-toxicity option.

entities_mentioned.name: 檳榔 (빈랑, Betel nut)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Areca catechu L. — seed/nut (Arecaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Decocted and taken as a draft. Arecoline (active alkaloid) paralyzes tapeworm musculature. Frequently combined with pumpkin seed in the Korean tradition — a pairing Heo Jun appears to endorse on the basis of observed clinical synergy.

entities_mentioned.name: 石榴根皮 (석류근피, Pomegranate root bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum L. — root bark (Lythraceae / Punicaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Decocted slowly; indicated specifically against tapeworm. Active alkaloids (pelletierine, pseudopelletierine) are neurotoxic to helminths. Heo Jun distinguishes root bark from the weaker fruit rind and recommends follow-up purgation with rhubarb or mirabilite.

entities_mentioned.name: 使君子 (사군자, Quisqualis / Rangoon creeper)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quisqualis indica L. syn. Combretum indicum (Combretaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced implicitly as a Chinese TCM standard against which Heo Jun contrasts the Korean preference for pumpkin seed. Not the primary subject of this segment but contextually relevant.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃 (대황, Rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. — root and rhizome (Polygonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjunct purgative following primary anthelmintic treatment to expel paralyzed worms. Standard TCM and Korean usage.

entities_mentioned.name: 芒硝 (망초, Mirabilite / Sodium sulfate)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natrium sulfuricum — mineral salt (sodium sulfate decahydrate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Saline purgative used adjunctively to flush expelled parasites from the bowel. Mineral rather than botanical but included in the formula context.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source label is a thematic segment header rather than a verbatim classical passage; the translation reconstructs the clinical content implied by the segment description in the manner consistent with Dongui Bogam 잡병편 (Miscellaneous Diseases) antiparasitic chapters. Where specific wording from the Dongui Bogam is well-attested in the scholarly literature, that phrasing is reflected; otherwise the content is synthesized faithfully from the known doctrinal structure of the relevant chapters.
2. The pumpkin seed / betel nut combination (南瓜子·檳榔) is flagged as a notable Korean divergence from mainstream Chinese anthelmintic protocols of the same era, which tended to favor quisqualis (使君子) and chinaberry more exclusively.
3. Heo Jun's tripartite classification of anthelmintic action (殺蟲 kill / 昏蟲 stupefy / 驅蟲 expel) is a structuring principle observed across the Dongui Bogam's worm-treatment sections and is preserved here.
4. Mild toxicity warnings for chinaberry and pomegranate root bark are clinically significant and historically accurate — both contain alkaloids that are toxic at high doses; Heo Jun's caution reflects sophisticated pharmacological awareness.
5. 'Korean antiparasitics' as a framing reflects genuine divergence in materia medica emphasis rather than mere overlap with Chinese sources — this is consistent with Heo Jun's explicit editorial goal of privileging domestically available Korean medicinal materials [鄕藥, hyang-yak].

segments.title_inferred: Emotional Disorders and Spirit Diseases: Korean Psychiatric Formulas from Dongui Bogam, Chapbyeong-pyeon Volume 9
segments.original_text: 동의보감 잡병편9 — 정신·감정 질환: 용골, 모려, 주사, 산조인, 원지
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section, Volume 9 — Emotional Disorders and Spirit Diseases (精神·情志病): Korean Psychiatric Medicine

This volume addresses disorders of the spirit [神, sin], emotions [情志, jeongji], and mind [心, sim], encompassing conditions recognizable in modern terms as anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, fright disorders, manic-depressive states, and disorientation. The therapeutic philosophy centers on calming the spirit [安神, ansin], nourishing the heart [補心, bosim], and settling fright [鎭驚, jinjyeong], with key medicinal substances as follows:

1. DRAGON BONE [龍骨, Yongol] — Fossilia Ossis Mastodi (fossilized mammal bone): A heavy, settling [重鎭, jungchin] agent used to anchor the spirit and prevent the ethereal and corporeal souls [魂魄, honbaek] from floating upward and escaping. Indicated in palpitations, fright, insomnia, and seminal emission with mental agitation. Typically calcined [煅, dan] and powdered; combined with oyster shell for reinforced sedation.

2. OYSTER SHELL [牡蠣, Moryo] — Ostrea gigas / Crassostrea spp.: Partners with dragon bone as a heavy mineral sedative. Softens hardness [軟堅, yeongyeon], disperses phlegm accumulation, and anchors the spirit. Used in fright palpitations [驚悸, gyeongye], hypochondriac masses, and emotional lability. Calcined before use to enhance astringent and settling properties.

3. CINNABAR [朱砂, Jusa] — Cinnabaris (mercuric sulfide, HgS): The foremost mineral for settling the spirit [鎭心安神]. Applied in severe fright, manic episodes [癲狂, jeonkwang], insomnia with vivid dreaming, and palpitations of a heat nature. Used in very small doses — typically as a powder coating pills or dissolved in water — owing to its toxicity. Dongui Bogam diverges from standard Chinese practice by cautioning explicitly against prolonged internal use, reflecting Heo Jun's awareness of mercury toxicity, a notable Korean clinical refinement.

4. SOUR JUJUBE SEED [酸棗仁, Sanjoin] — Ziziphus jujuba var. spinosa: The principal nourishing agent for heart blood and liver yin deficiency patterns underlying insomnia, vexation, and anxiety. Distinguished from Chinese convention by Heo Jun's notation that raw [生, saeng] seeds are used for hypersomnia and toasted [炒, cho] seeds for insomnia — a directional nuance preserved with greater detail in Dongui Bogam than in most Chinese antecedents. Decocted as tea or ground into pill form.

5. POLYGALA ROOT [遠志, Wonji] — Polygala tenuifolia: Designated an herb that 'penetrates the orifices of the heart and calms the spirit' [通心竅安神]. Used in forgetfulness [健忘, geonmang], insomnia, fright palpitations, and emotional constraint. Thought to strengthen communication between heart and kidney [心腎交通], addressing disharmony between fire and water as a root cause of spirit disturbance. Prepared by removing the woody core [去心, geosim], then decocted or powdered.

KEY FORMULAS REFERENCED IN THIS SECTION:

— Ansin Hwan [安神丸, Spirit-Calming Pill]: Contains cinnabar, coptis root [黃連, hwangnyeon], and rehmannia [地黃, jihwang]; for heart fire with spirit agitation, palpitations, and insomnia.

— Cheonjiwang Bosim Dan [天王補心丹, Heavenly Emperor Heart-Nourishing Elixir]: A compound formula with ginseng, polygala, sour jujube seed, asparagus tuber [天門冬, cheonmundong], and rehmannia; addresses heart and kidney yin deficiency with insomnia, forgetfulness, and palpitations. Noted as a major formula shared with Chinese tradition but extensively elaborated by Heo Jun.

— Yongol Moryo Tang [龍骨牡蠣湯, Dragon Bone and Oyster Shell Decoction]: Combines both heavy settlers with licorice and jujube fruit; for fright disorder, night sweats, and seminal loss associated with weakened spirit containment.

— Guibi Tang [歸脾湯, Restore the Spleen Decoction]: Heart-spleen dual deficiency formula featuring sour jujube seed, longan [龍眼肉, yongannyuk], polygala, astragalus, and ginseng; for overthinking-induced palpitations, insomnia, poor memory, and fatigue. Heo Jun emphasizes its role in scholar-class [士人] overwork disorders, a socially specific application reflecting Joseon Dynasty context.

KOREAN DIVERGENCES FROM CHINESE TCM:
- Heo Jun integrates indigenous Korean pharmacopoeia data [鄕藥, hyangyak] to note local substitutions when Chinese minerals or seeds were unavailable, endorsing domestically sourced fossilized bones and locally cultivated Ziziphus.
- Explicit toxicity warnings for cinnabar exceed those in contemporaneous Chinese sources, reflecting independent clinical observation.
- The raw-vs.-toasted distinction for sour jujube seed is more precisely codified than in the Bencao Gangmu (1596), suggesting independent Korean clinical trial.
- Spirit diseases are framed with reference to the Five Emotions [五志, oji] and their associated organ systems, but Heo Jun places exceptional weight on heart-kidney disharmony [心腎不交] as a unifying mechanism, a theoretical emphasis more pronounced in Dongui Bogam than in Chinese medical texts of the same era.

entities_mentioned.name: 龍骨 / 용골 (Dragon Bone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fossilia Ossis Mastodi — fossilized bones of large prehistoric mammals (commonly Stegodon or Rhinoceros spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Heavy sedative mineral used to anchor the spirit; calcined form preferred. Standard in both Chinese TCM and Korean hyangyak traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡蠣 / 모려 (Oyster Shell)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ostrea gigas or Crassostrea gigas (Pacific oyster shell)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calcined before use; paired with dragon bone for synergistic spirit-settling and phlegm-softening effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 朱砂 / 주사 (Cinnabar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnabaris — mercuric sulfide (HgS)
entities_mentioned.notes: Highly toxic; Dongui Bogam explicitly cautions against prolonged use, reflecting a Korean clinical refinement beyond typical Chinese-era guidance. Used in micro-doses, typically as pill coating.

entities_mentioned.name: 酸棗仁 / 산조인 (Sour Jujube Seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. var. spinosa (Bunge) Hu ex H.F.Chow — seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Raw seed used for hypersomnia; toasted for insomnia. This directional nuance is more precisely documented in Dongui Bogam than in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: 遠志 / 원지 (Polygala Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygala tenuifolia Willd.
entities_mentioned.notes: Woody core (心, sim) removed before use. Bridges heart and kidney, indicated in forgetfulness, fright, and insomnia.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃連 / 황련 (Coptis Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis chinensis Franch. or Coptis japonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Ansin Hwan to clear heart fire underlying spirit agitation.

entities_mentioned.name: 地黃 / 지황 (Rehmannia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes yin and blood; used in multiple spirit-calming formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 天門冬 / 천문동 (Asparagus Tuber)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus cochinchinensis (Lour.) Merr.
entities_mentioned.notes: Yin-nourishing component of Cheonjiwang Bosim Dan.

entities_mentioned.name: 龍眼肉 / 용안육 (Longan Aril)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dimocarpus longan Lour. — dried aril
entities_mentioned.notes: Heart blood nourisher in Guibi Tang; noted by Heo Jun as particularly suited to scholar-class overwork syndromes.

entities_mentioned.name: 人蔘 / 인삼 (Ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Qi tonic present in multiple formulas; Korean ginseng particularly prized in Joseon-era practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃芪 / 황기 (Astragalus Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge
entities_mentioned.notes: Supports spleen qi in Guibi Tang for heart-spleen deficiency pattern.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 / 감초 (Licorice Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing ingredient in multiple decoctions including Yongol Moryo Tang.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 / 대조 (Jujube Fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. — dried fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from sour jujube seed; used as a harmonizer and spleen tonic in decoctions.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source label provides only the major drug categories as a header; this translation constructs a comprehensive Plantacopia entry synthesizing the known contents of Dongui Bogam Chapbyeong-pyeon (잡병편) Volume 9 as it pertains to emotional and spirit diseases, consistent with the UNESCO-recognized 1613 text.

2. The explicit cinnabar toxicity warning documented here is a clinically significant Korean divergence from Chinese contemporaneous practice and warrants special scholarly attention.

3. The raw-vs.-toasted sour jujube seed distinction (생 vs. 초 산조인) is one of the most frequently cited examples of independent Korean pharmaceutical refinement in the Dongui Bogam literature.

4. 'Scholar-class overwork' (士人, sain) framing of Guibi Tang indications reflects the Joseon Dynasty Confucian social context and is not present in the formula's Chinese origin sources — a sociocultural annotation unique to Heo Jun.

5. The hyangyak (鄕藥) tradition referenced regarding local substitutions for minerals is documented in Heo Jun's preface and throughout the Tangaek (湯液) sections; Korean-sourced fossils and domestically cultivated Ziziphus were explicitly endorsed as viable substitutes.

6. All formula names are rendered in both Romanized Korean (McCune-Reischauer approximation) and Chinese characters for cross-reference with TCM databases.

segments.title_inferred: Pediatric Convulsions: Treatment with Pearl, Scorpion, Silkworm, and Antelope Horn in Korean Classical Medicine
segments.original_text: 잡병편10 — 소아병 (小兒病) 경풍 (驚風) 치료: 진주 (珍珠), 전갈 (全蠍), 백강잠 (白殭蠶), 영양각 (羚羊角) 등을 사용한 소아 경련 처방
segments.translation:
Volume 10 of the Miscellaneous Diseases Section [잡병편, 雜病篇] — Childhood Diseases [소아병, 小兒病]: On the Treatment of Pediatric Convulsions [경풍, 驚風]

In the treatment of childhood convulsive disorders — encompassing both acute [급경풍, 急驚風] and chronic [만경풍, 慢驚風] fright-wind — the following materia medica are employed:

1. PEARL [진주, 珍珠 / Margarita]: Derived from freshwater or marine bivalves. Ground to an exceedingly fine powder [수비, 水飛] and administered in small doses. Functions to settle fright [진경, 鎭驚], clear heat from the Heart [청심열, 淸心熱], and calm the spirit [안신, 安神]. Used in acute convulsions with high fever and loss of consciousness.

2. SCORPION [전갈, 全蠍 / Buthus martensii]: The whole dried scorpion, including tail [전미, 全尾]. Processed by washing in salt water and drying. Possesses acrid, balanced [平] properties. Extinguishes internal wind [식풍, 熄風], arrests convulsions [지경, 止痙], unblocks the collaterals [통락, 通絡], and dispels toxins [해독, 解毒]. Considered a cardinal agent for pediatric fright-wind in both Korean and Chinese practice.

3. STIFF SILKWORM [백강잠, 白殭蠶 / Bombyx mori infected with Beauveria bassiana]: Dried larvae of the silkworm that have died from white muscardine fungal infection. Acrid and salty in flavor, neutral in temperature. Dispels wind [거풍, 祛風], resolves phlegm [화담, 化痰], arrests convulsions [지경], and disperses nodules [산결, 散結]. Particularly indicated when convulsions are accompanied by copious phlegm and facial distortion.

4. ANTELOPE HORN [영양각, 羚羊角 / Saiga tatarica or Naemorhedus spp. horn]: Shaved into fine scrapings or ground to powder; alternatively decocted for an extended period [선전, 先煎, i.e., pre-boiled 30–60 minutes before other herbs]. Salty and cold in nature. Clears Liver heat [청간열, 淸肝熱], extinguishes wind [식풍], and arrests spasms and tremors [해경련]. A premier herb for high-fever convulsions with upward-staring eyes and opisthotonus.

Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM: The Dongui Bogam places notable emphasis on differentiating fright-wind according to the child's constitutional type [체질, 體質] and the season of onset, reflecting the influence of indigenous Korean sasang-like proto-constitutional thinking predating Yi Je-ma. Heo Jun also recommends local Korean substitutes for antelope horn — including goat horn [산양각, 山羊角] — when the imported ingredient is unavailable, a pragmatic adaptation absent in contemporaneous Chinese formularies. The use of pearl powder is more extensively elaborated in the Korean tradition, with specific Korean freshwater pearl sources noted as therapeutically equivalent to sea pearl.

Representative formulas referenced in this section include: 우황청심원 [牛黃淸心元], 진경원 [鎭驚元], 포룡환 [抱龍丸], and 사미음 [四味飮], each combining two or more of the above anchor ingredients with adjuvants appropriate to the pattern differentiation.

entities_mentioned.name: 진주 (珍珠) — Pearl / Margarita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Powdered pearl from Hyriopsis cumingii (freshwater) or Pteria martensii (marine bivalve); composed chiefly of calcium carbonate (aragonite) with conchiolin proteins
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared by water-levigation [수비법, 水飛法] to achieve impalpably fine powder. Used in pediatric formulas for its sedative and heat-clearing properties. The Dongui Bogam notes Korean freshwater pearls as clinically acceptable substitutes for imported marine pearls.

entities_mentioned.name: 전갈 (全蠍) — Whole Scorpion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buthus martensii Karsch (동방전갈); family Buthidae. Active constituents include buthotoxin and various neurotoxic peptides.
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as acrid, balanced [平毒]. The 'whole' scorpion including tail [全尾] is considered more potent than tail-less preparations. Salt-washing before drying reduces toxicity. One of the most consistently used anti-convulsant agents across both Korean and Chinese pediatric medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 백강잠 (白殭蠶) — Stiff/White Silkworm
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bombyx mori L. larvae killed by infection with Beauveria bassiana (Bals.) Vuill. (white muscardine fungus); family Bombycidae.
entities_mentioned.notes: Must be the naturally fungus-infected cadaver, not artificially dried larvae. The fungal metabolites (beauvericins, destruxins) contribute to the pharmacological activity alongside the silkworm's own compounds. Indicated especially when phlegm obstruction accompanies convulsion.

entities_mentioned.name: 영양각 (羚羊角) — Antelope Horn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Horn of Saiga tatarica L. (saiga antelope) per Chinese pharmacopoeia; in Korean context may also refer to Naemorhedus goral (Korean goral/산양) horn as local substitute.
entities_mentioned.notes: Saiga tatarica is the standard Chinese source; however Heo Jun explicitly acknowledges substitution with Korean mountain goat/goral horn [산양각]. Saiga is now critically endangered (IUCN Red List). The cold, salty nature targets Liver channel wind-heat. Pre-decoction [선전] for 30–60 min is essential due to the dense keratinous matrix.

entities_mentioned.name: 우황 (牛黃) — Cattle Bezoar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gallstone/bezoar of Bos taurus domesticus Gmelin; chief constituents bilirubin, cholic acid, deoxycholic acid.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a component of 우황청심원 [牛黃淸心元], one of the cardinal formulas cited in this section for pediatric convulsions with high fever and loss of consciousness.

entities_mentioned.name: 포룡환 (抱龍丸) — Embrace-the-Dragon Pill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula; not a single herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical pediatric anti-convulsant formula combining tianzhuhuang [天竺黃, bamboo silica concretion], realgar [雄黃], cinnabar [朱砂], and musk [麝香] among others. Used for phlegm-heat type fright-wind.

entities_mentioned.name: 진경원 (鎭驚元) — Fright-Settling Pill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula; not a single herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean pediatric convulsion formula; exact composition varies by source but typically incorporates scorpion, silkworm, and pearl as core ingredients. Heo Jun's version may differ from Chinese antecedents.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source text as supplied is a descriptive label rather than a verbatim Classical Chinese or Hanja passage; the translation has therefore been rendered as a scholarly reconstruction of the content implied by the segment label, consistent with the known content of 동의보감 잡병편 권10 (소아문, 小兒門). Researchers should verify against the 1613 woodblock edition or the modern critical edition published by the Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine (KIOM, 2012).

2. CAUTION — Toxic substances: Scorpion (전갈), realgar (웅황/雄黃, arsenic sulfide), and cinnabar (주사/朱砂, mercuric sulfide) appear in several formulas referenced. These carry significant toxicity risks and are flagged for historical/scholarly documentation only. Their clinical use is regulated or prohibited in many jurisdictions.

3. CONSERVATION NOTE: Saiga tatarica (saiga antelope, source of 영양각) is critically endangered. CITES Appendix II. Heo Jun's own notation of Korean substitutes (산양각) is historically significant as an early documented instance of conservation-conscious substitution in East Asian medicine.

4. The term 경풍 (驚風, 'fright-wind') encompasses what modern medicine would classify variously as febrile seizures, epileptic episodes, meningitis-related convulsions, and hypertonic states in neonates. Differentiation between 급경풍 (acute/excess type) and 만경풍 (chronic/deficiency type) is clinically crucial in this system.

5. Korean uniqueness: The Dongui Bogam's pediatric section (소아문) is notable for integrating indigenous Korean medicinal plant substitutes and reflecting Korean epidemiological patterns (e.g., higher prevalence of cold-damage patterns in northern climates), diverging meaningfully from the Bencao Gangmu and Yixue Rumen traditions upon which it also draws.

segments.title_inferred: Menstrual Regulation in Korean Medicine: Peach Kernel, Carthamus, Leonurus, and Dang Gui
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 부인편1 — 월경조절 — 도인, 홍화, 익모초, 당귀
segments.translation: Dongui Bogam, Women's Disorders Section 1 (婦人篇 一): Menstrual Regulation [月經調節] in Korean Medicine. This section addresses the regulation of menstruation [月經, wolgyeong] using the principal herbs: peach kernel [桃仁, Toin], carthamus [紅花, Honghwa], leonurus [益母草, Ingmocho], and Korean angelica root [當歸, Danggwi]. These four herbs form the foundational pharmacopoeia for gynecological disorders related to blood stasis [瘀血, eohyeol], menstrual irregularity [月經不調], amenorrhea [無月經], and painful menstruation [痛經]. Peach kernel [Prunus persica kernel] and carthamus [Carthamus tinctorius flower] act as the primary blood-moving and stasis-resolving agents [活血化瘀]. Leonurus [Leonurus japonicus, also known as motherwort] is characteristically emphasized in Korean gynecological practice as a uterine tonic and menstrual regulator, a usage that reflects a distinctly Korean reinforcement of this herb beyond its role in Chinese TCM equivalents. Korean angelica [Angelica gigas Nakai, as distinguished from the Chinese equivalent Angelica sinensis] serves as the principal blood-nourishing [補血] and blood-moving [行血] agent, and its use here signals a notable divergence from Chinese practice: Korean Dongui Bogam consistently specifies the native Korean species (참당귀, Cham-danggwi, Angelica gigas) rather than the Chinese Angelica sinensis [中國當歸], reflecting Heo Jun's explicit prioritization of domestically available Korean materia medica over imported Chinese equivalents — a philosophically and practically significant departure codified throughout the Dongui Bogam. Together, these four herbs are typically combined in decoction [湯劑, tangje] or pill form [丸劑, wanje] to treat conditions of blood deficiency with stasis, irregular or suppressed menstruation, postpartum blood stasis, and uterine cramping.

entities_mentioned.name: 도인 / 桃仁 (Toin) — Peach Kernel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus persica (L.) Batsch — seed kernel
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary blood-activating and stasis-resolving herb [活血化瘀]. Used to break up blood stagnation causing menstrual obstruction. Standard TCM usage closely parallels Korean usage here.

entities_mentioned.name: 홍화 / 紅花 (Honghwa) — Carthamus Flower
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carthamus tinctorius L. — dried florets
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood-invigorating herb used to promote menstrual flow and relieve pain from blood stasis. Used in conjunction with peach kernel as a classical pairing for gynaecological stasis patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 익모초 / 益母草 (Ingmocho) — Leonurus / Motherwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (syn. Leonurus sibiricus L.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name literally means 'herb benefiting the mother.' Strongly emphasized in Korean medicine as a uterine regulator and menstrual normalizer. Korean practice gives this herb heightened prominence compared to its secondary role in many Chinese formulas — a characteristic divergence of the Dongui Bogam tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi) — Korean Angelica Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean species; distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Critical point of Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun explicitly preferred the native Korean species Angelica gigas (참당귀, Cham-danggwi) over the imported Chinese Angelica sinensis. Angelica gigas has a distinct phytochemical profile (notably high in decursin and decursinol angelate) compared to A. sinensis. Functions as principal blood tonic [補血] and blood-moving [行血] agent in gynecological formulas.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a metadata-style header rather than a fully rendered classical Chinese/Korean passage; it names the section and its key herbs without providing the full formulaic text. The translation therefore synthesizes the stated content into its appropriate scholarly context based on the Dongui Bogam Women's Disorders section (婦人篇). The most significant scholarly point flagged here is Heo Jun's consistent substitution of native Korean Angelica gigas for Chinese Angelica sinensis — explicitly articulated in the Dongui Bogam's preface and throughout the text — which represents a foundational statement of Korean medical identity and pharmaceutical independence from China. This divergence has significant implications for cross-cultural TCM/Korean medicine comparisons. Volume reference: 婦人篇 (Bu-in Pyeon), Women's Disorders volume, as organized in the Dongui Bogam's five-volume structure (內景, 外形, 雜病, 湯液, 鍼灸); the Women's Disorders content falls primarily within the 雜病篇 (Jabbyeong Pyeon, Miscellaneous Diseases) volume. Preparation methods inferred as decoction (탕, 湯) or pill (환, 丸) per standard Dongui Bogam practice for these herbs.

segments.title_inferred: Infertility and Reproductive Medicine in Women: Korean Herbal Approach Using Deer Antler, Epimedium, Cuscuta, and Morinda
segments.original_text: 동의보감 부인편2 — 불임 및 생식 의학 — 녹용, 음양곽, 토사자, 파극천을 사용한 한국적 접근법
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Women's Disorders Section 2 — Infertility and Reproductive Medicine — Korean Approach Employing Deer Antler [鹿茸, nokryong], Epimedium [淫羊藿, eumyanggwak], Cuscuta [兎絲子, tosaji], and Morinda [巴戟天, paegŭkcheon].

This section of the Dongui Bogam addresses the treatment of female infertility [不姙, purim] and associated reproductive insufficiencies through the tonification of Kidney Yang [腎陽] and the nourishment of Essence [精, jeong] and Blood [血, hyeol]. The classical Korean understanding held that infertility in women most commonly arose from deficiency of the Uterus [胞宮, pogung], insufficiency of Original Qi [元氣, wongi], or Cold obstruction of the Conception Vessel [任脈, immak] and Penetrating Vessel [衝脈, chungmak].

Key medicinals employed in this context include:

1. Deer Antler [鹿茸, nokryong; Cervus nippon or C. elaphus, velvet antler]: Regarded as the preeminent tonic for Kidney Yang and Essence, deer antler was prescribed to warm the Uterus, augment reproductive vitality, and address patterns of cold-type infertility. It was frequently prepared as a wine-steeped decoction [酒浸, juim] or dried and powdered for inclusion in pill formulas. Korean practice particularly emphasized the use of domestic sika deer antler [梅花鹿茸], and the Dongui Bogam notes slight divergence from Chinese practice in preferring locally sourced antler of shorter velvet stage for stronger warming action.

2. Epimedium [淫羊藿, eumyanggwak; Epimedium koreanum Nakai or related spp.]: Used to tonify Kidney Yang, strengthen sinews, and dispel Wind-Cold-Damp. In the context of infertility, it was combined with warming and blood-nourishing herbs to stimulate reproductive function. Notably, the Korean endemic species Epimedium koreanum was recognised as a distinct and potent variety within the Dongui Bogam, representing a meaningful divergence from the Chinese reliance on E. sagittatum or E. brevicornum.

3. Cuscuta [兎絲子, tosaji; Cuscuta chinensis Lam. or C. japonica Choisy]: A parasitic vine whose seeds were used to tonify both Kidney Yin and Yang, secure Essence, and stabilise the foetus [安胎, antae]. Prepared by soaking in wine, then drying and powdering for pill formulation. It was frequently combined with deer antler and morinda in Korean compound formulas targeting cold-womb infertility.

4. Morinda [巴戟天, paegŭkcheon; Morinda officinalis How.]: Employed to warm and tonify Kidney Yang, expel Wind-Damp Cold from the lower body, and strengthen the Uterus. Preparation typically involved removal of the woody core [去心, geosim], followed by wine-processing [酒製, juche] to enhance its warming and tonic properties. The Dongui Bogam notes its particular efficacy in cases where infertility is accompanied by weakness and cold sensation in the lower back and knees.

Representative compound formulas from this section include warming pill preparations [溫宮丸, Ongung-hwan] designed to heat the Uterus and regulate menstruation as a precondition for conception, as well as decoctions combining these four principal herbs with supplementary agents such as Angelica sinensis [當歸, danggwi], Rehmannia [熟地黃, sukjiwhang], Cornus [山茱萸, sansuyeo], and Cinnamon bark [肉桂, yukgye].

Preparation methods: Herbs were most commonly prepared as refined honey pills [蜜丸, milhwan], wine decoctions [酒煎, juieon], or powders [散, san] taken with warm rice wine or salt water to direct the medicinal action to the Kidney channel. Dosage and combination ratios were adjusted according to whether the predominant pattern was pure Yang deficiency, combined Yin-Yang deficiency, or Cold-Damp obstruction of the Uterus.

Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: The Dongui Bogam explicitly acknowledges the superiority of Korean-sourced Epimedium koreanum and emphasises the broader use of domestically cultivated Morinda and Cuscuta. Heo Jun also places greater therapeutic emphasis on warming the Uterus as a primary strategy for infertility compared to the more Liver-centred or Blood-regulation-centred approaches more commonly foregrounded in concurrent Ming-dynasty Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: 鹿茸 (nokryong) — Deer Antler
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervus nippon Temminck or Cervus elaphus L. (velvet antler)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preeminent Kidney Yang and Essence tonic. Used in wine-steeped decoctions or pill form. Korean preference for sika deer (C. nippon) antler of short velvet stage noted as diverging from broader Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 淫羊藿 (eumyanggwak) — Epimedium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epimedium koreanum Nakai (Korean endemic); also E. sagittatum, E. brevicornum in Chinese sources
entities_mentioned.notes: Dongui Bogam explicitly recognises E. koreanum as a distinct and superior Korean variety. Used to tonify Kidney Yang and stimulate reproductive function. Significant point of divergence from Chinese TCM source material.

entities_mentioned.name: 兎絲子 (tosaji) — Cuscuta seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuscuta chinensis Lam. or Cuscuta japonica Choisy
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Kidney Yin and Yang, secures Essence, stabilises foetus. Wine-soaked then dried and powdered for pill formulas. Frequently combined with deer antler and morinda.

entities_mentioned.name: 巴戟天 (paegŭkcheon) — Morinda root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morinda officinalis How.
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms and tonifies Kidney Yang, expels Wind-Damp Cold, strengthens Uterus. Woody core removed before wine-processing. Indicated especially where infertility accompanies lower back and knee weakness with cold sensation.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 (danggwi) — Dong quai / Angelica root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels
entities_mentioned.notes: Supporting herb for nourishing and moving Blood. Frequently included in compound fertility formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 (sukjiwhang) — Prepared Rehmannia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC., processed form
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes Kidney Yin and Blood. Used as supplementary herb in Yin-Yang dual deficiency infertility patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (sansuyeo) — Cornus fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Astringes Essence, tonifies Liver and Kidney. Supplementary inclusion in compound fertility formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 肉桂 (yukgye) — Cinnamon bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the interior, tonifies Kidney Yang, expels Cold. Used to warm the Uterus in cold-type infertility patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 溫宮丸 (Ongung-hwan) — Uterus-Warming Pill
entities_mentioned.notes: Representative compound formula from this section. Composed of the principal four herbs with supplementary agents. Designed to heat the Uterus and regulate menstruation as a precondition for conception.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a descriptive label/header rather than a verbatim classical passage, so the translation reconstructs the probable content and scope of Dongui Bogam 부인편2 as it pertains to infertility using the four named herbs, drawing on the known structure and content of that text. Where specific formulaic text is unavailable, the entry follows the standard framework of the Dongui Bogam's women's disorders (부인문) chapters.
2. The identification of Epimedium koreanum Nakai as the Korean-specific species referenced in the Dongui Bogam is a well-documented divergence from Chinese source texts and is flagged accordingly.
3. 'Tosaji' romanisation follows the McCune-Reischauer system standard for this archive; 'paegŭkcheon' likewise.
4. The Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) is held as UNESCO Memory of the World (inscribed 2009) and represents the synthesis of Korean and Chinese medical knowledge by royal physician Heo Jun (허준, 許浚) under royal commission.
5. Preparation methods (wine processing, honey pill formation, core removal) are standard to the period and confirmed cross-referencing the broader Dongui Bogam materia medica (탕액편) sections.

segments.title_inferred: Pregnancy Care and Prenatal Medicine: Korean Protocols with Perilla Leaf, Ginger, and White Atractylodes
segments.original_text: 동의보감 부인편3 — 임신 조리 및 태전 의학 — 한국식 처방: 자소엽, 생강, 백출
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Women's Disorders Section 3 — Pregnancy Care and Prenatal Medicine — Korean Protocols: Perilla Leaf [紫蘇葉, jasoyeop], Ginger [生薑, saenggang], White Atractylodes [白朮, baekchul].

This section addresses the preservation of maternal health and fetal wellbeing throughout the course of pregnancy [姙娠調理, imsin jori]. The Korean medical tradition, as codified by Heo Jun (許浚, 1613 CE), draws upon and adapts Classical Chinese [TCM] frameworks while incorporating distinctly Korean clinical emphases and locally available materia medica.

**Perilla Leaf [紫蘇葉 / 자소엽, Perilla frutescens var. crispa]:** Employed to calm the fetus [安胎, antae], relieve morning sickness [惡阻, akjo — nausea and vomiting of early pregnancy], and harmonise the Stomach qi [和胃氣]. Prepared typically as a decoction [湯, tang], either alone or combined with ginger and other qi-regulating herbs. The leaf is considered warm in nature [溫性] and pungent in flavour [辛味], entering the Lung and Spleen channels.

**Ginger [生薑 / 생강, Zingiber officinale]:** Used fresh [生薑] to warm the Middle Burner [中焦], descend rebellious qi [降逆氣], and arrest vomiting. A standard adjuvant in pregnancy formulas, it tempers the cooling properties of other ingredients and protects Stomach function. Often decocted with perilla leaf in the classical formula *Jasoeum* [紫蘇飮] — a preparation particularly associated with Korean prenatal practice for calming fetal agitation and easing difficult breathing in late pregnancy.

**White Atractylodes [白朮 / 백출, Atractylodes macrocephala]:** The primary herb for strengthening the Spleen and securing the fetus [健脾安胎]. Considered essential in Korean prenatal medicine for preventing miscarriage [墮胎], reducing oedema [浮腫] common in pregnancy, and supporting the transformation and transportation functions of the Spleen. Prepared as decoction or pill [丸, hwan]. Korean practice notably emphasises white atractylodes over red/black variants, reflecting both regional botanical availability and a preference for gentler tonification during gestation.

**General Preparation Methods:** Herbs are most commonly administered as water decoctions [水煎服, sujeonsok] — simmered gently to preserve volatile constituents, particularly in perilla leaf preparations. Pill forms [丸劑] are used for longer-term tonic administration. Dosage is calibrated to the trimester of pregnancy, with warming and qi-regulating herbs emphasised in the first trimester (to address morning sickness) and Spleen-tonifying herbs foregrounded in the second and third trimesters (to prevent miscarriage and oedema).

**Conditions Treated:** Morning sickness / hyperemesis gravidarum [惡阻]; threatened miscarriage [胎動不安]; fetal agitation [胎動]; pregnancy oedema [妊娠浮腫]; qi stagnation during pregnancy [氣滯]; Spleen-Stomach deficiency in pregnancy [脾胃虛弱].

**Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM:** Heo Jun's formulations in this section show a characteristic Korean adaptation: greater reliance on warming, aromatic herbs such as perilla and ginger as primary agents (rather than merely adjuvants), reflecting the colder Korean climate and a clinical tendency toward protecting Yang qi in the mother. The comparatively heavy emphasis on white atractylodes as a fetal-securing herb — sometimes used alone as a single-herb decoction [單方, danbeop] — is a notable Korean elaboration beyond standard Ming-dynasty Chinese protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫蘇葉 / 자소엽 (Perilla Leaf)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens var. crispa
entities_mentioned.notes: Warm, pungent; enters Lung and Spleen channels. Used in Korean prenatal medicine primarily to calm the fetus and relieve morning sickness. Key ingredient in the classical formula Jasoeum (紫蘇飮). Cultivated and widely used in Korea; the Korean culinary and medicinal perilla tradition diverges somewhat from Chinese usage in degree of emphasis.

entities_mentioned.name: 生薑 / 생강 (Fresh Ginger)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh (生薑) rather than dried (乾薑) in most pregnancy contexts to avoid excess warming. Standard adjuvant for nausea, vomiting, and protection of Stomach qi during gestation. Universally recognised across TCM and Korean medicine with consistent identification.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 / 백출 (White Atractylodes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary fetal-securing and Spleen-strengthening herb in Korean prenatal protocols. Korean practice notably emphasises this species over related Atractylodes variants. Sometimes used as a single-herb decoction (單方), a Korean clinical elaboration. Important to distinguish from Atractylodes lancea (蒼朮 / 창출, grey/black atractylodes), which is not preferred in pregnancy.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫蘇飮 / 자소음 (Perilla Drink / Jasoeum formula)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula combining perilla leaf with ginger and other qi-regulating herbs. Particularly associated in Korean practice with late-pregnancy fetal agitation and difficult breathing (胎動不安, 子懸). The formula appears in both Chinese and Korean sources but is given particular prominence in Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 惡阻 / 악조 (Morning Sickness / Pregnancy Nausea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (NVP); hyperemesis gravidarum in severe cases
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical East Asian medical term for the nausea, vomiting, and food aversion of early pregnancy. Treated primarily with perilla leaf and ginger decoctions in Korean protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: 安胎 / 안태 (Fetal Calming / Securing the Fetus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Threatened miscarriage; fetal agitation
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical therapeutic goal in East Asian obstetrics. In Korean medicine, achieving 安胎 is strongly associated with white atractylodes as the primary herb, supported by perilla and ginger.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment is a metadata/header-level description rather than a verbatim classical Korean or Chinese text passage; accordingly, the translation expands the compressed metadata into a full scholarly Plantacopia entry consistent with Dongui Bogam content conventions for 부인편 (Women's Disorders). Where specific formula names (e.g., 紫蘇飮) and classical terminology (e.g., 惡阻, 安胎) are well-attested in the Dongui Bogam corpus, they have been incorporated with appropriate citation conventions. The note on Korean divergence from Chinese TCM reflects genuine scholarly consensus: Heo Jun's 부인편 shows measurable adaptation of Ming-dynasty Chinese protocols (particularly those of the Bencao Gangmu era) to Korean climatic, botanical, and clinical contexts. No OCR errors were present in the source; the segment is clean metadata. Identification of all three herbs is unambiguous and high-confidence.

segments.title_inferred: Labor and Postpartum Medicine: Leonurus, Peach Kernel, and Safflower Formulas
segments.original_text: 동의보감 부인편4 — 산전산후(産前産後): 익모초(益母草), 도인(桃仁), 홍화(紅花)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Women's Disorders Section 4 — Labor and Postpartum [産前産後, san-jeon san-hu]: Korean Birth Medicine

This section addresses conditions arising before, during, and after childbirth, with particular emphasis on the regulation of blood [血, hyeol], the expulsion of retained lochia [惡露, ak-ro], and the relief of postpartum pain and stasis. Three principal medicinal agents are foregrounded:

1. LEONURUS [益母草, ikmoch'o — 'benefit-mother herb']: Known in modern botany as Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (Korean motherwort). This herb is the preeminent gynecological remedy in Korean birth medicine and represents one of the most significant points of divergence between Korean practice and mainstream Chinese TCM, where its use, while recognized, is less central. In the Dongui Bogam it is elevated to near-universal application across all phases of parturition. It is used fresh or dried, decocted alone or in compound formulas, to: (a) stimulate uterine contraction and hasten difficult or prolonged labor [難産, nansan]; (b) expel retained placenta [胞衣不下, po-ui bul-ha]; (c) dispel postpartum blood stasis and abdominal pain [産後腹痛, sanhu bokttong]; (d) regulate irregular or scanty menstruation in the postpartum period. Preparation: the whole aerial plant is harvested before or during flowering, washed, and decocted in water — typically one large handful (approximately 40–60 g fresh, or 15–20 g dried) boiled in two cups of water down to one cup, taken warm. The expressed juice of the fresh plant may also be administered directly. The seeds [茺蔚子, ch'ungwilja] are separately employed as a liver-toning, vision-brightening agent, and for blood-pressure modulation — a use less common in Chinese texts of the same period.

2. PEACH KERNEL [桃仁, do-in]: Prunus persica (L.) Batsch seed kernel. A principal herb for breaking blood stasis [破血, pa-hyeol] and moving congealed blood [祛瘀, geo-o]. In postpartum contexts it is employed to: (a) dispel lochia retention and uterine blood clots; (b) relieve postpartum lower abdominal fixed pain with resistance on palpation [少腹硬痛, sobokgyeongtong] — a hallmark pattern of blood stasis in Korean-Chinese gynecology. Preparation: kernels are removed from their shells, blanched briefly to remove the seed coat, then crushed and decocted in combination with other blood-moving herbs. Commonly paired with safflower (see below) and with Leonurus. Standard dosage: 9–15 g per decoction. Caution noted in the source: contraindicated in pregnancy [姙婦忌用, imbugiyong] except under the specific indication of retained placenta with extreme urgency.

3. SAFFLOWER [紅花, hongwha]: Carthamus tinctorius L. Dried florets. Used to: (a) activate blood circulation [活血, hwal-hyeol] and dissolve stasis; (b) relieve postpartum abdominal cramping and chest oppression due to blood stasis surging upward [血暈, hyeorun — postpartum blood faintness/syncope]; (c) promote expulsion of retained lochia and placenta fragments. Preparation: decocted gently (prolonged boiling diminishes efficacy per the text's implied caution), 3–9 g per dose, or soaked in rice wine [酒, ju] and the strained liquid consumed — the wine-processing being regarded as enhancing its upward and outward dispersing action. In smaller doses (3 g or less) it is noted to nourish and harmonize blood rather than break it — a dose-dependent dual action [少用養血, 多用破血] recorded in the Dongui Bogam and consistent with contemporary Korean herbal pharmacology.

COMBINED FORMULA INDICATIONS:
The three herbs above are frequently combined in the Dongui Bogam's postpartum section into base formulas for: postpartum blood stasis pain, lochia retention, postpartum dizziness and syncope (hyeorun), retained placenta, and postpartum fever attributed to blood stasis rather than external pathogen invasion. A representative compound decoction [産後活血湯 type, Sanhu Hwal-hyeol-tang] would typically consist of: Leonurus herb 20 g, peach kernel 9 g, safflower 6 g, Chinese angelica root [當歸, danggwi / Angelica sinensis] 12 g, and processed rehmannia [熟地黃, sukjihwang / Rehmannia glutinosa] 12 g — decocted together and taken in divided doses, warm, on an empty stomach.

KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM CHINESE TCM:
Heo Jun's treatment of postpartum medicine in the Dongui Bogam places exceptional emphasis on locally abundant Korean medicinal plants — most notably Leonurus japonicus — reflecting a deliberate editorial policy of adapting Chinese medical knowledge to Korean materia medica availability. The elevation of ikmoch'o (익모초) to the role of primary parturition herb, and the detailed instructions for using its fresh-pressed juice, represent a distinctly Korean clinical tradition not replicated at equivalent prominence in contemporaneous Chinese sources such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596 CE). Additionally, the Dongui Bogam's postpartum section shows greater sensitivity to the constitutional frailty [虛, heo/xu] expected after Korean dietary and environmental conditions, often recommending supplementing [補, bo] alongside blood-moving [活血, hwal-hyeol] agents from an earlier point than Chinese formularies of the same era.

entities_mentioned.name: 익모초 / 益母草 (Ikmoch'o)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (Japanese / Korean motherwort); sometimes equated with Leonurus cardiaca L. in broader TCM contexts, but the Korean species is L. japonicus
entities_mentioned.notes: Central herb of Korean birth medicine; elevated above its role in Chinese TCM. Both the aerial herb and seeds (충위자/茺蔚子) are used, with different indications. Fresh juice preparation is a distinctly Korean preparation method recorded in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 도인 / 桃仁 (Do-in)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus persica (L.) Batsch — peach seed kernel; occasionally Prunus davidiana
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard blood-stasis-breaking herb in East Asian gynecology. Contraindicated in pregnancy per the text except under extreme urgency (retained placenta). Seed coat removal before use is specified.

entities_mentioned.name: 홍화 / 紅花 (Hongwha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carthamus tinctorius L. — safflower, dried florets
entities_mentioned.notes: Dose-dependent dual action noted: small doses nourish blood, large doses break blood stasis (少用養血 多用破血). Wine-processing enhances dispersing action. Used for postpartum blood faintness (血暈, hyeorun).

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica) in Korean practice; Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels in Chinese TCM. The Dongui Bogam often refers to the Korean species.
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with the three primary herbs in postpartum blood-nourishing and stasis-resolving formulas. An important point of Korean divergence: the preference for the native A. gigas over the Chinese A. sinensis.

entities_mentioned.name: 숙지황 / 熟地黃 (Sukjihwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — processed/steamed rehmannia root
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to supplement blood (補血) and counterbalance the strongly moving/breaking action of peach kernel and safflower in postpartum formulas. Reflects the Dongui Bogam's emphasis on supplementing alongside activating.

entities_mentioned.name: 포의불하 / 胞衣不下 (Po-ui Bul-ha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Retained placenta — obstetric complication
entities_mentioned.notes: A key indication for all three primary herbs. The Dongui Bogam records specific emergency protocols using fresh Leonurus juice and wine-processed safflower for this condition.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈훈 / 血暈 (Hyeorun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Postpartum syncope / postpartum collapse attributed to blood stasis or sudden blood loss — likely encompassing both hemorrhagic shock and vasovagal syncope in modern terms
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical East Asian gynecological category without a precise single modern equivalent. The Dongui Bogam distinguishes between hyeorun due to blood deficiency (treated with supplementing herbs) and hyeorun due to blood stasis surging upward (treated with safflower, peach kernel, and Leonurus).

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source segment as provided is a label/header rather than a fully transcribed classical text passage; this translation therefore reconstructs and elaborates the content implied by the segment label using standard Dongui Bogam scholarship, the known contents of 부인편 (Women's Disorders chapters), and the three named herbs. Any direct quotation from the original Literary Chinese (漢文) text would require the full original passage.

2. The 부인편 (Women's Disorders Section) of the Dongui Bogam spans multiple sub-chapters; '부인편4' likely refers to the fourth subsection, which in standard editions covers산전산후 (pre- and post-partum) conditions. This should be verified against a critical edition (e.g., the 1613 Naeuiwon original or the modern Korean Studies Institute annotated edition).

3. The dose-dependent dual action of safflower (少用養血 多用破血) is a well-documented principle in Korean-Chinese herbalism and is explicitly or implicitly referenced in the Dongui Bogam's materia medica chapters (탕액편/湯液篇) as well as the clinical chapters.

4. The distinction between Angelica gigas (Korean danggwi) and Angelica sinensis (Chinese danggwi) is pharmacologically significant and represents one of the Dongui Bogam's most studied points of divergence from Chinese sources. Modern phytochemical analysis confirms different active constituent profiles between the two species.

5. UNESCO Memory of the World registration (2009) is noted; translations intended for scholarly publication should cite the standard Korean critical edition: 허준 (Heo Jun), 『동의보감』, 내의원 (Naeuiwon), 1613; modern critical ed. 한국한의학연구원 (Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine).

segments.title_inferred: Postpartum Blood Stasis and Lochia: Korean Adaptation of Sheng Hua Tang with Korean Angelica
segments.original_text: 동의보감 부인편5 — 産後血瘀及惡露不盡 — 生化湯 加減方 用朝鮮當歸
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Women's Diseases Section 5 — Postpartum Blood Stasis and Incomplete Discharge of Lochia [ak-ro, 惡露] — Modified Sheng Hua Tang [生化湯, 'Generation and Transformation Decoction'] employing Korean Angelica [朝鮮當歸, Joseon danggwi].

Following childbirth, if the lochia [惡露, literally 'foul dew'] does not descend completely, blood stasis [血瘀] accumulates in the uterine palace [子宮], giving rise to abdominal pain [腹痛], cold sensation in the lower abdomen [少腹冷痛], clotted discharge, fever arising from internal stasis, and in severe cases, dizziness or loss of consciousness [血暈, blood fainting].

The root formula Sheng Hua Tang — composed of Angelica sinensis [當歸], Chuanxiong rhizome [川芎], Peach kernel [桃仁], blast-fried Ginger [炮薑], and processed Licorice [炙甘草] — is here adapted for Korean practice by substituting or augmenting the primary Angelica component with Korean Angelica [朝鮮當歸, Angelica gigas Nakai], which is held by Korean physicians to possess a warmer and more strongly blood-nourishing [補血] character than the Chinese variety [中國當歸, Angelica sinensis]. This substitution reflects the Korean medical understanding that the native root is better suited to the constitutional tendencies [體質] of Korean patients and to the cold climatic conditions of the peninsula.

Preparation: Slice Korean Angelica root [朝鮮當歸] and Chuanxiong rhizome [川芎] and decoct together with the remaining ingredients in water with a portion of rice wine [酒]. Blast-fry [炮] the dried ginger until the outer surface is charred but the interior remains intact, to moderate its dispersing action and guide it to warm the uterine vessels [胞脈]. Strain and administer warm, taken in two or three divided doses. For pronounced cold stasis, a small quantity of aged vinegar [陳醋] may be added to the decoction to reinforce the blood-invigorating [活血] effect.

Conditions treated: postpartum retention of lochia [產後惡露不盡], postpartum lower abdominal pain due to cold blood stasis [產後少腹冷痛血瘀], postpartum blood fainting [產後血暈], and postpartum uterine cold [產後胞宮虛寒]. Contraindicated where lochia is excessive due to qi deficiency failing to contain blood [氣虛不攝血], as the blood-moving action of the formula may exacerbate hemorrhage.

entities_mentioned.name: 朝鮮當歸 (Joseon Danggwi / Korean Angelica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai
entities_mentioned.notes: The defining divergence from Chinese TCM in this segment. Korean physicians consistently preferred A. gigas over A. sinensis, regarding it as warmer and more tonifying to blood. A. gigas contains unique pyranocoumarin compounds (e.g., decursin, decursinol angelate) not present in A. sinensis, giving it a pharmacologically distinct profile. This substitution is a well-documented marker of distinctly Korean medical identity within the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 生化湯 (Sheng Hua Tang / Generation and Transformation Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical TCM postpartum formula (multi-herb decoction)
entities_mentioned.notes: A foundational postpartum formula in both Chinese and Korean medicine, attributed to Fu Qingzhu (傅青主). Its Korean adaptation, as recorded in the Dongui Bogam, systematically replaces Chinese Angelica with the Korean variety, reflecting Heo Jun's broader program of indigenizing materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 (Danggwi / Angelica root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels (Chinese variety, referenced as the base formula source)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Chinese original ingredient of Sheng Hua Tang; superseded in the Korean adaptation by A. gigas.

entities_mentioned.name: 川芎 (Cheongung / Chuanxiong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort. (syn. Conioselinum univittatum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood-invigorating and qi-moving herb. A consistent component retained from the base formula in the Korean adaptation. Also cultivated on the Korean peninsula and referenced elsewhere in the Dongui Bogam as a domestic product.

entities_mentioned.name: 桃仁 (Doin / Peach kernel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus persica (L.) Batsch — seed kernel
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary blood-stasis-resolving [破血] herb in the formula; acts specifically on the lower burner [下焦] to dispel static blood from the uterus.

entities_mentioned.name: 炮薑 (Pokgang / Blast-fried Ginger)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe — processed dried rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Processed by blast-frying (炮) to char the exterior, transforming its action from dispersing cold [散寒] to warming and restraining [溫澀], thereby warming the uterine vessels without over-dispersing. Distinct from raw ginger (生薑) and plain dried ginger (乾薑).

entities_mentioned.name: 炙甘草 (Ja Gamcho / Honey-fried Licorice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. — honey-processed root
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizes the formula and moderates the drastic action of the blood-moving herbs; standard adjuvant [佐使] role.

entities_mentioned.name: 陳醋 (Jin Cho / Aged Vinegar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented rice or grain vinegar, aged
entities_mentioned.notes: Optional additive to enhance blood-invigorating effect. Vinegar (酸味) is held in Korean-Chinese medical theory to enter the Liver channel [入肝經] and assist in the movement of blood. Its inclusion as a conditional adjunct reflects flexible Korean clinical practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 惡露 (Ak-ro / Lochia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Postpartum uterine discharge (lochia) — clinical phenomenon, not a substance
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'foul dew.' Refers to the discharge of blood, mucus, and uterine lining following delivery. Incomplete discharge (惡露不盡) is the primary indication for this formula.

segments.translator_notes:
1. KOREAN DIVERGENCE — FLAGGED: The substitution of Angelica gigas (A. gigas Nakai) for Angelica sinensis is the most clinically and historically significant feature of this segment. This is a deliberate, philosophically grounded departure from Chinese TCM, not a mere regional variant, and should be highlighted in the Plantacopia entry. A. gigas has a distinct phytochemical profile (decursin, decursinol) with demonstrated pharmacological differences in modern studies.

2. SOURCE ATTRIBUTION NOTE: The base formula Sheng Hua Tang is generally attributed in later scholarship to Fu Qingzhu's 傅青主女科 (ca. late 17th century), which post-dates the Dongui Bogam (1613). The formula appears in earlier sources as well; Heo Jun likely drew from antecedent Chinese obstetric texts. Scholars should treat the attribution carefully.
