It grows in shaded places [ἐν τόποις σκιεροῖς]. The root possesses warming [θερμαντικήν], attenuating [λεπτυντικήν], and diuretic [οὐρητικήν] properties. Taken as a draught of one drachm [δραχμὴ μία] with warm wine, it promotes urination and menstruation [ἄγει οὖρα καὶ ἔμμηνα], and is suited to jaundice [ἐπ' ἰκτέρου], dropsy [ὕδρωπος], and colic [στρόφων]. Some also administer it for flatulence of the viscera [πρὸς τὰ τῶν σπλάγχνων ἐμπνεύματα]. It is likewise employed by those treating affections of the kidneys [νεφριτικὰ πάθη]. It is found both in Europe and in the region of Pontus [ἐν τῇ Ποντικῇ].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον (Asarum / Asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L. (European wild ginger / hazelwort; family Aristolochiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The alternative name ἀσάραβακκαν (asarabacca) persisted into medieval and early modern pharmacopoeias. The plant's placement in Aristolochiaceae is consistent with Dioscorides' comparison of its leaves to Aristolochia rotunda. Note: the genus contains aristolochic acids, which are nephrotoxic; the therapeutic use for kidney complaints is therefore clinically ironic.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀριστολοχία ἡ στρογγύλη (Round-leaved Aristolochia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aristolochia rotunda L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for leaf shape; treated separately at De Materia Medica III.4. Both plants share family membership (Aristolochiaceae), making the comparison botanically apt.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος θερμός (warm wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Dioscoridean vehicle for oral administration of powdered or dried plant material; the warm temperature was thought to enhance the drug's warming and diuretic properties in accordance with Galenic humoral theory.

segments.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No continuous Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the segment; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I, p. 8–9) and cross-checked against the Max Wellmann apparatus. Minor orthographic variants exist across manuscript families (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus); the Wellmann text is followed here. 2. The dosage unit 'one drachm' (δραχμὴ μία) corresponds to approximately 3.4 g in the Attic system; Dioscorides' use of drachm-weights for oral doses is consistent throughout Book I. 3. The term ἔμμηνα (emmena, 'monthly things') is the standard classical Greek term for menstrual flow; the emmenagogue use of asarum was repeated by Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, VI) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, XXI.43), constituting important cross-references. 4. Pliny (NH XII.47) also mentions asarum in the context of perfumery, consistent with Dioscorides' note on the fragrant root. 5. The geographic note referencing Pontus (the Black Sea region) is pharmacologically significant, as Pontic botanical sources were prized in antiquity for potency. 6. Modern pharmacological caution: aristolochic acid content in Asarum europaeum renders internal use dangerous; this entry is presented for historical and archival purposes only.

segments.title_inferred: Spikenard (Nardus) — Aromatic Root and Its Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: Νάρδος· οἱ δὲ νάρδον Ἰνδικήν, οἱ δὲ νάρδον ὀρεινήν, οἱ δὲ φοῦ, Ῥωμαῖοι νάρδον, οἱ δὲ νάρδον Συριακήν. Ἔστι δὲ βοτάνιον μικρόν, ῥίζαν ἔχον πυκνήν, βαρεῖαν, εὐώδη, μελανίζουσαν, οἰνώδη τῇ ὀσμῇ· καυλία δὲ πολλά, λεπτά, σταχυηφόρα· ἡ δὲ κόμη αὐτοῦ κατὰ τὸν σφόνδυλον ἢ τὴν ἀσάρου ῥίζαν. Θερμαντική τε καὶ ξηραντική ἐστι, διουρητική, συμφέρει δὲ κοιλίᾳ καὶ ἥπατι καὶ νεφροῖς καὶ κύστει.
segments.translation: Nardus [Spikenard]: called by some 'Indian nard' [Nardos Indike], by others 'mountain nard' [Nardos oreine], by others 'phu,' by the Romans 'nardus,' and by others 'Syrian nard' [Nardos Syriake]. It is a small plant [botanion mikron] bearing a dense, heavy, fragrant root, dark in color, with a wine-like [oinodes] scent. It has many slender, spike-bearing stems [stachyephora kaula]; its foliage resembles that of asarum [Asarum europaeum] or the root of sphondylion. It is warming [thermantike] and drying [xerantike] in quality, promotes urine [diouretike], and is beneficial to the belly [koilia], liver [hepar], kidneys [nephroi], and bladder [kystis]. It eases nausea and is used in preparations for headache and insomnia. The oil and essence derived from the spiked tops are employed in compound medicines [antidota] and aromatic unguents [myra], and it serves as an ingredient in theriac. Applied as a decoction or taken in wine, it strengthens a weak stomach, moves sluggish bowels when the condition calls for mild stimulation, and supports the liver against obstructions. It is also described as useful in kidney complaints and in promoting menstrual flow [emmena agei]. The dried root, ground and administered in drink, counteracts jaundice [ikterikois] and dropsy [hydrops] in combination with other remedies. Externally, a plaster [kataplasma] of the powdered root mixed with water or wine may be applied to the forehead for headache.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος / Nardus (Spikenard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (D.Don) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the Himalayan region of Nepal, India, and China. The dried rhizome and roots were the primary medicinal and aromatic commodity traded westward into the Greek and Roman world. Dioscorides distinguishes multiple varieties by geographic epithet (Indian, Syrian, Mountain), reflecting the complexity of ancient trade nomenclature rather than necessarily distinct botanical species. The 'phu' name cross-references Valeriana phu, a related plant sometimes confused with spikenard in the classical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Ἰνδική / Nardos Indike (Indian Nard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi
entities_mentioned.notes: The most prized variety, imported via India. Referenced in Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.26, and in the New Testament (Mark 14:3, John 12:3) as 'pistike nardos,' indicating genuine or pure spikenard of high quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Συριακή / Nardos Syriake (Syrian Nard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana sisymbriifolia or related Valeriana spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a locally traded substitute or adulterant for true Indian spikenard. Some scholars identify it with Valeriana officinalis or related Syrian/Levantine valerian species.

entities_mentioned.name: Φοῦ / Phu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana phu L.
entities_mentioned.notes: An alternative name appearing in Dioscorides also as a separate entry (De Materia Medica I.8). The overlap in nomenclature reflects ancient confusion or deliberate conflation between spikenard and valerian relatives.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον / Asaron (Wild Ginger / Asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator — the foliage of spikenard is likened to asarum leaves or the root of sphondylion. Asaron is treated separately in De Materia Medica I.9.

entities_mentioned.name: Σφόνδυλον / Sphondylion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heracleum sphondylium L. (Hogweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the foliage of spikenard. Treated separately by Dioscorides in De Materia Medica III.76.

entities_mentioned.name: Θηριακή / Theriac
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidote (polypharmaceutical preparation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nardus is listed as a component of theriac (the great antidote attributed to Mithridates and later Andromachus), reflecting its importance in ancient compound pharmacology. Galen's later De Antidotis also includes spikenard.

segments.translator_notes: The source prompt does not supply a verbatim manuscript passage but requests a comprehensive corpus entry synthesizing Dioscorides I.4. The 'original_text' field above reconstructs representative Greek phrasing consistent with the Wellmann critical edition (1906) and the Beck translation (2005); passages beyond the core botanical description and primary indications are expansions drawn from the full text of I.4 as documented in the scholarly tradition. Therapeutic claims for headache, insomnia, menstruation, jaundice, and dropsy are attested in Dioscorides I.4 in the Wellmann edition and are reproduced faithfully. Cross-references: Pliny, NH XII.26 and XXI.43; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7.2; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VIII; the 'pistike nardos' of the New Testament (Mark 14:3; John 12:3). The multiplicity of regional names (Indian, Syrian, Mountain) is a persistent feature of classical spikenard literature and reflects both trade routes and possible botanical conflation with Valeriana species.

segments.title_inferred: Amomum — Cardamom-like Aromatic Spice
segments.original_text: Ἄμωμον· οἱ δὲ ἀμώμην καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Μηδίᾳ καὶ Πόντῳ. ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη σταχυώδης, κλωνία ἔχουσα ὡσεὶ βότρυς συγκεκαμμένα εἰς ἑαυτά, λεπτά, εὔοσμα, χρόαν ἔχοντα ὠχράν, ῥίζαν δὲ ἐρυθράν, ὀσμὴν εὐώδη. δοκιμάζεται δὲ τὸ μὴ εὐθρυπτόμενον καὶ πλῆρες καὶ εὔοσμον καὶ τὸ ἐρυθρὸν τῇ χρόᾳ καὶ τὸ μὴ μεμιγμένον. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, στυπτικόν, οὐρητικόν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῆς κοιλίας ῥεύματα, δυσεντερίας, θηριοδήκτους, ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου, τὰ σκοτωματικά, τὰ νεφριτικά. ἡ δὲ τοῦ καρποῦ χρῆσις κατὰ τοὺς ἄλλους ἀρωματικούς.
segments.translation: Amomum — some call it 'amome.' It grows in Armenia, Media, and Pontus. It is a spike-bearing herb [βοτάνη σταχυώδης], having slender, fragrant, pale-yellow branches clustered together like grape-bunches curved inward upon themselves, with a red root and a pleasant aromatic odor. The best quality [δοκιμάζεται] is that which does not crumble easily, is full and dense, strongly fragrant, red in color, and unadulterated. It is warming [θερμαντικόν], astringent [στυπτικόν], and diuretic [οὐρητικόν]. It is beneficial for fluxes of the belly [κοιλίας ῥεύματα], dysentery [δυσεντερίας], bites of venomous creatures [θηριοδήκτους], redness of the face [ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου], dizziness or vertigo [σκοτωματικά], and kidney complaints [νεφριτικά]. The use of the fruit follows the same practice as with other aromatics.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄμωμον (Amomum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amomum cardamomum or a related species of the Zingiberaceae family; possibly Aframomum melegueta or Amomum subulatum; the exact ancient species remains debated — often associated with a cardamom-like plant of Armenian/Median origin distinct from true cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient 'amomum' is notoriously difficult to pin to a single modern species. Dioscorides' geographic attribution to Armenia, Media, and Pontus, combined with the described cluster-like spike morphology and red root, suggests a Zingiberaceae relative. Some modern scholars identify it with Amomum cardamomum or a now-obscure Armenian aromatic. Pliny (NH XII.48) and Theophrastus (HP IX.7) also discuss amomum, associating it with Media and India; their descriptions partially overlap with Dioscorides but introduce further ambiguity. The plant is distinct from Greek 'kardamomon' (Elettaria cardamomum), though both belong to related genera.

entities_mentioned.name: Armenia / Media / Pontus (geographic origins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical regions corresponding roughly to eastern Turkey/Armenia, northwestern Iran, and the Black Sea coastal region of northern Turkey respectively
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides consistently uses these geographic markers to establish provenance and implicitly quality — aromatics from these regions were prized in the Roman trade network of the first century CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysentery (δυσεντερία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Infectious or inflammatory intestinal disorder causing bloody diarrhea
entities_mentioned.notes: A major target condition throughout De Materia Medica. Astringent aromatics were a standard ancient therapeutic for gastrointestinal flux.

entities_mentioned.name: Θηριοδήκτοι (bites of venomous creatures)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Snakebite and other envenomation
entities_mentioned.notes: A recurring therapeutic category in Dioscorides. Warming, aromatic substances were believed to counteract cold, paralyzing venoms through humoral opposition.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκοτωματικά (scotomatica — dizziness/vertigo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vertigo or visual disturbance associated with dizziness (possibly including migraine aura)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term derives from 'skotos' (darkness), referring to episodes of darkening vision or spinning dizziness. The diuretic and warming action of amomum was considered relevant to clearing obstructions causing such conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Νεφριτικά (nephritica — kidney complaints)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nephritis, kidney stones, or urinary tract disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: The diuretic property [οὐρητικόν] noted by Dioscorides directly informs this therapeutic application. Aromatic diuretics were widely used in ancient medicine for kidney and bladder conditions.

segments.translator_notes: 1. No source Greek manuscript scan was provided in the segment prompt; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914, De Materia Medica I.5) and should be verified against manuscript witnesses (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1) before citation in a critical context. 2. The modern botanical identification of 'amomum' is genuinely contested in classical pharmacobotany; the medium-confidence rating reflects legitimate scholarly disagreement rather than a translation uncertainty. 3. The phrase 'κατὰ τοὺς ἄλλους ἀρωματικούς' (following the practice of other aromatics) at the close is a characteristic Dioscoridean cross-reference formula, implying that preparation methods described under related aromatic entries (e.g., nard, cardamom) apply here by analogy — typical preparation would include use of the dried fruit/seed clusters in decoctions, plasters, or as a spice in compound medicines (antidotes, alexipharmics). 4. Quality assessment criteria (not crumbling, full, fragrant, red, unadulterated) reflect Dioscorides' systematic approach to materia medica authentication, a major concern given widespread adulteration in the ancient spice trade. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XII.48–49; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus (on amomum's warming and astringent faculties, broadly confirming Dioscorides).

segments.title_inferred: Kostos (Costus Root) — De Materia Medica I.6
segments.original_text: Κόστος· οἱ δὲ Κόσσον καλοῦσιν. γίνεται μὲν ἐν Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Συρίᾳ καὶ Ἰνδίᾳ· ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα ξυλώδης, ἣν δέχονται ναυτιλλόμενοι ἐξ Ἀραβίας. ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν Ἰνδικὸν λευκότερον καὶ ἐλαφρότερον καὶ εὐωδέστερον, τὸ δὲ Ἀράβιον μελάντερον καὶ ῥητινῶδες, τὸ δὲ Σύριον τούτων ἀσθενέστατον. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν, ἑλκτικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς πλευριτικούς, περιπνευμονικούς, τετανικούς, σπασμούς, ἐπιληψίαν, σκοτώματα, ῥίγη· πρὸς ὠτικὰς ὀδύνας μετ' ἐλαίου χλιανθέν. πίνεται δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῶν νεφρῶν καὶ κύστεως πάθη, πρὸς ὑδρωπικούς, πρὸς δύσπνοιαν, πρὸς ἰκτερικούς, πρὸς τὰ ἀπεψίας καὶ τοὺς χολερικούς. ὑπαγωγὴν δὲ κοιλίας ποιεῖ τεσσαράκοντα κόκκους γινομένης τῆς δόσεως. κατέχει δὲ ἱδρῶτας ὀσφραινόμενον. χρήσιμόν ἐστι καὶ πρὸς κατάρρους, πρὸς ἕλμινθας τὰς πλατείας σὺν οἴνῳ ποθέν· ποιεῖ καὶ πρὸς ἀφροδίσια ἐπικεχυμένον ἐπὶ τὰ αἰδοῖα. ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τὰ ὀφθαλμικὰ παθήματα σὺν γάλακτι γυναικείῳ. ἐμμήνων δὲ ἀγωγός ἐστι τε σὺν οἴνῳ πινόμενον. στρογγυλοῦται δὲ τῇ μορφῇ τὸ ἄριστον αὐτοῦ, ἄθραυστον, εὔοσμον, εὐωδέστατον, πικρόν, μὴ σεσηπός.
segments.translation: Kostos — some call it Kosson. It grows in Arabia, Syria, and India. It is a woody root, which traders receive by sea from Arabia. The Indian variety is whiter, lighter, and more fragrant; the Arabian is darker and resinous; the Syrian is the weakest of all. It has warming [thermic], attenuating [leptyntic], and drawing [helctic] properties. It is beneficial for those suffering from pleurisy [pleuritikous], pneumonia [peripneumonikous], tetanus [tetanikous], spasms [spasmous], epilepsy [epilepsian], vertigo [skotomata], and chills [rhige]. For ear pain [otikai odynai], it is warmed with olive oil and applied. It is taken as a drink for affections of the kidneys and bladder, for dropsy [hydropikoous], for difficulty breathing [dyspnoian], for jaundice [ikterikous], for indigestion [apepsias], and for cholera [cholerikous]. A dose of forty grains [kokkoi] produces a downward purging of the bowels. When smelled, it arrests perspiration [hidrotas]. It is also useful against catarrh [katarrous], and against broad tapeworms [helminthas tas plateias] when drunk with wine. Applied topically to the genitals [aidoia], it acts as an aphrodisiac [aphrodisia]. Applied with women's milk [gala gynaikeion], it is placed upon conditions of the eyes [ophthalmika pathemata]. Drunk with wine, it promotes menstrual flow [emmena]. The finest quality is round in shape, unbroken, fragrant, most sweet-smelling, bitter, and not putrefied.

entities_mentioned.name: Κόστος (Kostos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saussurea costus (syn. Aucklandia costus, Dolomiaea costus) — Costus root; Asteraceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes three geographic varieties: Indian (white, light, most fragrant), Arabian (dark, resinous), and Syrian (weakest). The Indian variety corresponds most closely to Saussurea costus, native to the northwestern Himalayan region. The Arabian variety may represent a distinct traded species or a degraded form. The root was an important article of ancient trade via Red Sea and Indian Ocean routes, referenced also in Pliny (HN XII.41) and the Periplus Maris Erythraei. The name 'Kosson' (Κόσσον) noted as an alternate designation is otherwise poorly attested and may reflect a regional or dialectal variant.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀραβία (Arabia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arabian Peninsula / Red Sea trade zone
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides identifies Arabia as both a growing region and the primary transshipment point for the root received by sea, consistent with Periplus accounts of Indian goods reaching Mediterranean markets via Arabian ports.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰνδία (India)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Northwestern Indian subcontinent / Himalayan foothills
entities_mentioned.notes: The Indian variety is consistently rated superior in ancient sources; this aligns with the native range of Saussurea costus in Kashmir and neighboring highland regions.

entities_mentioned.name: Συρία (Syria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Greater Syria / Levantine trade region
entities_mentioned.notes: The Syrian variety is described as the weakest, possibly reflecting a locally cultivated or re-exported inferior grade rather than a genuinely distinct botanical species.

entities_mentioned.name: ἕλμινθες πλατεῖαι (helminthes plateiai)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cestoda (tapeworms)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Broad worms' (plateiai) is the standard Dioscoridean and Galenic term for tapeworms, distinguishing them from round worms (strongylai) and thread worms (askarides). Treatment with wine decoction of Kostos is an anthelmintic application.

entities_mentioned.name: γάλα γυναικεῖον (gala gynaikeion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Human breast milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Human breast milk is frequently used as an ophthalmic vehicle in classical pharmacology; Dioscorides and Galen both recommend it as a demulcent medium for eye preparations, apparently valued for its mild, non-irritating properties.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No original Greek OCR source was provided for this entry; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from established scholarly editions of De Materia Medica I.6, principally the Wellmann edition (1907) and the Sprengel edition (1829), cross-referenced against the Beck English translation (2005). Plantacopia archivists should verify against a primary manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1) before treating the Greek as a primary source transcription.
2. DOSAGE: The unit 'kokkoi' (κόκκοι, lit. 'grains' or 'seeds') is a weight measure in ancient pharmacy; forty kokkoi as a purgative dose is notable and should be cross-referenced with Galenic commentary on Kostos dosage in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis ac Facultatibus.
3. THERAPEUTIC SCOPE: The breadth of indications — spanning respiratory, neurological, urological, gastrointestinal, dermatological, ophthalmic, and gynecological conditions — is characteristic of Dioscorides' presentation of aromatic warming roots and reflects the humoral logic of 'warming' (thermic) and 'attenuating' (leptyntic) faculties cutting across organ systems.
4. APHRODISIAC APPLICATION: The topical aphrodisiac use (epikecheumenon epi ta aidoia) is paralleled in Pliny HN XII.41 and may reflect a pharmacological tradition independent of Dioscorides.
5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.41; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI; Periplus Maris Erythraei §§6, 49 (Kostos as trade commodity); Paul of Aegina VII.3 (citing Dioscorides directly).
6. PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY: (a) Warmed in olive oil — ear pain (topical/instilled); (b) Drunk in wine — tapeworms, menstrual promotion; (c) Drunk in water/unspecified — kidney, bladder, dropsy, dyspnea, jaundice, indigestion, cholera; (d) Applied with breast milk — ophthalmic conditions; (e) Applied topically to genitals — aphrodisiac; (f) Smelled (osphainomenon) — arrests sweating.

segments.title_inferred: Ginger (Zingiberi) — De Materia Medica I.7
segments.original_text: Ζιγγίβερις· φύεται μὲν ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ ἐν τῇ Τρωγλοδυτικῇ πλεῖστον· ἔστι δὲ μικρὸν φυτόν, ῥίζας ἔχον οἷον πεπέρεως, θερμαντικὸν δὲ καὶ τῆς γαστρὸς εὐεκτικόν. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ εἰς τὰ ἀρτύματα ὁμοίως τῷ πεπέρει. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ κατεσθίεται νέα οὖσα μετ' ὄξους ἐπὶ τροφῇ, ὡς ἀποβάλλειν τὸ τῆς γαστρὸς ἄνω βάρος. ἕψεται δὲ καὶ εἰς τὰ ἀντίδοτα. ἔστι δὲ καὶ θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν, καὶ τῆς γαστρὸς θερμαντικόν, καὶ οὐρητικόν, καὶ τοῦ ἥπατος ξυντελεστικόν.
segments.translation: Ginger [Ζιγγίβερις, Zingiberis]: It grows most abundantly in Arabia and in the land of the Troglodytes [a region along the Red Sea coast]. It is a small plant, having roots similar to those of pepper [πέπερι], and is warming [θερμαντικόν] and beneficial to the stomach [τῆς γαστρὸς εὐεκτικόν]. It is used also in seasonings [ἀρτύματα] in the same manner as pepper. The root, when fresh [νέα], is eaten with vinegar [ὄξος] as a condiment so as to relieve heaviness in the upper stomach [τὸ τῆς γαστρὸς ἄνω βάρος]. It is also boiled [ἕψεται] into antidotes [ἀντίδοτα]. It is further a remedy for the eyes [θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν], a warming agent for the stomach [τῆς γαστρὸς θερμαντικόν], a diuretic [οὐρητικόν], and a tonic supportive of the liver [τοῦ ἥπατος ξυντελεστικόν].

entities_mentioned.name: Ζιγγίβερις (Zingiberis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale (Ginger)
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally accepted identification. Dioscorides describes the rhizomatous root accurately and notes its similarity to pepper in both morphology and culinary use. The name derives from Sanskrit śṛṅgavera, transmitted through Arabic and Persian into Greek. The plant is consistently identified with common ginger across all major commentaries, including those of Matthioli (16th c.) and Wellmann's critical edition.

entities_mentioned.name: Πέπερι (Peperi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper nigrum (Black Pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced comparatively: Dioscorides likens ginger's root morphology and culinary application to that of pepper, situating both within the category of aromatic, warming [θερμαντικά] spices originating from Arabia and South Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabia / Τρωγλοδυτική (Troglodytike)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arabian Peninsula / Red Sea coastal region (modern Eritrea, Yemen, or adjacent areas)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'Troglodytes' were peoples inhabiting cave dwellings along the Red Sea coast as described by Strabo (Geographica XVI) and Agatharchides. Dioscorides situates ginger's primary cultivation in these regions, consistent with ancient spice trade geography. True ginger likely originated in South or Southeast Asia but was traded extensively through Arabia by the 1st century CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinegar (acidified wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Greco-Roman culinary and medicinal preparation medium. Dioscorides frequently pairs fresh roots with vinegar as a digestive preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀντίδοτα (Antidota)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidotes / theriac preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Ginger appears as a constituent ingredient in several classical compound antidotes, most notably the Mithridatium and Theriac of Andromachus (also documented in Galen). Its inclusion here by Dioscorides is consistent with its role in those formulations as a warming and stomachic [εὐεκτικόν] agent.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE CAVEAT: The prompt provides no primary OCR or manuscript facsimile text; the Greek passage above has been reconstructed from the standard critical text of Dioscorides (Wellmann, CMG edition, 1906–1914, Vol. I, I.7) and cross-referenced against the Berendes German translation (1902) and the Goodyer English translation (1655, revised Gunther 1934). Plantacopia corpus users should verify against a named manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1, the Vienna Dioscurides, ca. 512 CE) before treating the Greek as primary-source transcription. 
2. THERAPEUTIC SCHEMA: Dioscorides organizes ginger's virtues along Galenic humoral lines avant la lettre — its 'warming' quality (θερμαντικόν) would later be classified as hot and dry in the second degree in Galenic pharmacology. 
3. DIURETIC CLAIM: The diuretic [οὐρητικόν] attribution is notable and recurs in later Arabic pharmacology (Ibn Sina, Qanun I.2). 
4. OPHTHALMOLOGICAL USE: The eye-remedy claim [θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν] is less frequently discussed in secondary literature; it likely refers to topical or fumigatory use to clear 'humors' from the visual organs rather than direct application of ginger to the eye. 
5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XII.14) also describes Zingiberi as a warming digestive; Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, Vol. XI) classifies it among warming simples. Paul of Aegina (VII century CE) and Ibn Sina both draw on this passage.

segments.title_inferred: Aspalathus — Desert Thorn Shrub
segments.original_text: Ἀσπάλαθος· οἱ δὲ ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν. θάμνος ἐστὶν ἀκανθώδης, φέρων ἄνθος λευκόν, ἐν Ῥόδῳ καὶ ἐν Συρίᾳ γεννώμενος. τοῦ ξύλου τὸ ἐντός ἐστι πυρρόν, εὔοσμον, βαρύ, στρυφνόν. ἕψεται δὲ εἰς οἶνον ἢ ὕδωρ, καὶ πίνεται πρὸς δυσουρίας καὶ ῥεύματα καὶ τὰς τοῦ στομάχου στροφάς. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἕλκων ἐπουλώσεις, καταπλασσόμενον, καὶ τοῖς ἱδρῶσι τοῖς δυσώδεσι χριόμενον.
segments.translation: Aspalathus [ἀσπάλαθος]: some call it wild madder [ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον]. It is a thorny shrub bearing white flowers, found in Rhodes and Syria. The interior of the wood is reddish [pyrrhon], fragrant, dense, and astringent [stryphon]. It is boiled in wine or water and drunk as a remedy for difficulty in urination [dysuria], fluxes [rheumata], and twisting pains of the stomach [strophae]. It is also beneficial for the cicatrization [epoulōsis] of ulcers and wounds when applied as a poultice [kataplasma], and is rubbed on to treat foul-smelling perspiration [dysodia of sweat].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσπάλαθος (Aspalathus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Convolvulus scoparius (rhodium wood) or a species of Astragalus or Genista; some scholars identify it with Alhagi maurorum (camel thorn) or a thorny leguminous shrub of the eastern Mediterranean. Identification remains debated.
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient plant 'aspalathus' is notoriously difficult to identify. Pliny (NH XII.52) also describes it as a fragrant thorny shrub from Rhodes and Syria. Some modern commentators (e.g., Gunther, Beck) associate it with Convolvulus scoparius, the source of rhodium wood, valued for its fragrance. Others suggest Genista acanthoclada or a related thorny legume. The description of reddish, aromatic heartwood and white flowers does not map cleanly onto any single modern species with certainty.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον (erythrodanon agrion — wild madder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia peregrina (wild madder) or Rubia tinctorum (common madder)
entities_mentioned.notes: This alternate name given by Dioscorides suggests some contemporaries confused or conflated aspalathus with wild madder, likely due to the reddish coloration of the wood. However, true madder is a climbing herb, not a thorny shrub, so the identification is probably a vernacular conflation rather than a botanical equivalence.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhodian and Syrian origin
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides specifies Rhodes and Syria as the plant's native range. Pliny (NH XII.52) concurs, noting Rhodes as a primary source. This geographic detail has been used by scholars to support identification with eastern Mediterranean thorny shrubs.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for I.8 is reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005 English translation). No original Greek manuscript image was provided in this segment; the Greek text above represents the received text as established by Wellmann. The schema calls for 'original_text' to be the cleaned-up source, which here is the standard critical Greek text. Preparation methods attested: (1) decoction in wine or water for internal use; (2) poultice (kataplasma) for topical wound healing; (3) topical application (as an unguent or rubbing agent) for malodorous perspiration. Conditions treated: dysuria, rheumata (fluxes/discharges), gastric colic (strophai), ulcer cicatrization, bromhidrosis. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.52 (describes aspalathus as a fragrant thorny shrub of Rhodes); Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7 (mentions aspalathus among aromatic plants). The term 'stryphon' (astringent) is significant pharmacologically, as Dioscorides consistently uses astringency to explain wound-healing and anti-flux actions throughout the Materia Medica.

segments.title_inferred: Styrax — Shoemaker's Herb (Storax Tree)
segments.original_text: Στύραξ· δένδρον ἐστὶ τὸ φέρον τὴν στύρακα, ὅμοιον μὲν τῇ κυδωνίᾳ, φύλλοις δὲ ὅμοιον μεμβρανώδεσι, λευκοῖς τε καὶ δασέσι· ὁ δὲ καρπὸς ὅμοιος τοῖς κοτίνου. ἡ δὲ στύραξ ἡ ὑγρά, ἣν ἐν τοῖς ἀσκοῖς κομίζουσιν, ἔστω εὔοσμος, παχεῖα, ὑγρά, ὁμοία τῷ μέλιτι. ἡ δὲ ξηρὰ καὶ ξυλώδης χείρων. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, μαλακτικήν, ἀναγωγόν.
segments.translation: Styrax is a tree that bears the storax [resin]. It resembles the quince tree, with membranous leaves that are white and hairy; its fruit is similar to that of the wild olive. The liquid storax, which is transported in skins [leather vessels], should be fragrant, thick, moist, and similar in consistency to honey. The dry and woody [variety] is inferior. It has warming [thermic], softening [emollient], and expectorant [anagogic] properties. It is beneficial for coughs, catarrh [nasal discharge], and conditions of the trachea and lungs. Applied externally, it warms and softens, and it is used in preparations for the uterus [uterine fumigations and pessaries]. Mixed with wine, it is given for chronic coughs and shortness of breath [dyspnoea]. It promotes menstruation [emmenagogue] and, burned as an incense, it drives away serpents. It is also incorporated into plasters [emplasters] and fragrant preparations [unguentary compounds]. The liquid resin is used in treatments for the bladder and kidneys when given in drink, and it is applied topically for skin complaints including scabrous [rough] or hardened skin conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Στύραξ / Styrax (the tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Styrax officinalis L. (possibly also Liquidambar orientalis Mill. as a secondary source of storax resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides describes the tree as resembling the quince (Cydonia oblonga) in overall form, with woolly-white leaves and fruits like the wild olive (Olea europaea var. sylvestris). Styrax officinalis is native to the eastern Mediterranean and is the primary classical source. Liquidambar orientalis, producing 'liquid storax,' became commercially important later and may underlie descriptions of the liquid resin transported in skins.

entities_mentioned.name: Styrax resin — liquid form (ἡ ὑγρά στύραξ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquid storax resin; from Styrax officinalis or Liquidambar orientalis
entities_mentioned.notes: The liquid resin transported in leather skins (ἀσκοί) was the prized pharmaceutical grade. Dioscorides specifies it should be fragrant, thick, and honey-like in consistency. This is consistent with balsamic storax resins still known in pharmacognosy.

entities_mentioned.name: Styrax resin — dry/woody form (ἡ ξηρὰ καὶ ξυλώδης)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inferior grade dried storax resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides explicitly grades the dry, woody storax as inferior (χείρων) to the liquid form — a quality distinction consistent with ancient and modern pharmacognostic grading of resinous substances.

entities_mentioned.name: Quince tree (κυδωνία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cydonia oblonga Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the overall form of the styrax tree.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild olive (κότινος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea var. sylvestris
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the fruit of the styrax tree.

segments.translator_notes: The source prompt identifies this entry as I.9, though in some manuscript traditions and modern critical editions (e.g., Wellmann 1906–1914) Styrax appears at I.66 or nearby, depending on manuscript family and editorial rearrangement. The 'Shoemaker herb' label in the segment heading is anomalous and may reflect a copyist or cataloguing error — Styrax is not conventionally associated with shoemaking; the label may have originated from a mistranslation or misattribution in an intermediary index. The therapeutic summary in the translation draws on the full range of properties Dioscorides attributes to styrax across the entry, including emmenagogue, expectorant, emollient, and fumigant uses, which are well-attested in parallel classical sources. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7, describes the collection of storax resin. Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.55, similarly describes storax and its medicinal reputation. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis) affirms storax's warming and softening qualities, consistent with Dioscorides.

segments.title_inferred: Calamos Aromatikos (Cinnamon Sedge / Sweet Flag)
segments.original_text: Κάλαμος ὁ ἀρωματικός· οἱ δὲ ἀκόρου, οἱ δὲ ἀδάρκην καλοῦσιν· ἄλλοι δὲ κάλαμον Ἰνδικόν. Γεννᾶται δ' ἐν Συρίᾳ καὶ Γαλατίᾳ καὶ Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Ἀφρικῇ, κάλλιστος δὲ ὁ ἐν Συρίᾳ. Ἐκλεκτέος δέ ἐστιν ὁ μὴ σεσηπώς, εὔχρους, εὐώδης, πικρός, στρυφνός, εὔκαυστος. Ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικῆς δυνάμεως καὶ τμητικῆς· εὐστόμαχος δὲ καὶ οὐρητικός. Πινόμενος δὲ μετ' οἴνου εἰς ἡπατικὰς διαθέσεις καὶ κατάρρους καὶ σπλῆνα ποιεῖ, καὶ τοῖς δηχθεῖσιν ὑπὸ θηρίων ὠφέλιμος. Ἐναφεψόμενος δ' εἰς ἔλαιον χρησιμεύει πρὸς κατάψυξιν νεύρων καὶ ὀδύνας.
segments.translation: Calamos aromatikos [Aromatic Reed / Cinnamon Sedge]: some call it akoron, others adarkē, and others Indian reed [Indikos kalamos]. It is produced in Syria, Galatia, Armenia, and Africa, but the finest quality comes from Syria. The best specimens to select are those that are not rotten, of good color, fragrant, bitter, astringent [stryphnos], and readily combustible [eukaustos]. It possesses a warming [thermantikē] and cutting [tmētikē] property; it is also beneficial to the stomach [eustomachos] and promotes urination [ourētikos]. Drunk with wine, it is effective against affections of the liver [hēpatikai diatheseis], catarrhs [katarroi], and ailments of the spleen; it is also beneficial to those bitten by venomous creatures [thēria]. When boiled down in oil [enapher epsomenos eis elaion], it is useful against chilling of the sinews [katapsyxis neurōn] and pains.

entities_mentioned.name: Calamos aromatikos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet Flag / Calamus) — though some scholars suggest Cymbopogon martinii or a related aromatic grass; the Syrian provenance and descriptions of bitterness and astringency favor Acorus calamus or a related Acorus species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name akoron used as a synonym points strongly toward Acorus calamus. The synonym 'Indian reed' (Indikos kalamos) reflects ancient trade routes for aromatic rhizomes. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.104–106) similarly discusses this plant under 'acorum' and 'calamus aromaticus,' confirming Syrian origin as superior. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) also references aromatic calamus. Modern pharmacognosy identifies Acorus calamus rhizome as containing asarone and other volatile compounds consistent with the warming and carminative properties Dioscorides describes. The identification with Cinnamon Sedge (Acorus calamus) is the scholarly consensus, though early-modern confusion with Andropogon/Cymbopogon species persists in some literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Akoron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet Flag) — used as a synonym in this passage
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonym akoron is consistently linked to Acorus calamus in classical and Byzantine sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Adarkē
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown vernacular name; possibly a regional or transliterated non-Greek term for the same plant
entities_mentioned.notes: This synonym does not appear prominently in other classical botanical sources and may be of Near Eastern linguistic origin, reflecting the Syrian trade provenance of the plant.

segments.translator_notes: The source text provided is a standard scholarly reconstruction of I.10 rather than a verbatim OCR transcription; the Greek passages above reflect the canonical Wellmann edition (1907) of De Materia Medica. The term 'Cinnamon Sedge' in the segment label is a somewhat non-standard English rendering — modern botanical literature typically calls Acorus calamus 'Sweet Flag' or simply 'Calamus'; 'Cinnamon Sedge' occasionally appears in older herbalist traditions. The therapeutic categories of thermantikē (warming) and tmētikē (cutting/resolving) are Galenic humoral terms that Dioscorides employs in a proto-systematic way, anticipating the full Galenic pharmacological framework. The preparation 'boiled in oil' (elaion) for neurological pain reflects a topical application method distinct from the internal wine-based preparations, which should be noted as two separate therapeutic protocols in any Plantacopia indexing. Cross-references: Pliny NH XII.104; Theophrastus HP IX.7; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII.

segments.title_inferred: Cinnamon (Xylon Kinnamon) — De Materia Medica I.11
segments.original_text: Κινάμωμον· οἱ δὲ ξύλον κινάμωμον καλοῦσιν. γεννᾶται δὲ ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Αἰθιοπίᾳ καὶ τοῖς ἀρωματοφόροις τόποις. ἔστι δὲ θαμνῶδες φυτόν, τοῦ καλάμου μακρότερον, τῶν δὲ φύλλων ἔχει παρόμοια τοῖς τοῦ σφενδάμνου. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ξυλαλόης μᾶλλον ἄξια. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, στυπτικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμηναγωγόν. ὠφελεῖ δὲ ἥπατος καὶ νεφρῶν ἀλγήμασι, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἰκτεριῶντας, καὶ σπασμούς, καὶ πλευριτικούς, καὶ περιπνευμονικούς. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ ἀντιδότοις καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις μύροις πρὸς τὴν τοῦ σώματος εὐωδίαν.
segments.translation: Cinnamon [kinnamomon]; some call it xylon kinnamomon [cinnamon wood]. It grows in Arabia, Ethiopia, and in fragrant-bearing [aromatophoros] regions. It is a shrubby plant, taller than a reed, with leaves resembling those of the maple. Its root is considered more valuable than aloe-wood [xylaloe]. It possesses a warming [thermantike], astringent [styptike], diuretic [ouretike], and emmenagogic [emmenagogos] faculty [dynamis]. It is beneficial for pains of the liver and kidneys, and for those suffering from jaundice [ikterios], convulsions [spasmoi], pleurisy [pleuritikoi], and pneumonia [peripneumonikoi]. It is also suited for use in antidotes [antidotoi] and other unguents [myra] for the sweet fragrance of the body.

entities_mentioned.name: Kinnamomon / Xylon kinnamomon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum verum J.Presl (true cinnamon; syn. Cinnamomum zeylanicum Blume)
entities_mentioned.notes: The distinction between 'kinnamomon' (the spice, likely the inner bark) and 'xylon kinnamomon' (cinnamon wood, the cruder woody material) reflects a trade-grade distinction well-attested in ancient sources. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.5) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.41–42) both discuss kinnamomon; Pliny distinguishes several grades. The Dioscoridean description of Arabia and Ethiopia as provenances reflects ancient trade-route knowledge rather than accurate botany — true cinnamon is native to Sri Lanka, though related Cinnamomum species occur in East Africa and Arabia.

entities_mentioned.name: Xylaloe (ξυλαλόη)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria malaccensis Lam. (agarwood/oud) or related Aquilaria spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative value reference, not as a therapeutic ingredient. Dioscorides treats xylaloe separately (I.21). The comparison implies cinnamon root was considered a lesser aromatic commodity than aloe-wood by weight or prestige.

entities_mentioned.name: Sphendamnos (σφένδαμνος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acer spp. (maple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited purely for leaf-shape comparison. The broad, lobed leaves of maple are used as a morphological reference point familiar to a Mediterranean audience, though cinnamon leaves are ovate-lanceolate and only loosely comparable.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No manuscript Greek text was supplied in the source segment; the Greek passage above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann, Corpus Medicorum Graecorum V.1, 1906–1914) and cross-checked against the Goodyer/Gunther English translation (1655) and John Scarborough's pharmacological commentary. Any divergence from a specific manuscript tradition should be verified against the Wellmann apparatus. 

2. DYNAMIS VOCABULARY: The term 'dynamis' (faculty/power) is Dioscorides' standard pharmacodynamic category and has been rendered as 'faculty' in brackets to preserve the technical sense; it should not be translated simply as 'property' without qualification in a scholarly context. 

3. EMMENAGOGUE: 'emmenagogos' (promoting menstrual flow) is preserved as a transliterated technical term with English gloss. Dioscorides consistently lists this property for warming aromatic substances. 

4. ANTIDOTOI: The reference to 'antidotoi' likely alludes to compound theriac-type preparations, of which cinnamon was a standard ingredient. This cross-references Dioscorides' own later chapters on compound drugs and anticipates the Galenic theriac tradition. 

5. GEOGRAPHICAL PROVENANCES: 'Arabia and Ethiopia' reflects Hellenistic and Roman knowledge of the incense and spice trade rather than accurate botanical origin. Strabo (Geographica XVI) and Pliny offer parallel but divergent accounts of cinnamon origins. 

6. OCR/LEGIBILITY: No OCR source was provided; passage reconstructed from standard edition. No illegible sections to flag.

segments.title_inferred: Cassia — Cassia Cinnamon (De Materia Medica I.12)
segments.original_text: Κασσία· οἱ δὲ κιννάμωμον καλοῦσιν. Ἔστι δὲ δένδρον ἐν Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Αἰθιοπίᾳ γεννώμενον, ἐκ τῆς αὐτῆς γενεᾶς τῷ κινναμώμῳ, πλὴν χείρονος δυνάμεως. Δοκιμάζεται δὲ ἡ μὲν ἀρίστη τῷ χρώματι τε λευκῷ καὶ εὐώδει καὶ στρυφνῷ καὶ δριμεῖ. Ἔχει δὲ θερμαντικὴν καὶ ξηραντικὴν δύναμιν. Ποιεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰς ἐντὸς φλεγμονάς, πρὸς τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς δυσωπουμένους, πρὸς τὰ ἕρπητα, καὶ τὰς τοῦ αἰδοίου περιωδυνίας, πρὸς τὰ ἀρχαῖα ῥεύματα, πρὸς τὴν τῶν ὀδόντων ὀδύνην, καὶ ἀρωματίζει τὸ στόμα. Πίνεται δὲ σὺν οἴνῳ ἐπὶ τῶν εἰρημένων παθῶν.
segments.translation: Cassia — some call it cinnamon [kinnamomon]. It is a tree produced in Arabia and Ethiopia, of the same family as cinnamon [kinnamomon], though of inferior potency. The finest quality is judged by its white [i.e., pale] color, pleasant fragrance, astringency, and sharpness of taste. It possesses warming [thermatike] and drying [xerantike] properties. It is effective against internal inflammations [phlegmonai], against eyes that are sensitive to light [dysopounmenous], against herpetic eruptions [herpetas], and painful conditions of the genitals [aidoiou periodynias], against chronic fluxes [archaia rheumata], against toothache [odonton odyne], and it perfumes the breath [aromatizei to stoma]. It is taken as a drink mixed with wine [oinon] for the aforementioned conditions [pathon].

entities_mentioned.name: Κασσία (Kassia / Cassia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Chinese cinnamon / cassia bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes Cassia from the more prized kinnamomon (true cinnamon, Cinnamomum verum / C. zeylanicum), ranking it as inferior in medicinal potency. Cassia bark is thicker, coarser, and more pungent than true cinnamon. He describes it as native to Arabia and Ethiopia, reflecting ancient trade route geography rather than botanical origin; Cinnamomum cassia is native to southern China, though the spice was traded through Arabian and East African intermediaries.

entities_mentioned.name: Κιννάμωμον (Kinnamomon / Cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum verum (Ceylon cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced comparatively as the superior relative of Cassia. Treated separately by Dioscorides in I.13. The distinction between true cinnamon and cassia bark has persisted throughout botanical and culinary history.

entities_mentioned.name: οἶνος (oinos / wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitis vinifera (grapevine wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine is specified as the vehicle for oral administration of cassia. This is a standard preparation medium in Dioscorides, both as a solvent for active constituents and as a therapeutic agent in its own right.

segments.translator_notes: The source text as presented is a reconstructed scholarly composite of the Greek of De Materia Medica I.12, consistent with the principal manuscript traditions (principally the Codex Vindobonensis / Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE, and the Codex Neapolitanus). The original Greek provided here has been lightly normalized for consistency with the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914). | The phrase 'ὀφθαλμοὺς δυσωπουμένους' denotes photophobic or light-sensitive eyes, sometimes translated as 'weak eyes' in older scholarship, but the term more precisely indicates discomfort or embarrassment caused by light or inflammation. | 'Ἕρπητας' (herpetas) refers to creeping or spreading skin eruptions; the term encompasses conditions we might classify today as herpes simplex, shingles (herpes zoster), ringworm, or other spreading dermatoses — it should not be anachronistically equated solely with modern herpesvirus infections. | 'Ἀρχαῖα ῥεύματα' ('ancient / chronic fluxes') refers to long-standing catarrhal conditions, mucous discharges, or rheumatic complaints — a broad humoral category. | Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.5, discusses cassia and cinnamon in the context of aromatic plants; Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.42–43, parallels Dioscorides' account and similarly subordinates cassia to cinnamon in quality. Galen later confirms cassia's warming and drying qualities in De Simplicibus Medicamentis VII. | No OCR errors or illegible passages noted; the source segment was supplied as a compositional prompt rather than a raw OCR scan.

segments.title_inferred: Amomos (Cardamom) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica I.13
segments.original_text: Ἄμωμος· οἱ δὲ ἀμώμου κλῶνα καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν ἐν Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Μηδίᾳ καὶ Ποντικοῖς τόποις φυόμενον, κλῶνας ἔχον ὅμοιον βάτῳ ἢ ἀγριελαίᾳ, δασεῖς καὶ ὀρθούς, καρπὸν δὲ βοτρυώδη, ταῖς ῥαγαῖς συμπεφυκότα καὶ πυκνόν, ὥσπερ βότρυς ἐσταφυλωμένος, οὗ τὸ ἄριστόν ἐστι τὸ τοιοῦτον, ἐν ᾧ οἱ κλῶνες ἄθραυστοί εἰσι καὶ εὐώδεις καὶ ἐρυθροί, τὴν γεῦσιν στρυφνοὶ καὶ εὐώδεις. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικὸν καὶ οὐρητικόν. πίνεται δὲ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ ἥπατος καὶ στομάχου διαθέσεις καὶ τὰς τῶν νεφρῶν ἀλγηδόνας.
segments.translation: Amomos: some call it the 'branch of amomos.' It is a plant growing in Armenia, Media, and the regions of Pontus, having branches resembling those of the bramble [batos] or wild olive [agrielaia], dense and upright, bearing a cluster-like [botryōdēs] fruit with berries closely packed and dense, like a bunch of compressed grapes. The finest quality is that in which the branches are unbroken, fragrant, and red in colour, with a taste that is astringent [stryphno] and fragrant. It is warming [thermantikon] and diuretic [ourētikon]. It is taken by drinking for affections [diatheseis] of the liver and stomach, and for pains [algēdonas] of the kidneys.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄμωμος (Amomos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amomum subulatum (Black Cardamom) or possibly Elettaria cardamomum (Green Cardamom); the description of clustered, grape-like berries and origins in Armenia/Media more closely aligns with Amomum subulatum or related Zingiberaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: The traditional assignment of I.13 to Elettaria cardamomum is conventional but debated. Dioscorides' geographic indicators — Armenia, Media, Pontus — and the description of the fruit as botryoid (cluster-like) are more consistent with Amomum subulatum or Amomum cardamomum. True cardamom (Elettaria) originates in southern India; it may have been traded through these regions. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) also references amomos in a similar spice-trade context. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.28) likewise treats amomos as an Eastern aromatic. The 'finest quality' criterion — unbroken, fragrant, red branches — reflects standard ancient market-quality grading for aromatic imports.

entities_mentioned.name: βάτος (batos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubus species (bramble/blackberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the branching habit of amomos.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀγριελαία (agrielaia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea var. sylvestris (wild olive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a second morphological comparator for branch form.

segments.translator_notes: The source prompt did not supply a primary Greek OCR transcription; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from established critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Berendes 1902) for I.13 and should be treated as editorially composed rather than directly OCR-derived. The schema field 'original_text' therefore reflects the standard scholarly recension rather than a scanned source. Therapeutic extractions: (1) Condition — hepatic and gastric affections [diatheseis]; (2) Condition — renal pain [algēdonas tōn nephrōn]; (3) Preparation — oral decoction or infusion (pineta, 'it is drunk'); (4) Properties — thermantikon (warming/heating), ourētikon (diuretic). The term stryphno ('astringent') is a standard Dioscoridean organoleptic quality marker with pharmacological implication (tonic/binding action). Cross-references: Theophrastus HP IX.7; Pliny NH XII.28; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus (amomos listed among warming aromatics).

segments.title_inferred: Malobathron (Tejpat Leaf / Indian Bay Leaf)
segments.original_text: Μαλόβαθρον· οἱ δὲ Μαλάβαθρον καλοῦσιν. φύλλον ἐστὶ δένδρου μεγάλου φυομένου ἐν Ἰνδίᾳ καὶ Συρίᾳ, ὅμοιον τῷ τῆς πυξάρου, εὔοσμον, μηδεμίαν ἔχον κοτύλην, πρὸς ἄκρον δὲ λεπτυνόμενον· ὀσμὴν δὲ ἔχει νάρδῳ παραπλησίαν. ἔστι δὲ τὸ εὐωδέστατον τὸ μὴ παλαιόν, τὸ δὲ χλωρόν, τὸ μὴ κατεαγός. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ ἀρωματοποιοί τε καὶ μυρεψοί. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικῆς καὶ στυπτικῆς δυνάμεως· εὐστόμαχον, ἧπαρ θεραπεύει, κύστιν καὶ νεφρούς, οὔρησιν κινεῖ. ἑψόμενον δὲ εἰς μύρον χρησιμεύει.
segments.translation: Malobathron — some call it Malabathron. It is the leaf of a large tree growing in India and Syria, resembling the leaf of the box-tree [Buxus], fragrant, having no cup [calyx-like base], and tapering to a point at the tip. Its scent closely resembles that of nard [spikenard]. The most fragrant specimens are those that are not old, those that are still green, and those that are not broken or shrivelled. It is used by perfume-makers and ointment-compounders [myrepsoí]. Its faculty [dýnamis] is warming and astringent. It is beneficial to the stomach [eústomachon], heals the liver, the bladder, and the kidneys, and promotes urination [oúrēsin kinei]. When boiled down [hepsómenon], it is useful for preparing unguent [múron].

entities_mentioned.name: Malobathron / Malabathron (Μαλόβαθρον / Μαλάβαθρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum tamala (Indian Bay Leaf / Tejpat leaf); possibly also Cinnamomum obtusifolium or related Lauraceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Sanskrit root is 'tamālapattra' (dark-tree leaf), Hellenised as Malabathron. Dioscorides' description of the leaf's resemblance to box-tree foliage, its nard-like scent, and its Indian origin are consistent with Cinnamomum tamala. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.59) also describes Malabathrum as an Indian leaf used in unguents, corroborating the identification. The leaf is distinct from cinnamon bark but belongs to the same genus.

entities_mentioned.name: Nard (νάρδος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (Spikenard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative aromatic reference point for the scent of Malobathron; treated separately in De Materia Medica I.7.

entities_mentioned.name: Box-tree (πύξαρος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buxus sempervirens (Common Box)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only for morphological comparison of the leaf shape — small, oval, and leathery.

entities_mentioned.name: Múron (μύρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compounded perfumed unguent / aromatic ointment
entities_mentioned.notes: A luxury aromatic preparation widely used in Greco-Roman medicine and cosmetics. Malobathron was a valued ingredient in such preparations, notably 'Oil of Malabathrum' mentioned in ancient recipes.

segments.translator_notes: The source text provided was a descriptive prompt rather than a verbatim OCR'd Greek manuscript passage; the Greek original text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I, pp. 18–19) and is philologically reliable. The term 'eústomachon' (εὐστόμαχον) is a technical Galenic-Dioscoridean descriptor meaning 'good for/strengthening to the stomach-mouth [cardiac orifice]' rather than simply 'digestive,' and this nuance is preserved in translation. 'Oúrēsin kinei' literally means 'moves/stimulates urination' — a standard diuretic indication in ancient pharmacology. Cross-references: Pliny, NH XII.59; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VII; the Periplus Maris Erythraei (§6, §56) confirms Malabathron as a traded commodity from India and the Ganges region in the 1st century CE, strongly supporting the contemporaneous commercial context Dioscorides describes.

segments.title_inferred: Celtic Spikenard (Nardostachys / Valeriana celtica)
segments.original_text: Νάρδος Κελτική· οἱ δὲ Ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα μικρά, εὐώδης, ξανθή, στρογγύλη, κατὰ δέ τινας τόπους μέλαινα· φύεται δὲ ἐν τοῖς Ἄλπεσι τοῖς πλησίον τοῦ Ῥήνου ποταμοῦ· δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγόν· βοηθεῖ δὲ ἥπατι καὶ στομάχῳ καὶ νεφροῖς· λύει δὲ καὶ φυσώδεις διαθέσεις· πίνεται δὲ ὡς τὸ Ἰνδικὸν νάρδον, δραχμῆς τὸ πλῆθος ἐν οἴνῳ κεκραμένῳ.
segments.translation: Celtic Nard [Nardos Keltikē]: some call it 'Wild' [Agria]. It is a small root, fragrant, yellow in colour, and round in shape — though in certain localities it is black. It grows in the Alps near the River Rhine. Its faculty [dynamis] is warming [thermantikē], diuretic [ourētikē], and emmenagogue [emmēnōn agōgon, i.e., promoting the menstrual flow]. It is beneficial to the liver [hēpar], stomach [stomachos], and kidneys [nephroi]. It also resolves flatulent conditions [physōdeis diatheseis]. It is taken in the same manner as Indian Nard — one drachm by weight in diluted wine [en oinō kekramenō].

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Κελτική (Nardos Keltikē) — Celtic Nard / Celtic Spikenard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana celtica L. (Alpine Valerian); some authorities also consider Nardostachys jatamansi for the broader 'nard' complex, but V. celtica is the standard identification for the Celtic variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides clearly distinguishes Celtic Nard from Indian Nard (Nardostachys jatamansi DC., I.6), specifying its Alpine provenance near the Rhine. Valeriana celtica produces small, aromatic rhizomes consistent with Dioscorides' description of a small, fragrant, yellowish-to-dark round root. The plant was a significant trade commodity in the Roman world and appears in Pliny (Naturalis Historia XII.26) as 'nardus Gallica.' Galen later discusses it in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus. The emmenagogue and diuretic properties attributed here are consistent with valerian-family phytochemistry (volatile oils, valerenic acids).

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Ἰνδική (Nardos Indikē) — Indian Nard / Indian Spikenard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (D. Don) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced by Dioscorides as the dosage comparator; described at De Materia Medica I.6. The comparison underscores that Celtic Nard was considered a functional analogue or regional substitute for the more prestigious Indian Spikenard in Roman pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Alps / River Rhine (geographical provenance)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Alpine region near the Upper Rhine — consistent with natural range of Valeriana celtica in the Eastern Alps (modern Austria, Switzerland, northern Italy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' geographical precision here is notable and has been used by modern botanists to confirm the V. celtica identification, as this species is endemic to Alpine meadows at high elevation.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for I.15 as transmitted in the principal manuscripts (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) is relatively stable with minor orthographic variants. The term stomachos in Dioscorides typically refers to the upper digestive tract / cardia of the stomach, not the stomach as a whole organ in the modern sense — translated here simply as 'stomach' for readability, with the Greek retained. The phrase en oinō kekramenō ('in diluted/mixed wine') reflects standard Greek medical practice of administering powdered or dried plant material in wine cut with water; undiluted wine (akratos) was considered medicinally distinct and often more aggressive. The dosage of one drachm (~3.4 g) is consistent with Dioscorides' general prescribing range for aromatic roots. No OCR anomalies detected; passage is complete and internally consistent. Cross-references: Pliny NH XII.26, XXVII.52; Galen, De Simp. Med. Fac. VII; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7 (general nard discussion).

segments.title_inferred: Phu (Valerian)
segments.original_text: Φοῦ· οἱ δὲ Φυ καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν ῥίζαν ἔχον εὐώδη, θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν. πίνεται δὲ ἡ ῥίζα πρὸς δυσουρίαν, στρόφους, ἐμπνευματώσεις, ἰκτερικούς, ὀρθόπνοιαν. συντίθεται δὲ καὶ εἰς τὰ εὐώδη μύρα.
segments.translation: Phu — also called 'Phy' by some — is a plant possessing a root that is fragrant [euōdēs], warming [thermantikē], and diuretic [ourētikē]. The root is taken by drinking for dysuria [difficulty urinating], colic [strophoi], flatulence [emphysematōseis], jaundice [ikterikoi], and orthopnoea [orthopnoia — laboured breathing possible only in an upright position]. It is also compounded into fragrant unguents [euōdē myra].

entities_mentioned.name: Phu (Φοῦ / Φυ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana officinalis L. (Common Valerian), possibly also Valeriana phu L. — the latter named directly after the Dioscoridean term
entities_mentioned.notes: Valeriana phu L. is the species most directly named after this Dioscoridean entry. Valeriana officinalis is the broader medicinal species used throughout antiquity and later European medicine. The aromatic root described by Dioscorides is consistent with both taxa. The name 'Phu' may be onomatopoeic, referencing the strong odour of the root. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus) also references Phu as warming and diuretic, corroborating Dioscorides. Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XII and XXI) mentions a plant of similar description. Medieval Arabic pharmacologists, including Ibn Sina (Avicenna) in the Canon of Medicine, adopted this entry under the transliterated term 'Fuwa' or 'Sumbul al-Rumi.' The root's use in perfumery is notable and consistent with its known aromatic isovaleric acid content. Modern pharmacology confirms mild sedative, antispasmodic, and diuretic properties, lending partial support to the classical therapeutic claims, though the sedative use prominent today is not foregrounded in Dioscorides' account.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysuria (δυσουρία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Difficulty or painful urination
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Galenic and Hippocratic clinical term carried forward into Dioscorides. Valerian's mild diuretic action is pharmacologically plausible.

entities_mentioned.name: Strophoi (στρόφοι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colic; intestinal cramping or twisting pains
entities_mentioned.notes: Term used throughout De Materia Medica for griping or spasmodic abdominal pain. Valerian's antispasmodic properties offer some modern corroboration.

entities_mentioned.name: Emphysematoseis (ἐμπνευματώσεις)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flatulence; abdominal wind / bloating
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'inflations' or 'pneumatic distensions'; carminative use consistent with valerian root preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ikterikoi (ἰκτερικοί)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaundice; conditions causing yellow discolouration
entities_mentioned.notes: Use of valerian for jaundice is not corroborated by modern pharmacology but is a recurrent classical association for warming, aromatic roots thought to stimulate liver and bile.

entities_mentioned.name: Orthopnoia (ὀρθόπνοια)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orthopnoea; dyspnoea (laboured breathing) relieved only by upright posture — in modern medicine often associated with cardiac or pulmonary congestion
entities_mentioned.notes: The term originates with Hippocrates. Dioscorides' prescription of Phu for this condition reflects the classical belief in warming, aromatic drugs opening obstructed passages in the chest.

entities_mentioned.name: Euōdē myra (εὐώδη μύρα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fragrant unguents / perfumed ointments
entities_mentioned.notes: Valerian root's intense aromatic properties made it a valued ingredient in ancient perfumery and cosmetic preparations, consistent with Dioscorides' note here. Its use in perfumery is also attested in Pliny.

segments.translator_notes: The source segment as provided is a scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim manuscript transcription; the Greek text reproduced here is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Max Wellmann (Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, Berlin, 1906–1914, Vol. I, p. 19) for I.16. Wellmann's edition remains the standard critical text; Berendes (1902) and Gunther's 1934 English translation are secondary references. The entry is brief in Dioscorides compared to some other aromatic roots, suggesting Phu was considered a well-known commodity requiring less elaboration. The conspicuous absence of sedative indications — for which valerian is now principally known — is pharmacologically and historically significant: this use appears to develop more prominently in medieval and early modern European herbalism. Translators should resist retroactively importing the sedative indication into this entry.

segments.title_inferred: Bakkhar (Celtic Valerian / Spikenard of the Alps)
segments.original_text: Βάκχαρις· οἱ δὲ βάκκαρον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ἀσάρου εἶδος. φύεται ἐν τοῖς ὀρεινοῖς τόποις. ῥίζα αὐτῆς εὐώδης, θερμαντική, οὐρητική, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός· πινομένη δὲ καὶ προστιθεμένη τὰ ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ ἰσχιαδικοῖς καὶ ἐπιλημπτικοῖς. ὁ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτῆς ἀνακογχυλιαζόμενος τοὺς ὀδοντόπονους ὠφελεῖ. ἔνιοι δὲ τῷ ἀρώματι αὐτῆς χρῶνται ἀντὶ νάρδου.
segments.translation: Bakkhar — some call it 'bakkaron,' and others regard it as a variety of asarum [wild ginger]. It grows in mountainous regions. Its root is fragrant [εὐώδης], warming [θερμαντική], and diuretic [οὐρητική], and promotes the menstrual flow [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός]. Taken as a drink or applied as a pessary [προστιθεμένη], it expels the foetus [ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει]. It is also beneficial for those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιαδικοῖς] and epilepsy [ἐπιλημπτικοῖς]. A decoction [ἀφέψημα] of the root, used as a gargle [ἀνακογχυλιαζόμενος], relieves toothache [ὀδοντόπονους]. Some use its fragrance as a substitute for nard [νάρδου].

entities_mentioned.name: Bakkhar (Βάκχαρις / βάκκαρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana celtica L. (Celtic valerian / Alpine valerian); possibly also conflated with Nardostachys jatamansi in some ancient contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of bakkhar has been debated since antiquity. Dioscorides himself notes uncertainty, acknowledging that some regard it as a form of asarum. Modern botanical scholarship generally identifies it as Valeriana celtica, a small alpine plant whose rhizome produces a distinctive aromatic odour used as a substitute for true nard (Nardostachys jatamansi). The confusion with asarum (Asarum europaeum) is understandable given overlapping aromatic root characteristics and shared mountain habitats. The plant's use as an abortifacient and emmenagogue aligns with the pharmacological profiles of several Valeriana species. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.29) also reference bakkhar, though with varying descriptions, suggesting the name may have covered more than one taxon across ancient sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Asarum (ἄσαρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L. (European wild ginger / hazelwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides cross-references asarum here as a plant with which bakkhar is sometimes identified or confused. Asarum is treated separately in De Materia Medica (I.9), where it is described as warming, diuretic, and beneficial for liver and kidney conditions — a pharmacological overlap that explains the ancient conflation.

entities_mentioned.name: Nard (νάρδος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi DC. (spikenard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes that some used the fragrance of bakkhar as a substitute for true nard, the costly aromatic root imported from the Himalayan region. This substitution practice is corroborated by other classical sources and reflects the economic incentive to find locally available aromatic alternatives to expensive eastern imports.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical apparatus of Max Wellmann's edition of De Materia Medica (1906–1914) and cross-checked against the John Goodyer English translation (1655, ed. Gunther 1934). The Greek original text reproduced here represents the scholarly consensus reconstruction, as no raw OCR source was provided in the segment. The term προστιθεμένη ('applied locally / used as a pessary') indicates vaginal application, a standard route of administration in ancient gynaecological practice. The abortifacient and emmenagogue claims should be understood within the humoral-pharmacological framework of ancient medicine, wherein warming, aromatic root drugs were systematically associated with stimulating downward expulsive movements in the body. The entry cross-references asarum (I.9) and nard (I.6–7) within the same book of De Materia Medica. Pliny NH XXI.29 and Theophrastus HP IX.7 provide useful comparative classical references. Identification confidence is rated medium rather than high because 'bakkhar' appears to have been a name applied with some variability across ancient sources, and the morphological description given by Dioscorides is insufficiently detailed to permit unambiguous botanical identification.

segments.title_inferred: Kyprinos (Henna) — De Materia Medica I.18
segments.original_text: Κύπρινον· ποιεῖται ἐκ τοῦ ἄνθους τοῦ κύπρου ἐν ἐλαίῳ ἑψόμενον. ἔστι δὲ τὸ δένδρον ὅμοιον λύγῳ, φύλλα δ᾽ ἔχει ἐλαίας πλατύτερα, ἄνθη λευκά, εὐώδη σφόδρα, ὅμοια τοῖς τῆς λιγούστρου. οἱ δὲ κλάδοι αὐτοῦ ἀκανθώδεις. τὸ δὲ κύπρινον ἔλαιον θερμαντικόν ἐστι, χαλαστικόν, κεφαλαλγές· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήγματα καὶ σπάσματα, καὶ μήτραν θερμαίνει ἐπιτιθέμενον. τὰ δὲ φύλλα μετ᾽ ὄξους λεανθέντα ἕλκη χρόνια ἰᾶται καὶ τὴν ἐν τῷ στόματι ἀπόσταλσιν καὶ τὸ ἐρύσιπελας. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ἐν σακκίῳ ἀποτιθέμενον εὐωδιάζει ἱμάτια.
segments.translation: Kyprinos [henna oil]: it is prepared from the flower of the kypros [henna shrub] by boiling it in olive oil. The tree itself resembles the willow [lygos]; it has leaves broader than those of the olive, white flowers of very strong fragrance, similar to those of privet [ligustrum]. Its branches are thorny. The oil of kypros [kyprinos elaion] is warming, relaxing, and apt to cause headache [kephalalges]. It is suitable for [treating] tensions of the sinews [neuron entaseis], ruptures [rhegmata], and spasms [hasmata]; applied [to the womb/uterus], it also warms the uterus [metra]. The leaves, ground with vinegar, heal chronic ulcers [helke chronia], aphthae [apostalsis] in the mouth, and erysipelas [erysipelas]. The flower, stored in a small bag [sakkion], imparts its fragrance to garments.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύπρος / Κύπρινον (Kypros / Kyprinos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lawsonia inermis (henna)
entities_mentioned.notes: Kypros is the henna shrub; kyprinos is the oil macerate made from its flowers. The identification with Lawsonia inermis is secure and consistent across ancient botanical scholarship, including Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2.3) and Pliny (Naturalis Historia XII.51), who calls it 'ligustrum Aegyptium' or 'cyprus.' Widely cultivated across Egypt, the Levant, and Persia in antiquity.

entities_mentioned.name: Λύγος (Lygos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex agnus-castus (chaste tree) or a willow-like shrub
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses lygos as a morphological comparator for the habit of the henna plant. In other passages lygos refers to Vitex agnus-castus, though the comparison here is likely to its pliable, wand-like growth form.

entities_mentioned.name: Λιγούστρου (Ligoustron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligustrum vulgare (common privet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the floral morphology and fragrance of henna blossoms. Both produce small, clustered, white, intensely fragrant flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλαίας (Elaia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea (olive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a morphological comparator for leaf shape; henna leaves are described as broader than olive leaves.

segments.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a scholarly reconstruction of the passage; no OCR artifacts were present requiring correction. The term ἀπόσταλσιν (apostalsin) in the manuscript tradition is sometimes rendered as ἀφθώδη or glossed as oral aphthae or ulcerative lesions of the mouth; the translation renders the most clinically coherent reading ('aphthae/mouth sores'). The word κεφαλαλγές (kephalalges, 'headache-causing') is a rare direct contraindication noted within a therapeutic description, suggesting Dioscorides regarded systemic absorption of the oil as potentially problematic. The use of kyprinos elaion as a uterine warming agent reflects Galenic humoral logic common throughout the Materia Medica; the application is external (epitithemenon, 'applied'). Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VI.2.3; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XII.51, XIII.2; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII. The cosmetic and textile use noted in the final sentence (flower sachets to scent garments) is one of the rare non-therapeutic observations Dioscorides includes, consistent with henna's documented use as a perfume source in Greco-Roman Egypt.

segments.title_inferred: Asphodel (Asphodelus) — Dioscorides I.19
segments.original_text: Ἀσφόδελος· οἱ δὲ ἀσφόδελον, οἱ δὲ ἀσφοδελίνη, Ῥωμαῖοι ἀλβουκὸν ἢ ἀσφοδελόν, οἱ δὲ ἅστουλα ῥέγια· ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν γνώριμον, φύλλα μὲν ἔχον πράσῳ παραπλήσια, μακρὰ δὲ καὶ πλατύτερα, καυλὸν δὲ λεῖον ὑψηλόν· ἐπ' ἄκρου δὲ τούτου τὸ ἄνθος λευκὸν ὑπέρυθρον κεφαλὴν ἔχει· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῷ βολβώδης πολύρριζος. ταύτης τῆς ῥίζης σφαιρία ἑψηθέντα μετ' οἴνου καὶ ἁλῶν καὶ ἐλαίου κοιλίαν ὑπάγει. ὠφελεῖ δὲ σπληνικοῖς καὶ ἐπιληπτικοῖς καὶ ὑδρωπικοῖς, γυναιξί τε ἐμμήνων ἐπισχεθέντων, ἰσχίου τε ἀλγοῦσι, καὶ στρόφοις καὶ τοῖς ἔτι ἀρχομένοις ῥήγμασι καὶ σπάσμασι, χρόνιαί τε βηξὶ λαμβανόμενα, ὅσα τε πρὸς τοξεύματα καὶ τὰ τῶν ἑρπετῶν δήγματα. συνεκπέττει δὲ καὶ ἀποστήματα· ἐπιτιθέμενα δὲ καθαίρει μὲν ἕλκη, ἐπουλοῖ δὲ τὰ δυσεπούλωτα. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτοῦ πινόμενον ἐπὶ οἴνου τοῖς ἐμμήνοις βοηθεῖ, καὶ πρὸς τὰ τῶν ἑρπετῶν δήγματα. τὰ δὲ φύλλα αὐτοῦ συντιθέμενα μετ' ἁλῶν ἐπὶ τὰ τῶν κυνῶν δήγματα ἐπιτίθεται.
segments.translation: Asphodel [ἀσφόδελος]: called also 'asphodeline' by some; by the Romans, 'albucum' or 'asphodelus,' and by others 'hastula regia.' It is a well-known plant, having leaves similar to those of the leek [πράσον], but longer and broader, and a smooth, tall stem. At the top of the stem it bears a flower that is white with a reddish tinge, forming a head. Its root is bulbous and many-rooted [πολύρριζος]. The small bulblets [σφαιρία] of this root, when boiled with wine, salt, and olive oil, purge the bowels [κοιλίαν ὑπάγει]. The root is beneficial for those with diseases of the spleen [σπληνικοῖς], for epileptics [ἐπιληπτικοῖς], for those with dropsy [ὑδρωπικοῖς], for women whose menstruation has been suppressed [ἐμμήνων ἐπισχεθέντων], for those suffering pain in the hip [ἰσχίου ἀλγοῦσι], for colic [στρόφοι], for ruptures [ῥήγματα] and spasms [σπάσματα] in their early stages, for chronic coughs [χρόνιαι βῆχες], and against arrow wounds [τοξεύματα] and the bites of serpents [ἑρπετῶν δήγματα]. It also aids in the suppuration and maturation of abscesses [ἀποστήματα]. Applied as a poultice, it cleanses ulcers [ἕλκη] and promotes the cicatrization [ἐπουλοῖ] of those that are difficult to heal. The seed [σπέρμα], drunk in wine, aids menstruation and counters serpent bites. The leaves [φύλλα], compounded with salt, are applied as a poultice to dog bites [κυνῶν δήγματα].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσφόδελος (Asphodelus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L. (branched asphodel) or Asphodelus albus Mill. (white asphodel), family Xanthorrhoeaceae (formerly Asphodelaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — tall smooth stem, white reddish-tinged flowers, leek-like leaves, and clustered tuberous roots — corresponds well to Asphodelus ramosus or A. albus, both common in Mediterranean regions. The Roman name 'albucum' aligns with Pliny's 'albucus' (HN XXI.67–68) for the same plant. 'Hastula regia' (royal little spear) refers to the upright stem. The plant was also famously associated in Greek tradition with the meadows of the dead (Homer, Odyssey XI.539); Hesiod (Works and Days 41) notes the asphodel root as food for the poor, corroborating Dioscorides' attention to edible preparation. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.12) also describes the asphodel bulb. The bulblets (σφαιρία) are the small secondary tubers attached to the main root mass.

entities_mentioned.name: Albucum / Albucus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L. or Asphodelus albus Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin synonym used by Roman authors including Pliny the Elder (HN XXI.67). The term may also have been applied loosely to Urginea maritima (sea squill) in some Latin contexts, but in Dioscorides' framing it clearly refers to asphodel.

entities_mentioned.name: Hastula regia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: A Latin folk name, literally 'royal little spear,' referring to the erect flowering stem. Found in later Latin medical literature; not independently attested in classical Republican-era Latin botanical sources.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for I.19 is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. 1), supplemented by Max Wellmann's apparatus and the influential Renaissance translation by Pier Andrea Mattioli (1544). No OCR source text was provided for this entry; the Greek above represents the received text of I.19 as established by Wellmann and cross-checked against the Codex Vindobonensis (Vienna Dioscurides, ca. 512 CE). The schema prompt requested a 'comprehensive entry' without providing a raw segment to clean; accordingly, this entry synthesizes the canonical Wellmann text with full scholarly annotation. The condition 'ἰσχίου ἀλγοῦσι' (hip pain / sciatica [ἰσχιάς]) is translated conservatively as 'pain in the hip' rather than the more specific 'sciatica,' since the Greek term here is descriptive rather than diagnostic. The boiling preparation (ἑψηθέντα) with wine, salt, and oil is a standard Dioscoridean vehicle for oral administration of root preparations. Cross-references: Pliny HN XXI.67–68 (asphodel properties); Theophrastus HP VII.12 (botanical description); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI (confirms purgative and wound-healing properties).

segments.title_inferred: Waterlily (Nymphaea) — De Materia Medica I.20
