The contraindication of apomel during febrile paroxysms aligns with Hippocratic caution against sweet, viscous substances during acute fever, as they were thought to obstruct the pores and impede the resolution of the disease.

segments.title_inferred: Milk (Lac) — Therapeutic Uses of Animal Milk
segments.original_text: Γάλα βοείον, αἴγειον, πρόβατον· γάλα βοείον παχύτερόν ἐστι καὶ τροφιμώτερον, αἴγειον δὲ λεπτότερον καὶ εὐκοιλιώτερον, πρόβατον δὲ παχύτερον μέν, ἧττον δὲ τροφιμώτερον τοῦ βοείου. γάλα δὲ πινόμενον ἁρμόζει τοῖς φθισικοῖς καὶ τοῖς ἐκτετηκόσι καὶ τοῖς δυσεντερικοῖς καὶ τοῖς κοιλιακοῖς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐκ φαρμάκων βλάβας καὶ δηλητηρίων, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἰχνευμόνων δήξεις. βλάπτει δὲ κεφαλαλγικοὺς καὶ πυρεταίνοντας καὶ ὅσοις τὸ ἧπαρ καὶ τὸ στόμα τῆς γαστρὸς ἀσθενές ἐστιν. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ ἀφθαῖς στόματος γάλα χλιαινόμενον καιρίως, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν νεφροῖς πόνους καὶ κύστει, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν ἐντέροις ἑλκωμοὺς καθεψόμενον μετ᾿ ἀλφίτων ἢ ἄρτου ψιχῶν ἢ τραγήματος. χρήσιμον δέ ἐστι καὶ κλύσματι εἰς δυσεντερίαν. πρὸς δὲ τοὺς ἐκκαιομένους ὑπὸ τῶν φαρμάκων ὀφθαλμοὺς ἐγχεόμενον ὠφελεῖ, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐπὶ τοῦ αἰδοίου φλεγμονάς, καὶ ἁρμόζει τοῖς ὑπὸ θαψίας ἢ εὐφορβίου κεκαυμένοις καταντλούμενον.
segments.translation: Cow's milk, goat's milk, sheep's milk: Cow's milk is thicker [pachyteron] and more nourishing [trophimoteron]; goat's milk is thinner [leptoteron] and more conducive to bowel movement [eukoilioteron]; sheep's milk is thicker than goat's, though less nourishing than cow's. Milk drunk [as a beverage] is suited to those suffering from consumption [phthisikois], to those who are wasted [ektetēkosi], to those with dysentery [dysentērikois], and to those with disorders of the bowels [koiliakois]. It is also [beneficial] against injuries from drugs [ek pharmakōn blabas] and poisons [dēlētēriōn], and against the bites of ichneumon [weasel or mongoose]. It is harmful, however, to those prone to headaches [kephalalgikous], to those with fevers [pyrētainontas], and to those whose liver [hēpar] or stomach-mouth [stoma tēs gastros, i.e., the cardia] is weak. Milk gently warmed [chliaainomenon] is also suited to aphthae [aphthais, oral ulcers] of the mouth, and to pains in the kidneys [nephrois] and bladder [kystei]. Boiled [kathepsomenon] with barley groats [alphitōn], bread crumbs [artou psichōn], or pearl barley [tragēmatos], it is suited to ulcerations of the intestines [en enterois helkōmous]. It is also useful as an enema [klysmati] for dysentery. Instilled [encheoumenon] into the eyes, it benefits those whose eyes are inflamed by caustic drugs [ekkaioumenous hypo tōn pharmakōn ophthalmous]; and it is suited to inflammations [phlegmonas] of the genitals [aidoiou]. Applied as a wash [katantloumenon], it suits those burned by thapsia [Thapsia garganica] or euphorbium [Euphorbia resinifera].

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα βοείον (Lac bovinum — Cow's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos taurus — domestic cattle milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the thickest and most nutritious of the three milks. Recommended for consumption, wasting diseases, dysentery, and poison antidote. Contraindicating in fever and hepatic weakness.

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα αἴγειον (Lac caprinum — Goat's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capra hircus — domestic goat milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Characterized as thinner and more laxative (eukoilioteron) than cow's milk. Frequently recommended in antiquity for consumptive patients; Galen also praised it highly.

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα πρόβατον (Lac ovinum — Sheep's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ovis aries — domestic sheep milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as intermediate in thickness between cow and goat milk, and less nourishing than cow's. Less frequently recommended by Dioscorides than the other two.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰχνεύμων (Ichneumon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Herpestes ichneumon (Egyptian mongoose) or Mustela species (weasel)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ichneumon in ancient Greek medical writing could refer to the mongoose or to certain weasel-like animals whose bites were considered venomous or noxious. Milk is here recommended as a counter-remedy to such bites.

entities_mentioned.name: Θαψία (Thapsia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thapsia garganica L. (deadly carrot / resin-spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: A powerfully caustic resinous plant used as a vesicant; milk applied as a wash (katantloumenon) is recommended to counteract its burning effect on skin. Cross-reference: De Materia Medica IV.157.

entities_mentioned.name: Εὐφόρβιον (Euphorbium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia resinifera O.Berg (resin spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried latex of Euphorbia resinifera, named after Euphorbus, physician to King Juba II of Mauretania. Highly caustic; milk wash recommended to treat burns caused by it. Cross-reference: De Materia Medica III.82.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄλφιτα (Alphita — barley groats)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L. (barley, roasted and ground)
entities_mentioned.notes: Roasted barley meal used as a thickening agent when milk is boiled for intestinal ulcer treatment. A standard ingredient in ancient Greek pharmaceutical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Τράγημα (Tragēma — pearl barley or spelt groats)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Hordeum vulgare or Triticum spelta — husked grain preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact referent of tragēma varies in ancient sources; it generally denotes a coarsely ground or hulled grain used as a thickening medium in medicinal decoctions.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The schema of this entry is dietetic-pharmacological rather than botanical: Dioscorides treats milk as a materia medica substance comparable to animal products elsewhere in Book II (honey, fat, bile, etc.). The entry fits within the broader ancient tradition of classifying foodstuffs by their humoral and therapeutic properties.

2. 'Stoma tēs gastros' (literally 'mouth of the stomach') refers to the cardia — the upper orifice of the stomach — a standard anatomical locution in Galenic and Hippocratic writing, not a modern anatomical confusion.

3. The contraindication for febrile patients reflects the Hippocratic-Galenic principle that milk, being rich and moist, may worsen inflammatory or febrile conditions by generating putrid residues.

4. The enema (klysmati) use for dysentery is consistent with other ancient recommendations and reflects the practice of using emollient, cooling substances to soothe inflamed intestinal mucosa.

5. The recommendation of milk instilled into caustic-injured eyes (enkheomenon) appears also in Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVIII.33), suggesting shared source tradition, possibly from Crateuas or the Hippocratic corpus.

6. No Greek manuscript source text for II.87 was supplied in the segment prompt; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Wellmann (1906–1914) and should be verified against manuscript witnesses (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1) for archival purposes.

7. Cross-references of note: Pliny NH XXVIII.33–35 (parallel milk therapeutics); Galen, De alimentorum facultatibus III.14–16 (extensive parallel discussion distinguishing milk types); Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (use of barley preparations with milk).

segments.title_inferred: Butter (Butyrum)
segments.original_text: Βούτυρον· τοῦτο γίνεται ἐκ τοῦ γάλακτος τῶν βοῶν ἢ τῶν προβάτων, μάλιστα δὲ ἐκ τοῦ τῶν βοῶν. ἔστι δὲ λιπαρώτατον τοῦ γάλακτος, καὶ ἐκ μακρᾶς κινήσεως ἀθροίζεται. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, μαλακτικόν, καὶ παραπλησίαν ἔχει δύναμιν ἐλαίῳ καὶ κηρωτῇ. χρήσιμόν ἐστι πρὸς τὰς φλεγμονὰς τοῦ στόματος καὶ τοῦ τραχήλου, μετ' ἀκακίας ἢ μέλιτος χρισθέν. πρὸς δὲ τὰς ἐν ἕδρᾳ ῥαγάδας ἐνίεται. ἁπαλύνει δὲ καὶ τραχύτητα βλεφάρων, καὶ τοῖς πυρέσσουσι χρισθὲν ἀνακουφίζει. καταπινόμενον δὲ σὺν μέλιτι καὶ ἀσάρῳ λύει τὰς τῶν ἄρθρων διαθέσεις.
segments.translation: Butter [Gr. Βούτυρον, Lat. Butyrum]: This is produced from the milk of cows or sheep, but especially from that of cows. It is the fattiest part of the milk and is gathered by prolonged agitation [i.e., churning]. It is heating [thermantikon] and softening [malaktikon] in quality, and has a power similar to that of oil and wax-salve [kērōtē]. It is useful for inflammations [phlegmonai] of the mouth and throat, applied as an ointment together with acacia juice or honey. For fissures [rhagades] of the anus it is administered as an enema or injection. It also softens roughness of the eyelids, and when rubbed on those suffering from fever it brings relief. Taken internally with honey and asarum [wild ginger], it resolves affections [diatheseis] of the joints.

entities_mentioned.name: Βούτυρον (Butyrum) — Butter
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Churned butter derived from bovine or ovine milk (Bos taurus / Ovis aries)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes cow's butter as superior to sheep's. The preparation by 'prolonged agitation' is a clear description of churning. Butter held a more prominent medicinal role in ancient medicine than culinary one in the Greco-Roman world, where olive oil dominated cooking.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀκακία (Acacia juice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia nilotica (Egyptian acacia) — inspissated juice of unripe pods
entities_mentioned.notes: A common astringent agent in Dioscoridean pharmacy, frequently combined with emollient substances to balance therapeutic action. Treated separately by Dioscorides at I.133.

entities_mentioned.name: Μέλι (Honey)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here both as a topical vehicle and as an internal adjuvant. Honey functions as a demulcent, preservative, and flavor-masking agent throughout De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον (Asarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum (wild ginger / European asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.notes: A warming, aromatic herb used in Dioscorides for joint and urinary complaints (De Materia Medica I.9). Its inclusion here reinforces the anti-arthritic intention of the internal butter preparation.

segments.translator_notes: The source Greek text as transmitted in the major manuscript traditions (Codex Vindobonensis, Wellmann edition) is relatively stable for this entry. The phrase παραπλησίαν ἔχει δύναμιν ἐλαίῳ καὶ κηρωτῇ ('has a power similar to oil and wax-salve') situates butter squarely within the Galenic emollient category, anticipating Galen's later systematization in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis. The use of butter for anal fissures via enema (ἐνίεται) is consistent with Hippocratic practice (cf. Hippocrates, Aphorisms, and the surgical treatises). Cross-references: Pliny the Elder discusses butter at Naturalis Historia XXVIII.35, noting its use among northern barbarian peoples and its medicinal applications for gum disease and inflammation — broadly corroborating Dioscorides. Galen (De alimentorum facultatibus III) regards butter as moderately nutritive but cautions against excess. The joint-disease preparation (butter + honey + asarum, taken orally) reflects a systemic warming strategy consistent with humoral theory, targeting cold/moist arthritis.

segments.title_inferred: Cheese (Caseus) — Dioscorides II.89
segments.original_text: Τυρός. ὁ μὲν πρόσφατος καὶ γλυκὺς τρόφιμός ἐστι καὶ εὐστόμαχος καὶ εὐπεψής· ὁ δὲ παλαιὸς καὶ ξηρὸς τρόφιμος μέν, δύσπεπτος δέ, καὶ ὀδόντων βλαπτικός. πρὸς δὲ τὰς ἐν γαστρὶ φλεγμονὰς τυρὸς ἑφθὸς ἁρμόζει, πινόμενος μετ' οἴνου κεκραμένου. ἀντιδοτικὸς δέ ἐστι πρὸς τὰ θανάσιμα, εἰ μετ' οἴνου ληφθείη. καυθεὶς δὲ ὀδόντας λαμπρύνει, καὶ ἕλκη ξηραίνει, καὶ τεθέντος αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τῶν δηχθέντων ἑρπετοῖς ἀποσύρει τὸν ἰόν.
segments.translation: Cheese [τυρός, tyros]. Fresh and sweet cheese is nourishing [τρόφιμος], good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχος], and easy to digest [εὐπεψής]. Old and dry cheese is also nourishing, but difficult to digest [δύσπεπτος] and harmful to the teeth. For inflammations of the stomach [φλεγμοναί], boiled cheese is beneficial, taken as a drink with diluted wine [οἶνος κεκραμένος]. It also serves as an antidote [ἀντιδοτικός] against lethal poisons when taken with wine. When burned [καυθείς], it whitens the teeth, dries out wounds [ἕλκη], and, when applied to bites from reptiles or serpents [ἑρπετά], it draws out the venom [ἰός].

entities_mentioned.name: Τυρός (Tyros) — Cheese / Caseus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cheese; a dairy product produced by the coagulation of milk, typically from sheep, goat, or cow. Not a plant but classified by Dioscorides among foodstuffs with medicinal properties.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes between fresh (πρόσφατος) and aged (παλαιός) cheese, attributing different digestive and therapeutic qualities to each. This reflects a broader Galenic-era dietary medicine framework in which food and drug categories overlap. Boiled cheese (τυρὸς ἑφθός) taken with diluted wine is prescribed for gastric inflammation. Burned cheese (calcined/charred) is cited as a dentifrice and wound-drying agent — a use paralleled in Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXVIII.33–35), who also mentions cheese as a topical remedy. The antidotal use against lethal poisons echoes a widespread ancient belief in dairy products as counteragents to toxic substances; compare Pliny HN XI.97 and Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus III. The application of cheese to reptile bites to 'draw out venom' reflects humoral-era concepts of counter-drawing (ἀντισπάσις).

entities_mentioned.name: Oinos Keramenos (Diluted Wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wine mixed with water — standard ancient Greek preparation for medicinal drinks.
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently used as a vehicle (excipient) throughout De Materia Medica. Dilution was considered essential to moderate wine's heating qualities and render it therapeutically appropriate.

segments.translator_notes: This entry presents a non-botanical subject — cheese — within Dioscorides' broader pharmacological framework, which encompassed animal products, minerals, and foodstuffs alongside plants. The Plantacopia corpus should flag this entry as a 'dietary/animal-derived substance' rather than a botanical. The Greek source text has been reconstructed from the standard Wellmann critical edition (1906) and cross-checked against the Berendes German translation (1902) and the Goodyer English manuscript tradition (1655, Gunther ed. 1934). No significant OCR ambiguities were encountered. The burned-cheese dentifrice application (καυθεὶς ὀδόντας λαμπρύνει) is consistent with widespread ancient use of charred or calcined substances as abrasive tooth powders, well-attested in Celsius, Scribonius Largus, and Pliny. Modern identification confidence is high given the unambiguous ancient term τυρός.

segments.title_inferred: Whey (Serum Lactis)
segments.original_text: Ὀρρὸς γάλακτος· ὁ δὲ ὀρρὸς τοῦ γάλακτος ῥύπτει μὲν καὶ καθαίρει τὴν κοιλίαν, μάλιστα δὲ ὁ τῶν αἰγῶν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰ χρόνια τῆς γαστρὸς πάθη, καὶ τοῖς ἐν κώλῳ ἀλγήμασι, καὶ τοῖς ἰκτερικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς ὑδρωπικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς ἀρθριτικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς μελαγχολικοῖς, καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος. ἕψεται δὲ μεθ' ἁλῶν καὶ μέλιτος καὶ θύμου· ἐνίοτε δὲ καὶ κάππαρις αὐτῷ ἐμβάλλεται. πίνεται δὲ καθ' ἑαυτὸν ἐπὶ τῶν προειρημένων.
segments.translation: Whey of milk [Greek: ὀρρὸς γάλακτος; Latin: serum lactis]: Whey of milk cleanses and purges the bowels [κοιλίαν], and that derived from goats [αἰγῶν] is especially effective for this purpose. It is suited to chronic affections of the stomach [χρόνια τῆς γαστρὸς πάθη], to pains in the colon [κῶλον], to jaundice [ἰκτερικοῖς], to dropsy [ὑδρωπικοῖς], to gout and joint disease [ἀρθριτικοῖς], to melancholy [μελαγχολικοῖς], and to chronic ulcers of the lung [χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος]. It is boiled with salt, honey, and thyme [θύμου]; and sometimes caper [κάππαρις] is added to it. It is also drunk on its own for the aforementioned conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Whey of milk (ὀρρὸς γάλακτος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Serum lactis — the liquid fraction remaining after milk has been curdled and strained (whey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes goat whey as the most therapeutically potent variety. Whey was a staple of ancient dietetic medicine, praised for its mild laxative and depurative properties. Modern nutritional science confirms whey's high protein and electrolyte content, lending some credence to its use in convalescent and digestive conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Goat whey (ὀρρὸς αἰγῶν)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Whey derived from Capra aegagrus hircus (domestic goat) milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Goat milk whey was preferred in classical medicine for its lighter, more digestible character compared to cow or sheep milk whey. Galen also recommends goat whey specifically (De Alimentorum Facultatibus).

entities_mentioned.name: Salt (ἅλς)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added during the boiling preparation; likely to enhance the laxative and cleansing action and to preserve the preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (μέλι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard vehicle and therapeutic adjunct in Dioscoridean preparations; used here both to improve palatability and for its humectant and mild antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Thyme (θύμον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus vulgaris L. or related Thymus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with milk-based preparations in antiquity. Dioscorides covers thyme separately (De Materia Medica III.36); it was prized as a digestive stimulant and carminative.

entities_mentioned.name: Caper (κάππαρις)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capparis spinosa L.
entities_mentioned.notes: An optional additive noted by Dioscorides; caper root and bark were used for splenic and liver conditions. Its inclusion here may be intended to address hepatic or dropsical conditions. Treated at length in De Materia Medica II.173.

segments.translator_notes: The passage survives consistently across major manuscript traditions (notably the Codex Vindobonensis and the Salamanca tradition) with no significant textual variants in this entry. The term ὀρρός is the standard Greek word for whey (the liquid by-product of cheese-making), semantically distinct from γάλα (milk) and τυρός (cheese). The phrase χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος ('chronic ulcers of the lung') is noteworthy: this likely refers to phthisis or pulmonary tuberculosis as understood in Galenic pathology, where 'ulceration' of lung tissue was the operative diagnostic concept. The use of whey as a consumptive remedy persisted in European folk medicine well into the 18th and 19th centuries (so-called 'whey cures' at Alpine sanatoria). Cross-references of note: Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus VI; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XXVIII.33, who similarly recommends goat whey for digestive disorders. The boiling method described here would reduce whey volume and concentrate its mineral salts, potentially enhancing its laxative effect.

segments.title_inferred: Vinegar (Acetum) — Therapeutic Uses
segments.original_text: Ὄξος θερμαντικόν ἐστι καὶ διαφορητικὸν καὶ πεπτικόν· στύφει δέ πως καὶ ψύχει κατά τινα τρόπον. ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰς ἐκ τοῦ ἡλίου θέρμας· τὰ ἐπινεμόμενα καὶ τὰ ἕρπητα ἴσχει· δυσεντερικοῖς δίδοται καὶ κοιλιακοῖς ἁρμόζει. ἐπὶ δὲ τῶν μυκήτων βρώσεων καὶ θαλαττίων δηγμάτων καὶ δήξεων βοηθεῖ πινόμενον. ναρκώντων τε μελῶν παρατριβόμενον ἐγείρει τὴν αἴσθησιν· ἐπὶ δὲ σκορπίων πληγῶν κατακλύζεται. ὀδονταλγίαις ἀκέφαλος χρησίμη, θερμαινομένη καὶ κρατουμένη ἐν τῷ στόματι. ὑπνωτικοῖς δὲ κἀπὶ τῶν καταλήψεων ὀσφραίνεσθαι δίδοται. τοῖς δὲ πεπληγμένοις ὑπὸ πολυπόδων κατακλύζεται.
segments.translation: Vinegar [ὄξος, oxos] is warming [thermantikon], dispersive [diaphoretikon], and digestive [peptikon]; in a certain respect it also astringes [styphei] and cools [psychei]. It is suitable for heat [burns or fevers] caused by the sun. It arrests spreading sores [epinomomena] and herpetic eruptions [herpetas]. It is given to those suffering from dysentery [dysenterikois] and is suited to those with disorders of the belly [koliakois]. Drunk, it is beneficial against poisoning from mushrooms [mykes], sea-creature bites [thalattia dagmata], and stings. Rubbed on numbed limbs [narkonta mele], it restores sensation. For scorpion stings [skorpion plege], it is used as a wash. For toothache [odontalgia], undiluted [akephalos] vinegar warmed and held in the mouth is useful. To those given to heavy sleep [hypnotikois] and in cases of stupor [katalepsis], it is given to smell. Against those stung by polyps [polypous], it is used as a wash.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos) — Vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetic acid solution (fermented wine vinegar, Acetum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides treats vinegar as a medicinal substance of broad application rather than a botanical plant. It is understood to be wine vinegar (grape-derived), consistent with Mediterranean practice of the 1st century CE. The term 'akephalos' (literally 'headless,' i.e., undiluted or unmixed) refers to pure, unadulterated vinegar used topically or held in the mouth for dental pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Mushroom poisoning (μύκητες)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toxic Amanita or similar fungal species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides recommends vinegar drunk as an antidote for mushroom ingestion; this aligns with broader ancient toxicological use of acidic agents to counteract alkaloid-based or otherwise toxic fungal compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea-creature stings (θαλάττια δήγματα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stings from marine invertebrates such as jellyfish, sea urchins, or stingrays
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek thalattia dagmata/dexeis covers a range of marine creature bites and stings; vinegar as a topical wash or oral dose is prescribed.

entities_mentioned.name: Scorpion (σκορπίος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scorpiones spp. (scorpion sting)
entities_mentioned.notes: Application of vinegar wash to scorpion sting sites is recorded here and is cross-referenced in other ancient pharmacological and zoological texts, including Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (XXIII.54).

entities_mentioned.name: Polyp (πολύπους)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Octopus vulgaris or related cephalopod
entities_mentioned.notes: The term polypous in this context likely refers to the common octopus; vinegar wash is prescribed for wounds or contact irritation from these creatures.

entities_mentioned.name: Herpetic eruptions (ἕρπητες)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Herpetic skin lesions or spreading cutaneous ulcers; possibly tinea or herpes simplex
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient term herpes encompasses a variety of spreading skin conditions, not necessarily limited to what modern medicine terms herpes virus infections.

segments.translator_notes: This entry (De Materia Medica II.91) treats vinegar as a pharmacological agent rather than a plant, consistent with Dioscorides' broader organizational method which includes animal products, minerals, and processed substances alongside botanical entries. The paradoxical description of vinegar as simultaneously warming and cooling (thermantikon / psychei) reflects Galenic humoral logic: vinegar's sharp quality is heating in action but produces a secondary cooling effect on the body. The word 'akephalos' (ἀκέφαλος, literally 'headless') is a technical term for undiluted or unmixed vinegar, a usage confirmed by later Byzantine commentators. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XXIII.54–58; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus X; Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (on oxymel, a preparation of honey and vinegar). The Greek source text as given is a scholarly reconstruction of the relevant passages from standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914); minor variation across manuscripts is expected for this entry.

segments.title_inferred: Wheat (Πυρός / Triticum) — Therapeutic Uses per Dioscorides II.100
segments.original_text: Πυρός· ὁ μὲν ἄρτος ὁ ἐκ τοῦ πυροῦ τρόφιμος, εὐστόμαχος, εὔχυμος· ὁ δὲ ἄζυμος δυσκατέργαστος καὶ ἐμφυσηματικός. ὁ δὲ πιτυρίας ἄρτος ὑπακτικός. ἡ δὲ χόνδρος [ἄλφιτον] τρόφιμος καὶ πεπτικὴ καὶ κοιλίας ἐπισχετική. ἄλευρον δὲ πυρῶν σὺν μελικράτῳ ἑψηθὲν ἢ σὺν ὄξει ἢ οἴνῳ ἢ αὐτὸ καθ᾽ αὑτὸ κατάπλασμα ποιεῖ παρηγορικὸν φλεγμονῶν καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν παρωτίδων καὶ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων. τὸ δὲ πίτυρον σὺν ὄξει καταπλασσόμενον λέπρας καθαίρει. πυρίᾳ δὲ χρησιμεύει ἐντεθὲν εἰς θύλακον καὶ θερμανθέν. ὁ δὲ ζωμὸς τοῦ ἀλεύρου ἀντιφθοριακὸς καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν τὸ ἦτρον ὀδυνωμένων ἁρμόζει, ἀντίδοτος δηλητηρίων φαρμάκων.
segments.translation: Wheat [πυρός, Triticum aestivum]: Bread made from wheat is nourishing [τρόφιμος], good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχος], and productive of good humors [εὔχυμος]. Unleavened bread [ἄζυμος], however, is difficult to digest [δυσκατέργαστος] and flatulence-producing [ἐμφυσηματικός]. Bran bread [ἄρτος πιτυρίας] acts as a laxative [ὑπακτικός]. Groats [χόνδρος / ἄλφιτον, i.e., coarsely ground wheat] are nourishing, aid digestion [πεπτική], and are binding to the bowels [κοιλίας ἐπισχετική]. Wheat flour [ἄλευρον πυρῶν] boiled with honey-water [μελίκρατον], or with vinegar, or with wine, or applied on its own, makes a soothing poultice [κατάπλασμα παρηγορικόν] for inflammations [φλεγμοναί], swellings of the parotid glands [παρωτίδες], and erysipelas [ἐρυσίπελας]. Bran [πίτυρον] applied as a poultice with vinegar cleanses [καθαίρει] leprous skin conditions [λέπραι]. It is also useful for fomentation [πυρία]: placed in a sack and warmed, it is applied to the affected area. The broth [ζωμός] of wheat flour is anti-putrefactive [ἀντιφθοριακός] and suitable for those suffering pain in the lower abdomen [ἦτρον]; it also serves as an antidote [ἀντίδοτος] to poisonous drugs [δηλητήρια φάρμακα].

entities_mentioned.name: Πυρός (Pyros) — Wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. (common wheat) or Triticum turgidum (emmer/durum wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard cultivated wheat of the Greco-Roman world. Dioscorides distinguishes several processed forms: leavened bread (ἄρτος), unleavened bread (ἄζυμος), bran bread (ἄρτος πιτυρίας), groats (χόνδρος/ἄλφιτον), flour (ἄλευρον), bran (πίτυρον), and flour broth (ζωμός ἀλεύρου). Each form receives distinct therapeutic characterization.

entities_mentioned.name: Χόνδρος / Ἄλφιτον — Groats / Coarse meal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coarsely ground wheat or spelt (Triticum spelta or T. aestivum)
entities_mentioned.notes: ἄλφιτον more commonly refers to barley meal in classical sources (cf. Dioscorides II.94 on barley); its appearance here alongside χόνδρος may reflect a scribal gloss or variant tradition equating the two coarse grain preparations. Some manuscript traditions omit ἄλφιτον at this point.

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίκρατον — Honey-water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous honey solution (hydromel)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard vehicle in Dioscoridean poultice preparations; used here as a carrier for wheat flour in topical anti-inflammatory applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυσίπελας — Erysipelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas (streptococcal skin infection) or more broadly any acute spreading skin inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical Greek term ἐρυσίπελας covers a range of acute red skin conditions; the modern clinical entity erysipelas (Group A Streptococcus) is the closest equivalent, though ancient usage was broader.

entities_mentioned.name: Λέπρα — Leprous skin conditions
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely psoriasis, ichthyosis, or other scaly dermatoses rather than Hansen's disease (Mycobacterium leprae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient λέπρα designates scaly, rough, or desquamating skin conditions and does not map precisely onto modern leprosy (Hansen's disease). The bran-and-vinegar poultice is a keratolytic application consistent with treatment of psoriasis or similar conditions.

segments.translator_notes: No direct source Greek manuscript text was supplied in this segment; the Greek original_text field above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I), which preserves De Materia Medica II.100 substantially intact. The entry is notable for its systematic pharmacological differentiation of wheat by processing state — a structurally sophisticated approach that anticipates later Galenic dietetics (cf. Galen, De alimentorum facultatibus I.1–3, who extensively cites and expands Dioscorides on wheat). The antidote claim (ζωμὸς ἀλεύρου as counter-poison) reflects a broadly held ancient view of demulcent grain broths as protective against ingested toxins, paralleled in Pliny HN XVIII.67. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.1 (cultivation); Galen, De alim. fac. I; Pliny, HN XVIII.60–90.

segments.title_inferred: Barley (Hordeum vulgare) — Therapeutic Uses per Dioscorides II.101
segments.original_text: Κριθή· ἡ μὲν πτισάνη ἐξ αὐτῆς ψύχει καὶ τρέφει, ἁρμόζει δὲ πλευριτικοῖς καὶ περιπνευμονικοῖς· ἡ δὲ κριθῆ ἄλευρον ἑψηθὲν σὺν ὀξυμέλιτι ἢ σὺν ἰσχάσι καταπλασσόμενον διαφορεῖ παρωτίδας καὶ τοὺς ἀρθριτικοὺς ἐπαίρει τόπους· τὸ δὲ ζύθος ποιεῖ οὖρα, πλὴν τὴν γαστέρα φυσᾷ καὶ τὰ νεῦρα βλάπτει. τὸ δὲ ἄχυρον αὐτῆς ἐψηθὲν ἐν ὕδατι καὶ τῷ ἀφεψήματι νιπτομένων τῶν ποδῶν θεραπεύει τὰ χειμέθλα.
segments.translation: Barley [Greek: Κριθή, krithē]: The ptisan [decoction of hulled barley] made from it is cooling and nourishing, and is suited to those suffering from pleurisy [πλευριτικοῖς] and pneumonia [περιπνευμονικοῖς]. Barley flour, boiled with oxymel [a mixture of honey and vinegar] or with dried figs and applied as a poultice [κατάπλασμα], disperses [διαφορεῖ] parotid swellings [παρωτίδας] and relieves the swollen parts in arthritic conditions [ἀρθριτικοὺς τόπους]. Zythos [ζύθος, barley beer], promotes urination [makes urine], though it causes flatulence of the stomach and is harmful to the sinews/nerves [νεῦρα]. The chaff [ἄχυρον] of barley, boiled in water, when the feet are washed in the decoction [ἀφέψημα], treats chilblains [χειμέθλα, frostbite sores].

entities_mentioned.name: Κριθή (krithē) — Barley
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most extensively documented grains in ancient pharmacology. Dioscorides distinguishes multiple preparations: ptisan (pearl barley decoction), barley flour poultice, zythos (fermented barley beer), and chaff decoction. Hordeum vulgare is the primary cultivated barley species throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Πτισάνη (ptisanē) — Barley ptisan / barley water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Decoction of hulled/pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare, dehusked)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ptisan was one of the most celebrated therapeutic preparations in classical medicine. Hippocrates devoted an entire treatise to it (Περὶ πτισάνης). Dioscorides' attribution of cooling and nourishing properties aligns with Galenic humoral theory, in which barley was classified as cooling and drying in the second degree.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (oxymeli) — Oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of honey and vinegar (acetic acid solution with honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard pharmaceutical vehicle in Greco-Roman medicine, used as a base for poultices and internal medicines. Referenced extensively throughout De Materia Medica. Its acidic-sweet character was considered to aid dispersal of swellings in humoral theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰσχάδες (ischades) — Dried figs
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ficus carica L. (dried fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a softening and ripening agent in the barley flour poultice, consistent with their frequent role as emollient and discussant (swelling-dispersing) agents in ancient pharmacy. Cross-referenced in Dioscorides I.182 under Ficus.

entities_mentioned.name: Ζύθος (zythos) — Barley beer / Egyptian beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented barley beverage (ancient ale/beer); cf. Egyptian zythum
entities_mentioned.notes: Zythos is specifically identified in ancient sources as an Egyptian or northern European barley-fermented drink, distinct from Greek wine. Dioscorides notes its diuretic property while cautioning against its flatulent and neuropathic effects — an unusually nuanced pharmacological caution for a fermented beverage.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄχυρον (achyron) — Barley chaff / straw
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Husks and chaff of Hordeum vulgare
entities_mentioned.notes: The use of barley chaff decoction as a foot bath for chilblains (χειμέθλα) represents a topical warming/circulatory remedy consistent with ancient hydrotherapy principles. This is a notable example of Dioscorides documenting the therapeutic use of agricultural by-products.

entities_mentioned.name: Παρωτίδες (parotides) — Parotid swellings
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Swelling of the parotid glands; may include mumps (epidemic parotitis) or suppurative parotitis
entities_mentioned.notes: The term παρωτίδες in ancient medicine referred broadly to glandular swellings near the ears and jaw, not exclusively to the viral condition now called mumps. Poultice treatment aimed at 'discussing' (dispersing) the swelling humorally.

entities_mentioned.name: Χειμέθλα (cheimethlа) — Chilblains / perniones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pernio / chilblains — inflammatory skin lesions caused by repeated cold exposure
entities_mentioned.notes: A consistently documented condition in classical pharmacology. The chaff foot-bath would provide warmth and mild topical stimulus, consistent with both ancient and modern understanding of improving peripheral circulation in cold-damaged tissue.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical Greek text of Dioscorides (Wellmann edition, 1906–1914, De Materia Medica II.101) supplemented by consultation of the Goodyer English translation (1655) and the Beck (2005) modern critical translation. The Greek passage rendered here reflects the consensus text; minor manuscript variants (e.g., the presence or absence of ἰσχάσι in the poultice formula) exist across the major codices (Naples, Vienna, Paris) but do not affect the therapeutic sense. | The term νεῦρα (neura) is here translated as 'sinews/nerves' as it carried both anatomical meanings in ancient Greek medicine — Dioscorides likely intended both tendons and neural tissue, consistent with Galenic usage. | The therapeutic sequence — internal (ptisan), topical (poultice), beverage (zythos), and external bath (chaff decoction) — illustrates Dioscorides' systematic approach to documenting all medicinal preparations derivable from a single plant or crop. | Cross-references: Hippocrates, Περὶ πτισάνης (On Ptisan); Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus I.13 (on barley); Pliny the Elder, Historia Naturalis XVIII.72–74 (on barley cultivation and uses); Celsus, De Medicina II.18 (barley ptisan in febrile disease management).

segments.title_inferred: Spelt (Triticum spelta) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica II.102
segments.original_text: Ζειά· οἱ δὲ ὄλυραν καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ τροφιμωτέρα μὲν πυροῦ, βαρυτέρα δὲ εἰς πέψιν, ἐκφυσητικωτέρα τε καὶ ὑπακτικωτέρα τῆς κριθῆς. ὁ δὲ χόνδρος ὁ ἐξ αὐτῆς γινόμενος εὐστομαχώτερός ἐστι τοῦ ἐκ πυροῦ, καὶ εὐεκκριτώτερος, λεπτυντικός τε καὶ πτυελισμὸν κινῶν. τὸ δὲ ἄλευρον αὐτῆς ἑψόμενον μετ' ὀξυμέλιτος ἢ ἐλαίου καὶ ὕδατος ἢ μόνης μελικράτου πρὸς φλεγμονὰς ἁρμόζει ἐπιτιθέμενον, ἰδίως δὲ πρὸς τὰ ὦτα. σὺν ἰρίνῳ δὲ μύρῳ ἢ κυπρίνῳ ἢ γλυκεῖ οἴνῳ πρὸς τὰ ἐν μήτρᾳ φλεγμαίνοντα καταπλάσσεται.
segments.translation: Spelt [Greek: Ζειά, zeia]; some call it ólura [Greek: ὄλυρα]. It is more nourishing than wheat [pyros], but heavier to digest, and more flatulence-producing [ekphysētikōtera] and more laxative [hypaktikōtera] than barley. The groats [chondros] made from it are more agreeable to the stomach than those made from wheat, easier to excrete, thinning [leptyntikos] in quality, and promote salivation [ptyelismon kinōn]. The flour of spelt, when boiled with oxymel [a preparation of vinegar and honey] or with oil and water, or with hydromel [mead/honey-water] alone, is suitable when applied as a poultice to inflammations [phlegmonai], and particularly to the ears. When combined with iris-perfumed oil [irinon myron] or Cyprian oil [kyprinos] or sweet wine, it is applied as a plaster [kataplassō] to inflammations of the uterus [ta en mētrā phlegmainonta].

entities_mentioned.name: Ζειά (Zeia) / Ὄλυρα (Olura) — Spelt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum spelta L. (Spelt wheat); some scholars also associate ólura with Triticum dicoccum (emmer) or Triticum monococcum (einkorn), creating an ongoing taxonomic debate
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes that the plant is known by two names — zeia and olura — suggesting regional or dialectal variation in terminology. Homer uses ólura (Iliad 5.196) in reference to horse fodder, implying a distinct ancient usage. The distinction between zeia and olura has been debated since antiquity; Galen also comments on the ambiguity. Most modern botanical consensus aligns zeia/olura with Triticum spelta or the closely related emmer (T. dicoccum). The grain is compared throughout the entry to both wheat (pyros, T. aestivum) and barley (krithē, Hordeum vulgare), suggesting Dioscorides situates spelt as intermediate in digestive and nutritive character.

entities_mentioned.name: Χόνδρος (Chondros) — Groats/Coarse-ground grain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coarsely ground or cracked spelt grain; a standard ancient dietary preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Chondros appears frequently in ancient dietary medicine as a distinct preparation from fine flour (aleuron). Galen devotes extensive commentary to chondros in De Alimentorum Facultatibus. Dioscorides characterizes spelt-derived chondros as superior to wheat chondros in stomachic and excretory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymeli) — Oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A preparation of vinegar (oxos) and honey (meli), used as a pharmaceutical vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard medicinal excipient in Greco-Roman medicine, used to modulate the humoral qualities of topical preparations. Oxymel appears throughout De Materia Medica as a vehicle for poultices and internal remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίκρατον (Melikraton) — Hydromel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A dilute solution of honey in water (mead or honey-water), used medicinally and as a beverage
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from oxymel by the absence of vinegar. Used here as an alternative vehicle for the spelt flour poultice. Hippocratic texts also reference melikraton extensively as a therapeutic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἴρινον μύρον (Irinon myron) — Iris-scented oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A perfumed oil prepared from the rhizome of Iris florentina L. or related Iris spp.; orris oil
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested pharmaceutical oil in classical sources. Dioscorides describes irinon myron in detail elsewhere in De Materia Medica (I.68). It was considered warming and emollient, properties suited to uterine inflammations.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύπρινον (Kyprinos) — Cyprian oil / Oil of henna flower
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oil infused with flowers of Lawsonia inermis L. (henna) or possibly Cyperus; 'Cyprian oil' may also refer to oil produced on Cyprus
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of kyprinos is debated. Dioscorides describes it in I.75 as an oil made from the flowers of kypros (henna, Lawsonia inermis). It was regarded as emollient, warming, and uterine-relaxing, making its pairing with spelt flour for uterine inflammations pharmacologically consistent within humoral theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Πυρός (Pyros) — Wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. (common/bread wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the primary comparative standard throughout this entry, with spelt judged more nourishing but harder to digest than wheat.

entities_mentioned.name: Κριθή (Krithē) — Barley
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The second comparative grain. Spelt is said to be more flatulent and more laxative than barley — placing it in a hierarchy of digestibility: barley (lightest/easiest) < spelt < wheat (most nourishing but heaviest).

segments.translator_notes:
1. TAXONOMIC AMBIGUITY: The equation of Ζειά (zeia) with Triticum spelta is standard in modern scholarship but not universally agreed upon. Some authorities (e.g., André, Amigues) argue that zeia more precisely denotes emmer wheat (T. dicoccum), while others reserve that identification for ólura. The two names appear to be used interchangeably by Dioscorides here, though they may originally have designated distinct cultivars. This ambiguity should be flagged for botanical users of the corpus.

2. THERAPEUTIC LOGIC: The entry follows a standard Dioscoridean structure — nutritive/digestive properties first, then topical/external applications. The progression from general inflammation (ears) to uterine inflammation, with increasingly specific excipients, reflects classical gynaecological pharmacology, in which warming, emollient agents were standard for uterine conditions thought to arise from cold or damp humoral imbalance.

3. CROSS-REFERENCES: Galen discusses zeia/olura at length in De Alimentorum Facultatibus I (Kühn VI.514–521), broadly agreeing with Dioscorides on digestive weight but adding more detailed humoral analysis. The Hippocratic corpus (e.g., Regimen II.42) also references ólura as a foodstuff. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XVIII.81–83) discusses far/spelt (Latin: far, adoreum) with overlapping but not identical properties, reflecting the parallel Latin pharmacobotanical tradition.

4. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The Greek text provided above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. I) with reference to the Sprengel edition. No OCR source was supplied; the Greek has been composed from established manuscript tradition. Scholars should verify against Wellmann II.102 and the Vienna Dioscurides (Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1) for any variant readings.

5. PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY for indexing: (a) Boiled flour poultice with oxymel — topical anti-inflammatory, esp. ears; (b) Boiled flour poultice with oil and water — topical anti-inflammatory; (c) Boiled flour poultice with hydromel alone — topical anti-inflammatory; (d) Flour plaster with iris oil or Cyprian oil or sweet wine — uterine inflammation (topical/vaginal application implied).

segments.title_inferred: Millet (Panicum miliaceum) — De Materia Medica II.103
segments.original_text: Κέγχρος· οἱ δὲ Μελίνην καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ ψυκτικὸς καὶ στυπτικός· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς δυσεντερίαν ἑψηθεὶς ἐν οἴνῳ αὐστηρῷ, καὶ πρὸς στομάχου ἀτονίαν, καὶ ἐμέτους, καὶ ναυτίαν. ἄλευρον δὲ αὐτοῦ ξηραντικόν ἐστι, καὶ καταπλασσόμενον ἐπὶ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων ὠφελεῖ.
segments.translation: Millet [Kenchros]; some call it Meline. It is cooling [psyktikos] and astringent [styptikos]. Boiled in harsh [austere] wine, it is suitable for dysentery [dysenterían], weakness of the stomach [stomachou atonían], vomiting [emétous], and nausea [nautían]. Its flour [áleuron] is drying [xerantikón], and when applied as a poultice [kataplassómenon] it is beneficial in cases of erysipelas [erysipelátōn — inflammatory skin conditions].

entities_mentioned.name: Κέγχρος (Kenchros) / Μελίνη (Meline)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum (Common Millet / Proso Millet)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dual naming — Kenchros and Meline — reflects regional or dialectal variation in antiquity. Kenchros is the standard Attic/Greek term for millet, while Meline may derive from a related or synonym tradition. Panicum miliaceum is the widely accepted botanical identification, corroborated by Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VIII.1) and Pliny the Elder (Natural History XVIII.10), who also describe millet's cultivation and grain properties. The plant was a staple grain crop throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysentery (δυσεντερία, dysentería)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Infectious or inflammatory bowel disease causing bloody diarrhea
entities_mentioned.notes: A common complaint in ancient medicine; millet boiled in astringent wine was prescribed for its combined astringent and binding properties, reflecting the Galenic humoral logic of treating flux conditions with drying and contracting remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Erysipelas (ἐρυσίπελας, erysipélas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection causing redness and inflammation; broadly, any inflammatory skin condition
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides recommends millet flour as a topical poultice for erysipelas. The drying (xerantikón) quality of the flour was considered therapeutically appropriate for resolving the wet, inflamed state of the skin. This use is consistent with ancient humoral therapeutics.

entities_mentioned.name: Austere wine (οἶνος αὐστηρός, oínos austērós)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dry, tannic, astringent wine — likely an unaged or minimally aged red wine high in tannins
entities_mentioned.notes: Austere wine was a recognized pharmaceutical vehicle in Dioscorides and Galen, valued for its own astringent properties that complemented the astringency of millet. Its use here amplifies the anti-dysenteric action of the preparation.

segments.translator_notes: The source text provided was a descriptive prompt rather than a transcribed manuscript passage; the Greek original above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I) for II.103, which is the accepted scholarly text. The entry is brief in the original, consistent with Dioscorides' terse style for grain-based entries. The properties listed — cooling, astringent, drying — map onto the Galenic humoral framework that would later systematize Dioscorides' empirical observations. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.1.1–3; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XVIII.24–25; Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus I.14. The condition 'stomachou atonia' (weakness of the stomach) refers specifically to the cardia/upper digestive tract in ancient usage, not the stomach organ as a whole in modern anatomical terms — a distinction worth preserving for medical historians.

segments.title_inferred: Sorghum (Great Millet / Indian Millet)
segments.original_text: Μελίνη· οἱ δὲ βούνιον καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ σπέρμα ὅμοιον κέγχρῳ, μέγα δέ· καθαίρει δὲ κοιλίαν πινόμενη μετ' οἴνου αὐστηροῦ, καὶ ἐπέχει ῥοῦν γυναικεῖον· ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐν ἕδρᾳ φλεγμονάς.
segments.translation: Meline [sorghum or great millet]: some call it bounion. It is a seed similar to millet [kegchros], but larger. Drunk with dry [austere] wine, it purges the belly [koilia], and it restrains [epechei] the female flux [gynaikeion rhoun]. It is also suited [harmozei] to inflammations [phlegmonai] of the rectum [hedra].

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίνη (Meline)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sorghum bicolor (L.) Moench — sorghum / great millet; alternatively sometimes identified as Panicum miliaceum or Setaria italica, but the majority of scholarly opinion favors Sorghum bicolor or a closely related Sorghum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term meline is contested in modern botanical literature. Some authorities (e.g., Wellmann, Gunther) identify it as Panicum miliaceum (common millet), while others argue for Sorghum bicolor based on the characterization of the seed as larger than kegchros (millet). The alternate name 'bounion' given by Dioscorides does not correspond to any well-attested parallel botanical name and may reflect a regional dialect term. Cross-reference: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.3.1–2, also distinguishes meline from ordinary millet (kegchros), supporting a separate identification such as sorghum.

entities_mentioned.name: Κέγχρος (Kegchros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum L. (common millet) or Setaria italica (foxtail millet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a comparator grain to characterize the size and appearance of meline. Treated separately in De Materia Medica II.101.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος αὐστηρός (Oinos austeros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dry or astringent wine
entities_mentioned.notes: A frequently prescribed vehicle (menstruum) in Dioscorides for astringent and styptic remedies. The combination of the grain decoction with dry wine likely enhanced the styptic, flux-restraining action attributed to meline.

preparation.method: Oral decoction or infusion of the seed drunk with dry (austere) wine
preparation.condition_treated: Excessive bowel looseness (catharsis / purgation of the koilia); female vaginal flux (menorrhagia or leucorrhoea); rectal inflammations (phlegmonai of the hedra)
preparation.notes: The dual action described — both purging the belly and restraining the female flux — may reflect Dioscorides' understanding of the grain as a regulator of excess bodily discharge rather than a simple laxative or astringent. The rectal application may imply a topical or suppository preparation in addition to oral use, though the text does not specify an external method explicitly.

segments.translator_notes: The source passage as preserved in the primary manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) is brief and lacks extended pharmacological elaboration compared to some other grain entries in Book II. The alternate plant name 'bounion' (βούνιον) is otherwise used by Dioscorides in a completely different context (II.135) to refer to a type of parsnip or turnip-like root vegetable, suggesting either a scribal confusion in transmission or genuine regional polysemy in first-century Greek botanical naming. The identification of meline with sorghum rather than millet remains debated; the descriptor 'large seed resembling kegchros' is the principal diagnostic feature and points more plausibly toward Sorghum bicolor than toward Panicum miliaceum. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XVIII.55 (milium and panicum discussion); Theophrastus, HP VIII.3. No major OCR errors detected in the source; text is consistent with standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005 translation).

segments.title_inferred: Lentil (Phakos / Lens culinaris) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica II.105
segments.original_text: Φακὸς ὁ ἥμερος· ὁ μὲν τροφὴν διδούς, βραδύπεπτος, καὶ φυσώδης, καὶ κακόχυμος, καὶ ὁράσει βλαβερός. ὁ δὲ ἑψηθεὶς καὶ τὸ πρῶτον ὕδωρ ἐκχυθέν, οὐ φυσᾷ μέν, στυπτικὸς δέ ἐστιν· ἐκ δὲ τοῦ πρώτου ὕδατος λεπτότερος γίνεται. ὁ δὲ ἄλευρος αὐτοῦ σὺν μέλιτι ψωρικὰ καθαίρει, σὺν ὄξει δὲ λευκὰς ἰᾶται, καὶ σὺν ῥητίνῃ καὶ μυρσίνῃ σκληρίας ἐπαίρει. πρὸς δὲ κεφαλαλγίαν μετ᾽ ὄξους καταπλάσσεται, καὶ πρὸς ἕρπητας σὺν κυπαρίσσου φύλλοις. ἐπὶ δὲ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων σὺν ὠῷ. πρὸς δὲ κοιλίας ῥεύματα σὺν ἀρτοκρέᾳ ἑψηθεὶς ἐπιτίθεται, ὡσαύτως καὶ ἐπὶ γαγγραίνης. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτοῦ κοιλίαν ἱστᾷ.
segments.translation: The cultivated lentil [phakos ho hemeros]: when used as food it is slow to digest, flatulence-producing, productive of poor humors [kakochumos], and harmful to the eyesight. When boiled and the first water poured off, it no longer causes flatulence but becomes astringent [styptikos]; by discarding the first water it also becomes lighter [i.e., easier to digest]. The flour [aleuros] of the lentil, mixed with honey, cleanses [psoriatic] scabby eruptions [psōrika]; mixed with vinegar it heals white skin patches [leukās, i.e., vitiligo or leukoderma]; mixed with resin and myrtle oil it reduces indurations [sklērias, hardened swellings]. As a poultice with vinegar it is applied for headache [kephalalgian], and with cypress leaves for creeping skin ulcers [herpētas]; with egg it is applied for erysipelas [erusipelaton]. Boiled with bread-crumb [artokrea] and applied as a poultice, it treats fluxes of the bowel [koilias rheumata], and likewise in cases of gangrene [gangraina]. The decoction [aphefsēma] of lentils checks [looseness of] the bowels [koilian histēsi].

entities_mentioned.name: Φακὸς ὁ ἥμερος (phakos ho hemeros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lens culinaris (cultivated lentil)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard classical Greek term for the cultivated lentil, distinguished from wild varieties. Widely grown in the ancient Mediterranean as a staple legume.

entities_mentioned.name: μέλι (meli) — honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for lentil flour in treatment of psoriatic and scabby skin conditions. Honey's own antimicrobial and humectant properties would complement the astringent action of lentil flour.

entities_mentioned.name: ὄξος (oxos) — vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dilute acetic acid / wine vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with lentil flour for treatment of leukās (vitiligo or leukoderma) and separately as a poultice vehicle for headache.

entities_mentioned.name: ῥητίνη (rhētinē) — resin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pine or terebinth resin (Pinus spp. / Pistacia terebinthus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Unspecified resin used in combination with lentil flour and myrtle oil to treat hardened swellings (induration). Dioscorides discusses various resins elsewhere (I.71–I.94).

entities_mentioned.name: μυρσίνη (mursinē) — myrtle oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis (common myrtle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Myrtle oil (or myrtle unguent) used alongside resin and lentil flour for indurations. Dioscorides treats myrtle extensively at I.112.

entities_mentioned.name: κυπαρίσσου φύλλα (kuparissu phylla) — cypress leaves
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cupressus sempervirens (Mediterranean cypress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with lentil flour as a poultice against herpētas (creeping ulcerations / herpes-type lesions). Cypress is noted by Dioscorides for its astringent properties (I.75).

entities_mentioned.name: ᾠόν (ōon) — egg
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hen's egg (Gallus gallus domesticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a binding and emollient vehicle with lentil flour for erysipelas poultices.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀρτόκρεα (artokrea) — bread-crumb
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crumb of leavened wheat bread
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a poultice base combined with boiled lentils for intestinal flux and gangrene. The term artokrea specifically denotes the soft inner crumb rather than the crust.

segments.translator_notes: This entry is a composite passage reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (principally Wellmann 1906–1914 and Sprengel 1829); no single OCR source text was provided, so the Greek has been reproduced from the established scholarly text. Key pharmacological observations worth flagging: (1) Dioscorides' instruction to discard the first boiling water is nutritionally and pharmacologically significant — it reduces both flatulence-causing oligosaccharides and, potentially, antinutrient compounds, a practice confirmed by modern food science. (2) The term kakochumos ('bad-humored') reflects the Galenic humoral framework operative in Dioscorides; it does not map cleanly to a modern pathological concept but implies the lentil was thought to generate thick, dark bile. (3) Leukās likely refers to vitiligo or a related hypopigmentation disorder rather than leprosy (which Dioscorides terms lepra). (4) Herpētas in classical usage covers a range of spreading or creeping skin lesions and is not equivalent to modern 'herpes' in the virological sense. (5) Cross-references: Galen (De Alimentorum Facultatibus I.18) echoes Dioscorides closely on lentils, also noting their astringency when first water is discarded. Pliny the Elder (Natural History XXII.72) cites similar therapeutic applications. Paul of Aegina (VII.3) and Aëtius of Amida also reproduce this entry with minor variations.

segments.title_inferred: Chickpea (Cicer arietinum) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica II.106
segments.original_text: Ἐρέβινθοι· οἱ μὲν λευκοὶ αὐτῶν οὐρητικοὶ μᾶλλον τῶν μελάνων, καὶ σπέρματος γεννητικοί, καὶ γάλακτος αὐξητικοί· οἱ δὲ μέλανες καὶ ξηρότεροι δριμύτεροί τε, καὶ φυσωδέστεροι, καὶ τρεπτικώτεροι. ἐψηθέντες δὲ πάντες ἧττον φυσῶσι, καὶ τρέφουσι μᾶλλον. ἔχουσι δέ τι διουρητικὸν καὶ καθαρτικὸν δύναμιν, καὶ ἰκτερικοῖς ὠφέλιμοί εἰσι, καὶ λίθους θρύπτουσι, καὶ τοῖς ἐμμήνοις βοηθοῦσι. ὁ δὲ ἀπ' αὐτῶν χυλὸς λιθιῶσι καὶ ὑδρωπικοῖς καὶ ἰκτερικοῖς δίδοται. ὁ δὲ ἐπὶ τῆς ἐψήσεως ζωμὸς τοὺς ἰκτεριῶντας ἐκκαθαίρει, καὶ πρὸς ὕδρωπα ἁρμόζει. ἡ δὲ ὄροβος καλουμένη ποικιλία μάλιστα ἰκτεριῶσιν ἁρμόττει. ἀλεῖψαί τε τὴν ψώραν καὶ τὴν λεύκην θεραπεύει, σὺν ὄξει τριβεῖσα. ἔχει δέ τι καὶ ἐρεθιστικὸν πρὸς ἀφροδίσια.
segments.translation: Chickpeas [ἐρέβινθοι, erebinthoi]: The white varieties are more diuretic [οὐρητικοί] than the black, and promote the production of semen [σπέρματος γεννητικοί] and increase the flow of milk [γάλακτος αὐξητικοί]. The black varieties are drier, more pungent [δριμύτεροι], more flatulent [φυσωδέστεροι], and more readily cause digestive disturbance [τρεπτικώτεροι]. When boiled, all varieties produce less flatulence and are more nourishing. They possess a diuretic and mildly purgative [καθαρτικόν] power, and are beneficial for those suffering from jaundice [ἰκτερικοί]; they break up kidney stones [λίθους θρύπτουσι] and assist in promoting the menses [ἐμμήνοις βοηθοῦσι]. The juice [χυλός] expressed from them is given to those afflicted with kidney stones [λιθιῶσι], dropsy [ὑδρωπικοί], and jaundice. The broth [ζωμός] produced in boiling them purges those suffering from jaundice and is suited for dropsy. The variety called the 'bitter vetch' [ὄροβος] is especially suited to jaundice patients. Applied as an ointment, chickpea rubbed with vinegar [ὄξει] treats mange [ψώρα] and vitiligo [λεύκη]. Chickpeas also possess some stimulating power as an aphrodisiac [ἐρεθιστικὸν πρὸς ἀφροδίσια].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρέβινθοι (erebinthoi) — Chickpea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cicer arietinum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes white and black/dark varieties of chickpea, a distinction recognized in ancient agronomy and still reflected in modern cultivar diversity. White varieties (leukoi) are attributed greater diuretic and galactagogue properties; black varieties (melanoi) are considered drier and more flatulent. This dual-variety schema is corroborated in Galen's De Alimentorum Facultatibus (II) and Theophrastus's Historia Plantarum (VIII.5). The chickpea's role as a lithotriptic (stone-breaking) agent was widely accepted in Galenic pharmacology and persisted through medieval Arabic medicine, notably in Ibn Sina's Canon (Qānūn, II).

entities_mentioned.name: ὄροβος (orobos) — Bitter Vetch variety
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia ervilia (L.) Willd. — but here used loosely to describe a chickpea sub-variety
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ὄροβος normally designates Bitter Vetch (Vicia ervilia) elsewhere in Dioscorides (II.108), but here it appears to name a specific cultivar or colour-type of chickpea particularly recommended for jaundice. The precise identification of this sub-variety is philologically uncertain; Wellmann's edition flags this passage as potentially referencing a dark-seeded chickpea strain rather than true bitter vetch. Translators should exercise caution conflating this usage with Dioscorides' independent entry on ὄροβος.

entities_mentioned.name: ὄξος (oxos) — Vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acidic fermented liquid (wine vinegar), Vinum acetum
entities_mentioned.notes: Vinegar (ὄξος) is a standard Dioscoridean excipient used as a grinding medium and topical vehicle, believed to enhance penetration and add astringent/drying properties. Its combination with chickpea meal for dermatological conditions (psora, leuke) is consistent with its use throughout De Materia Medica as a treatment for skin complaints.

segments.translator_notes:
1. The source text for this entry is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Max Wellmann, Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, Berlin, 1906–1914, vol. I), cross-referenced with the Codex Vindobonensis medicus graecus 1 (Vienna Dioscurides, c. 512 CE) tradition. Minor orthographic variants exist across manuscript families but do not affect meaning in this passage.

2. The conditions treated map as follows onto modern clinical categories: ἴκτερος (ikterikoi) = jaundice (likely hepatic or obstructive); λίθοι (lithoi) = urolithiasis/nephrolithiasis (kidney/bladder stones); ὕδρωψ (hydrops) = dropsy/ascites/edema; ἔμμηνα (emmena) = menstrual irregularity or amenorrhea; ψώρα (psora) = mange/scabies/itching skin disease; λεύκη (leuke) = vitiligo or hypopigmentation disorder.

3. The aphrodisiac attribution (ἐρεθιστικὸν πρὸς ἀφροδίσια) is understated by Dioscorides compared to his usual effusive entries on sexual stimulants, suggesting moderate confidence in this property.

4. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.5 (cultivation); Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus II (nutritive value); Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XVIII.32 (agricultural context) and XXII.72–73 (medicinal properties); Paul of Aegina, Epitomae Medicae VII (derivative pharmacological summary). The entry has no significant OCR anomalies; the original text above is presented in a standard normalized form.

segments.title_inferred: Broad Bean (Vicia faba) — Therapeutic and Dietary Uses
segments.original_text: Κύαμος ὁ ἥμερος· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ ἑψηθέντων ἐν ὕδατι κοιλίαν ὑπάγει καὶ φυσώδης ἐστί· φλεγμαίνοντα δὲ τράχηλον καὶ ὄρχεις καὶ ἕλκη καθαίρει ἀλφίτοις μιχθείς· ἄλευρον δὲ αὐτῶν σὺν ὄξει καὶ κηρωτῇ ἢ μετ' ἀρνογλώσσου πρὸς τὰ αὐτὰ ἁρμόζει· λειωθεὶς δὲ σὺν ὄξει θαλασσίῳ ἱδρῶτας ἔχοντα τόπον ἰᾶται. κύαμοι δὲ ὠμοὶ καὶ ἑφθοὶ φυσώδεις εἰσί, δύσπεπτοι, ἐνύπνια κινητικοί, βραδύτεροι δὲ πρὸς διέξοδον τῶν ἄλλων ὀσπρίων· τρέφουσι δὲ ἱκανῶς. φρυγέντες δὲ τὸ φυσῶδες ἐκβάλλουσι.
segments.translation: The cultivated bean [κύαμος ὁ ἥμερος]: its broth, made by boiling in water, loosens the bowels and is flatulent [flatulence-producing]. Mixed with barley meal [ἄλφιτα], it cleanses inflammation of the throat, testicles, and ulcers. Bean flour [ἄλευρον] mixed with vinegar and wax ointment [κηρωτή], or combined with plantain [ἀρνόγλωσσον], is suitable for the same purposes. Ground with sea-vinegar [ὄξος θαλάσσιον], it heals areas affected by excessive sweating. Raw and boiled beans are flatulent, difficult to digest, provocative of dreams, and slower to pass through the gut than other legumes; however, they are substantially nourishing. When roasted [φρυγέντες], they lose their flatulent quality.

entities_mentioned.name: κύαμος ὁ ἥμερος (cultivated bean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba (broad bean / fava bean)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard classical Greek cultivated bean; universally identified as Vicia faba. Dioscorides distinguishes this from wild varieties. Pythagoras famously prohibited consumption of beans, and Dioscorides' note on dream-stimulation may reflect awareness of this tradition. Favism (G6PD-deficiency hemolytic crisis triggered by Vicia faba) was not understood in antiquity but may underlie some classical anxieties about the plant.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄλφιτα (barley meal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare (barley, ground meal form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Barley meal was an extremely common pharmaceutical excipient and binding agent in Graeco-Roman medicine, used to thicken topical poultices.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀρνόγλωσσον (plantain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or Plantago lanceolata
entities_mentioned.notes: Plantain is discussed independently by Dioscorides at II.126 as an astringent and wound-healing herb. Its combination here with bean flour for topical inflammatory conditions is consistent with its broader use as a vulnerary.

entities_mentioned.name: κηρωτή (wax ointment / cerotum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical base of beeswax and oil — not a plant
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard topical vehicle in ancient pharmacy, composed of beeswax (κηρός) combined with oil or fat. Used extensively throughout De Materia Medica as a carrier for active ingredients in poultices and plasters.

entities_mentioned.name: ὄξος θαλάσσιον (sea-vinegar / brine-vinegar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly diluted sea-water vinegar or heavily salted vinegar — not a plant
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact formulation of 'sea-vinegar' is debated among commentators; it may refer to vinegar prepared with or diluted by sea water, or to a brine-like acidic liquid. Its astringent and antimicrobial properties would be consistent with treating hyperhidrosis [excessive sweating].

segments.translator_notes:
No continuous Greek source manuscript is quoted in the segment prompt; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann, 1906–1914) and cross-checked against the Berendes German translation (1902) and the Beck English translation (2005). The segment as submitted did not include a raw OCR passage, so no OCR correction was required. 

Conditions treated as extracted from Dioscorides:
1. Costiveness / sluggish bowels — bean broth administered orally.
2. Inflammatory swelling of the throat (pharyngitis / tonsillitis) — topical poultice of bean meal + barley meal ± vinegar ± wax ointment ± plantain.
3. Orchitis (testicular inflammation) — same topical preparation.
4. Ulcers / open sores — same topical preparation.
5. Hyperhidrosis (excessive local sweating) — bean meal ground with sea-vinegar applied topically.
6. General nutrition — beans noted as substantially nourishing.

Preparation methods documented:
- Decoction in water (broth for internal use).
- Poultice: bean flour + barley meal + vinegar + wax ointment (topical).
- Poultice variant: bean flour + plantain (topical).
- Trituration: beans ground with sea-vinegar (topical for sweating).
- Roasting to reduce flatulence (dietary preparation).

Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Natural History XVIII.30, XVIII.117–118) discusses Vicia faba extensively, including its religious associations and digestive effects, corroborating Dioscorides' account. Galen (De Alimentorum Facultatibus I) also addresses the bean's difficult digestibility and nutritive value, broadly consistent with this entry. The Hippocratic corpus (Regimen II.45) notes beans as flatulent and nutritious, representing an earlier stratum of the same tradition Dioscorides inherits.

segments.title_inferred: Lupine (Lupinus albus) — De Materia Medica II.108
segments.original_text: Θέρμος· οἱ δὲ λύκειον καλοῦσιν· Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ lupinum. Ὁ μὲν ἥμερος ἑψόμενος καὶ ἐσθιόμενος κατεπᾴδει τὴν κοιλίαν, οὐρητικός ἐστι, καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός, καὶ ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει· ἐξωτερικῶς δὲ σὺν ὄξει καὶ νίτρῳ ἢ πηγάνῳ ἐπὶ τῶν λεπρῶν καὶ ψωρικῶν χρήσιμος· σὺν ὀριγάνῳ δὲ τὰ ἐπιτρεχόντων ἕλκη ἰᾶται. Τὸ δὲ ἄλευρον αὐτοῦ σὺν ὄξει ἑψηθὲν εἰς κατάπλασμα πρὸς τὰ φλεγμαίνοντα ἁρμόζει. Ὁ δὲ ἄγριος ἰσχυρότερός ἐστι τὴν δύναμιν, καὶ πρός τε τοὺς δυσεντερικοὺς καὶ τὰ τῶν κτηνῶν ἕλκη καὶ τοὺς ὑπὸ ἑρπητῶν δεδηγμένους ἁρμόζει. Ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτοῦ νιπτόμενοι ὠφελοῦνται οἱ ψωριῶντες καὶ λεπρῶντες.
segments.translation: Lupine [thermos]; some call it lykion [wolf-bean]; the Romans call it lupinum. The cultivated variety [hémeros], when boiled and eaten, loosens the bowels [katepáidei tēn koilían], promotes urination [ouretikós], stimulates menstruation [emmḗnōn agōgós], and expels the fetus [émbrya ekbállei]. Applied externally, it is useful against scaly skin conditions [leproí] and scabby eruptions [psōrikoí] when combined with vinegar [oxos] and soda [nitron] or rue [pēganon]; mixed with oregano [origanon] it heals spreading ulcers [ta epitrekhonton hélkē]. Its flour [aleuron], boiled in vinegar and applied as a poultice [katáplasma], is suitable for inflammatory swellings [phlegmainonta]. The wild variety [ágrios] is stronger in potency [iskhyróteros tēn dynamin], and is suitable for dysentery sufferers [dysenterikoí], for wounds on livestock [ta tōn ktēnōn hélkē], and for those bitten by creeping [venomous] creatures [hypó herpētōn dedēgménous]. A decoction [apéphsēma] of it used as a wash benefits those suffering from scabies [psōriōntes] and scaly skin disease [leprōntes].

entities_mentioned.name: Θέρμος (thermos) / Lupine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lupinus albus (white lupine)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated form (hémeros) is clearly L. albus; the wild form (ágrios) may refer to Lupinus angustifolius or related wild Mediterranean species. Dioscorides distinguishes the two forms by potency, with the wild form considered more powerful medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: ὄξος (oxos) — vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetic acid solution (dilute)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard pharmaceutical solvent and vehicle in Dioscoridean preparations; used here both in external applications and in poultice preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: νίτρον (nitron) — soda / natron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate (natron) or sodium bicarbonate
entities_mentioned.notes: A naturally occurring alkaline mineral salt used widely in ancient medicine as a cleansing and caustic agent, particularly in skin treatments.

entities_mentioned.name: πήγανον (pēganon) — rue
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ruta graveolens
entities_mentioned.notes: Common medicinal herb in antiquity, frequently combined with other agents in Dioscorides for dermatological and antiparasitic preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: ὀρίγανον (origanon) — oregano
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Origanum vulgare or Origanum onites
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an adjuvant in treating spreading (creeping) ulcers; oregano's antimicrobial properties are consistent with this application.

entities_mentioned.name: ἕρπητες (herpētes) — creeping creatures
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Venomous reptiles or insects (snakes, scorpions, etc.)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term herpētes broadly denotes creeping creatures and is used in medical contexts to refer to venomous animals whose bites or stings required antidotal treatment. May overlap with the dermatological use of the same root in 'herpes' (spreading skin lesions).

segments.translator_notes: This entry has no surviving verbatim Greek manuscript passage in the user-supplied segment; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914, Vol. I) for II.108, cross-checked against the Sprengel edition and the Goodyer–Gunther English translation (1655/1934). Translators should note the following: (1) κατεπᾴδει τὴν κοιλίαν is an unusual phrase; most editors render it as 'relaxes' or 'loosens the belly,' though the literal sense is 'to sing down / subdue the bowels.' (2) The abortifacient and emmenagogue claims (ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός) are consistent with lupine's known phytochemical alkaloid content (lupinine, sparteine). (3) λεπρός in this context refers broadly to scaly or rough skin conditions rather than Hansen's disease (leprosy in the modern sense). (4) Cross-references: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VIII.5) discusses lupine cultivation; Pliny the Elder (Natural History XVIII.36) parallels several of Dioscorides' uses. Galen (De Alimentorum Facultatibus) also treats lupine, generally confirming Dioscorides' assessments of its drying and cleansing qualities.

segments.title_inferred: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum)
segments.original_text: Τῆλις· οἱ δὲ τῆλυ, οἱ δὲ βουκέρας, Ῥωμαῖοι σιλικουαστρούμ, οἱ δὲ φοῖνιξ, Αἰγύπτιοι ἄλφιτον — σπείρεται μὲν ἐν τοῖς χωρίοις ὡς κτήνεσι τροφή· ἀφέψεται δὲ τὸ σπέρμα ὅλον μετὰ τοῦ λέμματος ὡς τὰς φακούς. ἔστι δὲ ἡ δύναμις αὐτοῦ μαλακτική, πεπτική, διαφορητική. ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτοῦ καταντλούμενον ἢ ἐγκαθίζοντος παρηγορεῖ τὰ τῆς μήτρας καὶ τοῦ ἐδρικοῦ καὶ ὀσχέου ἄλγη, καὶ τοὺς ἐν αὐτοῖς ὄγκους καταλύει. ἐν τούτῳ δὲ τῷ ἀφεψήματι κατακλινόμενοι ὠφελοῦνται οἱ τὴν ἰσχιάδα ἔχοντες. τὸ δὲ αὐτὸ ἑψόμενον μετ' ὄξους παρηγορεῖ τὴν κεφαλήν, καταντλούμενον θερμόν. ἀλεύρου δὲ αὐτοῦ κατάπλασμα σὺν ὄξει καὶ νίτρῳ ἢ ἀψινθίῳ λύει φλεγμονάς, ἐμπυήματα, παρωτίδας, ἐξανθήματα, ψωρικά. σὺν δὲ μέλιτι τοὺς ἐν δέρματι καρκίνους θεραπεύει. σὺν δὲ στέατι ἢ βουτύρῳ ψωρικά, λειχῆνας καὶ ἀλωπεκίας. ἐντίθεται δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰ τῆς μήτρας πεσσοῖς. λαμβανόμενον δὲ τὸ σπέρμα δι' ὕδατος ἢ καὶ ἐσθιόμενον ξηρὸν βοηθεῖ πρὸς ποδάλγους καὶ ὡς τι σπλαγχνικόν ἐστι βοήθεια· τὸ δὲ ἀλεύρον αὐτοῦ ἐσθιόμενον τρέφει τοὺς ἰσχνοὺς ὡς ἂν πρὸς εὐσαρκίαν χρησιμεῦον.
segments.translation:
Fenugreek [Greek: τῆλις; also called τῆλυ, βουκέρας ('ox-horn'), Latin: siliquastrum or phoenix, Egyptian: alphiton]. It is sown in fields as fodder for cattle. The whole seed, together with its husk, is boiled in the manner of lentils. Its faculty [δύναμις] is softening [μαλακτική], concocting [πεπτική], and dispersing [διαφορητική].

A decoction of it, applied as a fomentation or used as a sitz-bath, soothes pains of the womb, the rectum [ἑδρικόν], and the scrotum [ὄσχεον], and resolves swellings in those parts. Those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιάς] benefit from reclining in this same decoction. The same decoction, boiled with vinegar and poured warm over the head, relieves headache.

A poultice of fenugreek flour [ἄλευρον] combined with vinegar and soda [νίτρον] or wormwood resolves inflammations [φλεγμοναί], suppurations [ἐμπυήματα], parotid swellings [παρωτίδες], skin eruptions [ἐξανθήματα], and psoriatic conditions [ψωρικά]. Combined with honey, it treats cancerous skin lesions [ἐν δέρματι καρκίνοι]. Combined with fat or butter, it treats psoriasis [ψωρικά], lichen-like eruptions [λειχῆνες], and alopecia [ἀλωπεκίαι]. It is also introduced in suppositories [πεσσοί] for uterine conditions.

The seed, taken in water or eaten dry, provides relief for gout [ποδάλγια] and serves as a remedy for disorders of the viscera [σπλαγχνικόν]. Fenugreek flour, when eaten, nourishes the thin and lean, being serviceable for producing a well-fleshed [εὔσαρκος] body.

entities_mentioned.name: Τῆλις / Τῆλυ (Fenugreek)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trigonella foenum-graecum L. (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified plants in De Materia Medica. The alternative name βουκέρας ('ox-horn') refers to the shape of the long, curved seed pods. The Latin siliquastrum is used by Roman authors; the Egyptian name ἄλφιτον ('ground grain') reflects its use as meal. Cross-referenced in Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum, VI) and Pliny the Elder (Natural History XXIV.184), who calls it faenum Graecum and corroborates its softening and emollient properties. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VIII.1.4) mentions it among cultivated legumes sown for fodder.

entities_mentioned.name: Νίτρον (Soda / Natron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate (natron) or potassium nitrate, likely natron in this context
entities_mentioned.notes: In ancient medical contexts, νίτρον most commonly refers to natron (sodium carbonate), a natural mineral salt used as a caustic and cleansing agent in poultices. It is distinguished from modern chemical 'nitre' (saltpetre, potassium nitrate).

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀψίνθιον (Wormwood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia absinthium L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed here as an optional additive to the fenugreek poultice in place of nitron, used for its bitter, anti-inflammatory properties. Treated at length in De Materia Medica III.23.

entities_mentioned.name: Βούτυρον (Butter)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clarified butter or fresh butter (bovine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an emollient vehicle for topical application; common in Dioscoridean pharmacy for skin preparations.

segments.translator_notes:
This entry has no surviving Greek manuscript base text provided in the segment; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann 1906–1914, vol. I, pp. 188–189) and cross-checked against the Sprengel (1829) edition. Readers consulting primary manuscripts should verify against the Codex Vindobonensis medicus graecus 1 (Vienna Dioscorides, 512 CE) where applicable.

The term εὐσαρκία ('well-fleshed condition,' i.e., healthy body weight and musculature) is a Galenic-Hippocratic concept denoting an optimal, well-nourished bodily constitution, distinct from obesity. This nutritive claim for fenugreek flour is notable and represents one of the earliest documented uses of the seed as a body-building supplement, echoed in later Ayurvedic and Arabic medical traditions.

The treatment of 'cancerous skin lesions' (ἐν δέρματι καρκίνοι) with a honey-fenugreek poultice reflects the ancient broad usage of καρκίνος, which encompassed ulcerating surface lesions, hard tumours, and carbuncles, and should not be interpreted through a strictly modern oncological lens.

Source section: De Materia Medica II.109 (Wellmann numbering). Some editions number this entry as II.102 (Saracenus 1598 edition) due to differences in chapter divisions.

segments.title_inferred: Aconite (Wolfsbane) — Poison and Antidote
segments.original_text: Ἀκόνιτον· οἱ δὲ μυοκτόνον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ θηλυφόνον, οἱ δὲ καμοκτόνον, οἱ δὲ παρδαλιαγχές· Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ sagitta erratica· γεννᾶται δὲ περὶ τὴν Ἀκόνην τῆς Βιθυνίας, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα εἴληφεν. φύλλα δὲ ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ ἢ καὶ μᾶλλον τῷ τοῦ κυκλαμίνου, μικρά, πλείω, σκληρά· ἄνθος κυανοῦν ἢ ὑπόλευκον, ῥίζαν δὲ ὁμοίαν τοῖς ἀστακοῖς τοῖς μικροῖς, ᾗ χρῶνται ἐπὶ τοῦ παρδάλους καὶ λύκους καὶ ἄλλα θηρία ἀναιρεῖν, μίξαντες αὐτὴν κρέασι. θανατηφόρον δέ ἐστι καὶ ἀνθρώποις, εἰ μή τις χρήσαιτο αὐτῷ ἐπὶ σκορπιοδήκτων ἔξωθεν ἐπιτιθέμενον.
segments.translation: Aconite [Ἀκόνιτον]: some call it 'mouse-killer' [μυοκτόνον], others 'woman-killer' [θηλυφόνον], others 'camel-killer' [καμοκτόνον], others 'leopard-strangler' [παρδαλιαγχές]. The Romans call it sagitta erratica ['wandering arrow']. It grows in the region of Aconae in Bithynia, from which it takes its name. It has leaves similar to celery, or rather more like those of cyclamen — small, numerous, and stiff. The flower is blue or whitish. The root resembles small crayfish, and this is used to kill leopards, wolves, and other wild animals by mixing it into their meat.
