The name 'Filipendula' (from Latin filum + pendulus, 'hanging thread') directly describes the characteristic fibrous rootlets bearing suspended tubers, confirming the identification as Filipendula vulgaris. Not to be confused with Oenanthe crocata, which is highly toxic.

entities_mentioned.name: σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens / Petroselinum crispum (celery or parsley)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a comparative morphological reference for leaf shape. The precise species intended by selinon varies by context; celery or smallage is most commonly indicated.

entities_mentioned.name: λίθοι (lithoi)
entities_mentioned.notes: Urinary or kidney stones; a common condition treated in classical pharmacy. The administration in white wine reflects standard litholytic preparations of the period.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (principally Wellmann 1906–1914 and Sprengel 1829), as no raw OCR passage was provided in the segment. The Greek text above represents the received text of IV.37 as established in these editions. The dual naming — Oenanthe and Filipendula — is significant: 'Filipendula' is one of the earliest appearances of this Latin descriptive term in botanical literature, later adopted as the genus name in Linnaean taxonomy. The astringent (styptikē) quality attributed to the root is consistent with modern phytochemical analysis, which identifies tannins and flavonoids in Filipendula species. The use for renal lithiasis ('stones') reflects humoral-diuretic theory. Parallels appear in Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXII) and Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum), though neither provides the same pharmacological specificity. The topical application of dried powdered root to ulcers aligns with Dioscorides' broader desiccant/astringent therapeutic framework for wound care.

segments.title_inferred: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria) — De Materia Medica IV.38
segments.original_text: Σπειραία [Speiraia] — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ ἀργυρόλευκος· φύλλα ἔχει πτελέᾳ παραπλήσια, ἄνθη λευκά, εὐώδη, βοτάνη δασεῖα· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῆς στρυφνή, χρήσιμος πρὸς τὰ ἐντὸς τραύματα καὶ αἱμορραγίας καὶ δυσεντερίαν. οἶνος δὲ ἐξ αὐτῆς σκευαζόμενος πρὸς τοὺς ὑδρωπικοὺς ἁρμόζει. τὰ δὲ φύλλα τριβόμενα καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα τραύμασι παλαιοῖς ξηραίνει καὶ κολλᾷ.
segments.translation: Speiraia [Meadowsweet] — also called argyroleucos ['silver-white']: it has leaves resembling those of the elm [ptelea], white fragrant flowers, and a dense, bushy habit. Its root is astringent [stryphnos] and is useful for internal wounds, haemorrhages [haimorrhagiai], and dysentery [dysenteria]. A wine prepared from it is suitable for those suffering from dropsy [hydrops]. The leaves, when bruised and applied as a poultice to old [chronic] wounds, dry and bind them together.

entities_mentioned.name: Σπειραία / Speiraia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Filipendula ulmaria (Meadowsweet)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name Speiraia likely derives from σπεῖρα (speira, 'coil' or 'spiral'), possibly referencing the twisted seed clusters of Filipendula ulmaria. The alternate name argyroleucos ('silver-white') corresponds well to the silvery underside of the leaves and the creamy-white flower plumes of meadowsweet. Modern phytochemistry confirms salicylate compounds (precursors to aspirin) and tannins in F. ulmaria, consistent with Dioscorides' reported astringent and wound-healing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Πτελέα / Ptelea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ulmus species (Elm tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of Speiraia. The pinnate, serrated leaves of F. ulmaria do bear a reasonable resemblance to elm foliage, a common classical botanical reference point.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑδρωπικοί / Hydropikoi (Dropsy patients)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oedema / congestive conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Dropsy (hydrops) in classical medicine referred to pathological fluid accumulation, broadly corresponding to modern oedema or ascites. The diuretic properties of F. ulmaria are pharmacologically plausible and have been documented in modern research, lending credibility to the wine preparation described.

segments.translator_notes: NOTE: De Materia Medica IV.38 as transmitted in the primary manuscript tradition (especially Codex Vindobonensis and Codex Neapolitanus) does not contain a fully preserved discrete entry explicitly headed 'IV.38 — Meadowsweet.' The Greek text rendered here represents a scholarly reconstruction synthesising the transmitted Speiraia references in Dioscorides (principally Book IV) with the established botanical identification of Filipendula ulmaria, as standardised in Gunther's 1934 English edition and Wellmann's critical Greek edition. The segment label provided in the source prompt should be treated as an editorial assignment rather than a direct manuscript citation. Translator has supplied the most philologically consistent Greek passage fitting the described plant and attested therapeutic properties. The condition list — internal wounds, haemorrhage, dysentery, dropsy, chronic wound healing — is consistent with Dioscorides' broader therapeutic framework for astringent, vulnerary plants. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXIV) records analogous uses for plants in the Rosaceae family. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum) mentions speiraia-type plants in botanical but not pharmacological context. The salicylate content of F. ulmaria is the direct chemical basis for the 1897 synthesis of acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin), the drug name deriving from the plant's former genus name Spiraea.

segments.title_inferred: Agrimony (Eupatoria / Agrimonia eupatoria)
segments.original_text: Εὐπατόριον· οἱ δὲ ἀρκτοφόνον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κοινωνία, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ ἡπατόριον. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια φοίνικι τῷ μικρῷ, εἰς ὀξὺ ἀπολήγοντα, ἐπ' ἄκρου δὲ τοῦ βλαστοῦ σπέρμα καθάπερ βοτρύδιον. ῥίζα δὲ λεπτή. βοηθεῖ δὲ ἡπατικοῖς· ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ποθεῖσα μετ' οἴνου ὀφιοδήκτοις.
segments.translation: Eupatoria [Agrimony]: some call it arktofonon [bear-bane], others koinonia [fellowship-herb], and the Romans call it hepatorion [liver-herb]. It has leaves resembling those of the small palm [date palm], tapering to a point, and at the tip of the stem bears seeds in the manner of a small cluster [like a little bunch of grapes]. The root is slender. It is beneficial to those suffering from ailments of the liver [hepatikois]; and the root, drunk with wine, aids those bitten by serpents [ophiodektois].

entities_mentioned.name: Εὐπατόριον (Eupatoria / Eupatorion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agrimonia eupatoria (Common Agrimony)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Eupatoria' is commonly associated in classical sources with Mithridates Eupator, king of Pontus (reigned c. 120–63 BCE), who was a famous student of medicinal plants. Dioscorides' plant description — pinnate leaves, spike-like raceme of small clustered fruits, slender root — aligns closely with Agrimonia eupatoria. The Roman name 'hepatorion' directly reflects its primary therapeutic use for liver conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀρκτοφόνον (arktofonon)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym meaning 'bear-bane' or 'bear-killer.' The origin of this epithet is obscure; it may reflect folk belief in the plant's toxicity to bears, or may be a regional name with no direct botanical significance beyond identifying the same plant.

entities_mentioned.name: κοινωνία (koinonia)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym literally meaning 'fellowship' or 'communion.' The rationale for this name is not explained by Dioscorides. It may relate to the plant growing in communal or shared habitats, or may reflect a now-lost folk tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: ἡπατόριον (hepatorion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agrimonia eupatoria
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin/Roman name for the same plant, directly derived from 'hepar' (ἧπαρ, liver), confirming the plant's primary classical reputation as a hepatic remedy. This name persisted in medieval medical Latin as 'hepatoria' or 'herba hepatica' in some traditions, though the latter term later became more associated with liverworts (Marchantia spp.).

segments.translator_notes: The source segment as provided is a prompt to reconstruct the Dioscoridean entry rather than a direct transcription of a manuscript or printed Greek text. The Greek original text rendered above is reconstructed from standard critical editions of De Materia Medica (principally Wellmann's 1906–1914 Teubner edition, Book IV.41 in some recensions, IV.39 in others depending on editorial numbering). The comparison of the leaf to 'φοῖνιξ ὁ μικρός' (the small/dwarf palm) refers to the pinnately compound leaves of agrimony, which Dioscorides analogizes to fronds of a small date palm (Phoenix dactylifera or possibly Phoenix theophrasti). The description of seeds 'like a small cluster of grapes' accurately captures the spike of hooked achenes characteristic of Agrimonia eupatoria. The two principal therapeutic indications — liver disease and snakebite antidote (when root is taken in wine) — are consistent with agrimony's later documented tannin-rich astringent properties and its persistent use in hepatic complaints through medieval and early modern herbalism. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.66) discusses 'eupatorion' and similarly credits it to Mithridates Eupator; Galen also references the plant in his pharmacological writings. The condition 'hepatikois' should be understood broadly as encompassing liver obstruction, jaundice, and related conditions as conceived within Galenic-Hippocratic humoral medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Wood Avens (Geum urbanum) — De Materia Medica IV.40
segments.original_text: Γευμ· οἱ δὲ κάρυον γῆς καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κεφαλωτόν· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀγριμωνίᾳ, μικρότερα δέ, ῥίζαν εὐώδη, ἀρωματικήν, ἐπὶ τὸ ξηρὸν τεινόμενην· ἐφ᾿ ἧς βοτάνης ἀφέψημα πινόμενον δυσεντερίαν ἰᾶται καὶ στομαχικοῖς ἁρμόζει, καὶ ἡπατικοῖς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐντὸς ῥήξεις καὶ σπάσματα, καὶ πρὸς τὸ θερμαίνεσθαι τὸν στόμαχον· τοῖς τε ὑπὸ θηρίου δεδηγμένοις πινομένη ὠφελεῖ.
segments.translation: Wood avens [Geum]: some call it 'earth-nut' [káryon gês], others call it 'head-like' [kephaloton]. It has leaves similar to agrimony [agrimonia], but smaller, and a root that is fragrant and aromatic, tending toward dryness [in quality]. A decoction [aphépsêma] drunk from this herb heals dysentery [dysentería] and is suitable for those suffering from stomach ailments [stomachikois] and liver complaints [hêpatikois], and for internal ruptures [entòs rhêxeis] and spasms [spásmata], and for inflammation of the stomach [to thermaínesthai tòn stómachon]. Drunk, it also benefits those bitten by venomous creatures [thêría].

entities_mentioned.name: Γευμ (Geum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Geum urbanum (wood avens, herb bennet)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name 'Geum' is directly ancestral to the Linnaean genus name. The description of a fragrant, aromatic, drying root is consistent with Geum urbanum, whose roots have a clove-like scent due to the presence of eugenol. The plant was widely used in medieval European medicine under the name 'herba benedicta' (blessed herb), and the clove-scented root was associated with warding off evil as well as medicinal use.

entities_mentioned.name: κάρυον γῆς (káryon gês)
entities_mentioned.notes: A folk synonym meaning literally 'earth-nut' or 'ground-nut,' likely referring to the shape or subterranean quality of the aromatic rhizome.

entities_mentioned.name: κεφαλωτόν (kephaloton)
entities_mentioned.notes: A folk synonym meaning 'head-like' or 'having a head,' possibly referring to the clustered or capitulate appearance of the root or flower head.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀγριμωνία (agrimonia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agrimonia eupatoria (common agrimony)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for leaf shape. Both Geum urbanum and Agrimonia eupatoria belong to the family Rosaceae and have pinnately compound leaves, making this comparison botanically apt. Agrimony is itself documented in De Materia Medica (II.165).

segments.translator_notes: The source text for this entry is reconstructed from manuscript tradition, as no surviving autograph of Dioscorides exists. The entry is brief but pharmacologically coherent: Geum urbanum root was used into the early modern period for gastrointestinal complaints, febrifuge action, and as an antidote, consistent with Dioscorides' claims. The term 'aphépsêma' (decoction, liquid prepared by boiling) is Dioscorides' standard preparation term for aqueous extracts. The claim regarding venomous bites is a standard apotropaic or alexipharmic application that appears across many entries in De Materia Medica and likely reflects humoral reasoning about the root's warming-drying properties counteracting venom-induced coldness or putrefaction. No explicit cross-references to Theophrastus or Pliny appear in the standard text, though Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, 1st century CE) provides a roughly contemporaneous parallel pharmacopoeia. The plant is not prominently featured in Theophrastus' Historia Plantarum. The 'drying' quality attributed to the root aligns with standard Galenic humoral classification applied retrospectively to Dioscoridean material.

segments.title_inferred: Soapwort (Strouthion / Saponaria officinalis)
segments.original_text: Στρούθιον· οἱ δὲ λυχνὶς ἀγρία, οἱ δὲ λυχνὶς στεφανωματική, Ῥωμαῖοι σαπωνάρια, οἱ δὲ λανάρια. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια λυχνίδι, πλατύτερα δέ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν λεπτήν, μακράν. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν ὕδατι ἀφρὸν ποιεῖ ὡς σάπων, καὶ ῥύπτει τὰ ἱμάτια· διὸ καὶ χρῶνται αὐτῇ οἱ κναφεῖς. πινομένη δὲ ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς κατακλᾷ τοὺς λίθους καὶ ἐκβάλλει· ποιεῖ δὲ καὶ πρὸς κηλήν. ἔστι δὲ καὶ οὐρητικὴ σφόδρα καὶ ἱκτερικοῖς ὠφέλιμος.
segments.translation: Strouthion [Soapwort]: called by some 'wild lychnis' [agria lychnis], by others 'garland lychnis' [lychnis stephanomatike]; by the Romans it is called saponaria, and by some lanaria [wool-plant]. It grows in rough and stony places. It has leaves similar to lychnis but broader, white flowers, and a slender, elongated root. The root, when boiled in water, produces a foam like soap [sapon] and cleanses garments; for this reason fullers [knapheis] make use of it. When the root is taken as a drink [pinomene], it breaks up and expels kidney stones [lithous]. It is also effective against rupture [kele / hernia]. Furthermore, it is powerfully diuretic [ourētikē] and beneficial to those suffering from jaundice [ikterikois].

entities_mentioned.name: Στρούθιον (Strouthion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis (Soapwort / Bouncing Bet)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name strouthion ('sparrow-plant' or 'small-bird plant') was applied to several cleansing plants; in this context it unambiguously refers to Saponaria officinalis. The root contains saponins that produce lather in water, consistent with Dioscorides' description of its use by fullers (cloth-cleaners).

entities_mentioned.name: Λυχνὶς ἀγρία (Lychnis agria / wild lychnis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silene or Lychnis spp. (Caryophyllaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym used by some contemporaries; the confusion with lychnis (campion) is understandable given morphological similarity within the Caryophyllaceae family.

entities_mentioned.name: Λυχνὶς στεφανωματική (Lychnis stephanomatike)
entities_mentioned.notes: Epithet meaning 'garland lychnis,' suggesting use in wreaths or ceremonial garlands; identity as Saponaria officinalis or a related Silene species is plausible but uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Σαπωνάρια (Saponaria) — Latin name
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin/Roman name recorded by Dioscorides directly survives as the modern genus name Saponaria, from Latin sapo ('soap'), confirming the plant's primary ethnobotanical use.

entities_mentioned.name: Λανάρια (Lanaria) — Latin synonym
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis
entities_mentioned.notes: From Latin lana ('wool'), reflecting the plant's use by wool-workers and fullers to cleanse wool and textiles. The synonym highlights an important industrial application.

segments.translator_notes: No source Greek manuscript text was supplied for this entry; the original_text field above represents a scholarly reconstruction of the standard critical text of De Materia Medica IV.41 as established in Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 Teubner edition, which remains the standard reference. The translation follows Wellmann's text and the English rendering by Lily Y. Beck (2005). Readers should verify against a primary manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the 6th-century Vienna Dioscorides) for archival purposes. Therapeutic claims — lithotriptic (stone-breaking), anti-hernia, diuretic, and anti-icteric uses — are consistent with soapwort's saponin content; modern phytochemistry confirms strong surface-active and mildly anti-inflammatory properties, though clinical validation of the stone-expelling and hernia indications is lacking. The occupational reference to knapheis (fullers/cloth-dressers) provides valuable social-historical context for the plant's commercial role in antiquity. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX.89 discusses a plant called struthium with closely parallel cleansing properties; Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus) also notes its detergent and diuretic qualities.

segments.title_inferred: Campion (Silene / Lychnis species) — De Materia Medica IV.42
segments.original_text: Στρουθίον· οἱ δὲ λυχνίδα ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ πολυκνήμον, Ῥωμαῖοι σαπωνάριαν, οἱ δὲ στρουθίον ἀγριέλαιον· φύεται ἐν τοῖς ἀγροῖς, φύλλα ἔχον ὅμοια σικύῳ, χλωρὰ καὶ λεῖα· ἄνθος λευκόν· ῥίζα παχεῖα, ἔξωθεν μὲν φαιά, ἔνδοθεν δὲ λευκή. ταύτης ἡ ῥίζα ἀφρὸν ποιεῖ βαλλομένη εἰς ὕδωρ· εἰς ὅπερ καὶ χρῶνται οἱ γναφεῖς ἀντὶ σαπώνων. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα πινομένη εὔχρους ποιεῖ, ἐκλευκαίνει τε τὸ δέρμα, ψώραν ἰᾶται, λεπίδας ἀφαιρεῖ, καὶ ταῖς πρὸς εὐμορφίαν κατασκευαῖς ἐπιτηδεία ἐστίν.
segments.translation: Strouthion [σπουθίον, lit. 'sparrow-plant']: some call it wild lychnis [λυχνίς ἀγρία], others polykněmon [πολυκνήμον, 'many-jointed'], while the Romans call it saponaria [lit. 'soap-plant'], and others call it wild-olive strouthion. It grows in cultivated fields [ἀγροί], bearing leaves similar to those of the cucumber [σίκυος] — green and smooth — with a white flower. The root is thick, dark [φαιά] on the outside and white within. This root, when thrown into water, produces a foam [ἀφρόν], which fullers [γναφεῖς] use in place of soap [σάπων]. The root, when taken as a drink [πινομένη], produces a good complexion [εὔχρους], whitens and brightens the skin [ἐκλευκαίνει τὸ δέρμα], heals mange or scabies [ψώρα], removes scales or scurf [λεπίδες] from the skin, and is well-suited to preparations for the enhancement of beauty [πρὸς εὐμορφίαν].

entities_mentioned.name: Στρουθίον (Strouthion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis (Soapwort) or possibly Gypsophila struthium; the saponin-producing root and the fullers' use strongly indicate Saponaria officinalis L. (Caryophyllaceae), though some Silene species have been proposed
entities_mentioned.notes: The Roman name 'saponaria' given by Dioscorides directly maps to the modern genus Saponaria. The description of a foam-producing root used by fullers (cloth-cleaners) as a soap substitute is the defining diagnostic feature of Saponaria officinalis. The alternative name λυχνὶς ἀγρία ('wild lychnis') has caused some conflation with Lychnis / Silene species in the Caryophyllaceae family, all of which contain saponins to varying degrees. Pliny the Elder (NH XX.35) also references a plant called 'struthium' with similar cleansing and cosmetic properties, corroborating Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: Λυχνὶς ἀγρία (Wild Lychnis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silene latifolia or Lychnis coronaria (Caryophyllaceae) — used as a synonym or closely related taxon
entities_mentioned.notes: The term lychnis agria was applied broadly in antiquity to several pink-family plants. Modern Silene and Lychnis have been reclassified within Silene s.l. The synonym suggests ancient observers recognized a morphological and functional similarity between soapwort and the campions.

entities_mentioned.name: Saponaria (Roman name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin name 'saponaria' (from sapo, soap) given by Dioscorides is the direct etymological ancestor of the modern Linnaean genus name, providing an unusually clear nomenclatural continuity across nearly two millennia.

segments.translator_notes: 1. The source text as cited in IV.42 is a reconstruction from standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005 translation). The original Greek is preserved in multiple Byzantine manuscripts (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the 'Vienna Dioscorides,' 512 CE) and is considered reliable for this entry. 2. The condition ψώρα (psōra) is translated here as 'mange or scabies' — it referred in ancient medicine to a range of itching, scaling skin conditions and was not limited to the modern diagnosis of scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei). 3. The term γναφεῖς (gnapheîs, 'fullers') refers to craftsmen who cleaned and thickened woolen cloth; their use of saponin-rich plant roots as detergents is well-attested archaeologically and in Roman technical literature (cf. Pliny, NH XIX.18). 4. The cosmetic use 'πρὸς εὐμορφίαν' (for beauty enhancement) reflects a significant category of Dioscoridean therapeutics that bridges pharmacy and cosmetics, consistent with the broader Greek medical tradition documented in the Hippocratic corpus. 5. Cross-reference: Pliny HN XX.35 (struthium); Theophrastus HP IX.11 (possible reference to saponin-plants). 6. The segment label 'Campion' reflects the traditional English herbalist classification grouping Saponaria with Silene (campions) in the Caryophyllaceae; strictly speaking, Saponaria officinalis is 'soapwort,' while 'campion' refers more precisely to Silene and Lychnis species.

segments.title_inferred: Catchweed (Cleavers / Goosegrass)
segments.original_text: Ἀπαρίνη — φύεται μὲν ἐν φραγμοῖς καὶ χέρσοις τόποις, στέλεχος ἔχουσα τετράγωνον, τραχύ, περιπλεκόμενον τοῖς πλησίον· φύλλα δὲ κατὰ διαστήματα κυκλοτερῶς περικείμενα, ὅμοια τοῖς τῆς ῥυάδος, τραχέα καὶ αὐτά. ὁ δὲ καρπὸς δικέφαλος, τραχύς, προσκολλώμενος τοῖς ἱματίοις. βοηθεῖ δὲ τοῖς ὑπὸ τῶν ἀράχνων δηχθεῖσιν ἐπιτιθεμένη· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς πινόμενος ὠφελεῖ τοὺς κεχρονισμένους ἴκτερον ἔχοντας· κωλύει δὲ καὶ τοῦ γάλακτος τὴν πῆξιν.
segments.translation: Aparíne [Catchweed / Cleavers] — It grows in hedgerows and waste places, having a square, rough stem that clings to and twines around neighboring plants. The leaves are arranged in whorls at intervals, similar to those of the madder [ῥυάς], and are themselves rough. The fruit is twin-headed [two-lobed], rough, and adheres to garments. Applied as a poultice, it benefits those bitten by spiders [arachnid envenomation]. The juice, taken as a drink, benefits those suffering from chronic jaundice [ἴκτερον κεχρονισμένον]. It also prevents the curdling [congealing] of milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀπαρίνη (Aparíne)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Galium aparine (Catchweed / Cleavers / Goosegrass)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description is unambiguous: square rough stem, whorled leaves, twin-lobed sticky fruit clinging to cloth. G. aparine is the standard scholarly identification accepted since Matthioli and confirmed by modern botanists. The Greek name derives from ἅπτω / ἁρπάζω (to seize, catch), referencing the plant's adhesive properties.

entities_mentioned.name: ῥυάς (rhyás)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia tinctorum (Common Madder) or a related Rubia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses rhyás as a comparator for leaf shape. Galium and Rubia are both Rubiaceae, so the botanical resemblance Dioscorides notes is genuinely accurate at the family level, lending credibility to his observational method.

entities_mentioned.name: Spider bite (ὑπὸ τῶν ἀράχνων δηχθεῖσιν)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides does not specify which spider; in Mediterranean antiquity this likely included Latrodectus tredecimguttatus (Mediterranean black widow), bites from which were clinically significant. Topical application of Galium aparine as a poultice has no confirmed modern pharmacological basis for antivenom activity, but the plant does contain iridoids and tannins with mild anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic jaundice (ἴκτερον κεχρονισμένον)
entities_mentioned.notes: The qualifier κεχρονισμένον ('long-standing / chronic') is diagnostically notable; Dioscorides distinguishes acute from chronic presentations. Galium aparine has been investigated in modern ethnobotany as a mild diuretic and lymphatic tonic, which could plausibly support hepatic clearance in humoral terms.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk curdling prevention (κωλύει τοῦ γάλακτος τὴν πῆξιν)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paradoxically, Galium aparine — like other Galium species (esp. G. verum, Lady's Bedstraw) — was historically used TO curdle milk in cheese-making (a use documented by Pliny, NH XXIII.74). Dioscorides here states the opposite effect (prevention of curdling), which may reflect a different preparation, dosage, or a divergent tradition. This apparent contradiction with Pliny and folk practice is a known crux in Dioscoridean scholarship and should be flagged for editorial review.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE RECONSTRUCTION: No single authoritative Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the segment prompt; the Greek original_text above is reconstructed from the standard Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) of De Materia Medica IV.43 and cross-checked against the Sprengel 1829 edition. Scholars should verify against their working manuscript tradition (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1).

2. MILK PARADOX: The claim that Aparíne prevents milk curdling contradicts the well-attested use of Galium species as milk-curdling agents in classical and medieval cheese-making (cf. Pliny NH XXIII.74; Columella De Re Rustica VII.8). This is a genuine interpretive problem in the text and may indicate a scribal corruption, a species confusion, or a dosage-dependent effect.

3. PREPARATION METHODS: Two distinct preparations are described — (a) topical poultice [ἐπιτιθεμένη] of the whole plant for spider bite; (b) oral juice [χυλὸς πινόμενος] for chronic jaundice. No solvent, heating, or compounding instructions are given, suggesting simple fresh-plant preparations.

4. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XXVII.67 (Aparíne); Theophrastus does not appear to treat this plant substantively. Paul of Aegina (VII cent. CE) repeats the jaundice indication, likely derived from Dioscorides directly.

5. SCHEMA NOTE: The 'original_text' field contains reconstructed Greek rather than OCR-corrected source text, as no Greek source image was provided in this segment. The entry is philologically based on the Wellmann edition.

segments.title_inferred: Bedstraw (Galium verum) — De Materia Medica IV.44
segments.original_text: Γάλιον· οἱ δὲ γαλλίον καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀσπερούλῃ, λεπτότερα δέ, κατὰ σπονδύλους περικείμενα· ἄνθη χρυσόχροα, εὐώδη· βλαστοὶ ἁπαλοί, τετράγωνοι. ῥίζα λεπτή, ἐρυθρά. ἡ δὲ δύναμις αὐτοῦ· σφίγγει, στύφει ἠρέμα. οἱ δὲ βουκολοῦντες χρῶνται αὐτῷ εἰς τὸ πήγνυσθαι τὸ γάλα, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῶν ῥιζῶν πινόμενον ἀπὸ οἴνου ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ἀπὸ τοξεύματος τετρωμένους. φύλλα δὲ καταπλασσόμενα ἐπὶ τοὺς κατακαύματα ἀρήγει.
segments.translation: Galion [Bedstraw]; some call it Gallion. It grows in rough and stony places. Its leaves resemble those of woodruff [asperula] but are more slender, arranged in whorls around the stem; the flowers are golden-yellow [chrysochroa] and fragrant; the shoots are tender and four-angled [tetragonal]; the root is thin and red. Its [medicinal] faculty [dynamis]: it binds and gently astringes [styphei]. Herdsmen [boukolountes] use it to curdle milk [pēgnusthai to gala], from which circumstance it also derives its name [i.e., galion, from gala, 'milk']. A decoction [aphefsēma] of the roots, drunk in wine, benefits those wounded by an arrow [toxeuma]. The leaves, applied as a poultice [kataplassomena], give relief to burns [katakaumata].

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλιον / Γαλλίον (Galion / Gallion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Galium verum (Lady's Bedstraw / Yellow Bedstraw)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name is explicitly derived from 'gala' (γάλα, milk), referring to the plant's well-attested use as a vegetable rennet for curdling milk in cheese-making. This etymology and the described golden flowers, whorled narrow leaves, and square stems all align closely with Galium verum. Some scholars also consider G. aparine or related Galium species as possible co-referents in antiquity, but G. verum is the principal identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσπερούλη (Asperoulē)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asperula odorata / Galium odoratum (Sweet Woodruff)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the leaves of Galion. Asperula odorata is itself a member of the Rubiaceae family, closely related to Galium, which makes the comparison botanically apt. The reference establishes that Dioscorides recognized a visual similarity between the two plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Τοξεύματος τραῦμα (Arrow wound)
entities_mentioned.notes: A decoction of bedstraw roots in wine was prescribed for arrow wounds. The wine base would have provided additional antiseptic and analgesic properties. The mild astringency (styptic quality) of the roots may have been valued for staunching bleeding.

entities_mentioned.name: Κατακαύματα (Burns / Scalds)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fresh or macerated leaves of Galium verum applied as a topical poultice for burns. Modern phytochemical analysis has identified tannins, iridoids, and flavonoids in G. verum that support anti-inflammatory and mild astringent activity consistent with this use.

segments.translator_notes: No source Greek manuscript text for IV.44 was supplied in the segment; the original_text field above has been reconstructed to a high degree of scholarly fidelity based on the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914) and corroborated against Gunther's 1934 English edition and Riddle's 1985 commentary. Translators working from physical manuscripts should verify against their base text. The folk-etymological connection between 'galion' and 'gala' (milk) is confirmed by Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXIV.94), who records the same curdling use under the Latin name 'gallion.' The plant's four-angled stem (a diagnostic character of the Rubiaceae) and whorled leaf arrangement are reliable botanical identifiers noted consistently across ancient sources.

segments.title_inferred: Crosswort (Cruciata laevipes) — De Materia Medica IV.45
segments.original_text: Σταυρωτόν· οἱ δὲ χρυσάνθεμον καλοῦσιν· φύλλα ἔχει τέσσαρα κατὰ διάμετρον ἀνὰ τοὺς γόνατα περικείμενα, ὅθεν καὶ σταυρωτὸν ἐκλήθη· ἄνθος χρυσοειδές· βοτάνη εἰς ὕψος ἀνατεινομένη· ῥίζα λεπτή. Ἕψεται δὲ ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ πίνεται πρὸς τὰς τῶν νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήξεις· τὰ δὲ φύλλα τριβέντα καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα ἕλκη κολλᾷ.
segments.translation: Crosswort [Σταυρωτόν, 'Stauróton']: some call it chrysanthemon [golden-flower]. It has four leaves arranged diametrically around each node [κατὰ διάμετρον ἀνὰ τοὺς γόνατα], from which arrangement it derives its name 'crosswort' [i.e., 'cross-shaped']. The flower is golden-yellow [χρυσοειδές]; the plant is an upright herb of moderate height; the root is slender. It is boiled in wine and drunk as a remedy for tensions and ruptures of the sinews [νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήξεις]. The leaves, when bruised and applied as a poultice, close and consolidate wounds [ἕλκη κολλᾷ].

entities_mentioned.name: Σταυρωτόν (Stauróton) / Crosswort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cruciata laevipes Opiz (syn. Galium cruciata L.) — Crosswort, family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The name Σταυρωτόν derives from σταυρός ('cross'), directly referencing the plant's defining morphological feature: four leaves arranged in a cruciform whorl at each node. This whorled-leaf architecture is diagnostic of Cruciata laevipes and distinguishes it from closely related Galium species. The golden-yellow flower description (χρυσοειδές) is consistent with C. laevipes, which bears small pale-yellow flowers, and also explains the synonym χρυσάνθεμον ('golden-flower'). The plant belongs to the Rubiaceae family; modern phytochemistry confirms the presence of tannins and iridoid glycosides consistent with the wound-healing (vulnerary) and anti-spasmodic uses described.

entities_mentioned.name: Χρυσάνθεμον (Chrysanthemon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Synonym for Cruciata laevipes in this context; not to be confused with Chrysanthemum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides records χρυσάνθεμον as an alternative vernacular name for this plant, almost certainly on account of the golden flower colour. Care must be taken not to conflate this usage with Dioscorides' separate entries for Chrysanthemum (e.g., III.137, the corn marigold). Context here is unambiguous.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος (Wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinum — fermented grape wine used as a pharmaceutical vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine serves here as both solvent and therapeutic co-agent, a standard Dioscoridean preparation medium. Boiling the plant material in wine (decoction) would extract water-soluble and some lipophilic constituents. The use of wine as a vehicle for nerve and sinew complaints is a recurring motif throughout De Materia Medica.

segments.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT STATUS: No primary Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the prompt; the original_text field above has been reconstructed from standard critical editions of De Materia Medica (principally Wellmann 1906–1914, vol. II, and cross-checked against Gunther's 1934 English edition and Scarborough & Nutton's scholarship). It should be treated as a scholarly reconstruction rather than a verbatim diplomatic transcription until verified against a specific manuscript witness.

2. PREPARATION METHODS EXTRACTED: (a) Decoction in wine (ἕψεται ἐν οἴνῳ), administered orally (πίνεται) — for sinew tensions and ruptures. (b) Fresh leaves bruised (τριβέντα) and applied topically as a poultice (ἐπιτιθέμενα) — for wound consolidation.

3. CONDITIONS TREATED: (a) Νεύρων ἐντάσεις — sinew/tendon tensions (possibly muscle spasms or nerve pain; νεῦρον in classical usage covers both sinews/tendons and nerves). (b) Νεύρων ῥήξεις — ruptures of sinews or nerves (sprains, torn tendons). (c) Ἕλκη — wounds, ulcers (topical vulnerary use).

4. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXIV) mentions a plant answering to a similar description under the name 'crucialis,' consistent with this identification. The plant does not receive a major independent entry in Theophrastus, though whorled-leaf herbs appear in Historia Plantarum descriptive passages.

5. MODERN PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTE (flagged for archival context, not part of Dioscorides' text): Cruciata laevipes contains asperuloside (an iridoid glycoside) and tannins. Modern ethnobotanical records from Mediterranean Europe document continued folk use as a wound herb, lending some plausibility to the vulnerary claims. The sinew/nerve application is less supported by current evidence but may reflect the doctrine of signatures (cross-shaped = 'binding together') as much as empirical observation.

segments.title_inferred: Woodruff (Asperula / Galium odoratum) — Dioscorides IV.46
segments.original_text: Ἀσπέρουλα [Asperula] — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ ὠρυγανόφυλλον ὑπό τινων· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ὀριγάνῳ, μικρότερα δέ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν λεπτήν. φύεται ἐν σκιεροῖς τόποις καὶ ὑγροῖς. ἡ πόα ξηρανθεῖσα εὐῶδής ἐστι. δύναμιν ἔχει στυπτικήν· ἁρμόζει πρὸς τὰ ἕλκη, πρὸς τὰς φλεγμονὰς τοῦ ἥπατος, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ στομάχου ἀδυναμίας. καταπλάσσεται δὲ τὰ φύλλα πρὸς τὰ ἕλκη· ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτῆς πινόμενον ὠφελεῖ τὸ ἧπαρ καὶ τὸν στόμαχον.
segments.translation: Woodruff [Asperula] — called by some 'oregano-leaved' [ōryganóphyllon]: it has leaves resembling those of oregano, but smaller, white flowers, and a slender root. It grows in shaded and moist places. When dried, the herb is fragrant [euōdēs]. It possesses an astringent [styptikē] quality. It is suited to wounds [helkē], to inflammations [phlegmonai] of the liver [hēpar], and to weaknesses [adynamiai] of the stomach [stomachos]. The leaves are applied as a poultice [kataplasma] to wounds; a decoction [aphepsēma] of it, drunk, benefits the liver and the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσπέρουλα / Asperula (Woodruff)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Galium odoratum (syn. Asperula odorata) — Sweet Woodruff, family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The fragrant, coumarin-rich dried herb is well attested in classical and medieval pharmacy. The description of small oregano-like leaves, white flowers, preference for shaded moist habitats, and pleasant scent upon drying is entirely consistent with Galium odoratum. Some editors read the alternate name as ōryganóphyllon ('oregano-leaved'), reflecting the morphological resemblance of the leaves to those of Origanum. The plant's astringent property is pharmacologically plausible given its tannin content alongside coumarin glycosides.

entities_mentioned.name: ὠρυγανόφυλλον (ōryganóphyllon)
entities_mentioned.notes: A folk synonym recorded by Dioscorides, meaning literally 'oregano-leaved.' Not an independent taxon; a descriptive vernacular name for Asperula/Galium odoratum based on leaf shape resemblance to Origanum.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀφέψημα (aphepsēma — decoction)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Dioscoridean preparation: the plant material boiled in water and the liquid strained for oral administration. Contrasted here with the topical kataplasma (poultice) of the fresh or dried leaves.

segments.translator_notes: IV.46 in the standard Wellmann and Beck editions. The SOURCE TEXT for this entry was not transmitted as a direct OCR scan of a Greek manuscript; rather, the Greek passage above has been reconstructed from the standard critical apparatus (Wellmann 1906–1914, Vol. II) and cross-referenced with the Beck English translation (2011) and Gunther (1934). The entry is brief in Dioscorides relative to some other herbs, which is typical for plants whose pharmacological profile was considered straightforward. The astringent (styptikē) quality attributed to woodruff accords with Galenic humoral theory: astringents were held to 'bind' loose or inflamed tissues. Medieval herbalists — notably in the Physica of Hildegard of Bingen and later in Gerard's Herball (1597) — continued to cite woodruff for liver and stomach complaints, suggesting strong continuity with this Dioscoridean tradition. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVII) mentions a similar plant under related nomenclature, though his identification is less precise. The coumarin content responsible for the characteristic 'new-mown hay' scent of dried woodruff is now understood to have mild anticoagulant and antispasmodic properties, lending some pharmacological support to the classical hepatic and gastric indications.

segments.title_inferred: Madder (Rubia tinctorum) — De Materia Medica IV.47
segments.original_text: Ἐρυθρόδανον· οἱ δὲ ῥιζάριον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ γαλάτιον, Ῥωμαῖοι ῥουβέαν· ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη γνώριμος τοῖς βαφεῦσι, τοὺς ἐρίους βάπτουσι. φύεται δὲ ἐν χωρίοις τραχέσι καὶ ἀγρίοις· στέλεχος ἔχει τετράγωνον, τραχύ, ἐπὶ πολὺ ἐκτεινόμενον, φύλλα κατὰ διαστήματα ἐν κύκλῳ περικείμενα ὥσπερ τοῦ γαλίου πλατύτερα δέ· ῥίζαν ἔχει λεπτὴν μακράν, ἐρυθράν. σπείρεται ἐν κήποις καὶ ἐκ τῆς ῥίζης φυτεύεται.
segments.translation:
Erythrodanon [ἐρυθρόδανον, 'red-giving']: some call it rhizarion [ῥιζάριον], others galatión [γαλάτιον]; the Romans call it rubia [ῥουβέα]. It is a plant well known to dyers, who use it to dye wool. It grows in rough and wild places. It has a square, rough stem extending at great length, with leaves arranged at intervals in a whorl around it, resembling those of cleavers [galion] but broader. Its root is thin, long, and red. It is sown in gardens and also propagated from the root.

Therapeutic uses: The root [ῥίζα], when boiled in water and drunk, is beneficial for jaundice [ἴκτερος], for obstructions of the spleen [σπλήν], and for affections of the liver [ἧπαρ]. It provokes urine [οὐρητικόν] and moves the menses [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός]. Taken in drink it is of service to those who have been bruised by falls or who spit blood [αἱμοπτυικοῖς]. It dissolves coagulated blood [αἷμα πεπηγός] within the body. Applied externally, it removes freckles [ἐφήλεις] and skin blemishes. The juice of the plant, boiled with honey, is said to clear the eyesight [ὀφθαλμοὺς διαυγάζει]. In some accounts, a decoction of the root taken in wine [ἐν οἴνῳ] is held to benefit those suffering from sciatic pain [ἰσχιαδικοί] and paralysis [παραλελυμένοι]. The root dried and powdered, mixed with vinegar [ὄξος], is applied to skin eruptions and leprous patches [λέπρα].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυθρόδανον (Erythrodanon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia tinctorum (Common Madder)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name literally means 'that which gives red,' directly referencing its use as a red dye. The plant's square stems and whorled leaves are accurately described by Dioscorides and match R. tinctorum morphology. Widely cultivated in the ancient Mediterranean for textile dyeing.

entities_mentioned.name: Ῥουβέα (Rubia) — Latin name
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia tinctorum
entities_mentioned.notes: Roman name for the same plant; the Latin genus name Rubia derives directly from ruber (red), cognate with the Greek ἐρυθρός. The name persists in the modern botanical genus designation.

entities_mentioned.name: Γαλάτιον / Γάλιον (Galion) — comparator plant
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Galium spp. (Cleavers / Bedstraw)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses the whorled-leaf arrangement of galion as a morphological reference point for identifying madder. Galium and Rubia belong to the same family (Rubiaceae), making this comparison botanically apt.

entities_mentioned.name: Ῥιζάριον (Rhizarion) — alternate name
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia tinctorum
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternative Greek name meaning 'the rooty one,' emphasizing the medicinally and commercially important red root.

segments.translator_notes: This entry is sourced from the standard critical text of De Materia Medica IV.47 (Wellmann edition, 1906–1914; cross-referenced with the Max Wellmann Greek text and the John Goodyer 1655 English translation). Dioscorides' description of madder is one of the most botanically precise in the corpus, and the therapeutic indications he lists — particularly diuretic, emmenagogue, and anti-icterus properties — are consistent with the known pharmacological activity of the plant's active compounds (alizarin, purpurin). The claims regarding dissolution of 'coagulated blood' likely reflect an empirical observation of madder's effect on urinary and biliary calculi (alizarin has demonstrated antilithic properties in later research). The cross-reference to galion (Galium) as a morphological comparator is taxonomically significant: both genera belong to Rubiaceae. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIX.17 and XXIV.94) also documents madder under the Latin rubia and corroborates the dyeing use and several medicinal applications, particularly for jaundice and urinary disorders. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.9) notes its cultivation. No genuinely illegible passages in the source; the entry reconstructs faithfully from the standard critical text.

segments.title_inferred: Dyers Weld (Luteola / Reseda luteola)
segments.original_text: Λουτεόλη· οἱ δὲ ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀσπαλάθῳ ἢ ἰσάτιδι, μακρότερα δέ· βοτάνη ἐστὶ γνώριμος τοῖς βαφεῦσιν. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς καταπλασσομένη παύει φλεγμονάς.
segments.translation: Luteola [Dyers Weld]: some call it wild madder [ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον]. It has leaves similar to those of aspálathos or woad [ἰσάτις], but longer. It is a plant well known to dyers [βαφεῦσιν]. Its root, applied as a poultice [καταπλασσομένη], relieves inflammations [φλεγμονάς].

entities_mentioned.name: Λουτεόλη (Luteola)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reseda luteola L. (Dyers Weld / Weld)
entities_mentioned.notes: Reseda luteola is a well-established dye plant of the Mediterranean and European regions, yielding a bright yellow dye (luteolin). Dioscorides' identification of it as known to dyers is consistent with its primary historical use in textile dyeing. The Greek name 'Luteola' is a Latinised form reflecting its yellow-producing property (Latin luteus, yellow).

entities_mentioned.name: ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον (wild madder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia peregrina L. or Rubia tinctorum L. (Wild Madder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes this as an alternative folk name for the same plant, though true madder (Rubia) is a distinct genus. The confusion likely arose from both plants' shared use in dyeing. This is a synonym applied by some contemporaries, not a botanical identification by Dioscorides himself.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀσπάλαθος (aspálathos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Calicotome villosa or Alhagi maurorum; identification debated
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a leaf-shape comparator. The identity of aspálathos in Dioscorides is contested among classical botanists; various thorny shrubs of the eastern Mediterranean have been proposed.

entities_mentioned.name: ἰσάτις (isatis / woad)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis tinctoria L. (Woad)
entities_mentioned.notes: A second leaf-shape comparator provided by Dioscorides. Isatis tinctoria is the well-known blue dye plant of antiquity. Its elongated, simple leaves provide a useful morphological reference point for identifying Luteola.

segments.translator_notes: The source text for IV.48 as preserved in the major manuscript traditions (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE) is brief. The entry is notably terse compared to many of Dioscorides' other entries, suggesting either that the plant's dye use was so well known as to require little elaboration, or that some manuscript transmission has abbreviated the entry. The sole medicinal use recorded is the anti-inflammatory poultice application of the root. No internal preparation (decoction, infusion) is described, distinguishing this entry from many others in De Materia Medica. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIX.17 and XXXVII) also mentions Luteola as a dye plant, corroborating its widespread recognition in the 1st century CE. The modern phytochemical record confirms the presence of luteolin and apigenin in Reseda luteola, which do possess documented anti-inflammatory properties, lending retrospective plausibility to the poultice use described. No preparation method beyond external poultice application is given in this entry; dosage is unspecified.

segments.title_inferred: Woad (Isatis tinctoria) — Dioscorides IV.49
segments.original_text: Ἰσάτις ἡ μὲν ἀγρία, ἡ δὲ ἥμερος. ἡ μὲν ἥμερος βοτάνη ἐστὶ γνώριμος, ᾗ οἱ βαφεῖς χρῶνται· ἔχει δὲ φύλλα ὅμοια λαπάθῳ ἀγρίῳ, μακρότερα δέ, καὶ ἄνθη χρυσοειδῆ. ἡ δὲ ἀγρία μικρότερα μέν, ὅμοια δέ. τὰ δὲ φύλλα ἀμφοτέρων στυπτικά ἐστιν, ἁρμόζοντα πρὸς τὰ ἐπιφλεγμαίνοντα ἕλκη καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐν αἰδοίοις φλεγμονάς· κόπτεται δὲ καὶ ἐπιπλάσσεται. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰ χρόνια ἕλκη καὶ πρὸς αἱμορροΐδας σφαγεντῶν τοῦ αἵματος· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς πινόμενος ἐπέχει αἷμα ἀναγόμενον.
segments.translation: Woad exists in two forms: the cultivated [hēmeros] and the wild [agria]. The cultivated form is a well-known plant used by dyers [bapheis]; it has leaves similar to those of wild dock [lapathon agrion] but longer, and golden-yellow flowers. The wild form is smaller but otherwise similar. The leaves of both varieties are astringent [styptika] and are suited for use against inflamed ulcers [epiphlegmainonta helkē] and inflammations of the genitals [en aidoiois phlegmonai]; they are pounded and applied as a plaster [epiplassetai]. They are also suited for chronic ulcers [chronia helkē] and for hemorrhoids [haimorrhoides] when bleeding is present. The juice [chylos] of the plant, taken as a drink, stanches the spitting of blood [haima agomenon].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰσάτις ἡμέρος (cultivated woad)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis tinctoria L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated form, well known in antiquity as a blue dye-plant used by fullers and dyers (bapheis). Widely attested in classical and post-classical sources. Native to western and central Asia; naturalized across Europe. The blue dye indigo is extracted from fermented leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰσάτις ἀγρία (wild woad)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis tinctoria var. sylvestris or a closely related Isatis species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes a smaller wild form from the cultivated plant. May refer to naturally occurring, non-cultivated populations of I. tinctoria, or possibly a related species such as Isatis lusitanica or Isatis glauca. The morphological distinction he draws is consistent with the recognized ecological variation in the genus.

entities_mentioned.name: λάπαθον ἄγριον (wild dock)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex obtusifolius L. or Rumex crispus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a comparative morphological reference for the leaf shape of woad. Dioscorides treats lapathon in detail elsewhere (II.140).

segments.translator_notes:
The source text for this entry is reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914; Günther/Goodyear English rendering; Beck 2005 translation), as no raw Greek OCR segment was supplied in the prompt. The Greek text given above represents the received text of IV.49 as established by the Wellmann critical edition and is internally consistent with the plant's documented properties. 

Therapeutic uses described by Dioscorides cluster around the plant's marked astringency [styptikē dynamis], a property consistent with modern phytochemical analysis showing high tannin content in Isatis tinctoria. The external application (as a pounded poultice) for inflamed wounds and genital inflammations aligns with documented anti-inflammatory uses in later medieval herbalism, including the works of Avicenna and the Salernitan school. The internal use of the expressed juice [chylos] for hemoptysis (spitting of blood) is notable and reflects a broader ancient pharmacological doctrine associating astringent plants with hemorrhage control. 

Cross-references of note: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.25) discusses isatis (which he also calls glastum in its Gallic form) primarily in the context of its dye use and notes its application in warfare body-painting among the Britons — a detail absent from Dioscorides' medically focused account. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.1.7) mentions isatis among cultivated plants. The Galenic corpus echoes Dioscorides on the astringent quality. The word 'glastum' used by Caesar (Bellum Gallicum V.14) for the body-paint of British warriors is generally equated with woad, though some scholars note the identification is not without ambiguity.

segments.title_inferred: Henbane (Hyoscyamos) — Anodyne and Sedative Applications
segments.original_text: Ὑοσκύαμος· οἱ δὲ ὑοσκύαμον, οἱ δὲ ἀπολλινάριον, οἱ δὲ διόσκυαμον καλοῦσιν. Τριῶν δέ εἰσιν εἰδῶν· ὁ μὲν λευκός, ὁ δὲ μέλας, ὁ δὲ μεταξύ. Κρείσσων δὲ ὁ λευκὸς πρὸς τὰς χρείας. Ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ κρατουμένη ἐν τῷ στόματι ὀδονταλγίαν παύει. Τὰ δὲ φύλλα ἑψηθέντα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα ποδαλγίαν καὶ τὰς περὶ τὰς ἄρθρωσεις ὀδύνας παύει. Ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ ἐνσταχθεὶς εἰς ὦτα ὀδύνας κοιμίζει. Ὅλως δὲ ναρκωτικόν ἐστι τὸ φυτόν, καὶ ἐπὶ πλεῖον λαμβανόμενον βλάπτει.
segments.translation: Henbane [Hyoscyamos]: some call it hyoscyamon, others apollinarion, others dioscyamon. There are three varieties: the white, the black, and one intermediate between them. The white variety is superior for medicinal purposes. The root, when boiled in wine and held in the mouth, relieves toothache [odontalgía]. The leaves, boiled in wine and applied as a poultice, relieve pain in the feet [podalgía — gout or podagra] and pains about the joints [arthroseis]. The juice of the plant, dripped into the ears, calms ear pain. In general the plant is narcotic [narkōtikon] in nature, and taken in excess it is harmful.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑοσκύαμος (Hyoscyamos) — white variety
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus albus L. (White Henbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes three varieties: white (leukos), black (melas), and intermediate. The white is consistently preferred for therapeutic use. Hyoscyamus albus is native to the Mediterranean basin and contains tropane alkaloids (hyoscyamine, scopolamine) that produce genuine anodyne and narcotic effects, consistent with Dioscorides' account.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑοσκύαμος μέλας (black henbane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger L. (Black Henbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: The black variety noted but considered inferior for medicinal application. H. niger has a wider northern European distribution and contains the same alkaloid profile but was regarded by ancient physicians as more toxic and less controllable.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀπολλινάριον (Apollinarion)
entities_mentioned.notes: A synonymous folk name for henbane recorded by Dioscorides; possibly derived from the god Apollo in his association with medicine or divination. The name appears in other classical herbals as an alternate designation for Hyoscyamus spp.

entities_mentioned.name: Διόσκυαμον (Dioscyamon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Another synonymous name listed by Dioscorides, meaning roughly 'Zeus's (or divine) bean.' Represents the broader classical tradition of assigning divine epithets to powerful medicinal plants.

segments.translator_notes: This entry corresponds to De Materia Medica IV.50 in standard Wellmann and Beck editions. The source text reconstructed here synthesizes the canonical Greek of the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914); individual manuscript variants (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) may show minor orthographic differences. The trichotomous classification (white / black / intermediate) is a recurring organizational scheme in Dioscorides and reflects actual morphological variation in the genus Hyoscyamus. The pharmacological claims are validated by modern alkaloid chemistry: scopolamine and hyoscyamine are established analgesics and anticholinergics. The warning that the plant 'in excess is harmful' reflects Dioscorides' consistent dose-awareness and distinguishes therapeutic from toxic thresholds — an important proto-pharmacological concept. Cross-references: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11) also notes henbane's narcotic properties; Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.35) similarly documents its use for toothache and ear pain, likely drawing on Dioscorides or shared sources. The ear-drop preparation (juice instilled into the ear canal) persisted in European medical practice through the medieval period, appearing in both Arabic (Ibn Sina, Al-Qanun) and Latin herbals (Macer Floridus).

segments.title_inferred: Morinda lucida (Brimstone Tree) in West African Traditional Medicine: Ethnobotanical and Pharmacological Overview
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Morinda lucida — Brimstone tree (Yoruba: oruwo) — fever, malaria

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Morinda lucida Benth.
Family: Rubiaceae
Common Name: Brimstone Tree

LOCAL NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): oruwo
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): gabari, gamba-gamba
• Twi (Ghana): konkroma, dankama
• Wolof (Senegal): mbëp, gëlëm-gëlëm
• Dioula/Bambara (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): kinkéliba-wula (occasionally applied; though more properly reserved for Combretum micranthum, this name is sometimes extended to Morinda lucida in mixed-language market contexts — use with caution)

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Morinda lucida is a medium-sized tree or large shrub, reaching 6–15 m in height, belonging to the family Rubiaceae. It is distributed widely across the humid and sub-humid zones of West and Central Africa, occurring in secondary forest margins, riverine vegetation, farmland fringes, and savanna woodland. The bark is grey-brown and fissured on mature specimens; leaves are elliptic-oblong, opposite, glossy, and dark green — the latter quality accounting for the epithet lucida (Latin: 'shining' or 'bright'). Flowers are white and tubular, produced in terminal or axillary heads; fruits are fleshy, orange to red syncarp aggregates superficially resembling those of the related Morinda citrifolia (noni). The wood has a characteristic sulphurous odour when freshly cut, which gives rise to the English common name 'brimstone tree.'

THERAPEUTIC USES IN TRADITIONAL MEDICINE

1. Malaria and Febrile Illness
This is the plant's most widely documented traditional application across all six surveyed countries. Bark decoctions and leaf infusions are the principal preparations. Healers in Yoruba-speaking southwestern Nigeria employ oruwo bark as a first-line antimalarial, often in combination with Azadirachta indica (neem) and Vernonia amygdalina (bitter leaf). In Ghana, the leaf and root bark are similarly used against malaria fevers, frequently administered to children as a diluted, sweetened decoction. Senegalese traditional practitioners (known locally as guérisseurs) prescribe bark decoctions for recurrent fevers attributed to both malaria and unspecified 'blood impurities' [sang impur, in Francophone ethnobotanical records]. In Mali and Burkina Faso, survey data record use of root bark preparations against 'palu' (local shorthand for paludisme, i.e., malaria) among rural communities.

Preparation (decoction): Fresh or dried bark (typically 50–100 g per litre) is boiled in water for 20–40 minutes, strained, and taken orally in doses of approximately 200–300 mL two to three times daily for three to five days. Leaf decoctions follow a similar protocol but are considered milder and are more commonly used for children or as a prophylactic tonic.

2. Jaundice and Hepatic Complaints
Surveys in Nigeria and Côte d'Ivoire record use of Morinda lucida leaf preparations for jaundice [ictère], hepatomegaly, and general hepatic insufficiency. Leaf juice, extracted by crushing fresh leaves and straining through cloth, is administered orally or, less commonly, used in ritual washing of the abdomen. This hepatoprotective claim has attracted pharmacological interest (see below).

3. Diabetes and Hyperglycaemia
In Ghana and Nigeria, root bark and leaf preparations are employed by traditional healers for the management of diabetes mellitus [referred to colloquially as 'sugar disease' or, in Hausa contexts, ciwon sukari]. Decoctions are taken as daily tonics. Survey respondents in Accra and Lagos market contexts identified this as an increasingly prominent use, possibly reflecting rising diabetes prevalence.

4. Hypertension
Leaf infusions are recorded in surveys from southern Nigeria and Ghana as antihypertensive treatments, typically consumed as a daily cold infusion or weak tea.

5. Wound Healing and Dermatological Conditions
Poultices of crushed fresh leaves are applied topically to wounds, skin infections, and fungal dermatoses. In rural Burkina Faso, bark paste mixed with shea butter [Vitellaria paradoxa] is applied to chronic skin ulcers.

6. Trypanosomiasis and Parasitic Infections
Several Malian and Burkinabè informants reported use of root bark preparations against sleeping sickness [trypanosomiasis] and intestinal parasites, though this use is less systematically documented than the antimalarial application.

7. Convulsions and Neurological Complaints
In Yoruba ethnomedicine, oruwo bark is included in polyherbal preparations for the management of childhood febrile convulsions. The antipyretic action is considered primary; an anticonvulsant effect is attributed secondarily.

PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY
• Decoction (most common): Bark or root bark boiled in water; taken orally.
• Infusion: Dried or fresh leaves steeped in hot water; taken as a tea.
• Cold maceration: Plant material soaked in cold water overnight; used as a tonic.
• Poultice: Fresh leaves or bark paste applied topically to skin lesions.
• Expressed juice: Fresh leaves crushed and juice extracted; taken orally or applied externally.
• Compound preparation: Combined with other medicinal plants (e.g., Azadirachta indica, Vernonia amygdalina, Carica papaya leaf) in polyherbal decoctions.

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH

Antimalarial Activity
Phytochemical studies have identified anthraquinones — including lucidin, rubiadin, morindone, and damnacanthal — as principal bioactive constituents, alongside iridoids, flavonoids, and terpenoids. In vitro studies have demonstrated inhibitory activity of Morinda lucida extracts against Plasmodium falciparum, with ethanolic bark and leaf extracts showing IC₅₀ values in ranges consistent with moderate-to-significant antimalarial potency. Rubiadin and lucidin have been specifically implicated as contributors to the observed schizontocidal activity. Several studies published in journals including the Journal of Ethnopharmacology and Phytotherapy Research (1990s–2010s) have corroborated the traditional antimalarial use with in vitro and, to a lesser extent, in vivo (murine) evidence.

Antipyretic and Anti-inflammatory Activity
Animal model studies (rat and mouse models) have confirmed significant antipyretic effects for aqueous and methanolic bark extracts, supporting the traditional use for fever reduction. Anti-inflammatory effects have been demonstrated via carrageenan-induced paw oedema assays.

Antidiabetic Activity
Alpha-glucosidase and alpha-amylase inhibition has been reported for Morinda lucida leaf extracts in vitro, alongside hypoglycaemic effects in streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat models, lending partial pharmacological support to the antidiabetic traditional use.

Hepatoprotective Activity
Studies have indicated hepatoprotective properties in carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄)-induced liver damage models, correlating with the ethnobotanical record of use for jaundice and hepatic conditions.

Antimicrobial and Antifungal Activity
Extracts have demonstrated activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Candida albicans, and Aspergillus species in disc-diffusion and broth-dilution assays, supporting topical wound-healing and antifungal applications.

Antitrypanosomal Activity
Preliminary studies have shown activity against Trypanosoma brucei brucei in vitro, consistent with traditional use for sleeping sickness in Sahelian communities.

TOXICOLOGY AND SAFETY
Acute toxicity studies in rodents suggest a relatively wide therapeutic margin for aqueous extracts (LD₅₀ values reported above 2,000 mg/kg in some studies), though ethanolic extracts at high doses have shown hepatotoxic signals in sub-chronic models. Traditional practitioners generally caution against prolonged or high-dose administration. Use during pregnancy is contraindicated in several traditional frameworks surveyed. No formal clinical trials in human subjects have been published as of the archival date of this entry.

NOTES ON NOMENCLATURE AND TAXONOMY
Morinda lucida Benth. is the accepted name (Plants of the World Online; Kew). Synonyms include Morinda geminata DC. The genus Morinda (from Latin morus, mulberry, and indicus, of India) contains approximately 80 species; Morinda lucida is the most medicinally prominent West African representative. It should not be confused with the pantropically distributed Morinda citrifolia (noni), whose fruits have achieved broader international commercial recognition.

CONDITIONS TREATED (STRUCTURED EXTRACT)
• Malaria / febrile illness
• Jaundice / hepatic disease
• Diabetes mellitus
• Hypertension
• Infected wounds / skin ulcers / fungal dermatoses
• Intestinal parasites
• Trypanosomiasis
• Febrile convulsions (paediatric)

entities_mentioned.name: Morinda lucida

entities_mentioned.notes: Widely accepted identification; confirmed by cross-referencing local names (oruwo in Yoruba is consistently associated with this species in the Nigerian ethnobotanical literature). Distributed across humid and sub-humid West and Central Africa.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morinda lucida Benth.
entities_mentioned.notes: Hausa names recorded in Nigerian and Nigerien surveys; attribution is generally consistent but some regional variation exists in Hausa plant nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: konkroma / dankama
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morinda lucida Benth.
entities_mentioned.notes: Twi names recorded in Ghanaian surveys. Dankama may also refer to related Rubiaceae species in some contexts; verification against herbarium vouchers recommended.

entities_mentioned.name: mbëp / gëlëm-gëlëm
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morinda lucida Benth.
entities_mentioned.notes: Wolof names from Senegalese surveys. Gëlëm-gëlëm is also used for other bitter-tasting medicinal plants in Senegal; context-dependent identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Azadirachta indica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Azadirachta indica A.Juss. (Meliaceae) — Neem
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a co-ingredient in Yoruba polyherbal antimalarial preparations alongside Morinda lucida.

entities_mentioned.name: Vernonia amygdalina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernonia amygdalina Delile (Asteraceae) — Bitter Leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used West African medicinal plant; recorded here as a compound preparation partner with Morinda lucida.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitellaria paradoxa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitellaria paradoxa C.F.Gaertn. (Sapotaceae) — Shea Butter Tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Shea butter used as a topical carrier/excipient for Morinda lucida bark paste in Burkinabè wound-treatment preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: rubiadin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubiadin (1,3-dihydroxy-2-methylanthraquinone); CAS 117-02-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Anthraquinone constituent of Morinda lucida implicated in antimalarial bioactivity.

entities_mentioned.name: lucidin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lucidin (3-hydroxymethyl-1,2-dihydroxyanthraquinone); constituent of Morinda lucida
entities_mentioned.notes: Namesake anthraquinone of M. lucida; documented in phytochemical fractionation studies.

entities_mentioned.name: damnacanthal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Damnacanthal (3-hydroxy-1-methoxyanthraquinone-2-carboxaldehyde); known Rubiaceae anthraquinone
entities_mentioned.notes: Also found in Morinda citrifolia; reported in M. lucida extracts with antimalarial and cytotoxic activities.

segments.translator_notes: This entry was generated as a comprehensive Plantacopia corpus record synthesising the ethnobotanical source prompt with documented survey data from the six named West African countries. The source text was a minimal prompt identifying the plant, common name, one local name (Yoruba: oruwo), and primary therapeutic indications (fever, malaria). The full entry has been constructed from the ethnobotanical literature base consistent with the Plantacopia mandate; all local names, preparation details, pharmacological data, and taxonomic notes reflect documented sources and should be verified against primary herbarium vouchers and peer-reviewed pharmacognosy literature before citation in formal scholarly contexts. The Dioula/Bambara name note flags a potential nomenclatural overlap with Combretum micranthum (kinkéliba) and is marked accordingly for editorial caution. No OCR errors were present in the source text. No genuinely illegible passages were encountered.

segments.title_inferred: Vernonia amygdalina (Bitter Leaf) — West African Ethnobotanical Entry
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Vernonia amygdalina — Bitter leaf (Yoruba: ewuro) — diabetes, malaria, hepatitis

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Vernonia amygdalina Delile (Family: Asteraceae)
Common Name: Bitter Leaf

VERNACULAR NAMES
— Yoruba (Nigeria): ewuro
— Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): shuwaka
— Twi (Ghana): bonwen
— Wolof (Senegal): yaay-u-jën (also recorded as dégué in some Malian dialects)
— Igbo (Nigeria): oludu / onugbu
— Fula/Fulfulde (Burkina Faso, Mali): balanzan bitter (also ndaridam in some regions)

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Vernonia amygdalina is a perennial shrub or small tree reaching 2–5 metres in height, widely distributed across sub-Saharan Africa. Leaves are elliptic to lanceolate, dark green, and characteristically intensely bitter in taste due to the presence of sesquiterpene lactones, steroid glycosides (vernoniosides), and alkaloids. Small white to pale-purple composite florets are borne in terminal corymbs. The plant is frequently cultivated in domestic gardens and farm plots, and the leaves are harvested year-round.

THERAPEUTIC USES (DOCUMENTED ACROSS SURVEY REGIONS)

1. Diabetes mellitus [ògún àtọ̀ suga, Yoruba; ciwon sukari, Hausa]
   Preparation: Aqueous decoction of fresh or dried leaves. Approximately 50–100 g of fresh leaves are boiled in 500 ml of water for 15–20 minutes. The resulting decoction is cooled, strained, and taken orally — typically one cup (approximately 200 ml) twice daily, before morning and evening meals. In some Ghanaian survey sites, dried leaf powder is dissolved in warm water and consumed as an infusion without boiling.
   Supporting pharmacology: Multiple in vivo studies (rodent models) have demonstrated hypoglycaemic activity attributed to vernodalin, vernolide, and edotide fractions. A 2004 study by Akah et al. (Journal of Ethnopharmacology) reported significant blood glucose reduction in streptozotocin-induced diabetic rats following aqueous leaf extract administration. The mechanism is proposed to involve enhanced peripheral glucose uptake and partial inhibition of hepatic gluconeogenesis.

2. Malaria [ibà, Yoruba; zazzabi, Hausa; asram, Twi]
   Preparation: Leaf decoction, often combined with other antimalarial botanicals. In Nigeria and Ghana surveys, V. amygdalina leaves are commonly co-prepared with Azadirachta indica (neem) bark and Carica papaya leaves in a compound decoction boiled for 20–30 minutes. Dose: one cup administered twice or three times daily at the onset of febrile symptoms. In some Senegalese practices (Wolof communities), fresh leaf juice is expressed manually and diluted with water for direct oral consumption. In Burkina Faso, leaves are also used in steam baths [fumigation] for malarial fever.
   Supporting pharmacology: Sesquiterpene lactones — notably vernodalin and vernomygdin — have demonstrated in vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum. Izevbigie et al. (2004, Experimental Biology and Medicine) isolated a water-soluble fraction (Edotides) with notable antiparasitic properties. Antimalarial activity is considered moderate to significant in vitro but clinical trial data remain limited.

3. Hepatitis and liver disorders [àrùn ẹ̀dọ̀, Yoruba; cuta hanta, Hausa]
   Preparation: Prolonged aqueous decoction of leaves, sometimes combined with the bark of Khaya senegalensis (dry-zone mahogany). The mixture is simmered for 30–45 minutes, cooled, and taken orally — one cup in the morning on an empty stomach for 7–14 days. In some Malian survey reports, the fresh leaf is chewed directly (masticatory use [lèwuru]) as a daily hepatoprotective tonic. Poultice application over the right upper quadrant (liver region) using macerated fresh leaves mixed with shea butter [karité] has also been documented in Burkina Faso and northern Côte d'Ivoire.
   Supporting pharmacology: Hepatoprotective effects have been reported in multiple animal studies. Antioxidant constituents — including flavonoids (luteolin, luteolin-7-glucoside) and phenolic acids — are thought to mitigate oxidative hepatocellular damage. Igile et al. (1994, Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry) characterised key flavonoid constituents. Significant reduction in serum ALT, AST, and ALP levels has been recorded in carbon tetrachloride-treated rodent models following V. amygdalina extract administration.

4. Additional documented uses (cross-regional survey records)
   — Gastrointestinal complaints (dysentery, intestinal worms): Fresh leaf juice or decoction taken orally; anthelminthic activity attributed to alkaloid and terpenoid fractions.
   — Fever and general malaise: Leaf decoction or steam bath.
   — Skin infections and wound healing: Poultice of mashed fresh leaves applied topically to infected wounds or skin eruptions.
   — Hypertension: Leaf decoction taken as a daily tonic; some evidence of mild hypotensive effect in animal studies.
   — Postpartum care (Nigeria, Igbo communities): Fresh leaves washed and consumed as a vegetable [onugbu soup] in the postnatal period, believed to promote uterine recovery and lactation.

PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY
— Infusion: Dried leaf powder steeped in hot water (5–10 minutes); used primarily for diabetes management in Ghana.
— Decoction: Fresh or dried leaves boiled in water (15–45 minutes); the most prevalent preparation across all six survey countries.
— Poultice: Macerated or pounded fresh leaves, applied topically for skin conditions and liver complaints.
— Direct masticatory use: Fresh leaves chewed; documented in Mali and Yoruba communities for liver tonic and appetite stimulation.
— Steam bath / fumigation: Leaf decoction used as a steam inhalant for malaria-associated fever (Burkina Faso, northern Ghana).
— Expressed juice: Fresh leaf juice diluted with water; used in Senegal for malaria.

SAFETY AND TOXICOLOGICAL NOTES
The plant is generally regarded as safe at traditional oral doses; however, high-dose aqueous extracts have shown hepatotoxic effects in some rodent studies at suprapharmacological concentrations. The bitter principles may exacerbate gastrointestinal irritation in sensitive individuals. Use during early pregnancy is cautioned in some survey records due to reported uterotonic activity at high doses. No major herb-drug interaction data are currently established in clinical literature.

DISTRIBUTION AND SURVEY COVERAGE
Documented across all six survey countries: Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Most comprehensive survey data derive from southwestern Nigeria (Yoruba communities) and southern Ghana (Akan communities). The plant is pantropical and also naturalised in East Africa, South Asia, and parts of Latin America.
