segments.title_inferred: Bridelia ferruginea: West African Ethnobotanical Entry on Diabetes and Wound Healing
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Bridelia ferruginea — Obi ata (Yoruba) — diabetes, wound healing

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Bridelia ferruginea Benth.
Family: Phyllanthaceae (formerly Euphorbiaceae)

LOCAL NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): Obi ata; also recorded as Ira, Ìrà
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): Kirni; also Tsadar-masar in some northern Nigerian dialects
• Twi (Ghana): Opam-fufuo; also Opam-kokoo (red variant reference)
• Wolof (Senegal): Mbep; also Kër-bu-dëkk in some coastal communities
• Dioula/Bambara (Mali/Burkina Faso/Côte d'Ivoire): Sinin; also Guélé in Mossi-speaking areas of Burkina Faso

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Bridelia ferruginea is a deciduous to semi-evergreen shrub or small tree typically reaching 4–12 metres in height. The bark is characteristically rough and dark grey to brown, becoming fissured with age and exhibiting a reddish-brown [ferruginous] inner cortex upon cutting — the source of the specific epithet ferruginea ('iron-coloured' or 'rust-coloured'). Leaves are alternate, elliptic to oblong, 5–15 cm long, with a distinctive reticulate [net-veined] underside bearing fine rusty-coloured hairs along the midrib. Flowers are small, yellowish-green, and inconspicuous, borne in axillary clusters. Fruits are small, fleshy drupes, turning dark purple to black at maturity, and are consumed by birds and small mammals, aiding seed dispersal. The species is widely distributed across the West African savanna-forest transition zone, occurring in secondary forest, forest margins, farmland edges, and gallery forest.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Diabetes Mellitus [Antidiabetic Use]
Across Nigeria, Ghana, and Mali, the stem bark and root bark of B. ferruginea constitute one of the most frequently cited antidiabetic preparations in traditional medicine. Practitioners [traditional healers; Yoruba: babalawo or onisegun; Hausa: boka] administer preparations primarily to patients presenting with symptoms consistent with type 2 diabetes mellitus — characterised locally by excessive thirst [polydipsia], frequent urination [polyuria], progressive weight loss, fatigue, and in advanced cases, slow-healing wounds. In Yoruba ethnomedicine, the plant is considered a principal remedy specifically targeting the 'sweetening of urine' [ito adun], a direct vernacular correlation with glucosuria.

2. Wound Healing and Dermatological Conditions
The leaves, bark, and root preparations are extensively applied in wound management across all surveyed countries. Uses include the treatment of fresh lacerations, chronic ulcers [including diabetic foot ulcers, noted particularly in Nigeria], skin infections, furuncles [boils], and scabies. In Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, the plant is also applied to manage eczema and generalised pruritic [itching] skin conditions. The astringent [tannin-rich] quality of the bark is locally recognised and deliberately exploited to promote haemostasis [arrest of bleeding] and tissue contraction.

3. Antimicrobial and Anti-inflammatory Applications
In Senegal and Burkina Faso, decoctions of the bark are used for febrile conditions [including malaria-associated fever], sore throat, and oral infections including dental caries and gingivitis. Gargling with a diluted decoction is a recorded practice among Wolof communities in Senegal. Anti-inflammatory use is documented for rheumatic joint pain and post-traumatic oedema.

4. Gastrointestinal Disorders
Bark decoctions are administered for dysentery, diarrhoea, and intestinal parasitism across multiple surveyed sites. The astringency of the preparation is considered therapeutically appropriate for arresting hypermotility.

5. Reproductive and Gynaecological Use
In Mali and northern Côte d'Ivoire, root decoctions are used by traditional birth attendants to manage post-partum haemorrhage and irregular menstruation.

PREPARATION METHODS

[i] Decoction (most common method):
Stem bark or root bark is harvested, typically in 5–10 cm sections, washed, and cut into small fragments or coarsely powdered using a wooden mortar [Yoruba: odó]. Approximately 50–100 g of dried or fresh bark material is simmered in 500 ml–1 litre of water for 20–45 minutes until the liquid reduces by one-third and turns a deep reddish-brown colour. The preparation is strained through cloth or leaves and cooled. Dosage: one cup (approximately 200 ml) taken orally two to three times daily before meals for antidiabetic use; or applied topically for wound care.

[ii] Infusion:
Leaves (fresh or dried) are steeped in hot water for 10–20 minutes. Used primarily for mild febrile conditions and as a general tonic. Less common than decoction for primary therapeutic indications.

[iii] Poultice:
Fresh leaves are crushed or macerated [pounded] directly and applied as a wet mass to wounds, skin infections, and inflamed joints. The poultice is secured with a cloth and replaced one to two times daily. In some documented Nigerian practices, the leaf paste is mixed with palm kernel oil [Elaeis guineensis] or shea butter [Vitellaria paradoxa] to improve adherence and add emollient [skin-softening] properties.

[iv] Powder application:
Dried and finely ground bark is applied directly to wounds as a haemostatic [blood-clotting] and antimicrobial dusting agent. Documented in Burkina Faso and northern Ghana.

[v] Aqueous maceration (cold extraction):
In some Malian and Senegalese communities, bark fragments are soaked in cold water overnight and the resulting liquor consumed directly. Used for diabetes management and gastrointestinal complaints.

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH AND SUPPORTING EVIDENCE

A substantial body of laboratory and limited clinical research has investigated the pharmacological basis for the traditional uses of B. ferruginea:

Phytochemical profile: The plant is rich in tannins (ellagitannins and condensed tannins), alkaloids, flavonoids (including quercetin and kaempferol glycosides), saponins, terpenoids, and phenolic acids. The high tannin content is consistent with the observed astringent and haemostatic properties.

Antidiabetic activity: Multiple in vivo studies using streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat models have demonstrated significant hypoglycaemic [blood glucose-lowering] activity of aqueous and ethanolic bark extracts. A study published in the Journal of Ethnopharmacology (Ojewole, 2002, and subsequent replication studies) recorded statistically significant reductions in fasting blood glucose levels in treated animals, with effects attributed partly to α-glucosidase inhibition and enhanced peripheral glucose uptake. Tannin fractions have been specifically implicated in insulin-potentiating activity.

Antimicrobial activity: Extracts have demonstrated in vitro inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Candida albicans, supporting the traditional use in wound infection management and oral infections.

Anti-inflammatory activity: Ethanolic extracts have shown significant reduction of carrageenan-induced paw oedema in rodent models, with activity profiles suggesting both COX-inhibitory and membrane-stabilising mechanisms.

Wound healing: Topical application of B. ferruginea leaf extract has shown accelerated wound closure, increased tensile strength of healing tissue, and enhanced collagen deposition in excision wound models in rats, consistent with traditional poultice applications.

Toxicology: Acute toxicity studies have generally reported relatively low toxicity at therapeutic doses, with LD50 values in rodents suggesting a reasonable safety margin for short-term oral use. However, prolonged or high-dose consumption has not been systematically evaluated in humans, and concurrent use with synthetic antidiabetic agents (e.g., metformin, sulphonylureas) carries theoretical risk of hypoglycaemia [abnormally low blood sugar] and warrants caution.

CONSERVATION AND SUSTAINABILITY NOTES
Harvesting pressure on wild populations, particularly bark stripping, is a documented concern in heavily farmed areas of southwestern Nigeria and southern Ghana. Bark harvesting is frequently conducted non-sustainably (full ring-barking), which can kill individual trees. Community-based agroforestry initiatives in Ghana have begun to incorporate B. ferruginea as a boundary and medicinal species.

CROSS-REFERENCES
• Bridelia micrantha (a related species with overlapping uses in East and Central Africa)
• Elaeis guineensis (co-ingredient in some poultice formulations)
• Vitellaria paradoxa (co-ingredient in some topical preparations)
• Phyllanthaceae family entries

Local name 'Obi ata' confirmed in Yoruba ethnobotanical literature, though some sources record variant spellings (e.g., 'Ira'). The specific epithet ferruginea refers to the rust-coloured inner bark, a reliable field identification marker.

segments.title_inferred: Chromolaena odorata (Siam Weed) in West African Traditional Medicine: Wound Healing and Antifungal Applications
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Chromolaena odorata — Siam weed — wound healing, antifungal

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Chromolaena odorata (L.) R.M. King & H. Rob.
Family: Asteraceae
Common Name: Siam weed; also known as devil weed, triffid weed, and independence weed.

LOCAL NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): Akintola; also recorded as Ewe Akintola or Efinrin-nla in some localities.
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): Oyinbo, occasionally rendered as Toje or Ciyawar oyibo ('foreign weed').
• Twi (Ghana): Acheampong plant; locally referred to as Acheampongwura or Bonkye-mene ('the one that heals wounds') in some Ashanti-region surveys.
• Wolof (Senegal/Gambia): Xorom bu dëkk or informally as the 'herbe étrangère' in Francophone zones; Wolof vernacular names remain incompletely standardized across survey literature.
• Dioula/Bambara (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): Recorded variously as Wulonba or Kamalen-jiri ('youth tree') in Malian surveys; Fula-speaking communities in Burkina Faso employ the term Leppi.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Chromolaena odorata is a fast-growing, perennial, aromatic shrub, native to Central America and the Caribbean, now extensively naturalized across sub-Saharan Africa as an invasive weed. Stems are woody at the base, branching profusely; leaves are opposite, deltoid-ovate, with toothed margins and a characteristic pungent, resinous odor when crushed. Flower heads are borne in dense terminal corymbs, pale lavender to white. The plant colonizes disturbed land, forest margins, fallows, and roadsides with notable aggressiveness. Despite—or arguably because of—its invasive character, it has been widely incorporated into West African ethnomedical systems.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Wound Healing [Cicatrization / Hemostasis]
The most widely documented and cross-culturally consistent application is the topical use of fresh leaf material for the arrest of bleeding and acceleration of wound closure. Across Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso, healers and lay users alike apply crushed or squeezed fresh leaves directly to lacerations, abrasions, cutaneous ulcers, and post-circumcision wounds. The plant is considered among the most reliable first-aid hemostatic agents available in rural settings where pharmaceutical wound-care products are inaccessible. Ethnobotanical informants in Yoruba-speaking southwestern Nigeria frequently rank it alongside Carica papaya leaf and Bryophyllum pinnatum for wound management.

2. Antifungal and Dermatological Conditions
Leaf preparations—both fresh and dried—are applied as poultices or washes to treat ringworm [tinea corporis], athlete's foot [tinea pedis], candidal skin infections, and general pruritic dermatoses. Hausa herbalists in northern Nigeria prepare a concentrated leaf decoction applied warm to affected areas twice daily. In Senegalese surveys, Wolof informants report use against scabies and secondary bacterial infection of skin lesions. Twi-speaking communities in Ghana's Ashanti and Eastern Regions use the leaf extract diluted in water as a wash for neonatal skin conditions, though this practice is noted with some caution by contemporary ethnobotanists.

3. Malaria and Febrile Illness
Leaf decoctions taken orally are documented across all six survey countries as a treatment for malaria-associated fever. This use is particularly prominent in rural Mali and Burkina Faso, where informants describe boiling leaves and drinking the cooled liquid two to three times daily during febrile episodes. Whether the antiparasitic activity is direct or primarily antipyretic remains under pharmacological investigation.

4. Anti-inflammatory and Analgesic Applications
Poultices of fresh or slightly warmed leaves are bound over swollen joints, sprains, and areas of localized pain. In Côte d'Ivoire, Dioula healers combine C. odorata leaf with the bark of Nauclea latifolia for compound analgesic preparations used in post-traumatic swelling.

5. Gastrointestinal Complaints
Leaf infusions and decoctions are drunk for dysentery, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and intestinal parasitism, particularly in Yoruba and Fula ethnomedicinal traditions. Some informants combine the leaf with ginger [Zingiber officinale] or lime juice to treat nausea.

6. Respiratory Conditions
Steam inhalation of leaf decoctions is practiced in Ghana and Nigeria for relief of cough, common cold symptoms, and sinusitis. Leaves are boiled in a covered pot; the patient inhales the aromatic steam.

PREPARATION METHODS

(i) Fresh Leaf Poultice [topical — hemostatic / antifungal / anti-inflammatory]
Fresh leaves are gathered, washed if available water permits, and crushed between the palms or on a flat stone. The resulting pulp and expressed juice are applied directly to the wound, ulcer, or affected skin area and held in place—sometimes bound with a broad leaf or cloth strip. Reapplication occurs every few hours until hemostasis is achieved or the wound surface is visibly granulating.

(ii) Aqueous Decoction [oral — antipyretic / antidiarrheal / antiparasitic; topical — antifungal wash]
A substantial handful of fresh leaves (or an equivalent volume of dried, crumbled leaf) is placed in a clay or metal pot with approximately one liter of water and boiled for fifteen to thirty minutes until the liquid is noticeably darkened and aromatic. The decoction is strained through cloth and allowed to cool. For oral use, a cupful (roughly 200–250 mL) is consumed two to three times daily. For topical antifungal use, the warm decoction is applied to affected skin by cloth or direct immersion of the affected limb.

(iii) Simple Infusion [oral — mild antipyretic / digestive]
Leaves are steeped in hot but not boiling water for ten to fifteen minutes, producing a lighter preparation considered more appropriate for children or for mild febrile states. Sugar or honey is occasionally added to improve palatability.

(iv) Steam Inhalation
Leaves are placed in a large quantity of boiling water; the patient bends over the vessel and inhales steam, often with a cloth draped over the head to concentrate the vapor. Used for respiratory complaints.

(v) Dried Leaf Powder
In some northern Nigerian and Malian contexts, leaves are sun-dried and ground to a powder that is insufflated [blown] into wounds as a dry hemostatic agent, or applied as a dry antifungal dressing.

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH

The ethnomedical claims surrounding Chromolaena odorata have attracted substantial scientific investigation, and the plant is among the better-pharmacologically-characterized West African medicinal species.

• Wound Healing: Multiple in vitro and in vivo studies have demonstrated that aqueous and ethanol leaf extracts accelerate wound closure in experimental models. Key mechanisms include platelet aggregation promotion, stimulation of fibroblast proliferation [fibroplasia], and upregulation of collagen synthesis. A 2004 study (Irobi et al., referenced in Nigerian Journal of Natural Products and Medicine) documented significant reduction in wound-closure time in excision wound models in rats treated with C. odorata leaf extract compared to controls. The phenolic compounds—particularly flavonoids such as quercetin and isorhamnetin—are proposed as primary bioactive constituents.

• Antimicrobial and Antifungal Activity: Ethanol and methanol leaf extracts have demonstrated significant minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values against dermatophytes including Trichophyton rubrum and Microsporum canis, as well as against Candida albicans, in disk-diffusion and broth-microdilution assays. Alkaloids, saponins, and terpenes—particularly the sesquiterpene fraction—are implicated. Antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, relevant to secondary wound infection, has also been confirmed in multiple laboratory studies.

• Antiplasmodial Activity: Crude extracts of C. odorata have shown modest in vitro antiplasmodial activity against Plasmodium falciparum chloroquine-sensitive strains, though IC₅₀ values generally fall above the threshold considered clinically meaningful for standalone antimalarial therapy. The antipyretic effect, documented in rat models via reduction of yeast-induced pyrexia, may partly account for the ethnomedical perception of efficacy against malarial fever.

• Anti-inflammatory Activity: Carrageenan-induced paw edema models in rodents confirm anti-inflammatory activity of leaf extracts, consistent with topical analgesic applications. The mechanism appears to involve inhibition of cyclooxygenase pathways [COX-1 / COX-2], analogous to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.

• Phytochemical Profile: Documented phytochemicals include flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin), alkaloids, saponins, tannins, essential oils (dominated by monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes including β-caryophyllene and α-pinene), and phenolic acids. The pungent aromatic odor is largely attributable to the volatile terpene fraction.

SAFETY NOTES AND CONTRAINDICATIONS
Some ethnobotanical reports note caution regarding prolonged oral ingestion of strong decoctions, particularly in pregnant women—several informants across Nigeria and Ghana spontaneously cited potential abortifacient effects of high doses, though this claim lacks systematic experimental confirmation. Neonatal topical application, as noted in some Twi-community surveys, warrants further safety evaluation. Contact dermatitis has been reported occasionally in individuals with sensitized skin exposed to expressed leaf juice.

ECOLOGICAL AND SOCIOCULTURAL NOTES
As an invasive species, Chromolaena odorata generates complex relationships in West African agricultural communities. While regarded as an agricultural pest due to its smothering of crops and fallow land, it simultaneously functions as a readily available, free, and locally trusted pharmacopoeia resource. Several ethnobotanists have noted the irony that the plant's invasive success ensures its perpetual medicinal availability, and community attitudes toward the plant are accordingly ambivalent rather than uniformly negative. In parts of southeastern Nigeria, the plant's rapid wound-healing reputation has given rise to the folk explanation that the plant 'heals the land it wounds,' a conceptual parallel to its use in human wound care.

Local name attestations reflect data drawn from published ethnobotanical literature for these regions; Wolof and Fula vernacular names for C. odorata are less consistently recorded in the primary survey literature than Yoruba or Twi names, and the entries above should be treated as approximate. The pharmacological summary cites representative findings consistent with the peer-reviewed literature through approximately the mid-2020s; specific citation details (author, year, journal) are included where commonly referenced in secondary ethnobotanical literature but should be independently verified against primary sources before formal citation. segments.title_inferred: Parkia biglobosa (Locust Bean / Dawadawa) — West African Ethnobotanical and Pharmacological Overview
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Parkia biglobosa — Locust bean (dawadawa) — skin disorders

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Parkia biglobosa (Jacq.) R.Br. ex G.Don
Family: Fabaceae (Leguminosae), subfamily Mimosoideae
Common Names: Locust bean (English); dawadawa (Hausa, widely adopted across Sahelian West Africa); nété (Wolof, Senegal); iru or eware (Yoruba, Nigeria); dawa (Twi, Ghana); néré (French-language West African usage, from Bambara/Mandé: nèrè, Mali and Burkina Faso); soumbala or soumbara (Mandinka/Dyula trade name, Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso)

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION AND DISTRIBUTION
Parkia biglobosa is a large, deciduous savanna tree attaining heights of 15–20 m, with a broad, spreading, umbrella-shaped crown. The bark is dark grey-brown, rough, and fissured. Leaves are bipinnate. Flowers are red and arranged in distinctive pendulous, globose capitula. The fruit is a long, indehiscent pod (20–35 cm) containing seeds embedded in a sweet, yellow, mealy pulp [mesocarp]. The species is distributed across the Sudano-Sahelian and Guinea savanna zones, from Senegal and Gambia east to Sudan and Uganda, and south into northern Nigeria and Ghana. It is particularly abundant in agroforestry parkland systems where it is selectively retained by farmers owing to its multiple economic and medicinal values.

LOCAL NAMES BY LANGUAGE GROUP
— Yoruba (Nigeria): iru (refers primarily to the fermented seed condiment; the tree itself is called eware or igba-iru in some dialects)
— Hausa (Nigeria, Niger, Ghana): dawadawa (fermented seed product and, by extension, the tree); the tree is also called dorowa
— Twi / Akan (Ghana): dawa, agyinamoa-dua
— Wolof (Senegal): nété
— Bambara / Mandé (Mali, Burkina Faso): nèrè
— Dyula / Mandinka (Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso): néré, soumbala (the latter referring primarily to the fermented seed cake)
— Fula / Fulfulde (across the Sahel): néré, caski
— Moore (Burkina Faso): raogo

PARTS USED MEDICINALLY
All parts of P. biglobosa are exploited in traditional medicine: bark (most widely documented), roots, leaves, seeds, seed pulp [mesocarp], and flowers. The bark is by far the most frequently cited part in ethnobotanical surveys across all six countries.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Skin Disorders (primary condition documented for this segment)
The bark decoction is used topically and, in some formulations, orally to treat a spectrum of dermatological conditions including: fungal skin infections (dermatophytoses, ringworm [tinea capitis, tinea corporis]); scabies; infected wounds and ulcers (including tropical ulcers [phagadenic ulcers]); eczema and pruritic dermatoses; boils, abscesses, and furuncles; and general skin eruptions and rashes. In Ghana (documented among Akan-speaking communities) and Burkina Faso (Moore-speaking communities), pounded fresh bark is applied directly as a poultice [local: application of macerated plant material bound to the skin surface] to infected wounds, fungal lesions, and inflamed skin eruptions. In Nigeria (Yoruba and Hausa communities), bark decoctions are used as wash preparations [external lavage] applied twice daily to affected skin areas. In Senegal (Wolof and Serer communities), bark preparations combined with shea butter [Vitellaria paradoxa] are used as an emollient treatment for chronic eczema.

2. Malaria and Febrile Conditions
Decoctions of the bark and, less commonly, the roots are administered orally as antipyretic [fever-reducing] preparations. This use is documented in Nigeria, Ghana, Mali, and Burkina Faso. The preparation typically involves boiling 50–100 g of dried bark in approximately 1 litre of water for 20–30 minutes, allowing to cool, and administering one cup (approximately 200 ml) two to three times daily until fever subsides.

3. Respiratory Infections
Leaf and bark infusions [prepared by steeping plant material in hot water, not prolonged boiling] are used for the treatment of coughs, bronchitis, and pneumonia-like symptoms. In Mali, inhalation of steam from bark decoctions is practised for nasal and chest congestion.

4. Gastrointestinal Disorders
Bark decoctions are used in the management of diarrhoea, dysentery, and gastroenteritis across all surveyed countries. The astringent [tannin-rich] properties of the bark are widely recognized by practitioners. Root decoctions are additionally cited in Nigeria and Côte d'Ivoire for stomach pain and intestinal parasites.

5. Oral and Dental Health
Twigs are used as chewing sticks [dental hygiene implements] in Nigeria, Ghana, and Senegal. Bark infusions are used as mouthwashes for gum disease, toothache, and oral infections.

6. Diabetes Mellitus
Bark decoctions are used in Ghana and Burkina Faso as hypoglycaemic [blood-sugar-lowering] preparations. This use has attracted pharmacological interest (see below).

7. Hypertension and Cardiovascular Conditions
Bark and leaf preparations are used in Nigeria and Senegal as antihypertensive remedies. Oral decoctions are taken daily as a chronic management strategy.

8. Anaemia and General Debility
The fermented seed product (dawadawa/soumbala) is recognized by traditional practitioners as a restorative food-medicine [cf. nutraceutical], particularly for postpartum women and malnourished children. It is a significant source of protein, lipids, and micronutrients.

9. Obstetric and Gynaecological Uses
Bark decoctions are used to facilitate labour and as postpartum uterotonic [stimulating uterine contraction] preparations in Nigeria and Mali. Caution is noted in the literature regarding use in early pregnancy.

10. Snake Bite and Wound Healing
Root preparations are applied locally to snake bites in Burkina Faso and Mali; bark poultices are applied to lacerations and burns.

PREPARATION METHODS

[Decoction]: The most commonly documented method. Dried or fresh bark (typically 50–150 g) is placed in 1–2 litres of water and brought to a boil, then simmered for 20–45 minutes. The liquid is strained and consumed orally (for systemic conditions) or applied topically (for skin conditions, wounds). Some preparations call for the decoction to be concentrated by prolonged boiling.

[Infusion]: Dried leaves or powdered bark are steeped in freshly boiled water for 10–20 minutes, strained, and consumed. Used primarily for fevers and respiratory conditions.

[Poultice]: Fresh bark is pounded in a mortar until a paste is formed, which is applied directly to the affected skin surface, covered with a leaf bandage or cloth, and left for several hours or overnight. Used for skin infections, wounds, and fungal conditions.

[Maceration]: Bark or roots are soaked in cold water for 12–24 hours; the macerate is then filtered and consumed. This cold-water extraction is used in Senegal and Mali for hypertension and fever.

[Steam inhalation]: Bark decoction is prepared and the steam is inhaled while covered with a cloth. Used for respiratory infections in Mali and northern Ghana.

[Topical wash / lavage]: Warm decoction is applied with a cloth or used to bathe affected body areas. Particularly noted for skin disorders, wound care, and eye infections.

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH AND SUPPORTING EVIDENCE

Phytochemical constituents identified in P. biglobosa include: tannins (condensed and hydrolysable), saponins, alkaloids, flavonoids (including quercetin, luteolin, and their glycosides), phenolic acids, terpenoids, sterols (including β-sitosterol and stigmasterol), and trypsin inhibitors in the seeds.

Antimicrobial / Antifungal Activity: Multiple in vitro studies (notably from Nigerian and Ghanaian research institutions, 1990s–2020s) have demonstrated antimicrobial activity of P. biglobosa bark extracts against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Candida albicans, and several dermatophyte species (Trichophyton rubrum, Microsporum canis). These findings lend pharmacological support to the documented use of bark preparations for skin infections. The antifungal activity against dermatophytes is of particular relevance to ethnobotanical claims regarding ringworm and fungal dermatoses.

Antimalarial / Antipyretic Activity: Aqueous and ethanolic bark extracts have demonstrated in vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum in studies conducted in Nigeria and Burkina Faso. Anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties have been confirmed in rodent models, consistent with traditional fever-management use.

Antidiabetic Activity: Studies from Ghana and Burkina Faso have reported significant hypoglycaemic effects of bark extracts in alloxan-induced diabetic rat models, attributed in part to tannin and flavonoid fractions inhibiting α-glucosidase and α-amylase activity.

Antioxidant Activity: High total phenolic content and free radical scavenging activity (DPPH and ABTS assays) have been consistently reported, supporting use in inflammatory and degenerative skin conditions.

Wound Healing: Topical application of bark extracts has accelerated wound closure and reduced infection rates in excision wound models in rats, corroborating poultice use.

Toxicology: Available data suggest that aqueous bark extracts are of relatively low acute toxicity in rodent models at therapeutic doses, though high-dose administration has produced hepatotoxic effects in some studies. Traditional practitioners note that bark preparations should not be taken in excess or during early pregnancy.

ETHNOBOTANICAL NOTES
P. biglobosa occupies a culturally central role in West African agroforestry and food systems. The fermented seed product (dawadawa/soumbala/iru) functions simultaneously as a food condiment and a therapeutic agent, illustrating the blurred boundary between food and medicine [cf. nutraceutical, functional food] in West African traditional practice. The tree is protected under customary land-use norms in many communities and is not felled even when land is cleared for agriculture, reflecting deep cultural recognition of its value. Ethnobotanical surveys consistently note that knowledge of P. biglobosa medicinal use is held by both specialist healers [traditional medicine practitioners, herbalists] and non-specialist community members, suggesting deep and widespread integration into domestic health practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Parkia biglobosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parkia biglobosa (Jacq.) R.Br. ex G.Don — Fabaceae (Mimosoideae); Locust bean / African locust bean
entities_mentioned.notes: Species identity is unambiguous. Widely distributed savanna tree of sub-Saharan West Africa. Source text explicitly names the species. All local names (dawadawa, nété, nèrè, iru, soumbala) are well-documented synonyms in the ethnobotanical literature. Primary documented use in this segment is skin disorders, though the comprehensive entry covers the full therapeutic profile documented across six countries.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitellaria paradoxa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitellaria paradoxa C.F.Gaertn. — Sapotaceae; Shea tree / Shea butter tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a combination ingredient with P. biglobosa bark in Senegalese preparations for eczema. Identification is standard and unambiguous. Not the primary subject of this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: dawadawa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented seed product of Parkia biglobosa; also used as a vernacular name for the tree itself in Hausa
entities_mentioned.notes: Hausa term. Functions as both the name of the tree and the fermented condiment derived from it. The ambiguity is well-documented in the literature and is flagged here.

entities_mentioned.name: soumbala / soumbara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented seed cake of Parkia biglobosa; Dyula/Mandinka trade name
entities_mentioned.notes: Regional variant name (Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso) for the fermented seed product equivalent to dawadawa (Hausa) and iru (Yoruba).

entities_mentioned.name: iru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yoruba name for the fermented seed product of Parkia biglobosa; also used for the tree in some dialects
entities_mentioned.notes: Yoruba (Nigeria). Primarily denotes the fermented condiment; the tree is sometimes called eware or igba-iru.

segments.title_inferred: Vitex doniana (Black Plum) in West African Traditional Medicine: Anemia and Liver Conditions
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Vitex doniana — Black plum — anemia, liver

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Vitex doniana Sweet (Family: Lamiaceae / formerly Verbenaceae)
Common name: Black Plum; African Black Plum
Segment focus: Anemia; hepatic [liver] conditions

— LOCAL NAMES —
Yoruba (Nigeria): oriri; oori-nla
Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): dinya; duniya
Twi (Ghana): onyame-dua; fufuo
Wolof (Senegal): sump; soump
Dioula/Bambara (Mali/Burkina Faso/Côte d'Ivoire): ntaba; sebe
Fulfulde (Sahel region): lekki nyiiri
Moore (Burkina Faso): beoogo

— BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION —
Vitex doniana is a medium to large deciduous tree, reaching 15–25 m in height, with a spreading crown and deeply fissured, grey-brown bark. Leaves are palmately compound, typically with five obovate leaflets. Flowers are small, tubular, pale blue to violet, borne in terminal panicles. The fruit is a drupe, ovoid, turning shiny black-purple at maturity, approximately 2–3 cm long, with a sweet, edible pulp surrounding a hard stone. The tree occurs widely across the Sudano-Guinean savanna zone, riparian forests, and woodland margins from Senegal eastward through Mali, Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Nigeria, extending into East and Central Africa.

— TRADITIONAL THERAPEUTIC USES —

1. Anemia [iron-deficiency and general blood-deficiency states]
Across all six surveyed countries, the ripe fruit pulp and, more significantly, decoctions of the leaves and bark are prescribed by traditional healers [herbalists; diviners; 'babalawos' in Yoruba tradition] for conditions described variously as 'thin blood,' 'tired blood,' pallor, chronic fatigue, and post-partum blood loss — symptom clusters consistent with anemia in biomedical terminology. In Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, the leaves are combined with those of Moringa oleifera and dried Hibiscus sabdariffa calyces to prepare a daily tonic infusion. The fresh ripe fruit is consumed directly as a dietary supplement for anemia, particularly in Mali and Burkina Faso, where the fruit's iron and vitamin C content is recognized empirically by healers as restorative. In Nigeria (Yoruba and Hausa practice), the dried, powdered bark is stirred into warm millet or sorghum porridge and administered twice daily over a course of several weeks to patients presenting with pallor and weakness.

2. Hepatic [Liver] Conditions
The root bark and stem bark are the primary plant parts used across the Sahel and Guinea savanna zones for ailments attributed to the liver, including jaundice [described in Wolof practice as 'yellowing of the eyes'], hepatic swelling [hepatomegaly as perceived through palpation], and general 'liver weakness' or toxic overload following illness or excessive alcohol consumption. In Senegal (Wolof healers) and Mali (Bambara tradition), a decoction of the root bark — boiled for 20–40 minutes in water — is cooled, strained, and taken as an oral preparation, typically one cup (approximately 200–250 mL) two to three times daily for 7–14 days. In Burkina Faso (Moore-speaking communities), a leaf poultice is applied externally over the right hypochondrium [liver region of the abdomen] in conjunction with the oral decoction, particularly for perceived hepatic swelling. In Ghana (Akan/Twi communities), the inner bark is macerated in cold water overnight and the resulting infusion consumed on an empty stomach for liver complaints and to 'cleanse the blood.'

3. Additional Documented Uses (from surveyed literature)
— Malaria and febrile illness: Leaf decoctions taken orally; widely documented across all six countries.
— Wound healing: Fresh leaf poultice applied topically to cuts, ulcers, and skin infections.
— Dysentery and diarrhea: Bark decoction taken orally.
— Diabetes [described as 'sweet urine' sickness in some traditions]: Leaf infusions used in Nigeria and Ghana.
— Toothache and oral infections: Chewed stem (miswak-type use) or bark decoction used as a mouth rinse.
— Fertility and obstetric use: Root decoctions administered in some traditions to facilitate labor or treat post-partum complications.

— PREPARATION METHODS —

Infusion: Fresh or dried leaves (approximately 20–50 g per liter) steeped in boiling water for 10–20 minutes, cooled, and strained. Used for anemia, fever, and general tonic purposes. Cold maceration of inner bark in water (overnight) is also documented.

Decoction: Stem bark, root bark, or a combination (30–100 g dried material per liter of water) simmered over low heat for 20–40 minutes, strained, and cooled. Primary preparation for liver conditions and febrile illness. Dosage: 200–250 mL, 2–3 times daily.

Poultice: Fresh leaves crushed or pounded, applied directly to wounds, skin infections, or over the liver region for hepatic complaints. Sometimes warmed before application.

Powder in food: Dried bark ground to a fine powder, mixed into porridge or gruel (e.g., tuwo, tô, or ogi). Used for anemia and tonic purposes.

Fresh fruit consumption: Ripe drupes eaten directly for nutritional supplementation in anemia management.

— PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH —

A growing body of scientific literature provides partial corroboration for the ethnobotanical uses documented above:

1. Hematological / Anti-anemic Activity: Studies have demonstrated that V. doniana fruit contains measurable quantities of iron, ascorbic acid (vitamin C), and other micronutrients relevant to erythropoiesis [red blood cell production]. Phytochemical analyses of leaves and bark have identified flavonoids, iridoid glycosides, and phenolic compounds with potential roles in hematopoietic support. An in vivo study conducted in Nigeria (Olusanya et al., reported in regional phytotherapy journals) demonstrated that aqueous leaf extract administered to phenylhydrazine-induced anemic rats produced a statistically significant increase in packed cell volume (PCV), hemoglobin concentration, and red blood cell count compared to untreated controls, supporting the traditional anti-anemic use.

2. Hepatoprotective Activity: Several in vitro and in vivo studies have investigated the hepatoprotective potential of V. doniana bark and leaf extracts. Ethanolic and aqueous bark extracts have been shown to significantly reduce serum levels of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in acetaminophen- and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4)-induced hepatotoxicity models in rodents — biomarkers consistent with a liver-protective effect. These findings have been attributed to the presence of antioxidant flavonoids (including vitexin and isovitexin), iridoids, and terpenoids identified in bark extracts.

3. Antimalarial Activity: Aqueous and methanolic leaf extracts have demonstrated in vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum, consistent with the widespread use of V. doniana for febrile and malarial illness. Iridoid glycosides have been proposed as the primary bioactive constituents.

4. Antimicrobial Activity: Bark and leaf extracts have shown inhibitory activity against a range of bacterial and fungal pathogens in disk-diffusion assays, supporting wound-healing and anti-dysenteric uses.

5. Antidiabetic Activity: Preliminary in vivo studies have reported hypoglycemic effects of leaf extracts in streptozotocin-induced diabetic rodents, consistent with ethnobotanical reports from Nigeria and Ghana.

Note on safety: No systematic human clinical trials have been conducted to establish therapeutic dosage, safety profile, or drug interactions. Traditional preparations involve variable concentrations and are administered without standardization. Caution is warranted regarding root bark preparations, which may be more potent and carry greater risk of hepatotoxicity at high doses — a pharmacological paradox given the plant's use for liver conditions that has been flagged by some researchers.

— CONSERVATION STATUS AND NOTES —
V. doniana is currently listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List but is subject to significant harvesting pressure for bark and roots in several range states. Bark-stripping practices have been noted to damage or kill individual trees in some localities in Mali and Burkina Faso. Sustainable harvesting guidelines have been recommended by ethnobotanical field researchers.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitex doniana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex doniana Sweet (Lamiaceae; formerly placed in Verbenaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Medium to large deciduous savanna tree widely distributed across sub-Saharan Africa. Known vernacularly as 'Black Plum' or 'African Black Plum.' Edible drupes; all plant parts (fruit, leaves, bark, roots) used medicinally across West Africa. Phytochemistry dominated by iridoid glycosides (vitexin, isovitexin), flavonoids, and terpenoids.

entities_mentioned.name: Moringa oleifera
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moringa oleifera Lam. (Moringaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a combination ingredient with V. doniana in anti-anemia infusion preparations documented in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire. Known for high iron, vitamin C, and protein content.

entities_mentioned.name: Hibiscus sabdariffa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hibiscus sabdariffa L. (Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried calyces mentioned as a combination ingredient with V. doniana leaves and M. oleifera for anti-anemia tonic infusion in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire. Rich in organic acids and anthocyanins.

entities_mentioned.name: oriri / oori-nla
entities_mentioned.notes: Yoruba (Nigeria) vernacular name(s) for Vitex doniana. 'Oori-nla' (large oori) distinguishes the species from smaller related species. Orthography varies across sources.

entities_mentioned.name: dinya / duniya
entities_mentioned.notes: Hausa (Nigeria/Niger/northern Cameroon) vernacular names for Vitex doniana. 'Duniya' is also a common Hausa word meaning 'world/life,' which may reflect cultural significance attributed to the tree.

entities_mentioned.name: sump / soump
entities_mentioned.notes: Wolof (Senegal) vernacular name(s) for Vitex doniana. Widely recognized in Senegambian ethnobotanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: vitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitexin (apigenin-8-C-glucoside); CAS 3681-93-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Iridoid flavone C-glycoside identified in bark and leaf extracts of V. doniana. Associated with hepatoprotective and antioxidant activity in pharmacological studies.

entities_mentioned.name: isovitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isovitexin (apigenin-6-C-glucoside); CAS 29702-25-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurring flavone C-glycoside with vitexin in V. doniana extracts. Documented antioxidant and hepatoprotective properties in experimental models.

segments.title_inferred: Lannea acida (Wild Grape) in West African Traditional Medicine: Ethnobotanical Survey of Uses for Dysentery and Diarrhea
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Lannea acida — Wild grape — dysentery, diarrhea

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Lannea acida A.Rich. (Family: Anacardiaceae)
Common Name: Wild Grape; African Wild Grape

LOCAL NAMES:
— Yoruba (Nigeria): 'Atori' or 'Orurún' [vernacular terms vary by region; also recorded as 'Ajéké' in some southwestern Yoruba-speaking communities]
— Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): 'Farar Kanya' or 'Yan gyada daji' [lit. 'bush groundnut relative'; usage inconsistent across dialects]
— Twi (Ghana): 'Kuntunkuri' [also rendered 'Konton-kuro' in some Asante Twi-speaking communities of central Ghana]
— Wolof (Senegal/Gambia): 'Mbep' or 'Mbép bu dëkk' [the qualifier 'bu dëkk' distinguishing it from related Lannea spp. in local classification]
— Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): 'Ntomi' or 'Tomini' [recorded in surveys from Sikasso region, Mali, and Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso]
— Mooré (Burkina Faso): 'Kamsé' [documented among Mossi communities of the central plateau]

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:
Lannea acida is a deciduous shrub or small tree reaching 3–12 metres in height, belonging to the cashew family (Anacardiaceae). It is native to the Sudano-Sahelian and Guinea savannah zones of West Africa, distributed from Senegal eastward through Mali, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and Nigeria. The bark is grey to brown, longitudinally fissured with age. Leaves are alternate, imparipinnate [odd-pinnate], with 3–5 pairs of leaflets; leaflets are elliptic to obovate, glabrous or sparsely pubescent on the lower surface. Flowers are small, yellowish-green, borne in axillary racemes [racemose inflorescences], appearing before or with new leaves in the dry season (approximately November–March across the Sahel). Fruits are fleshy drupes [stone fruits], ellipsoid, approximately 1–2 cm long, turning red to dark purple-black at maturity; they are edible and mildly acidic in taste, giving rise to the common name 'wild grape' and the species epithet acida. The seed contains a single hard endocarp [stone].

ETHNOBOTANICAL USES AND THERAPEUTIC APPLICATIONS:

1. PRIMARY INDICATIONS: DYSENTERY AND DIARRHEA
The treatment of dysentery [including amoebic dysentery, Entamoeba histolytica infection, and bacillary dysentery caused by Shigella spp.] and acute diarrhea constitutes the most widely documented therapeutic application of Lannea acida across all six survey countries. The bark, roots, and leaves are the principal plant parts employed. This usage is convergently recorded in ethnobotanical surveys conducted in Ghana (Ashanti and Northern Regions), Nigeria (Kogi, Kwara, and Kano States), Senegal (Casamance and Tambacounda regions), Mali (Sikasso and Mopti regions), Côte d'Ivoire (Hambol and Poro districts), and Burkina Faso (Centre-Ouest and Hauts-Bassins regions), suggesting either a shared pharmacological tradition or independently convergent recognition of the plant's antidiarrheal properties.

PREPARATION METHODS:

a) Bark Decoction [antidiarrheal; anti-dysenteric]:
The most commonly described preparation involves stripping sections of the outer and inner bark from mature stems (typically 15–30 cm lengths are harvested, avoiding complete ring-barking [girdling] of the tree). The bark is coarsely chopped or pounded and boiled in water (approximately 1–2 litres) for 20–40 minutes until the liquid reduces to a dark reddish-brown or tannin-rich [tannic] decoction. The decoction is strained through cloth and cooled. Dosage: adults consume approximately one cup (200–250 mL) two to three times daily until symptoms resolve, typically over 3–5 days. In paediatric use recorded among Mossi communities (Burkina Faso) and Hausa communities (northern Nigeria), the decoction is diluted and given in smaller quantities (approximately 50–100 mL) to children, sometimes sweetened with honey or locust bean paste to improve palatability.

b) Root Decoction [antidiarrheal; anti-dysenteric; antispasmodic]:
In Mali (Bambara practitioners, Sikasso region) and Côte d'Ivoire (Dioula-speaking communities), root sections are preferred over bark by certain traditional healers [herbalists], who report the root preparation to be more potent. Roots are washed, chopped, and decocted in a similar manner to the bark. The root decoction is specifically indicated for dysentery with blood in the stool [dysentery with haematochezia], which informants distinguish from watery diarrhea [choleric-type diarrhea] requiring different formulations.

c) Leaf Infusion [mild diarrhea; preventive use]:
Fresh or dried leaves are used to prepare a cold or warm infusion [tisane] for milder cases of diarrhea, particularly in Ghana (Twi-speaking communities) and Senegal (Wolof communities near Tambacounda). Leaves are washed, bruised, and steeped in boiled water for 10–15 minutes. This preparation is considered less potent than bark or root decoctions and is used for prophylaxis during seasonal diarrhea outbreaks, as well as for mild gastroenteritis in children.

d) Bark Poultice [topical application for wound healing; secondary use]:
Although not directly related to the antidiarrheal indication, pounded moist bark is applied as a poultice [cataplasm] to wounds, skin ulcers, and abscesses in several survey communities (recorded in Ghana and Burkina Faso). This application is attributed to astringent [tannin-mediated] and antimicrobial properties of the bark.

2. SECONDARY AND ASSOCIATED INDICATIONS:
— Oral hygiene: Stem sections are used as chewing sticks [dental hygiene sticks] in Nigeria and Mali, believed to reduce oral bacteria and prevent tooth decay.
— Fever and malaria: Bark decoctions are used as febrifuges [fever-reducing agents] in Burkina Faso and Senegal, sometimes in combination with Azadirachta indica (neem) bark.
— Wound healing and skin conditions: Topical bark paste for infected wounds and scabies, documented in Ghana and Nigeria.
— Aphrodisiac and reproductive health: Root preparations consumed by men are reported as aphrodisiacs in Mali and Côte d'Ivoire; bark decoctions are used for postpartum uterine cleansing in some Yoruba-speaking communities.
— Toothache: Bark chewed or held in the mouth against the affected tooth (recorded in Nigeria and Senegal).
— Haemorrhoids [piles]: Sitz baths prepared from bark decoctions are used in Burkina Faso (Mooré-speaking communities).

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL RESEARCH:

Phytochemical investigations of Lannea acida have identified several classes of secondary metabolites consistent with its reported ethnomedicinal properties:

— Tannins [condensed and hydrolysable]: Present in high concentrations in the bark and roots; tannins are well-established astringents that reduce intestinal secretion, precipitate mucosal proteins, and create a protective barrier on inflamed intestinal mucosa, providing a plausible mechanistic basis for the antidiarrheal and anti-dysenteric activity.
— Flavonoids [including quercetin and kaempferol glycosides]: Documented in leaf and bark extracts; these compounds possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity relevant to gut inflammation in dysentery.
— Saponins: Detected in bark and root extracts; reported to exhibit antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp., and Shigella spp., pathogens commonly implicated in infectious diarrhea.
— Alkaloids: Trace alkaloids have been identified in root extracts in some studies; their contribution to observed bioactivity remains under investigation.
— Phenolic acids [gallic acid, ellagic acid]: Identified in bark extracts; possess documented antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities.

In vitro studies (notably: Koné et al., 2004, Journal of Ethnopharmacology; Ouédraogo et al., 2012, African Journal of Pharmacy and Pharmacognosy; and related work from Ghanaian institutions) have demonstrated:
— Significant antibacterial activity of Lannea acida bark methanol and aqueous extracts against Shigella dysenteriae, Escherichia coli (enteropathogenic strains), Salmonella typhi, and Staphylococcus aureus at minimum inhibitory concentrations [MICs] ranging from 3.125–25 mg/mL.
— Antiamoebic [antiprotozoal] activity against Entamoeba histolytica has been suggested in some assays, though in vivo confirmation is limited.
— Antidiarrheal activity in rodent models (castor-oil-induced diarrhea model; charcoal meal transit model) has been demonstrated for bark aqueous and ethanolic extracts, with statistically significant reductions in stool frequency and intestinal transit time compared to controls.
— Acute toxicity studies in mice suggest relatively low toxicity of aqueous extracts at therapeutic doses; LD50 [lethal dose, 50%] values reported above 2,000 mg/kg body weight in oral administration studies, consistent with traditional safety profiles described by informants.

CLINICAL AND SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS:
No formal randomised clinical trials [RCTs] have been conducted on Lannea acida preparations as of the compilation of this corpus entry. Safety in pregnancy has not been systematically evaluated; traditional informants in Nigeria and Burkina Faso indicate that large doses of root preparations should be avoided in pregnant women. Interactions with pharmaceutical antidiarrheal agents (e.g., loperamide, oral rehydration salts) are undocumented. The high tannin content may reduce absorption of co-administered medications or dietary iron if consumed in large quantities over extended periods.

CONSERVATION STATUS AND SUSTAINABILITY:
Lannea acida is not currently listed as threatened under IUCN criteria; however, ethnobotanical informants in multiple survey sites (particularly in Burkina Faso and Mali) note localised population decline attributed to bark over-harvesting for medicinal use and land-use change (agricultural expansion, charcoal production). Sustainable harvesting guidelines (partial bark removal without girdling) are recommended by several national herbalist associations documented in the surveys.

REFERENCES (SURVEY-DOCUMENTED; PARTIAL LIST):
— Koné, W.M., Atindehou, K.K., Terreaux, C., Hostettmann, K., Traoré, D., & Dosso, M. (2004). Traditional medicine in North Côte d'Ivoire: screening of 50 medicinal plants for antibacterial activity. Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 93(1), 43–49.
— Ouédraogo, S., et al. (2012). Ethnobotanical and pharmacological study of plants used for treating diarrhea in Burkina Faso. African Journal of Pharmacy and Pharmacognosy.
— Ethnobotanical survey data: University of Ghana (Legon), Obafemi Awolowo University (Ile-Ife, Nigeria), Université Cheikh Anta Diop (Dakar, Senegal), Université de Ouagadougou (Burkina Faso).

entities_mentioned.name: Lannea acida
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lannea acida A.Rich. (Family: Anacardiaceae); synonym: Caillea acida (A.Rich.) Chiov.; commonly known as Wild Grape or African Wild Grape
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-characterised West African savannah tree. The species epithet acida refers to the tart, acidic taste of the fleshy drupe. Not to be confused with Lannea microcarpa (African plum) or Lannea kerstingii, which share overlapping ranges and some overlapping vernacular names. Placement in Anacardiaceae (cashew family) is taxonomically stable.

entities_mentioned.name: Entamoeba histolytica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Entamoeba histolytica (protozoan parasite; causative agent of amoebic dysentery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the aetiological agent of amoebic dysentery in the pharmacological research context.

entities_mentioned.name: Shigella dysenteriae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Shigella dysenteriae (gram-negative bacterium; causative agent of bacillary dysentery/shigellosis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as a target organism in in vitro antibacterial assays of Lannea acida extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Azadirachta indica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Azadirachta indica A.Juss. (Family: Meliaceae); Neem tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a companion plant in combination fever/malaria preparations in Burkina Faso and Senegal.

segments.title_inferred: Combretum micranthum (Kinkeliba) — West African Traditional Medicine Entry
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Combretum micranthum — Kinkeliba — malaria, fever (Senegal)

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant/Drug Name: Combretum micranthum G. Don (1827)
Family: Combretaceae
Common/Trade Name: Kinkeliba

LOCAL NAMES
• Wolof (Senegal): kinkeliba (also rendered quinquéliba in Francophone literature)
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): tafaruwa, dattijai
• Bambara/Mandé (Mali): quéléliba, kinkéliba
• Twi (Ghana): akyeampong (some sources record variants: akyempong)
• Yoruba (Nigeria): etiponla (recorded in some surveys; attribution requires verification)
• Fula/Peul: lélé
• Dioula (Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): kinkéliba

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Combretum micranthum is a large shrub or small tree (1–5 m) with dense, spreading branches. Leaves are opposite, elliptic to ovate, with prominent venation and a pale or greyish-green underside characteristically covered with silver-grey scales [lepidote indumentum]. Flowers are small, cream to yellowish-white, borne in dense axillary or terminal spikes. Fruit is a four-winged samara, pale straw to pinkish-tan at maturity. The plant is deciduous or semi-deciduous depending on rainfall regime and is widespread across the Sudano-Sahelian zone from Senegal eastward through Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and northern Nigeria, with populations extending into the Guinea savanna zone.

PRIMARY THERAPEUTIC USES
1. Malaria and Febrile Illness
Kinkeliba leaf decoction is the predominant and most widely documented traditional preparation across the West African Sahel and Sudan zones. Leaves are gathered, often dried in shade, and decocted in water. The resulting tea — sometimes commercially available in Senegal and Mali in sachet form — is administered orally as an antipyretic and antimalarial. Healers in Senegal (particularly among Wolof, Serer, and Lebu communities) and in Mali (Bambara communities) consistently identify it as a first-line treatment for uncomplicated febrile episodes presumed to be malaria.

2. Hepatic and Biliary Complaints
Across survey sites in Burkina Faso and Côte d'Ivoire, C. micranthum leaf preparations are used for conditions described by informants as 'liver heaviness,' jaundice, and bilious attacks [in older Francophone literature: 'embarras gastrique']. Preparations are typically aqueous decoctions taken orally over several days.

3. Dysentery and Gastrointestinal Disorders
Leaf and root bark decoctions are employed in Ghana and northern Nigeria for dysentery, diarrhoea, and intestinal parasitism. Informants describe the taste as strongly bitter and astringent [tannic quality], which practitioners associate with therapeutic efficacy.

4. Diabetes and Glycaemic Regulation
Ethnobotanical surveys in Mali and Senegal record use of C. micranthum leaf tea for managing symptoms attributed to diabetes mellitus, particularly polyuria and fatigue. This use is reported more frequently among older informants and has attracted pharmacological interest (see below).

5. Hypertension and Cardiovascular Complaints
In Burkina Faso (Mossi and Bissa communities) and in southern Mali, leaf decoctions are consumed regularly as a tonic believed to reduce blood pressure and improve cardiac function.

6. Urinary Tract Infections and Renal Complaints
In Senegal and Gambia, kinkeliba tea is prescribed by traditional practitioners for dysuria, haematuria, and urinary frequency. Its diuretic effect is widely acknowledged in ethnobotanical accounts.

7. Headache and General Malaise
Simple infusion of dried leaves in boiling water, analogous to a common tea preparation, is consumed widely across the region as a general-purpose tonic for fatigue, headache, and convalescence.

PREPARATION METHODS

a) Standard Leaf Decoction (most prevalent preparation)
Fresh or shade-dried leaves (approximately 30–50 g dried material per litre of water, though proportions vary by practitioner) are placed in water and brought to a rolling boil for 15–30 minutes. The liquid is strained, cooled, and consumed in doses of one to two cups [approximately 200–400 mL] two to three times daily. Duration of treatment ranges from three to seven days for acute febrile illness.

b) Cold-Water Infusion
In some Sahelian communities where fuel is scarce, crushed fresh leaves are macerated in cold water overnight and the liquid strained and consumed in the morning. This preparation is considered milder and is used more for tonic or preventive purposes.

c) Hot Infusion (tea)
Dried leaf fragments are steeped in near-boiling water for 5–10 minutes, analogous to conventional herbal tea preparation. This is the method used in commercially marketed kinkeliba sachets widely available in Dakar markets and exported to Francophone diaspora communities.

d) Poultice / Topical Application
In Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, bruised fresh leaves are applied as a poultice to wounds, skin infections, and swollen joints. The application is held in place with cloth and changed once or twice daily.

e) Root Bark Decoction
Recorded in Nigeria (Hausa practitioners) and Burkina Faso: root bark is scraped, dried, and decocted separately or combined with leaf material. Used principally for gastrointestinal complaints and as an anthelmintic.

CONDITIONS TREATED (Summary)
• Malaria and febrile illness (primary indication, pan-regional)
• Hepatic and biliary disorders (jaundice, 'liver complaints')
• Dysentery, diarrhoea, intestinal parasitism
• Diabetes mellitus (glycaemic management)
• Hypertension
• Urinary tract infection, diuresis
• Headache, fatigue, general malaise
• Wound healing, skin infections (topical)
• Rheumatic/joint pain (topical)

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL NOTES
Phytochemical investigations have identified a range of bioactive constituents in C. micranthum, principally:

1. Flavonoids and Flavonoid Glycosides: Vitexin and isovitexin (C-glycosyl flavones) are the most consistently reported constituents and are regarded as marker compounds. Hyperoside and rutin have also been isolated.

2. Tannins: Significant hydrolysable and condensed tannin content consistent with the astringency noted by traditional practitioners; likely contributes to antidiarrhoeal and antimicrobial activities.

3. Alkaloids: Traces reported in some analyses; precise characterisation varies between studies.

4. Terpenoids: Ursolic acid and related pentacyclic triterpenoids have been isolated from leaf material.

Pharmacological Studies:
— In vitro and in vivo antimalarial activity against Plasmodium falciparum has been demonstrated in multiple studies, with aqueous and ethanolic leaf extracts showing activity, though potency is generally moderate compared to reference antimalarials (chloroquine). The flavonoid fraction is implicated as a contributor.
— Hypoglycaemic effects have been demonstrated in streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat models (multiple studies, predominantly from Malian and Senegalese research institutions).
— Hepatoprotective activity has been reported in rodent models using CCl₄-induced hepatotoxicity protocols.
— Antioxidant activity (DPPH radical scavenging) is well-documented and is consistent with the high flavonoid and tannin content.
— Diuretic activity has been confirmed in rodent models, supporting the traditional use for urinary complaints.
— Antimicrobial activity against enteric pathogens (Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp., Shigella spp.) has been reported, partially validating antidiarrhoeal use.
— Antihypertensive and vasodilatory properties have been investigated in vitro and in isolated tissue preparations.

SAFETY AND TOXICOLOGICAL NOTES
Oral toxicity studies in rodents generally indicate a low acute toxicity profile at doses corresponding to traditional use levels. Prolonged or very high-dose consumption has not been systematically evaluated in clinical populations. No significant adverse events are documented in the ethnobotanical literature, though practitioners in some areas caution against use during pregnancy. Interaction with conventional antimalarials or antidiabetic medications has not been formally studied. Commercial kinkeliba tea is regulated as a traditional herbal medicine in Senegal and is subject to quality control by the Senegalese Pharmacopoeia authority.

ETHNOBOTANICAL SURVEY DATA SUMMARY
• Senegal: High citation frequency across Wolof, Serer, and Fula communities; considered a household remedy; commercially available.
• Mali: Widely used among Bambara and Dogon communities; primary indication is malaria and fever.
• Burkina Faso: Used by Mossi, Bissa, and Bwa communities; additional indications include hypertension and hepatic complaints.
• Côte d'Ivoire: Recorded in northern savanna zone (Dioula communities); use consistent with Malian patterns.
• Ghana: Recorded in northern regions; less prominent than in Sahelian zone; topical use more frequently noted.
• Nigeria: Recorded principally in Hausa-speaking northern communities; Yoruba use is less well-documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Combretum micranthum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Combretum micranthum G. Don (1827), family Combretaceae; kinkeliba or kinkelibah
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomy is well-established and unambiguous. Widely distributed across the Sudano-Sahelian zone. The binomial Combretum micranthum G. Don is accepted in all major African plant databases (GBIF, Plants of the World Online, Flora of West Tropical Africa). Should not be confused with Combretum glutinosum, which shares some vernacular names in certain localities and overlapping uses; field survey notes should specify if local names were confirmed against voucher specimens.

entities_mentioned.name: Kinkeliba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular trade/common name for Combretum micranthum; also rendered quinquéliba or kinkéliba in French-language literature
entities_mentioned.notes: The name kinkeliba is strongly associated with C. micranthum in Senegalese commerce and ethnobotanical literature; however, in some field surveys the name has occasionally been applied to Combretum glutinosum, a closely related species with similar morphology and uses. Voucher verification is recommended when name is encountered in survey data.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 8-C-glucosylapigenin; flavone C-glycoside; CAS 3681-93-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal marker compound isolated from C. micranthum leaves; implicated in antimalarial and antioxidant activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Isovitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6-C-glucosylapigenin; flavone C-glycoside; CAS 29702-25-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurs with vitexin in C. micranthum leaf extracts; frequently used as co-marker in phytochemical standardisation.

entities_mentioned.name: Plasmodium falciparum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plasmodium falciparum (Welch, 1897); causative agent of severe malaria
entities_mentioned.notes: Target organism in in vitro antimalarial bioassay studies referenced in pharmacological literature on C. micranthum.

segments.title_inferred: Cochlospermum planchonii (Yellow Cotton Tree) in West African Traditional Medicine — Antimalarial and Hepatoprotective Uses
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Cochlospermum planchonii — yellow cotton tree — malaria (Ghana)

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Cochlospermum planchonii Hook.f. ex Planch. (family Bixaceae / Cochlospermaceae)
Common English name: Yellow cotton tree; West African yellow cotton tree
Local vernacular names:
  — Yoruba (Nigeria): 'Pápá' or 'Igunmu'; also recorded as 'Aborisa'
  — Hausa (Nigeria / Niger / northern Ghana): 'Rawaya' (lit. 'the yellow one'), sometimes 'Tsadar masar' in wider Sahelian usage
  — Twi (Ghana): 'Kokoo ahoma' or 'Kyenekyene'
  — Wolof (Senegal): 'Diwël' or 'Dioulé'
  — Dioula / Bambara (Mali / Côte d'Ivoire / Burkina Faso): 'Boulounguê' or 'Balonge'
  — Mooré (Burkina Faso): 'Koanga'

Botanical description and distribution:
Cochlospermum planchonii is a small to medium-sized deciduous savanna shrub or subshrub, typically 0.5–2 m in height, arising from a large, tuberous, woody rootstock that persists through fire and dry season. It is distributed across the Guinea and Sudan savanna zones of West Africa, from Senegal and Gambia eastward through Mali, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and Nigeria into Cameroon. The plant is characteristically found in open woodland, fallow farmland, and rocky lateritic soils. It produces bright yellow, five-petalled flowers (hence the common name) before or alongside the emergence of deeply lobed, palmate leaves. The mature fruit is a capsule containing cottony fibres surrounding the seeds — a morphological feature shared with the closely related C. tinctorium and C. religiosum (the Asian silk-cotton). Roots are starchy, yellowish-orange in cross-section due to the presence of arjunolic acid and cochlospermins.

Ethnobotanical and therapeutic uses:

1. MALARIA (primary documented use — Ghana, Burkina Faso, Mali, Nigeria)
The root and root bark of C. planchonii constitute the most widely documented antimalarial remedy in the Guinea-Sudan savanna belt. In Ghana, ethnobotanical surveys conducted in the northern, upper-east, and Brong-Ahafo regions consistently record the plant as a first-line traditional treatment for uncomplicated febrile malaria, particularly in peri-urban and rural communities without ready access to biomedical facilities.
Preparation method (decoction): The dried or fresh roots, or root bark, are cut into small pieces, boiled in water for 20–45 minutes until a dark amber-to-orange decoction is produced, then cooled and strained. Adults typically consume one to two cups (approximately 200–400 mL) of the decoction two to three times daily for three to five days. In some Hausa-speaking communities of northern Nigeria and Niger, the decoction is prepared from roots combined with those of Guiera senegalensis J.F.Gmel. (Combretaceae) to enhance efficacy, a preparation locally called 'zogale malaira.'
Preparation method (infusion): In Sahelian communities (Senegal, Mali), the dried root is sometimes cold-infused in water overnight and drunk in the morning as a prophylactic tonic during the wet (transmission) season.
Preparation method (combined decoction): In Burkina Faso (Mossi communities), the roots of C. planchonii are combined with the stem bark of Khaya senegalensis (Desr.) A.Juss. and leaves of Azadirachta indica A.Juss. in a compound decoction used for severe febrile episodes attributed to malaria.

2. JAUNDICE AND HEPATIC CONDITIONS (Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso)
The root decoction is widely used across multiple ethnic groups for the treatment of jaundice [icterus], hepatitis-like syndromes locally described as 'yellow eyes' or 'yellow sickness,' and general hepatomegaly. In Twi-speaking communities of Ghana, a root decoction is drunk twice daily for one to two weeks for conditions described as 'liver sickness.' In Côte d'Ivoire, the treatment is sometimes administered alongside dietary restriction of palm oil and fats.

3. ANAEMIA AND HAEMATINIC USE (Nigeria, Burkina Faso)
The root decoction is consumed as a general haematinic [blood tonic] in communities across northern Nigeria and Burkina Faso, particularly for post-febrile anaemia and general debility following illness. The perceived mechanism in traditional explanatory frameworks relates to the orange-yellow colour of the root, interpreted through colour-sympathy doctrine as beneficial to blood production.

4. FEVER AND ANTIPYRETIC USE (widespread)
Across all surveyed countries, the root decoction or cold infusion is used as a general antipyretic for undifferentiated febrile illness in adults and children. In children, diluted decoctions are administered; tepid sponging with the decoction is also recorded in Hausa communities.

5. GASTROINTESTINAL CONDITIONS (Nigeria, Ghana)
In Yoruba-speaking southwestern Nigeria, the root decoction is used for dysentery and diarrhoea. In Ghana (northern regions), a weaker decoction is drunk for stomach pain and indigestion.

6. WOUND HEALING AND TOPICAL USE (Mali, Senegal)
A poultice prepared from the fresh or dried root, ground with water to a paste, is applied directly to wounds, skin infections, and oedematous swellings in Mali and Senegal. The paste is also applied to the forehead and temples as an external antipyretic.

Pharmacological and phytochemical research:
Cochlospermum planchonii has attracted substantial pharmacological investigation, particularly in relation to its antimalarial claims. The following represents a synthesis of published research findings as archived in the Plantacopia corpus:

— Phytochemical constituents: Major identified compounds include arjunolic acid (a pentacyclic triterpenoid), cochlospermins A and B (guaiane-type sesquiterpenes), flavonoids (kaempferol, quercetin glycosides), phenolic acids (gallic acid, ellagic acid), and tannins. The yellow-orange pigmentation of the root is attributable principally to arjunolic acid derivatives and to the presence of cochliodines.

— Antimalarial activity: In vitro studies using Plasmodium falciparum cultures (chloroquine-sensitive and chloroquine-resistant strains, including K1 and 3D7 strains) have demonstrated significant antiplasmodial activity of root extracts and isolated fractions. IC50 values for crude aqueous and ethanolic extracts reported in multiple studies range from approximately 0.8 to 12 µg/mL, with purified arjunolic acid fractions showing IC50 values in the low microgram range. In vivo murine models (P. berghei ANKA in BALB/c mice) confirm significant parasite suppression at oral doses of 100–400 mg/kg body weight. Clinical surveys (observational, non-randomised) in Ghana and Burkina Faso have recorded high patient-reported efficacy for uncomplicated malaria, though randomised controlled clinical trials as of the time of corpus compilation remain limited.

— Hepatoprotective activity: Hepatoprotective properties have been demonstrated in rodent models of CCl4-induced hepatotoxicity, with extracts significantly reducing serum ALT, AST, and bilirubin levels relative to controls, consistent with the traditional use for jaundice and hepatic conditions.

— Antipyretic and anti-inflammatory activity: Aqueous and methanol root extracts have demonstrated significant antipyretic effects in yeast-induced hyperthermia models and anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw oedema models in rodents, supporting the traditional antipyretic use.

— Toxicological profile: Acute toxicity studies in rodents report LD50 values for oral administration generally exceeding 2000 mg/kg body weight, suggesting a relatively wide therapeutic margin for traditional use doses. Sub-chronic toxicity studies at repeated doses show mild hepatic and renal changes at very high doses; at doses within the traditional preparation range, no significant organ toxicity has been reported.

Notes on related species and potential confusion:
C. planchonii is frequently confused in field surveys and local nomenclature with the closely related C. tinctorium A.Rich., which shares many vernacular names (particularly in Hausa, where both may be called 'rawaya') and is used for largely overlapping therapeutic purposes. Some researchers have suggested that a significant proportion of the ethnobotanical literature and pharmacological studies attributed to C. planchonii may involve C. tinctorium, as morphological differentiation in the field — particularly of roots — is difficult without mature floral material. Plantacopia entries treat these as distinct taxa pending further herbarium verification but note this important caveat.

Conservation and sustainability:
Harvesting pressure on C. planchonii is considered moderate to high in areas of intensive traditional medical practice, as the entire root system is typically extracted, killing the individual plant. Regeneration from seed is possible but slow. Cultivation trials have been initiated in Burkina Faso and Mali as part of traditional medicine sustainability programmes.

References (representative; see full Plantacopia bibliography):
Asase A. et al. (2005) — Ghana ethnobotanical surveys. Pousset J.-L. (1989) — Plantes médicinales africaines. Soh PN et al. (2009) — antimalarial activity. Diallo D. et al. — Mali traditional medicine documentation. Nigerian Natural Medicine Development Agency surveys (various years). TRAMIL network documentation (Côte d'Ivoire node).

entities_mentioned.notes: Target organism in in vitro antiplasmodial assays of C. planchonii extracts; both chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (K1) strains tested.
